# 🟤 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q1.** *Define lignite. Mention any two of its characteristic properties that distinguish it from bituminous coal.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Lignite, also called **brown coal**, is the **lowest rank of coal** formed from peat through partial coalification. It is a soft, brown-to-black solid fossil fuel with high moisture and low calorific value.

> **Property 1 (1 mark):** Lignite has a **high moisture content of 30–60%** (as-received basis), whereas bituminous coal has only 5–15%. This high moisture significantly **reduces its net calorific value**.

> **Property 2 (1 mark):** Lignite has a **low carbon content of 60–75%** (dry basis) and **lacks caking/coking ability**, making it unsuitable for metallurgical coke production, unlike bituminous coal which is the preferred coking coal.

---

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q2.** *What is the significance of proximate analysis in evaluating lignite as a fuel? List the four components it determines.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Significance (1.5 marks):** Proximate analysis provides a **rapid, empirical assessment** of lignite's combustion behavior, storage stability, and handling characteristics. For lignite, it is especially critical because the **high moisture** and **volatile matter** directly affect boiler design and spontaneous combustion risk.

> **Four Components (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
> 1. **Moisture** (M) — water content; high in lignite (30–60%).
> 2. **Volatile Matter** (VM) — combustible gases released on heating; 25–35% in lignite.
> 3. **Fixed Carbon** (FC) — residual combustible solid; 25–30% in lignite (low).
> 4. **Ash** (A) — non-combustible mineral residue; 3–15% in lignite.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 1, Question A]`

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** *With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **formation of lignite** from peat through the coalification process. Highlight the role of temperature, pressure, and time. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A lignite sample has the following composition by mass (as-received): C = 30%, H = 3.5%, O = 19%, S = 1%, N = 0.5%, Moisture = 32%, Ash = 14%. Calculate: (i) GCV by Dulong's formula, (ii) NCV, and (iii) theoretical air required per kg of fuel. (7 marks)*

---

#### 📘 Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Coalification Process (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** **Peat Formation** — Accumulation of dead plant material (trees, mosses) in **anaerobic swampy environments** like river deltas. Anaerobic conditions prevent oxidation, preserving organic carbon. Peat is soft, fibrous, and brown, with ~70–90% moisture.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** **Lignite Formation** — Burial under sediments causes **geostatic pressure** and mild thermal action (T < 100 °C). Bacterial action + heat cause **dehydration, decarboxylation, and loss of oxygen** from organic molecules. Result: **lignite** — still soft, brown, but with moisture reduced to 30–60% and carbon increased to 25–35% (as-received).

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** **Role of T, P, Time** —
- **Temperature:** Drives off moisture and volatiles; higher T = higher rank.
- **Pressure:** Compacts the organic mass, expelling water and gases.
- **Time:** Coalification takes **millions of years**; longer duration = higher rank.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** **Continued Metamorphism** — Lignite further transforms to **sub-bituminous → bituminous → anthracite** with progressive burial, heat, and time. The figure below shows the coalification series:

$$
\text{PEAT} \xrightarrow{\Delta T, P, t} \text{LIGNITE} \xrightarrow{\Delta T, P, t} \text{SUB-BITUMINOUS} \xrightarrow{\Delta T, P, t} \text{BITUMINOUS} \xrightarrow{\Delta T, P, t} \text{ANTHRACITE}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark — diagram):** Include a flow diagram with arrows indicating progressive transformation and key parameters (T, P, time).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:**
C = 30%, H = 3.5%, O = 19%, S = 1%, M = 32%, A = 14%

**(i) GCV Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
GCV = \frac{8080 \cdot 30 + 34500 \cdot \left(3.5 - \frac{19}{8}\right) + 2240 \cdot 1}{100}
$$

$$
= \frac{242400 + 34500 \cdot (3.5 - 2.375) + 2240}{100}
$$

$$
= \frac{242400 + 34500 \cdot 1.125 + 2240}{100}
$$

$$
= \frac{242400 + 38812.5 + 2240}{100} = \frac{283452.5}{100}
$$

$$
\boxed{GCV = 2834.525 \ \text{kcal/kg}}
$$

**(ii) NCV Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
NCV = GCV - 0.09 \cdot H \cdot 587 = 2834.525 - 0.09 \cdot 3.5 \cdot 587
$$

$$
= 2834.525 - 184.905 = \boxed{2649.62 \ \text{kcal/kg}}
$$

**(iii) Theoretical Air Required (2 marks):**

$$
O_2 = \frac{11.6 \cdot 30 + 34.8 \cdot 1.125 + 4.35 \cdot 1}{100}
$$

$$
= \frac{348 + 39.15 + 4.35}{100} = \frac{391.5}{100} = 3.915 \ \text{kg O}_2\text{/kg fuel}
$$

$$
Air = \frac{3.915}{0.232} = \boxed{16.875 \ \text{kg air/kg fuel}}
$$

---

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 1, Question B]`

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** *Discuss the **Indian deposits of lignite**, their geographical distribution, and the major challenges in their utilization. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Explain the differences between **proximate and ultimate analyses** of lignite. Why are both analyses essential for fuel evaluation in a thermal power plant? (7 marks)*

---

#### 📘 Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Indian Lignite Deposits (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Neyveli, Tamil Nadu:**
- **Location:** Cuddalore district, Tamil Nadu.
- **Operator:** Neyveli Lignite Corporation (NLC) India Ltd.
- **Reserves:** ~**3,000+ million tonnes** — **largest in India**.
- **Depth:** 60–120 m — open-cast mining.
- **Use:** Pit-head thermal power station (≈ **2,490 MW** capacity from lignite-based plants).

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Rajasthan:**
- **Locations:** Palana, Khari, Bikaner, Nagaur.
- **Reserves:** ~**1,200 million tonnes**.
- **Use:** Rajasthan Rajya Vidyut Utpadan Nigam (RRVUNL) power plants.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Gujarat:**
- **Locations:** Kutch, Bharuch, Surat, Bhavnagar.
- **Reserves:** ~**1,300 million tonnes**.
- **Use:** GMDC (Gujarat Mineral Development Corporation) operations; also exports to industries.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Other states:**
- **Jammu & Kashmir:** Nichahom, Kalakot, Rakh; lignite used locally.
- **Kerala:** Varkala (minor deposits), Kasaragod (exploratory).
- **Pondicherry, West Bengal:** Minor occurrences.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Challenges:**
- **High moisture** → high transportation cost per unit of heat.
- **Spontaneous combustion** during storage.
- **Environmental concerns** — mining displacement, dust, SO₂ emissions.
- **Low calorific value** → larger fuel volumes, larger boiler sizes.
- **Slacking** (disintegration) on drying → handling problems.

---

**Part (b) — Proximate vs Ultimate Analysis (7 marks)**

**Proximate Analysis (3 marks):**
- Determines **operational categories**: Moisture, Volatile Matter, Fixed Carbon, Ash.
- **Methods:** Air drying → moisture; controlled heating at 950 °C → VM; muffle furnace at 815 °C → ash; FC by difference.
- **Indicates:** Combustion behavior, ignition ease, storage stability, handling.

**Ultimate Analysis (2 marks):**
- Determines **elemental composition**: C, H, O, N, S (and sometimes Cl, P).
- **Methods:** CHN analyzer, Eschka method for S, etc.
- **Indicates:** Heat release potential (C, H), pollution potential (N, S), and combustion air requirement.

**Why Both Are Essential (2 marks):**
- Proximate analysis is **quick and cheap** — used for routine boiler-feed control.
- Ultimate analysis gives **accurate elemental data** for stoichiometric calculations (air, flue gas).
- For **lignite**, both are critical: high moisture (proximate) dictates drying; high O and S (ultimate) dictate air preheating and desulfurization.
- **Together** they enable complete combustion calculations, efficiency estimates, and emission predictions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on Lignite Problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in $(H - O/8)$** — Students forget that the **O/8 term SUBTRACTS** from H. If O is large (lignite has 15–25% O), this can reduce H to a small value, drastically lowering GCV. Always show the intermediate step.
> 2. **Unit confusion** — Dulong's formula gives GCV in **kcal/kg** (NOT kJ/kg). For kJ/kg, multiply by 4.184.
> 3. **Using NCV formula with dry H instead of as-received H** — Lignite's H is reported on the as-received basis; do NOT convert to dry basis before applying Dulong's formula.
> 4. **Ignoring H in NCV deduction** — If H = 0 is mistakenly assumed, NCV = GCV, which is wrong.
> 5. **Air calculation pitfall** — Forgetting the **0.232 (mass fraction of O₂ in air)**. Some students compute O₂ and stop; full marks require converting to **air**.
> 6. **Misclassifying lignite as bituminous** — Color alone is misleading; always use CV and moisture to classify.
> 7. **Skipping the diagram in theory questions** — In a 7-mark question, a missing block diagram costs at least **1–2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist — Lignite (Module 1)**

- [x] Lignite = **brown coal** = lowest rank coal between **peat** and **sub-bituminous**.
- [x] **Moisture:** 30–60% (as-received) — **HIGHEST** among coals.
- [x] **Carbon:** 60–75% (dry basis) — **LOW** compared to bituminous.
- [x] **Calorific Value:** 2,500–5,000 kcal/kg — confirms **lignite classification**.
- [x] **Non-coking** — cannot be used for metallurgical coke.
- [x] **Formation:** Peat → Lignite via **anaerobic burial, mild heat, geostatic pressure, time**.
- [x] **Indian Deposits:** **Neyveli (TN) — largest**, followed by **Rajasthan, Gujarat, J&K, Kerala (Varkala)**.
- [x] **Key Industries:** NLC (Neyveli Lignite Corporation) — pit-head CFBC power plants.
- [x] **Dulong's Formula:** $GCV = \dfrac{8080 \cdot C + 34500 \cdot \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240 \cdot S}{100}$ — always in **kcal/kg**.
- [x] **NCV formula:** $NCV = GCV - 0.09 \cdot H \cdot 587$ — 587 = latent heat of vaporization of water in kcal/kg.
- [x] **Theoretical O₂:** $O_2 = \dfrac{11.6 \cdot C + 34.8 \cdot \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 4.35 \cdot S}{100}$ kg O₂/kg fuel.
- [x] **Theoretical Air:** divide O₂ by **0.232** (mass fraction of O₂ in air).
- [x] **Proximate analysis gives:** Moisture, VM, FC, Ash — sums to 100%.
- [x] **Ultimate analysis gives:** C, H, O, N, S (elemental).
- [x] **High O content** in lignite reduces NCV; **high S** causes SO₂ pollution.
- [x] **Spontaneous ignition** is a major storage hazard due to high VM + moisture.
- [x] **Color:** brown to black — depends on rank within lignite subcategories (e.g., xylite, humodite).
- [x] **CFBC boilers** are the standard technology for lignite combustion in modern Indian power plants.
- [x] **Geological time-scale:** Lignite is typically **Cretaceous to Miocene age** (≈ 65–5 million years old).
- [x] **Kerala context:** Varkala beach cliff (Thiruvananthapuram) — small lignite seams exposed; relevant for Kerala PSC and KTU regional knowledge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 1 Notes — Lignite | KTU 2024 Scheme | OEBBT832 Fuel Engineering**
> *For maximum marks, always pair every numerical answer with a one-sentence **engineering interpretation** — this is what distinguishes a 14/14 answer from a 12/14 answer in KTU 2024 valuations.*

topics.title: Sub-Bituminous Coal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sub-Bituminous Coal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sub-bituminous coal** is an intermediate-rank black coal that falls between **lignite** (brown, low-rank coal) and **bituminous coal** (high-rank coal) in the standard coal classification hierarchy. According to the **ASTM D388** standard (the international benchmark accepted by KTU syllabus), sub-bituminous coal is defined as a non-agglomerating coal with a **gross calorific value (GCV)** ranging from **8,300 to 11,500 Btu/lb** (approximately **19,300 to 26,700 kJ/kg**) on a moist, mineral-matter-free basis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Sub-bituminous coal is characterized by higher moisture content (15–30%) and lower fixed carbon (35–45% on a dry, mineral-matter-free basis) compared to bituminous coal. It is widely used in **thermal power plants** for electricity generation and in **industrial boilers**.

### 1.2 Position in the Coalification Series

Coal is formed through a long geological process called **coalification**, where plant matter is progressively transformed under heat and pressure over millions of years. Sub-bituminous coal represents a "teenage" stage in coal's life cycle:

$$
\text{Peat} \rightarrow \text{Lignite} \rightarrow \text{Sub-Bituminous} \rightarrow \text{Bituminous} \rightarrow \text{Anthracite}
$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Coffee Roast" Model

Imagine coal maturation as a coffee roasting process:

- **Peat** = Raw, green coffee beans (just harvested plant matter).
- **Lignite** = Light roast (still soft, brown, moist).
- **Sub-bituminous** = Medium roast (darker, firmer, but still aromatic with moisture).
- **Bituminous** = Dark roast (hard, black, glossy).
- **Anthracite** = Burnt, carbonized roast (shiny black, very hard, almost pure carbon).

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a medium-roast coffee still retains some moisture and oils, sub-bituminous coal retains considerable **moisture (15–30%)** and **volatile matter (35–50%)** but has developed significantly more **fixed carbon** than lignite.

### 1.4 Key Identifiers

| Parameter | Sub-Bituminous Coal Range |
|---|---|
| Gross Calorific Value (GCV) | **19,300 – 26,700 kJ/kg** |
| Fixed Carbon (dry, mmf basis) | **35 – 45%** |
| Moisture (as-received) | **15 – 30%** |
| Volatile Matter (dry basis) | **35 – 50%** |
| Sulfur Content | **0.5 – 1.5%** (low) |
| Color | Black to dark brown |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ASTM D388 Classification Logic

The **American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) D388** classifies coal by rank based on two primary parameters:
1. **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** on a moist, mineral-matter-free basis.
2. **Fixed Carbon** or **Volatile Matter** on a dry, mineral-matter-free basis.

For sub-bituminous coal, the GCV falls between 8,300 and 11,500 Btu/lb. There are four sub-classes within sub-bituminous coal:

- **Sub-bituminous C**: GCV = 8,300 – 9,500 Btu/lb
- **Sub-bituminous B**: GCV = 9,500 – 10,500 Btu/lb
- **Sub-bituminous A**: GCV = 10,500 – 11,500 Btu/lb

### 2.2 Proximate vs. Ultimate Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proximate Analysis** determines moisture, volatile matter, fixed carbon, and ash by simple heating tests. **Ultimate Analysis** determines the elemental composition (C, H, N, S, O).

### 2.3 Why Sub-Bituminous Coal Matters in Engineering

- **Economic Power Generation**: Cheaper than bituminous coal, abundant in India (e.g., **Jharkhand, Odisha, Chhattisgarh, parts of Kerala**).
- **Lower Sulfur Emissions**: Burns cleaner than high-sulfur coals, reducing SO₂ pollution.
- **Boiler Design Implications**: Higher moisture means **more flue gas loss** and slightly lower flame temperature.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dulong's Formula** (GCV) | $\text{GCV} = 8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S$ | kcal/kg | Approximate GCV from ultimate analysis |
| **Modified Dulong (SI)** | $\text{GCV} = \dfrac{33813\,C + 144158\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9418\,S}{1000}$ | kJ/kg | SI version of Dulong's |
| **Net Calorific Value** | $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9\,H \cdot \dfrac{M}{100}$ | kJ/kg | NCV = GCV – latent heat of water vapor |
| **Air Required for Combustion** | $\text{Air} = 11.6\,C + 34.8\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 4.35\,S$ | kg/kg fuel | Stoichiometric O₂ demand × (100/23) |
| **Volatile Matter Ratio** | $\text{VM Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{VM}}{\text{FC} + \text{VM}}$ | dimensionless | Predicts combustion behavior |
| **Mineral-Matter-Free Basis** | $\text{X}_{mmf} = \dfrac{X}{100 - M_{ash} \cdot 1.08}$ | % | ASTM correction for ash |

> [!NOTE]
> In Dulong's formula, $C$, $H$, $O$, and $S$ are mass percentages of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and sulfur respectively, obtained from **ultimate analysis**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Thermal Power Plants** | Primary fuel for sub-critical and super-critical boilers (e.g., NTPC stations in India) |
| **Cement Industry** | Kiln firing where consistent, low-ash coal is preferred |
| **Industrial Steam Generation** | Textile, paper, and chemical industry boilers |
| **Domestic (Limited)** | Some household applications in rural India |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Dulong's Formula (Working Principle)

Dulong's formula is **empirical**, derived by observing that the heat released during combustion is the sum of the heats released by the combustion of each individual combustible element (C, H, S). The latent heat of water vapor is subtracted because in ultimate analysis, hydrogen's oxygen contribution has already been accounted for.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Each kg of carbon releases } 8080 \text{ kcal when burned to } \text{CO}_2 \\
&\text{Step 2: Each kg of hydrogen releases } 34{,}500 \text{ kcal when burned to } \text{H}_2\text{O (liquid)} \\
&\text{Step 3: Each kg of sulfur releases } 2240 \text{ kcal when burned to } \text{SO}_2 \\
&\text{Step 4: The oxygen already present in fuel combines with hydrogen,}\\
&\text{thus reducing the effective hydrogen available: } H_{eff} = H - \dfrac{O}{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

Combining:

$$
\text{GCV} = 8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S \quad \text{kcal/kg}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Calculating GCV of Sub-Bituminous Coal

**Problem:** A sub-bituminous coal has the following ultimate analysis:
- C = 42%, H = 5.5%, O = 18%, N = 1.2%, S = 0.8%, Ash = 8.5%
- Moisture (as-received) = 22%

Find the **GCV** and **NCV** per kg of fuel.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Identify elemental percentages}\\
&C = 42,\quad H = 5.5,\quad O = 18,\quad S = 0.8 \quad (\text{in \%})\\[6pt]
&\text{Step 2: Apply Dulong's formula}\\
&\text{GCV} = 8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\\[6pt]
&\text{Step 3: Substitute values}\\
&\text{GCV} = 8080 \times 42 + 34500 \times \left(5.5 - \dfrac{18}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 0.8\\[6pt]
&\text{Step 4: Compute each term}\\
&8080 \times 42 = 339{,}360\\[6pt]
&\dfrac{18}{8} = 2.25\\[6pt]
&5.5 - 2.25 = 3.25\\[6pt]
&34500 \times 3.25 = 112{,}125\\[6pt]
&2240 \times 0.8 = 1792\\[6pt]
&\text{Step 5: Sum the terms}\\
&\text{GCV} = 339{,}360 + 112{,}125 + 1792 = 453{,}277 \text{ kcal/kg}\\[6pt]
&\text{Step 6: Convert to kJ/kg (1 kcal = 4.186 kJ)}\\
&\text{GCV} = 453{,}277 \times 4.186 = 1{,}897{,}418 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Numerical Check Trap:** The Dulong formula gives GCV on a **dry, ash-free basis** if the percentages are taken from ultimate analysis directly. Since the analysis includes ash, we should first convert to a dry-ash-free basis OR explicitly state the result as per the given basis. In the above, the result **453,277 kcal/kg ≈ 1897 kcal/kg** of coal — which is unrealistically high because we did not convert to dry-ash-free basis.

**Corrected Approach (Convert to dry, mineral-matter-free basis first):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Total dry basis} = 100 - 22 = 78\%\\[6pt]
&\text{Dry-ash-free (daf) conversion factor} = \dfrac{100}{100 - 8.5} = 1.0929\\[6pt]
&\text{C}_{daf} = 42 \times 1.0929 = 45.9\%\\[6pt]
&\text{H}_{daf} = 5.5 \times 1.0929 = 6.01\%\\[6pt]
&\text{O}_{daf} = 18 \times 1.0929 = 19.67\%\\[6pt]
&\text{S}_{daf} = 0.8 \times 1.0929 = 0.874\%\\[6pt]
&\text{GCV}_{daf} = 8080 \times 45.9 + 34500 \times \left(6.01 - \dfrac{19.67}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 0.874\\[6pt]
&\text{GCV}_{daf} = 370{,}872 + 34500 \times (6.01 - 2.46) + 1957\\[6pt]
&\text{GCV}_{daf} = 370{,}872 + 34500 \times 3.55 + 1957\\[6pt]
&\text{GCV}_{daf} = 370{,}872 + 122{,}475 + 1957 = 495{,}304 \text{ kcal/kg (daf)}\\[6pt]
&\text{GCV}_{daf} \approx 495.3 \text{ kcal/kg on daf} \equiv 2073 \text{ kJ/kg (daf)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Calculate NCV:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9\,H \cdot \dfrac{M}{100}\\[6pt]
&\text{NCV} = 495{,}304 - 9 \times 5.5 \times \dfrac{22}{100} \text{ (in kcal/kg of fuel as-received)}\\[6pt]
&\text{NCV}_{ar} = 495{,}304 - 10.89 = 495{,}293 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Better approach for exam**: Apply Dulong's formula directly using the **as-received ultimate analysis** but remember that the resulting GCV is on the **as-received** basis. The numerical example above (per kg of fuel as-fired) gives a more realistic answer when applied without daf conversion.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Coal Ranks

| Property | Lignite | **Sub-Bituminous** | Bituminous | Anthracite |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture (% ar) | 30–60 | **15–30** | 2–15 | <2 |
| Fixed Carbon (% daf) | 25–35 | **35–45** | 46–86 | >86 |
| Volatile Matter (% daf) | 50–65 | **35–50** | 14–50 | <14 |
| GCV (kJ/kg, daf) | <19,300 | **19,300–26,700** | 26,700–35,000 | >35,000 |
| Color | Brown | **Black to dark brown** | Black, glossy | Black, metallic |
| Indian Example | Neyveli | **Jharia (lower seams)** | Jharia, Raniganj | Sparse in India |

### 3.4 Air Requirement Derivation

Stoichiometric oxygen needed per kg of fuel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&O_2 \text{ for C} = \dfrac{32}{12} \cdot \dfrac{C}{100} = 2.667\,C\\[6pt]
&O_2 \text{ for H} = \dfrac{32}{4} \cdot \dfrac{H}{100} = 8\,H\\[6pt]
&O_2 \text{ for S} = \dfrac{32}{32} \cdot \dfrac{S}{100} = S\\[6pt]
&\text{Net } O_2 \text{ required} = 2.667\,C + 8\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + S\\[6pt]
&\text{This is equivalent to: } 2.667\,C + 8H - O + S\\[6pt]
&\text{Air required} = \dfrac{\text{Net }O_2}{0.232} = 11.6\,C + 34.8\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 4.35\,S \text{ kg/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

For our example:

$$
\text{Air} = 11.6 \times 42 + 34.8 \times 3.25 + 4.35 \times 0.8 = 487.2 + 113.1 + 3.48 = 603.78 \text{ kg/kg fuel}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define sub-bituminous coal. List its key characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

Sub-bituminous coal is a **low-to-medium rank black coal** classified under ASTM D388 with a gross calorific value (moist, mineral-matter-free basis) between **8,300 – 11,500 Btu/lb (19,300 – 26,700 kJ/kg)**. It lies between lignite and bituminous coal in rank.

**Key characteristics:**
1. **Moisture content:** 15–30% (as-received)
2. **Fixed carbon:** 35–45% (daf basis)
3. **Volatile matter:** 35–50% (daf basis)
4. **Sulfur content:** 0.5–1.5% (relatively low)
5. **Color:** Black to dark brown, dull to slightly waxy luster
6. **Non-agglomerating** (does not cake on heating)
7. **Weathers easily** during storage due to moisture loss

#### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Compare lignite, sub-bituminous coal, and bituminous coal on the basis of (i) moisture content and (ii) calorific value.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Lignite | Sub-Bituminous Coal | Bituminous Coal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Moisture (%)** | 30–60 | 15–30 | 2–15 |
| **GCV (kJ/kg)** | < 19,300 | 19,300 – 26,700 | 26,700 – 35,000 |

> As rank increases, **moisture decreases** and **calorific value increases** due to progressive carbonization and loss of oxygen-containing functional groups.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each - Internal Choice)

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **classification of coal by rank** according to ASTM D388. Explain where sub-bituminous coal fits in this classification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis: C = 65%, H = 5%, O = 12%, N = 1.5%, S = 1%, Ash = 15.5%. Calculate the **GCV** and **NCV** using Dulong's formula. Take moisture = 0% (dry basis). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **ASTM D388** classification ranks coal based on:
1. **Fixed Carbon (FC)** content on dry, mineral-matter-free basis
2. **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** on moist, mineral-matter-free basis
3. **Volatile Matter (VM)** content on dry, mineral-matter-free basis

**Coal Rank Hierarchy (Increasing Rank):**

1. **Peat** – Pre-coal, fibrous plant matter
2. **Lignite** – Brown coal, GCV < 8,300 Btu/lb (moist, mmf)
3. **Sub-bituminous coal** – GCV 8,300 – 11,500 Btu/lb
   - Sub-class C: 8,300–9,500 Btu/lb
   - Sub-class B: 9,500–10,500 Btu/lb
   - Sub-class A: 10,500–11,500 Btu/lb
4. **Bituminous coal** – GCV 11,500–15,000 Btu/lb
5. **Anthracite** – GCV > 15,000 Btu/lb, FC > 86%

**Position of Sub-Bituminous Coal:**
Sub-bituminous coal is the **second-lowest rank** of black coal. It represents a transitional stage where most of the original plant cellular structure has been destroyed, but significant moisture and oxygenated compounds remain. It does **not agglomerate** (cake) when heated, distinguishing it from bituminous coal.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: C = 65%, H = 5%, O = 12%, S = 1%, N = 1.5%, Ash = 15.5%, Moisture = 0%

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Apply Dulong's formula}\\
&\text{GCV} = 8080 \cdot C + 34500 \cdot \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240 \cdot S\\[6pt]
&\text{Step 2: Substitute}\\
&\text{GCV} = 8080 \times 65 + 34500 \times \left(5 - \dfrac{12}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 1\\[6pt]
&\text{Step 3: Compute}\\
&= 525{,}200 + 34500 \times (5 - 1.5) + 2240\\[6pt]
&= 525{,}200 + 34500 \times 3.5 + 2240\\[6pt]
&= 525{,}200 + 120{,}750 + 2240\\[6pt]
&= 648{,}190 \text{ kcal/kg}\\[6pt]
&\text{Convert to kJ/kg: } 648{,}190 \times 4.186 = 2{,}713{,}323 \text{ kJ/kg} \approx 2713 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert**: The GCV calculated via Dulong is in **kcal/kg** of the fuel as given. The result 6,48,190 kcal/kg is mathematically correct but physically unrealistic for a coal sample (typical coals are 4,000–8,000 kcal/kg). This discrepancy arises because **Dulong's formula is calibrated for the dry, ash-free basis** but applied here without correction. Students often lose marks by not converting to daf basis first.

**Corrected Procedure (Convert to daf basis):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Excluding ash (15.5%): correction factor} = \dfrac{100}{100-15.5} = 1.183\\[6pt]
&\text{C}_{daf} = 65 \times 1.183 = 76.9\%\\
&\text{H}_{daf} = 5 \times 1.183 = 5.92\%\\
&\text{O}_{daf} = 12 \times 1.183 = 14.20\%\\
&\text{S}_{daf} = 1 \times 1.183 = 1.183\%\\[6pt]
&\text{GCV}_{daf} = 8080 \times 76.9 + 34500 \times \left(5.92 - \dfrac{14.20}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 1.183\\[6pt]
&= 621{,}352 + 34500 \times (5.92 - 1.775) + 2649.92\\[6pt]
&= 621{,}352 + 34500 \times 4.145 + 2649.92\\[6pt]
&= 621{,}352 + 143{,}002.5 + 2649.92\\[6pt]
&= 767{,}004 \text{ kcal/kg (daf)}\\[6pt]
&\text{GCV (as-fired)} = 767{,}004 \times (1 - 0.155) = 648{,}158 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

The two methods yield consistent results. For **NCV:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9 \cdot H \cdot \dfrac{M}{100}\\[6pt]
&\text{Since } M = 0\text{: } \text{NCV} = \text{GCV} = 648{,}158 \text{ kcal/kg} \approx 648.2 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Convert to kJ/kg:**

$$
\text{NCV} = 648{,}158 \times 4.186 = 2{,}713{,}189 \text{ J/kg} \approx 2713 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2022]**

**(a)** Explain **proximate analysis** and **ultimate analysis** of coal. Compare their significance for sub-bituminous coal characterization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sub-bituminous coal has the following composition by mass: C = 38%, H = 4.2%, O = 22%, S = 0.6%, N = 1.0%, Moisture = 24%, Ash = 10.2%. Calculate (i) **stoichiometric air required** per kg of coal, and (ii) **GCV** using Dulong's formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Proximate Analysis:**
A series of standardized heating tests that determine four parameters:
- **Moisture (M)**: Weight loss when heated to 105–110°C for 1 hour.
- **Volatile Matter (VM)**: Weight loss when heated to 950°C for 7 minutes (in absence of air).
- **Ash (A)**: Residue after complete combustion at 700–750°C.
- **Fixed Carbon (FC)**: Determined by difference: $\text{FC} = 100 - (M + VM + A)$

**Ultimate Analysis:**
Determines the elemental composition:
- **Carbon (C)**: By combustion and CO₂ absorption.
- **Hydrogen (H)**: By catalytic combustion.
- **Nitrogen (N)**: By Kjeldahl or Dumas method.
- **Sulfur (S)**: By Eschka method.
- **Oxygen (O)**: By difference: $\text{O} = 100 - (C + H + N + S + \text{Ash})$

**Significance for Sub-Bituminous Coal:**

| Aspect | Proximate Analysis | Ultimate Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Quick industrial classification | Detailed combustion/environmental study |
| **Key Output** | Moisture, VM, FC, Ash | C, H, N, S, O percentages |
| **For Sub-bit** | High moisture, moderate VM detected | Low C/H ratio, higher O content |
| **Engineering Use** | Boiler design, firing rate | GCV calculation, emission prediction |

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: C = 38%, H = 4.2%, O = 22%, S = 0.6%, Moisture = 24%, Ash = 10.2%

**Verification**: C + H + O + S + N + M + Ash = 38 + 4.2 + 22 + 0.6 + 1.0 + 24 + 10.2 = 100% ✓

**(i) Stoichiometric Air Required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Air} = 11.6\,C + 34.8\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 4.35\,S\\[6pt]
&= 11.6 \times 38 + 34.8 \times \left(4.2 - \dfrac{22}{8}\right) + 4.35 \times 0.6\\[6pt]
&= 440.8 + 34.8 \times (4.2 - 2.75) + 2.61\\[6pt]
&= 440.8 + 34.8 \times 1.45 + 2.61\\[6pt]
&= 440.8 + 50.46 + 2.61\\[6pt]
&= 493.87 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Gross Calorific Value:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{GCV} = 8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\\[6pt]
&= 8080 \times 38 + 34500 \times 1.45 + 2240 \times 0.6\\[6pt]
&= 307{,}040 + 50{,}025 + 1344\\[6pt]
&= 358{,}409 \text{ kcal/kg}\\[6pt]
&\text{In SI units: } 358{,}409 \times 4.186 = 1{,}500{,}300 \text{ kJ/kg} \approx 1500 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**:
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Students forget to subtract $O/8$ from hydrogen before applying Dulong's formula. This leads to overestimation of GCV by ~50,000 kcal/kg.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Confusion between GCV and NCV. NCV = GCV – 9H(M/100) only when moisture is present; otherwise NCV = GCV.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Forgetting to convert between kcal/kg and kJ/kg (1 kcal = 4.186 kJ). KTU examiners are strict about SI units in the final answer.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Not stating the basis (as-received, daf, dry) of the analysis.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Sub-bituminous coal has GCV = **19,300 – 26,700 kJ/kg** (8,300–11,500 Btu/lb) on moist, mmf basis.
- ✅ Lies between **lignite** and **bituminous coal** in rank.
- ✅ Moisture content is **15–30%** (high compared to bituminous).
- ✅ Fixed carbon is **35–45%** (daf); volatile matter is **35–50%** (daf).
- ✅ **Non-agglomerating** — does not cake during heating (distinguishes from bituminous).
- ✅ Classified as **Sub-C, Sub-B, Sub-A** based on GCV ranges.
- ✅ **Dulong's formula**: $\text{GCV} = 8080\,C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240\,S$ kcal/kg.
- ✅ **NCV correction**: $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9H(M/100)$ accounts for latent heat of vaporization.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric air**: $11.6\,C + 34.8(H - O/8) + 4.35\,S$ kg/kg fuel.
- ✅ Predominant in **Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, and parts of Odisha** in India.
- ✅ Used in **thermal power plants, cement kilns, and industrial boilers**.
- ✅ Lower sulfur (0.5–1.5%) makes it **environmentally cleaner** than many bituminous coals.
- ✅ Higher moisture → **lower adiabatic flame temperature** and **higher flue gas losses**.
- ✅ **ASTM D388** is the international standard for rank classification; KTU accepts this nomenclature.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bituminous Coal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Bituminous Coal # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bituminous Coal** is a dense, black, sedimentary rock formed from compressed and thermally altered (coalified) plant matter. In the ASTM D388 classification system, it is the middle-rank coal (between sub-bituminous and anthracite), containing **69–86 % fixed carbon** and **14–31 % volatile matter** on a dry, mineral-matter-free basis, with a heating value typically ranging between **24–35 MJ/kg (10,500–15,000 Btu/lb)**. Bituminous coal is the most abundant rank of coal mined globally and constitutes the principal feedstock for thermal power plants, coke ovens (via coking bituminous coal), and industrial boilers. The term "bituminous" derives from its tendency to soften and become plastic (forming *bitumen-like* tarry compounds) when heated in the absence of air, a property known as **coking plasticity**. > [!NOTE] > **Coalification Ladder (Low → High Rank):** > Peat → Lignite → Sub-bituminous → **Bituminous** → Anthracite → (Meta-anthracite) > > Bituminous coal occupies the *commercial sweet spot* — high enough in energy density to be economical, yet low enough in rank to be combustible in conventional boilers without excessive ignition difficulty. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Charcoal Burger" Think of bituminous coal as the **"well-done middle burger"** on the coalification grill: - **Peat** is raw, soggy vegetation (mostly water and decaying leaves) — terrible fuel. - **Lignite** is a lightly toasted patty — brown, crumbly, lots of moisture. - **Bituminous coal** is the **perfectly charred patty** — black, dense, smells tarry, and burns with a thick, smoky flame. It's rich in carbon and volatile tars (just like a juicy burger with fat dripping onto coals). - **Anthracite** is the overcooked charcoal briquette — almost pure carbon, hard to ignite but extremely hot once lit. The black "juice" that oozes out when you heat bituminous coal is literally **coal tar** (a mixture of aromatic hydrocarbons), and the fluffy residue is **coke** (porous carbon). This combination is what makes bituminous coal irreplaceable in **metallurgical coke-making**. ## 1.3 Sub-Categories of Bituminous Coal > [!IMPORTANT] > Bituminous coal is subdivided into **five sub-classes** in ASTM D388 based on **Fixed Carbon (FC)** and **Volatile Matter (VM)** content, expressed on a dry, mineral-matter-free (dmmf) basis: | Sub-Class | FC (% dmmf) | VM (% dmmf) | Agglomerating? | Typical Use | |-----------|-------------|-------------|----------------|-------------| | High Volatile A (HVAb) | 69 – 78 | ≥ 31 | Yes | Steam raising | | High Volatile B (HVBb) | 78 – 81 | > 22 – 31 | Yes | Steam raising | | High Volatile C (HVCb) | 81 – 86 | 22 – 31 | Yes | Industrial / domestic | | Medium Volatile (MVb) | 86 – 88 | 14 – 22 | Yes | **Coke making** | | Low Volatile (LVb) | 88 – 92 | 7 – 14 | Yes | Premium coking | ## 1.4 Physical & Visual Properties - **Color:** Jet-black with a glossy to dull lustre (vitreous or sub-vitreous). - **Streak:** Dark brown to black. - **Hardness:** 2–2.5 on the Mohs scale (softer than steel). - **Density:** 1.2 – 1.5 g/cm³ (apparent bulk density). - **Fracture:** Conchoidal to blocky. - **Structure:** Often shows distinct **bands** (vitrain, clarain, durain, fusain) — the four *macerals* of humic coal. - **Moisture (as-mined):** 2 – 15 %. - **Sulphur content:** 0.5 – 5 % (variable; critical for acid rain and SO₂ emissions). - **Ash content:** 3 – 30 % (mineral impurities). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Proximate Analysis — The "First Look" at Fuel Quality > [!NOTE] > **Proximate analysis** is the empirical decomposition of coal into four operationally-defined fractions. It does NOT tell you *what* the carbon is chemically bonded to, but it tells you *how the coal will behave* in a furnace. The four constituents (expressed in mass %): 1. **Moisture (M)** — water held within the coal matrix; lost at ≤ 110 °C. Acts as a thermal ballast (penalty on GCV). 2. **Volatile Matter (VM)** — gases + tars liberated when coal is heated at **950 ± 20 °C for 7 minutes** in a covered crucible (excluding moisture). Determines flame length, ignition stability, and smoke. 3. **Ash (A)** — incombustible mineral residue remaining after complete combustion at 815 °C. 4. **Fixed Carbon (FC)** — calculated **by difference**: $$FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$$ ## 2.2 Ultimate Analysis — The "Molecular Fingerprint" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ultimate analysis** reports the elemental composition of coal (dry, ash-free basis) as mass percentages of **C, H, N, S, O**. This is required for stoichiometric combustion calculations and emission predictions. Standard ultimate analysis of a typical **High Volatile A Bituminous Coal** on a dry, ash-free basis: | Element | Symbol | Typical Range (% daf) | |---------|--------|------------------------| | Carbon | C | 75 – 85 | | Hydrogen | H | 4.5 – 6.0 | | Oxygen | O | 5 – 15 | | Nitrogen | N | 1 – 2 | | Sulphur | S | 0.5 – 5 | ## 2.3 Coking Behaviour — The Metallurgical Identity > [!NOTE] > Bituminous coal's defining industrial property is **plasticity on heating** (coking). When heated from 350 °C → 550 °C through an inert atmosphere, the coal passes through: > - **Softening** (~350 °C) → particles begin to deform > - **Fluid/plastic stage** (~400 – 500 °C) → maximum fluidity (measured by Gieseler plastometer) > - **Resolidification** (~500 – 550 °C) → forms a coherent **coke mass** Only **Medium Volatile** and **High Volatile A** bituminous coals with controlled VM, low inert content, and good rheology produce **metallurgical coke** suitable for blast-furnace iron reduction. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are KTU-board-favorite and frequently tested in ESE, KTU university examinations, and internal assessments. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Notes | |---|--------------|----------|-------|-------| | 1 | Fixed Carbon | $FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$ | mass % | Air-dry or as-received basis | | 2 | Dulong's Formula (GCV) | $GCV = \dfrac{8080 \, C + 34500 \, \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240 \, S}{100}$ | kcal/kg | C, H, O, S in % mass | | 3 | Dulong's Formula (SI) | $GCV = 0.338 \, C + 1.428 \, \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 0.095 \, S$ | MJ/kg | Multiply kcal/kg by 4.1868 × 10⁻³ | | 4 | NCV from GCV | $NCV = GCV - \dfrac{9 \, H}{100} \times \left(587 - t\right)$ | kcal/kg | $t$ = flue gas temp (°C); H in % | | 5 | NCV (latent heat 600 °C) | $NCV = GCV - 0.09 \, H \times 587$ | kcal/kg | Approximate at 600 °C exit | | 6 | Stoichiometric O₂ | $O_2^{stoich} = \dfrac{32\,C}{12} + \dfrac{32\,H}{4} + 32\,S - O$ | kg O₂/kg fuel | Based on ultimate analysis | | 7 | Stoichiometric Air | $Air^{stoich} = \dfrac{4.31 \, C + 4.34 \, H_{2} - 1.03 \, O_{2} + 2 \, S}{100}$ | kg/kg | Per kg of fuel (O₂ in %) | | 8 | Hydrogen penalty | $\Delta H_{vap} = 9 \, \left(\dfrac{H}{100}\right) \times 587$ | kcal/kg fuel | Latent heat loss in H₂O from H | | 9 | Coal rank index (ASTM) | Uses FC + VM in % dmmf | — | Plots on Seyler's chart | | 10 | Carbon-to-oxygen ratio | $C/O = \%C / \%O$ | — | Increases with rank; ~15 for bituminous | ### 2.5 Engineering Significance > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Power plants (CFBC, pulverized coal):** Use HVAb/HVBb for cost-effective combustion. > - **Steel industry (blast furnace):** Demands MVb/LVb with low S (<0.7 %), low P (<0.05 %), and high CSN (Coke Strength after Reaction). > - **Cement kilns:** Bituminous coal with high VM (32–38 %) provides long, luminous flames for calcination. > - **Domestic / industrial boilers:** High VM bituminous coal is preferred for ease of ignition. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Dulong's Formula for GCV of Bituminous Coal Dulong (1880) empirically correlated the higher heating value (GCV) of a fuel with its elemental composition. The reasoning rests on **Hess's Law of constant heat summation**: the heat of combustion equals the sum of the heats of combustion of each combustible element, *less* the heat required to dissociate any pre-existing chemical bonds (chiefly C–O and O–H already present in the fuel). **Step 1 — Write combustion reactions of individual elements** (per unit mass of element): $$\begin{aligned} \text{C} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad ; \quad \Delta H = -8080 \text{ kcal/kg C} \\ 4\text{H} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad ; \quad \Delta H = -34500 \text{ kcal/kg H (gross, condenses H}_2\text{O)} \\ \text{S} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{SO}_2 \quad ; \quad \Delta H = -2240 \text{ kcal/kg S} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Account for oxygen already bonded in the coal.** When a fuel contains free or chemically bound oxygen, that oxygen has *already* burned some carbon/hydrogen. So we must subtract the latent heat equivalent of that internal combustion. Approximately, every 8 kg of pre-existing O combines with 1 kg of H, so the *effective* hydrogen available for new combustion is $\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right)$. **Step 3 — Weight by mass fractions.** If the fuel contains C, H, O, S as mass percentages, the contribution to GCV (per 100 kg of fuel) is: $$\begin{aligned} GCV &= 8080 \cdot \dfrac{C}{100} + 34500 \cdot \dfrac{H - O/8}{100} + 2240 \cdot \dfrac{S}{100} \\ &= \dfrac{8080\,C + 34500\,(H - O/8) + 2240\,S}{100} \quad \text{[kcal/kg of fuel]} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to MJ/kg** (multiply kcal/kg by $4.1868 \times 10^{-3}$): $$GCV = 0.338\,C + 1.428\,\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 0.095\,S \quad \text{[MJ/kg]}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU Pattern, 14-Mark Style) **Problem:** A bituminous coal has the following ultimate analysis (as-received basis): C = 72 %, H = 5.2 %, O = 9.0 %, N = 1.5 %, S = 0.8 %, Moisture = 3.0 %, Ash = 8.5 %. Find: (a) GCV by Dulong's formula; (b) NCV at a flue-gas exit temperature of 200 °C; (c) theoretical air required per kg of fuel. ### (a) Gross Calorific Value (GCV) $$\begin{aligned} GCV &= \dfrac{8080(72) + 34500\left(5.2 - \dfrac{9.0}{8}\right) + 2240(0.8)}{100} \\ &= \dfrac{581760 + 34500(5.2 - 1.125) + 1792}{100} \\ &= \dfrac{581760 + 34500(4.075) + 1792}{100} \\ &= \dfrac{581760 + 140587.5 + 1792}{100} \\ &= \dfrac{724139.5}{100} = 7241.4 \text{ kcal/kg} \end{aligned}$$ Converting to MJ/kg: $$GCV = 7241.4 \times 4.1868 \times 10^{-3} = 30.32 \text{ MJ/kg}$$ ### (b) Net Calorific Value (NCV) Using $NCV = GCV - \dfrac{9 \, H}{100} \times (587 - t)$ with $t = 200$ °C: $$\begin{aligned} NCV &= 7241.4 - \dfrac{9 \times 5.2}{100} \times (587 - 200) \\ &= 7241.4 - 0.468 \times 387 \\ &= 7241.4 - 181.1 = 7060.3 \text{ kcal/kg} \end{aligned}$$ In SI units: $$NCV = 7060.3 \times 4.1868 \times 10^{-3} = 29.56 \text{ MJ/kg}$$ ### (c) Theoretical (Stoichiometric) Air per kg of Fuel Using the standard empirical formula for solid fuels (with O₂ in % mass): $$\begin{aligned} \text{Air}^{stoich} &= 4.31\,C + 4.34\,H_2 - 1.03\,O_2 + 2\,S \quad \text{[all in % mass]} \\ \text{Air}^{stoich} &= 4.31(72) + 4.34(5.2) - 1.03(9.0) + 2(0.8) \\ &= 310.32 + 22.57 - 9.27 + 1.60 \\ &= 325.22 \text{ kg air / 100 kg fuel} \end{aligned}$$ Per kg of fuel: $$\boxed{\text{Air}^{stoich} = 3.252 \text{ kg / kg fuel} \;\approx\; 3.25 \text{ kg/kg}}$$ In volumetric terms (at STP, air density ≈ 1.293 kg/m³): $$V_{air} = \dfrac{3.25}{1.293} = 2.514 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{kg fuel}$$ ## 3.3 Verification via Energy Content Sanity Check A typical **High Volatile A Bituminous Coal** has GCV = 28–32 MJ/kg. Our result of **30.32 MJ/kg** lies comfortably inside this range ✔ — confirming both the fuel is correctly classified and Dulong's formula has been correctly applied. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Coalification Ladder — Position of Bituminous Coal ```mermaid flowchart LR peat["PEAT
FC less than 40
MC 70 to 90 percent
GCV 5 to 10 MJ per kg"] lignite["LIGNITE BROWN COAL
FC 40 to 55
MC 30 to 50 percent
GCV 10 to 20 MJ per kg"] subbit["SUB BITUMINOUS
FC 55 to 69
MC 15 to 30 percent
GCV 20 to 24 MJ per kg"] bit["BITUMINOUS COAL
FC 69 to 86
MC 2 to 15 percent
GCV 24 to 35 MJ per kg"] anth["ANTHRACITE
FC 86 to 98
MC 1 to 5 percent
GCV 35 to 38 MJ per kg"] meta["META ANTHRACITE
FC greater than 98
MC less than 1 percent
GCV 34 to 35 MJ per kg"] peat --> lignite --> subbit --> bit --> anth --> meta style bit fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 style peat fill:#8B7355,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF style lignite fill:#A0522D,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF style subbit fill:#556B2F,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF style anth fill:#1C1C1C,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF style meta fill:#000000,stroke:#FFFFFF,color:#FFFFFF ``` ## 4.2 Proximate & Ultimate Analysis — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD rawcoal["RAW BITUMINOUS COAL SAMPLE
100 grams"] subgraph prox["PROXIMATE ANALYSIS Block"] p1["1. Air dry at 110 deg C
MOISTURE M percent"] p2["2. Heat covered at 950 deg C
VOLATILE MATTER VM percent"] p3["3. Burn to constant mass at 815 deg C
ASH A percent"] p4["4. BALANCE BY DIFFERENCE
FC = 100 minus M plus VM plus A"] rawcoal --> p1 --> p2 --> p3 --> p4 end subgraph ult["ULTIMATE ANALYSIS Block"] u1["CHNS Elemental Analyzer
Reports C H N S mass percent"] u2["OXYGEN by difference
O = 100 minus C plus H plus N plus S plus Ash"] rawcoal --> u1 u1 --> u2 end subgraph calc["ENGINEERING CALCULATIONS Block"] c1["GCV via Dulong
GCV = 8080 C + 34500 H minus O by 8 + 2240 S over 100"] c2["NCV via latent heat
NCV = GCV minus 0.09 H times 587"] c3["Stoichiometric air
Air = 4.31 C + 4.34 H2 minus 1.03 O2 + 2 S"] p4 --> c1 --> c2 u2 --> c1 u2 --> c3 end output["FUEL PERFORMANCE METRICS
GCV kcal per kg
NCV kcal per kg
Air kg per kg fuel
Rank classification"] c2 --> output c3 --> output style rawcoal fill:#2F4F4F,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF style prox fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 style ult fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 style calc fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 style output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.3 Maceral Composition — Microscopic Architecture of Bituminous Coal ```mermaid flowchart LR mac["BITUMINOUS COAL
Maceral Group"] vit["VITRINITE GROUP
Vitrinite
Derived from wood and bark
Most abundant in bituminous coal
Glossy black bands"] in["INERTINITE GROUP
Inertinite
Fossilized charcoal
Fusinite and semifusinite
High reflectance"] ex["EXINITE GROUP
Exinite or Liptinite
Sporinite Cutinite Resinite
High volatile rich
Fluorescent under UV"] mac --> vit mac --> in mac --> ex vit -- "Forms COKE matrix
during carbonization" --> coke["METALLURGICAL COKE
Porous carbon residue"] ex -- "Drives VM release
during coking" --> coke style mac fill:#1C1C1C,stroke:#FFFFFF,color:#FFFFFF style vit fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 style in fill:#696969,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF style ex fill:#DAA520,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 style coke fill:#2F2F2F,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Define bituminous coal. State any two characteristic properties that distinguish it from anthracite.** **Model Answer:** > Bituminous coal is a middle-rank coal containing 69–86 % fixed carbon and 14–31 % volatile matter on a dry, mineral-matter-free basis, with GCV between 24–35 MJ/kg. Two distinguishing properties versus anthracite: 1. **Higher volatile matter (14–31 % vs 2–14 %)** — bituminous coal burns with a long, smoky, luminous flame, while anthracite produces a short, blue, almost smokeless flame. 2. **Agglomerating / coking behaviour** — bituminous coal softens and fuses on heating to form coke; anthracite does not fuse (non-agglomerating). 3. *(Additional valid point)* Bituminous coal has lower fixed carbon, higher H/C atomic ratio, and lower reflectance (vitrinite $R_o$ = 0.6–1.9 %) compared to anthracite ($R_o$ > 2.5 %). --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **Explain the significance of proximate analysis in evaluating bituminous coal.** **Model Answer:** > Proximate analysis is the most widely-used empirical test in fuel engineering because it provides rapid, reproducible data on four operationally-defined fractions: > > - **Moisture (M):** Indicates storage stability and net energy loss during drying. > - **Volatile Matter (VM):** Predicts flame length, ignition ease, and combustion efficiency. High VM bituminous coals ignite easily but produce smoke. > - **Ash (A):** Quantifies non-combustible residue — affects boiler slagging, fouling, and ash-handling cost. > - **Fixed Carbon (FC):** Determined by difference; governs coke yield and combustion duration. > > The **Fuel Ratio** $\dfrac{FC}{VM}$ is a key rank indicator. For bituminous coal it lies between 1.1 and 6.5, and the **coking property** is assessed using VM, fluidity, and dilatation tests on the proximate-based framework. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **A bituminous coal has the following composition (mass %, as-received):** C = 70.5 %, H = 5.0 %, O = 10.0 %, N = 1.4 %, S = 0.6 %, Moisture = 2.5 %, Ash = 10.0 %. **(a)** Calculate the **GCV** using Dulong's formula in kcal/kg and MJ/kg. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Determine the **NCV** at a flue-gas temperature of 180 °C and the **theoretical air required per kg of fuel**. Comment on the rank. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) GCV by Dulong's formula:** $$\begin{aligned} GCV &= \dfrac{8080\,C + 34500\,(H - O/8) + 2240\,S}{100} \\ &= \dfrac{8080(70.5) + 34500\!\left(5.0 - \dfrac{10}{8}\right) + 2240(0.6)}{100} \\ &= \dfrac{569640 + 34500(5.0 - 1.25) + 1344}{100} \\ &= \dfrac{569640 + 34500(3.75) + 1344}{100} \\ &= \dfrac{569640 + 129375 + 1344}{100} \\ &= \dfrac{700359}{100} = 7003.6 \text{ kcal/kg} \end{aligned}$$ Converting to MJ/kg: $$GCV = 7003.6 \times 4.1868 \times 10^{-3} = 29.32 \text{ MJ/kg}$$ **(b) NCV at t = 180 °C and stoichiometric air:** $$\begin{aligned} NCV &= GCV - \dfrac{9\,H}{100}(587 - t) \\ &= 7003.6 - \dfrac{9 \times 5.0}{100}(587 - 180) \\ &= 7003.6 - 0.45 \times 407 \\ &= 7003.6 - 183.15 = 6820.5 \text{ kcal/kg} = 28.55 \text{ MJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ Stoichiometric air: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Air}^{stoich} &= 4.31(70.5) + 4.34(5.0) - 1.03(10.0) + 2(0.6) \\ &= 303.86 + 21.70 - 10.30 + 1.20 \\ &= 316.46 \text{ kg air / 100 kg fuel} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Air}^{stoich} = 3.165 \text{ kg/kg fuel} \approx 3.17 \text{ kg/kg}}$$ **Rank Comment:** GCV of 29.32 MJ/kg falls in the **High Volatile A Bituminous** category (24–35 MJ/kg). The C/H ratio = 70.5/5.0 = 14.1 is typical of bituminous coal. Hence the fuel is classified as **High Volatile A Bituminous Coal — suitable for thermal power generation**. --- ### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]` **(a)** With a neat flowchart, describe the **proximate analysis** of bituminous coal. Mention the apparatus, temperature, and significance of each fraction. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **coking property of bituminous coal** in detail, including the Gieseler plastometer test, the role of macerals, and the metallurgical importance. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution Outline **(a) Proximate Analysis Procedure:** 1. **Moisture:** Weigh 1 g of -212 µm coal in a crucible. Heat in **air oven at 105–110 °C for 1 hour**. Cool in desiccator and reweigh. Mass loss = Moisture (%). 2. **Volatile Matter:** Heat the moisture-free sample in a **muffle furnace at 950 ± 20 °C for exactly 7 minutes** in a *closed* (with lid) silica crucible. Cool, weigh. Loss = VM (%). 3. **Ash:** Burn the residue from step 2 in an **open crucible at 815 ± 10 °C for 1 hour** (or until constant mass). Cool, weigh. Residue = Ash (%). 4. **Fixed Carbon:** Calculated by difference: $FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$. **Significance** (already discussed in SECTION 5.1 Q2 above). **(b) Coking Property:** The coking (agglomerating) property is **unique to bituminous coal** and is the cornerstone of the **iron & steel industry**. - **Plastic stage:** When bituminous coal is heated from 350–550 °C in the absence of air, it passes through a **plastic/fluid state**. Adjacent coal particles fuse, forming a coherent porous mass — **coke**. - **Gieseler plastometer:** A standardized test that measures the **fluidity (dial divisions per minute, ddpm)** as the coal is heated at 3 °C/min. - **Softening temperature (Ts):** Coal begins to deform (~350 °C). - **Maximum fluidity (Tf):** Peak ddpm occurs (~400–470 °C for HVAb; ~470–500 °C for MVb). - **Resolidification temperature (Tr):** Coal resolidifies (~500–550 °C). - **Plastic range** ΔT = Tr − Ts. A wide plastic range indicates **good coking coal**. - **Maceral role:** - **Vitrinite** (~70–80 %) → reactive, forms the **coke matrix**. - **Exinite/Liptinite** → high VM, drives **plasticity**. - **Inertinite** → non-reactive, dilutes coking strength; excess inerts (e.g., fusain) weakens coke. - **Metallurgical importance:** - **Coke** (carbon > 87 %, ash < 12 %, S < 0.7 %, P < 0.05 %) acts as a **fuel + reductant + structural support** in a blast furnace. - **CSR (Coke Strength after Reaction)** and **CRI (Coke Reactivity Index)** are critical quality indices. - India relies heavily on **Jharia & Raniganj** bituminous coking coals for the Bokaro, Bhilai, Durgapur, and Rourkela steel plants. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Bituminous Coal Questions:** > > 1. **Unit confusion between kcal/kg and MJ/kg** — Dulong's formula gives kcal/kg by default. Forgetting to multiply by **0.0041868** to get MJ/kg costs full marks. > 2. **Wrong constant in NCV** — The latent heat of vaporization at 100 °C is **587 kcal/kg** (some students wrongly use 540). Always write the full equation showing the correction factor $(587 - t)$. > 3. **Confusing "as-received" vs "dry-ash-free" basis** — Dulong's formula is applied on the **as-received** or **dry** basis with raw H content. Converting to **daf** *and then* applying Dulong's formula gives wrong GCV because the volatile-bound H has been over-extrapolated. > 4. **Stoichiometric air formula: O₂ not subtracted correctly** — The standard formula is $\text{Air} = 4.31\,C + 4.34\,H - 1.03\,O + 2\,S$. Forgetting the minus sign on the oxygen term inflates the answer by 5–10 %. > 5. **Coking vs Caking confusion** — **Caking** = bituminous coal softens on heating; **Coking** = the resulting cake hardens to a strong coke. Not all caking coals are coking coals. > 6. **Mis-classifying rank from GCV** — A coal with GCV = 26 MJ/kg could be either **sub-bituminous or HVAb bituminous**. Use **vitrinite reflectance** or **Fuel Ratio** to disambiguate. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Bituminous Coal** - ☐ **Definition:** 69–86 % FC, 14–31 % VM (dmmf basis); GCV = **24–35 MJ/kg**. - ☐ **Coal rank position:** Lignite < Sub-bituminous < **Bituminous** < Anthracite. - ☐ **Five sub-classes:** HVAb, HVBb, HVCb, MVb, LVb (per ASTM D388). - ☐ **Proximate analysis fractions:** Moisture, VM, Ash, FC (FC by difference). - ☐ **VM test:** 950 ± 20 °C, 7 min, **closed crucible**. - ☐ **Ash test:** 815 ± 10 °C, **open crucible**, until constant mass. - ☐ **Ultimate elements:** C (75–85 %), H (4.5–6 %), O (5–15 %), N (1–2 %), S (0.5–5 %) — all on daf. - ☐ **Dulong's formula:** $GCV = \dfrac{8080\,C + 34500\,(H - O/8) + 2240\,S}{100}$ in kcal/kg. - ☐ **NCV:** $NCV = GCV - 0.09\,H \times (587 - t)$ in kcal/kg. - ☐ **Stoichiometric air:** $4.31\,C + 4.34\,H - 1.03\,O + 2\,S$ in kg/100 kg fuel. - ☐ **Conversion factor:** 1 kcal/kg = **4.1868 × 10⁻³ MJ/kg**. - ☐ **Coking property:** Plasticity at 350–550 °C; **Gieseler fluidity** (ddpm) is the standard measure. - ☐ **Macerals:** Vitrinite (matrix), Liptinite (plasticizer), Inertinite (diluent). - ☐ **Industrial uses:** Thermal power (HVAb), Coke making (MVb/LVb), Cement kilns, Industrial boilers. - ☐ **Indian source basins:** Jharkhand (Jharia, Raniganj, Bokaro), West Bengal, parts of Madhya Pradesh & Maharashtra. - ☐ **Environmental flag:** Bituminous coal typically has **higher sulphur (1–3 %)** than anthracite; flue-gas desulphurization (FGD) is often mandated.

topics.title: Anthracite Coal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Anthracite Coal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Anthracite Coal

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Anthracite Coal** is the highest metamorphic rank of coal, characterized by a very high fixed carbon content (typically **86%–98%**), extremely low volatile matter (usually **< 14%**), and a high calorific value ranging between **30–35 MJ/kg** (approximately **7,200–8,300 kcal/kg**). It represents the terminal stage of the **coalification series**, beyond which further metamorphic transformation converts the carbonaceous matter into graphite.

According to the **ASTM D388-12 classification standard**, anthracite is designated as **Class I (Meta-Anthracite)** and **Class II (Anthracite)**, distinguished by their respective volatile matter ranges and fixed carbon percentages on a dry, mineral-matter-free (dmmf) basis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Coalification (Diagenesis → Metamorphism):** The progressive transformation of buried plant debris (peat) into progressively carbon-richer, drier, and harder fuel substances under the influence of heat, pressure, and time. Anthracite lies at the extreme end of this geochemical maturation continuum.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **piece of toast** that has been left in an oven for progressively longer durations:

- The **bread** (moist, fibrous, light) = **Peat** (incipient fuel)
- The **lightly toasted bread** (brown, still soft) = **Lignite**
- The **medium brown toast** (firm, combustible) = **Bituminous Coal**
- The **almost-burnt, jet-black charcoal-like crust** (very hard, smoky when heated) = **Anthracite**
- The **carbonized skeleton** (no flame, only glow) = **Meta-Anthracite / Graphite**

Just as the long, slow heating expels moisture, oils, and volatile gases, leaving behind concentrated carbon, **geological forces over millions of years** compress and heat buried plant matter, driving off volatiles and concentrating carbon until anthracite is formed.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Chemical Identifiers

| Property | Anthracite Value |
|----------|------------------|
| Fixed Carbon (dry basis) | **86%–98%** |
| Volatile Matter (dry basis) | **2%–14%** |
| Moisture Content | **< 5%** (typically 1–3%) |
| Ash Content | **5%–15%** (often 5–10% in premium grades) |
| Sulfur Content | **0.5%–2%** (low-to-moderate) |
| Hardness (Mohs Scale) | **2.2 – 2.8** |
| Specific Gravity | **1.3 – 1.8** |
| Color & Luster | Jet-black with **semi-metallic / submetallic luster** |
| Fracture Pattern | **Conchoidal** (smooth, curved break like glass) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):** Anthracite is positioned as the **benchmark premium solid fuel** in the fuel engineering curriculum due to its (a) highest heating value among natural coals, (b) near-complete combustion with negligible smoke, and (c) role as a model for understanding the coal rank–property relationship.

### 1.4 Geological Origin

Anthracite originates from **bituminous coal** that has been subjected to intense **tectonic pressure** and **low-grade regional metamorphism**, typically at temperatures between **170 °C and 250 °C**. Major anthracite deposits globally occur in:

- **Pennsylvania (USA)** – the Lehigh and Lackawanna basins
- **Wales (UK)** – the South Wales Coalfield
- **Russia** – the Donets Basin
- **Vietnam** – the Quang Ninh Province
- **South Africa** – limited reserves
- **India** – small pockets in **Gujarat (Akrimota, Kachchh)** and **Jammu & Kashmir (Kalakot)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indian Context (KTU-Exam Relevant):** India possesses only **~1%** of global anthracite reserves. The **Gujarat Mineral Development Corporation (GMDC)** operates the **Akrimota Thermal Power Station (Akrimota, Kachchh)**, one of the few Indian plants fueled by indigenously mined Indian anthracite.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Coalification Series — A Rank-Based Hierarchy

Coal is classified by **rank**, which describes the degree of metamorphic alteration. Anthracite occupies the pinnacle of this hierarchy.

$$
\text{Peat} \rightarrow \text{Lignite} \rightarrow \text{Sub-Bituminous} \rightarrow \text{Bituminous} \rightarrow \text{Anthracite} \rightarrow \text{Meta-Anthracite (Graphite)}
$$

As rank increases:

- **Carbon (C)** content increases
- **Volatile Matter (VM)** decreases
- **Moisture (M)** decreases
- **Calorific Value (CV)** increases up to anthracite, then plateaus/drops into graphite
- **Hydrogen-to-Carbon (H/C) atomic ratio** decreases
- **Oxygen-to-Carbon (O/C) atomic ratio** decreases

### 2.2 Proximate Analysis — The KTU Standard Test Battery

Proximate analysis is the **primary characterization tool** mandated in all KTU Fuel Engineering problems. It determines four key parameters on an as-received (ar), dry (d), and dry-ash-free (daf) basis.

**The Four Proximate Parameters (must total 100% on dry basis):**

1. **Moisture (M)** – mass loss when heated to **110 °C**
2. **Volatile Matter (VM)** – mass loss when heated to **950 °C** in a covered crucible (7 min)
3. **Ash (A)** – non-combustible residue after combustion at **815 °C**
4. **Fixed Carbon (FC)** – calculated by difference

### 2.3 Ultimate Analysis — Elemental Composition

This analysis reports the elemental percentages of **C, H, N, S, O** (and ash), providing insights into combustion stoichiometry and emission behavior.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Relationship | Description |
|------------------------|-------------|
| $FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$ | Fixed Carbon (as-received basis) |
| $CV_{daf} = \dfrac{33{,}800 \cdot FC_{daf} + 144{,}200 \cdot \left( \dfrac{H_{daf} - \dfrac{O_{daf}}{8} \right) }{100}$ | **Dulong's Formula** for calorific value (kcal/kg, daf basis) |
| $CV_{gross} = CV_{net} + 0.09 \cdot H \cdot 5.85$ | Conversion of net to gross CV (kcal/kg) |
| $\text{Air Required} = \dfrac{11.6 \cdot C + 34.8 \cdot \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 4.35 \cdot S}{100}$ | Theoretical O₂ in kg/kg fuel |
| $\text{Actual Air} = 1.2 \times \text{Theoretical O}_2$ | With **20% excess air** (typical boiler practice) |
| $\text{Flue Gas} \approx 1.5 \times \text{Air Supplied}$ | Empirical volumetric estimate |
| $H/C \text{ atomic ratio} = \dfrac{H/1}{C/12}$ | Coal rank indicator (lower = higher rank) |
| $CV \ (\text{MJ/kg}) = \dfrac{kcal/kg}{238.85}$ | Unit conversion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Note:** Anthracite's **low volatile matter (<14%)** means its combustion is governed primarily by **fixed-carbon surface reaction kinetics**, not flame-driven devolatilization. This results in **slower ignition**, **longer particle residence time**, and the need for **larger combustion zones** compared to bituminous coal. Pulverized-fuel burners and cyclone furnaces are commonly used.

### 2.5 Combustion Behavior — Why Anthracite Burns Differently

- **Ignition Temperature:** **450–600 °C** (substantially higher than bituminous coal's 350–450 °C)
- **Flame Characteristics:** Short, blue, non-luminous, **almost smokeless**
- **Combustion Mode:** Heterogeneous **surface combustion** of solid carbon particles
- **Smoke Generation:** Negligible (low VM, high C)
- **Pollutants:** Low SO₂ (if low-sulfur), but higher **NOₓ** due to high flame temperatures

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Sector | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Domestic Heating** | Smokeless fuel briquettes, fireplace logs (premium markets) |
| **Power Generation** | Pulverized coal combustion (PCC) boilers, CFBC |
| **Metallurgy** | Sintering of iron ore, foundry coke blends |
| **Filtration** | Anthracite-based water filtration media (dual-media filters) |
| **Carbon Electrodes** | Calcined anthracite for steel-making EAF electrodes |
| **Cement Industry** | High-temperature kiln fuel |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Demonstrations

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Proximate to Ultimate Conversion via Dulong's Formula

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
A sample of anthracite coal has the following proximate analysis (as-received basis):

- Moisture (M) = 2.0 %
- Volatile Matter (VM) = 8.0 %
- Ash (A) = 6.0 %
- Fixed Carbon (FC) = 84.0 %

The ultimate analysis on a **dry-ash-free (daf) basis** is given as:
- Carbon (C) = 92.0 %
- Hydrogen (H) = 3.5 %
- Oxygen (O) = 2.5 %
- Sulfur (S) = 0.4 %
- Nitrogen (N) = 1.6 %

**Calculate:**
1. The calorific value (GCV) using **Dulong's Formula**.
2. The **Net Calorific Value (NCV)**.
3. The **stoichiometric oxygen requirement** per kg of fuel.
4. The **theoretical air requirement** (kg/kg fuel), assuming air is 23% O₂ by mass.

---

#### Step 1 — Convert daf Values to As-Received Basis

We first convert each ultimate parameter from the **daf basis** to the **as-received (ar) basis** using:

$$
X_{ar} = X_{daf} \times \dfrac{100 - M_{ar} - A_{ar}}{100}
$$

where the non-moisture, non-ash combustible fraction is:

$$
\text{Combustible fraction} = 100 - M - A = 100 - 2.0 - 6.0 = 92.0 \%
$$

Converting each element:

$$
C_{ar} = 92.0 \times \dfrac{92.0}{100} = 84.64\ \%
$$

$$
H_{ar} = 3.5 \times \dfrac{92.0}{100} = 3.22\ \%
$$

$$
O_{ar} = 2.5 \times \dfrac{92.0}{100} = 2.30\ \%
$$

$$
S_{ar} = 0.4 \times \dfrac{92.0}{100} = 0.368\ \%
$$

$$
N_{ar} = 1.6 \times \dfrac{92.0}{100} = 1.472\ \%
$$

**Verification of Mass Balance:**

$$
C_{ar} + H_{ar} + O_{ar} + S_{ar} + N_{ar} + M + A = 84.64 + 3.22 + 2.30 + 0.368 + 1.472 + 2.0 + 6.0 = 100.00\ \% \ \checkmark
$$

---

#### Step 2 — Apply Dulong's Formula for GCV

**Dulong's Formula (per kg of fuel, using as-received mass percentages):**

$$
GCV = \dfrac{8080 \cdot C + 34500 \cdot \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240 \cdot S}{100} \quad [\text{kcal/kg}]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **8080 kcal/kg** is the heat released by burning 1 kg of carbon to CO₂. The factor **O/8** corrects for the hydrogen already bound to oxygen.

**Substituting the as-received values:**

$$
GCV = \dfrac{8080 \times 84.64 + 34500 \times \left(3.22 - \dfrac{2.30}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 0.368}{100}
$$

Compute each term individually:

$$
\text{Term 1: } 8080 \times 84.64 = 683{,}891.2
$$

$$
\text{Term 2: } \dfrac{2.30}{8} = 0.2875 \quad ; \quad 3.22 - 0.2875 = 2.9325 \quad ; \quad 34500 \times 2.9325 = 101{,}171.25
$$

$$
\text{Term 3: } 2240 \times 0.368 = 824.32
$$

Summing the numerators:

$$
683{,}891.2 + 101{,}171.25 + 824.32 = 785{,}886.77
$$

$$
GCV = \dfrac{785{,}886.77}{100} = 7{,}858.87 \ \text{kcal/kg}
$$

Converting to **MJ/kg**:

$$
GCV = \dfrac{7{,}858.87}{238.85} = 32.91 \ \text{MJ/kg}
$$

**This value is consistent with the premium anthracite range of 30–35 MJ/kg.** ✓

---

#### Step 3 — Compute the Net Calorific Value (NCV)

The latent heat of water vapor formed from hydrogen combustion must be subtracted. The standard correction is:

$$
NCV = GCV - 0.09 \cdot H_{ar} \cdot 588.6 \quad [\text{kcal/kg}]
$$

where the factor **0.09** represents 9 times the hydrogen mass (each kg of H produces 9 kg of H₂O on combustion) and **588.6 cal/g = 5.886 × 10⁵ cal/kg** is the latent heat of vaporization of water at standard conditions.

$$
NCV = 7{,}858.87 - 0.09 \times 3.22 \times 588.6
$$

$$
NCV = 7{,}858.87 - 170.61 = 7{,}688.26 \ \text{kcal/kg}
$$

In SI units:

$$
NCV = \dfrac{7{,}688.26}{238.85} = 32.19 \ \text{MJ/kg}
$$

---

#### Step 4 — Stoichiometric Oxygen Requirement

Using the standard combustion stoichiometric formula:

$$
O_{2,\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{8}{3} C + 8 H + S - O \quad [\text{kg O}_2 / 100\ \text{kg fuel}]
$$

*(Derived from the molecular stoichiometry: C + O₂ → CO₂, 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O, S + O₂ → SO₂)*

Substituting the **as-received percentages** directly (the formula is per 100 kg of fuel):

$$
O_{2,\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{8}{3} \times 84.64 + 8 \times 3.22 + 0.368 - 2.30
$$

$$
= 225.71 + 25.76 + 0.368 - 2.30
$$

$$
= 249.54 \ \text{kg O}_2 / 100\ \text{kg fuel}
$$

Per kg of fuel:

$$
O_{2,\text{stoich}} = 2.4954 \ \text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg fuel}
$$

---

#### Step 5 — Theoretical Air Requirement

Air contains **23% O₂ by mass** (standard dry air composition: 23.2% O₂, 76.8% N₂ by mass).

$$
\text{Air}_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{O_{2,\text{stoich}}}{0.23} = \dfrac{2.4954}{0.23} = 10.85 \ \text{kg air} / \text{kg fuel}
$$

With **30% excess air** (typical for anthracite, which has slower kinetics):

$$
\text{Air}_{\text{actual}} = 1.30 \times 10.85 = 14.10 \ \text{kg air} / \text{kg fuel}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Proximate Analysis Mass-Balance Conversion

**Problem Statement:**
The proximate analysis of an anthracite (as-received) gives: M = 3 %, VM = 9 %, A = 7 %, FC = 81 %. Convert this to a **dry basis**.

---

#### Step 1 — Apply the Dry-Basis Conversion Formula

For any component other than moisture:

$$
X_{d} = X_{ar} \times \dfrac{100}{100 - M_{ar}}
$$

Dry basis fuel fraction:

$$
\dfrac{100}{100 - 3} = \dfrac{100}{97} = 1.0309
$$

Converting each component:

$$
VM_d = 9.0 \times 1.0309 = 9.28\ \%
$$

$$
A_d = 7.0 \times 1.0309 = 7.22\ \%
$$

$$
FC_d = 81.0 \times 1.0309 = 83.50\ \%
$$

**Check (must equal 100% on dry basis):**

$$
9.28 + 7.22 + 83.50 = 100.00\ \% \ \checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** When converting **as-received → dry → dry-ash-free**, always do the conversions in that order. Skipping the dry step leads to inconsistent values, and 1–2 marks are routinely lost.

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix — Anthracite vs. Other Coals

| Parameter | Peat | Lignite | Bituminous | **Anthracite** | Graphite |
|-----------|------|---------|------------|----------------|----------|
| Fixed Carbon (%) | < 25 | 25–35 | 45–86 | **86–98** | > 98 |
| Volatile Matter (%) | > 60 | 40–55 | 14–45 | **2–14** | < 2 |
| Moisture (%) | > 75 | 30–50 | 5–15 | **< 5** | < 1 |
| GCV (MJ/kg) | 5–10 | 10–20 | 24–35 | **30–35** | ~32 |
| H/C atomic ratio | ~1.0 | 0.8–1.0 | 0.6–0.8 | **0.2–0.4** | ~0 |
| Ignition (°C) | < 200 | 200–300 | 350–450 | **450–600** | Non-combustible |
| Smoke | Heavy | Heavy | Moderate | **Negligible** | None |
| Primary Use | None | Power | Power, Coke | **Heating, Sintering, Filtration** | Lubricant, Electrode |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Anthracite Coal. List its key identifying properties.**

**Model Answer:**

Anthracite coal is the **highest metamorphic rank of coal**, formed when bituminous coal undergoes intense geological pressure and low-grade metamorphism, expelling nearly all volatile matter and moisture to concentrate fixed carbon.

**Key identifying properties:**

- **Fixed Carbon:** 86%–98% (highest among all coal ranks)
- **Volatile Matter:** < 14%
- **Moisture:** < 5%
- **Calorific Value:** 30–35 MJ/kg
- **Hardness:** 2.2–2.8 Mohs
- **Luster:** Submetallic / semi-metallic
- **Fracture:** Conchoidal
- **Ignition Temperature:** 450–600 °C
- **Combustion:** Slow, smokeless, short blue flame

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Proximate Analysis and Ultimate Analysis of coal.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Proximate Analysis | Ultimate Analysis |
|---------|--------------------|-------------------|
| **Purpose** | Determines combustion behavior in practical furnaces | Determines elemental chemical composition |
| **Parameters** | Moisture, Volatile Matter, Ash, Fixed Carbon | Carbon, Hydrogen, Nitrogen, Sulfur, Oxygen, Ash |
| **Methods** | Gravimetric (heating to 110 °C, 950 °C, 815 °C) | Combustion train + gas chromatography / CHNS analyzer |
| **Information Given** | Combustion efficiency, smoke tendency, rank indication | Stoichiometric air requirement, GCV via Dulong's formula |
| **Standards** | ASTM D3172, IS 1350 Part I | ASTM D3176, IS 1350 Part IV |
| **Time Required** | Few hours | Several hours |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Q.A. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **coalification process** with a neat diagram and discuss how anthracite differs from lignite in terms of carbon content, volatile matter, moisture, calorific value, and ignition temperature.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A sample of anthracite coal has the following composition on as-received basis:
- C = 82%, H = 3.0%, O = 2.0%, S = 0.5%, N = 1.5%, M = 4.0%, A = 7.0%
Calculate (i) the Gross Calorific Value using Dulong's formula, and (ii) the **theoretical air required** for complete combustion per kg of fuel.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A. (a) — Coalification & Anthracite vs Lignite**

**Coalification:**

Coalification is the **geochemical and physical transformation** of accumulated plant debris (peat) into progressively denser, drier, and carbon-richer solid fuels, driven by **heat, pressure, and time** in a reducing sedimentary environment.

**Stages:**

1. **Peat Formation:** Plant remains accumulate in swampy, anaerobic conditions; cellulose and lignin partially decompose.
2. **Lignite Stage:** Compaction expels water; biogenic methane, CO₂, and water are released. Carbon rises to 25–35%.
3. **Bituminous Stage:** With deeper burial (1–5 km) and temperatures of 100–200 °C, **devolatilization** intensifies; bituminous coal forms with 45–86% C.
4. **Anthracite Stage:** Regional metamorphism at 170–250 °C and tectonic folding further concentrates carbon to 86–98%, with VM < 14%.
5. **Meta-Anthracite/Graphite:** Continued metamorphism at higher temperatures and pressures yields ordered crystalline carbon.

**Anthracite vs. Lignite Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Lignite | Anthracite |
|-----------|---------|------------|
| Carbon (%) | 25–35 | **86–98** |
| Volatile Matter (%) | 40–55 | **2–14** |
| Moisture (%) | 30–50 | **< 5** |
| GCV (MJ/kg) | 10–20 | **30–35** |
| Ignition Temp (°C) | 200–300 | **450–600** |
| Color | Brown | **Jet Black** |
| Smokiness | Heavy | **Negligible** |
| Use | Power only | **Heating, Metallurgy, Filtration** |

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A. (b) — Dulong's Formula & Air Calculation**

**Step 1 — Dulong's Formula (per 100 kg of fuel):**

$$
GCV = \dfrac{8080 \cdot C + 34500 \cdot \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240 \cdot S}{100} \quad [\text{kcal/kg}]
$$

Substituting the as-received values directly (C, H, O, S are already on the as-received basis):

$$
GCV = \dfrac{8080 \times 82 + 34500 \times \left(3.0 - \dfrac{2.0}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 0.5}{100}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{8080 \times 82 + 34500 \times (3.0 - 0.25) + 2240 \times 0.5}{100}
$$

Compute each numerator term:

$$
\text{Term 1: } 8080 \times 82 = 662{,}560
$$

$$
\text{Term 2: } 3.0 - 0.25 = 2.75 \quad ; \quad 34500 \times 2.75 = 94{,}875
$$

$$
\text{Term 3: } 2240 \times 0.5 = 1{,}120
$$

Summing:

$$
662{,}560 + 94{,}875 + 1{,}120 = 758{,}555
$$

$$
GCV = \dfrac{758{,}555}{100} = 7{,}585.55 \ \text{kcal/kg}
$$

Converting to SI:

$$
GCV = \dfrac{7{,}585.55}{238.85} = 31.76 \ \text{MJ/kg}
$$

This lies within the **anthracite GCV range (30–35 MJ/kg)**. ✓

**Step 2 — Theoretical Air Requirement:**

Using the standard stoichiometric formula (per kg of fuel, mass percentages):

$$
O_2 = \dfrac{\dfrac{8}{3} \cdot C + 8 \cdot H + S - O}{100} \quad [\text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg fuel}]
$$

$$
O_2 = \dfrac{\dfrac{8}{3} \times 82 + 8 \times 3.0 + 0.5 - 2.0}{100}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{218.67 + 24.0 + 0.5 - 2.0}{100}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{241.17}{100} = 2.4117 \ \text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg fuel}
$$

Converting O₂ to air (23% O₂ by mass in dry air):

$$
\text{Air}_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{2.4117}{0.23} = 10.49 \ \text{kg air} / \text{kg fuel}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Frequently Lost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply the O/8 correction** for hydrogen bound to oxygen in Dulong's formula — costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing the conversion factor**: some students use **238.85** while others use **239** — both are accepted, but the **specific value used in working must be shown** to avoid the 1-mark "approximation penalty".
> 3. **Unit mismatch**: Mixing kcal/kg with MJ/kg in the same line of the answer. Always state the **final answer with a single unit** and put conversions in a separate line.
> 4. **Mass-balance error**: In numericals, students often forget to verify that C+H+O+S+N+M+A = 100%. The examiner does check this and may deduct for inconsistency.

---

#### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**Q.B. (a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **proximate analysis of coal** in detail. Explain the significance of each parameter with specific reference to anthracite coal.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The dry-basis composition of an anthracite is:
- C = 86%, H = 3.5%, O = 3.0%, S = 0.8%, N = 1.5%, A = 5.2%

Convert this analysis to an **as-received basis** if the in-situ moisture content is **3.5%**, then compute the **GCV in kcal/kg and MJ/kg** using Dulong's formula.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B. (a) — Proximate Analysis**

**Proximate Analysis** is the standard empirical test (ASTM D3172 / IS 1350 Part I) that quantifies four key parameters reflecting a coal's combustion behavior.

**The Four Parameters:**

1. **Moisture (M):** Determined by heating 1 g of coal (passing 212 μm) at **108 ± 2 °C for 1 hour** in an air-oven. Mass loss is reported as % moisture.

2. **Volatile Matter (VM):** The covered-crucible test. 1 g of coal is heated to **950 ± 20 °C for 7 minutes** in a muffle furnace. The mass loss minus moisture gives the VM.

3. **Ash (A):** The combustion residue. 1 g of coal is burned in air at **815 ± 10 °C** until constant mass is attained. Ash represents the non-combustible mineral matter (clays, pyrites, silicates).

4. **Fixed Carbon (FC):** Computed by difference:
$$
FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)
$$

**Significance for Anthracite:**

- **Low Moisture (< 5%):** Anthracite's low moisture means **high net calorific value** and minimal latent-heat penalty.
- **Low VM (< 14%):** Anthracite **does not smoke** and yields short blue flames — a premium fuel for domestic use.
- **High Ash variability (5–15%):** Affects slagging and ash-handling system design.
- **High FC (86–98%):** Sustained, long-duration heat release from surface carbon combustion.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B. (b) — Basis Conversion + Dulong's Formula**

**Step 1 — Convert dry basis to as-received basis:**

The dry fuel fraction is reduced by the in-situ moisture. Conversion formula:

$$
X_{ar} = X_{d} \times \dfrac{100 - M_{ar}}{100} = X_{d} \times \dfrac{100 - 3.5}{100} = 0.965 \cdot X_{d}
$$

Applying to each element:

$$
C_{ar} = 86.0 \times 0.965 = 82.99\ \%
$$

$$
H_{ar} = 3.5 \times 0.965 = 3.378\ \%
$$

$$
O_{ar} = 3.0 \times 0.965 = 2.895\ \%
$$

$$
