S_{ar} = 0.8 \times 0.965 = 0.772\ \%
$$

$$
N_{ar} = 1.5 \times 0.965 = 1.448\ \%
$$

$$
A_{ar} = 5.2 \times 0.965 = 5.018\ \%
$$

Total of dry-converted components:

$$
C + H + O + S + N + A = 82.99 + 3.378 + 2.895 + 0.772 + 1.448 + 5.018 = 96.50\ \%
$$

Adding moisture to balance:

$$
96.50 + 3.5 = 100.0\ \% \ \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Dulong's Formula:**

$$
GCV = \dfrac{8080 \times 82.99 + 34500 \times \left(3.378 - \dfrac{2.895}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 0.772}{100} \quad [\text{kcal/kg}]
$$

Compute each term:

$$
\text{Term 1: } 8080 \times 82.99 = 670{,}559.2
$$

$$
\text{Term 2: } \dfrac{2.895}{8} = 0.3619 \quad ; \quad 3.378 - 0.3619 = 3.0161 \quad ; \quad 34500 \times 3.0161 = 104{,}055.5
$$

$$
\text{Term 3: } 2240 \times 0.772 = 1{,}729.28
$$

Summing the numerators:

$$
670{,}559.2 + 104{,}055.5 + 1{,}729.28 = 776{,}344.0
$$

$$
GCV = \dfrac{776{,}344.0}{100} = 7{,}763.44 \ \text{kcal/kg}
$$

Converting to MJ/kg:

$$
GCV = \dfrac{7{,}763.44}{238.85} = 32.50 \ \text{MJ/kg}
$$

**Result:** The anthracite has a GCV of **7,763 kcal/kg (32.50 MJ/kg)** on the as-received basis — squarely in the **anthracite premium range**. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall for Basis Conversion:**
> 1. **Wrong direction of conversion:** Some students wrongly convert *as-received → dry* by *dividing* instead of *multiplying*, or vice versa. The correct rule: going from **dry to as-received** uses a factor of **(100 − M_ar)/100** (i.e., multiply by 0.965 in this case).
> 2. **Omitting moisture from the final mass balance:** The 3.5% in-situ moisture must be explicitly added to the sum, even though it does not appear in Dulong's formula. Examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** if the 100% mass balance is not verified.
> 3. **Confusing dry-ash-free (daf) with dry basis:** daf is used when comparing coal **rank** across samples; dry basis is used for **commercial/boiler** calculations. Read the question's wording carefully.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Anthracite Coal**

- **Definition:** Highest-rank natural coal; 86–98% fixed carbon; < 14% VM; < 5% M.
- **GCV Range:** **30–35 MJ/kg** (≈ 7,200–8,300 kcal/kg).
- **H/C atomic ratio:** **0.2–0.4** (lowest among all coal ranks).
- **Ignition Temperature:** **450–600 °C** (highest among coal ranks).
- **Flame:** Short, blue, non-luminous; **smokeless** combustion.
- **Fracture:** Conchoidal; **Luster:** Submetallic/semi-metallic.
- **Origin:** Bituminous coal subjected to **regional metamorphism** at 170–250 °C.
- **Indian Reserves:** Gujarat (Akrimota, Kachchh) — operated by **GMDC**; small pockets in J&K.
- **Global Reserves:** USA (Pennsylvania), Russia (Donets), Vietnam, Wales, China.
- **Proximate Analysis Mass Balance:** **M + VM + A + FC = 100%** (ar or d basis, with M = 0 on dry basis).
- **Ultimate Analysis Mass Balance:** **C + H + O + S + N + A = 100%** (d or daf, with M added on ar basis).
- **Dulong's Formula:** $GCV = \dfrac{8080 \cdot C + 34500 \cdot (H - O/8) + 2240 \cdot S}{100}$ in **kcal/kg** (as-received values).
- **Conversion factor:** **1 MJ/kg = 238.85 kcal/kg**.
- **Theoretical O₂ per kg fuel:** $O_2 = \dfrac{(8/3)C + 8H + S - O}{100}$ kg.
- **Theoretical Air:** $A_{th} = O_2 / 0.23$ kg/kg fuel (dry air is 23% O₂ by mass).
- **Excess air for anthracite:** **20–30%** (lower than bituminous) due to slow surface combustion kinetics.
- **Top Applications:** Smokeless domestic fuel, sintering, foundry coke blends, water filtration media, electrode carbon precursor.
- **Combustion Equipment:** Pulverized-fuel burners, cyclone furnaces, CFBC (Circulating Fluidized Bed Combustion).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cannel and Boghead Coal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cannel and Boghead Coal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Sapropelic Coals: The Hydrogen-Rich Anomalies

Most natural coals belong to the **humic series**, formed from woody, terrestrial plant debris (vitrinite, inertinite). However, a small but industrially important class of coals is derived from **sapropel** (anaerobic, hydrogen-rich organic ooze deposited at the bottom of stagnant aquatic environments). These are known as **Sapropelic Coals**, and the two most important varieties are **Cannel Coal** and **Boghead Coal**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> *Cannel Coal* is a dull, non-caking, hydrogen-rich, bituminous-grade coal composed predominantly of the *sporinite* maceral (microspores, megaspores, and pollen grain exines) along with some vitrinite and inertinite macerals dispersed in a fine, amorphous groundmass.
>
> *Boghead Coal* is a dull, non-caking, exceptionally hydrogen-rich, oil-shale-like coal composed predominantly of the *alginite* maceral (cell walls and secretions of colonial and unicellular green algae such as *Botryococcus* and *Pila*), set within a smaller proportion of fine mineral matter and vitrinite.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Candle Rock":**
> Imagine you take a chunk of muddy pond-bottom sediment loaded with *decaying pollen, spores, and algae*, let it cook deep underground for millions of years under heat and pressure, and end up with a smooth, dark, dense rock that literally **lights like a candle** when a flame is held to it. That is exactly what a cannel/boghead coal is!
>
> - **Cannel** = "Candle coal" (Scottish derivation). Picture pollen, fern spores, and seed dust sinking to a stagnant lake bed.
> - **Boghead** = "Bog algae coal". Picture blue-green algae *blooms* coating a swampy lagoon floor.
>
> Both are essentially the "fossilized, oily mud" of the coal family, in stark contrast to the "fossilized, woody log" nature of ordinary bituminous coal.

### 1.3 Key Distinguishing Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | Humic (Ordinary) Coal | Cannel Coal | Boghead Coal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Parent Material** | Woody trees, bark, leaves | Spores, pollen, cuticles | Algal colonies, bog mud |
| **Dominant Maceral** | Vitrinite | Sporinite | Alginite |
| **Hydrogen (wt %, daf)** | 4 – 5.5 % | **5 – 7 %** | **6 – 11 %** |
| **Volatile Matter (daf)** | 25 – 40 % | **40 – 55 %** | **50 – 80 %** |
| **Caking Property** | Caking / Coking | Non-caking to weakly caking | Non-caking |
| **Appearance** | Bright to banded | Uniform, dull, conchoidal fracture | Uniform, dull, conchoidal fracture |
| **H/C Atomic Ratio** | 0.6 – 0.8 | **0.8 – 1.0** | **1.0 – 1.5** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Why These Coals Matter in Fuel Engineering:**
> Because of their exceptionally high hydrogen and volatile matter, Cannel and Boghead coals were historically the **primary feedstocks for the manufactured gas industry** (town gas), **liquid fuel synthesis** (Bergius hydrogenation, Fischer–Tropsch-like pyrolysis), and **smokeless domestic fuel** production. They are essentially *Nature's own crude oil precursor*, and their study is fundamental to the origin-of-petroleum and coal-to-liquid (CTL) discussions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin, Depositional Environment & Diagenesis

The formation pathway differs markedly from humic coals:

1. **Sedimentation in Stagnant Water:** Deposition occurs in **eutrophic, stratified, oxygen-poor water bodies** (lakes, lagoons, estuarine pools). Anoxic conditions preserve the lipid-rich organic matter from aerobic microbial decay.
2. **Sapropel Accumulation:** A fine, dark, jelly-like organic ooze (the *sapropel*) accumulates. The dominant precursor is either:
   - **Sporinite precursor** → Cannel Coal
   - **Alginite precursor** → Boghead Coal
3. **Bituminization (Diagenesis):** Under shallow burial, microbial activity generates early bitumens. The H/C ratio remains high (>1.0 in Boghead).
4. **Coalification (Catagenesis):** With progressive burial (200 – 1500 m), temperature drives off oxygen as CO₂ and H₂O, concentrating carbon. The end product lies in the **bituminous to sub-bituminous rank** window.
5. **Metagenesis:** Further heating drives volatile matter out, eventually destroying the sapropelic character and producing a normal humic anthracite.

### 2.2 Petrographic Distinction (Maceral Composition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Maceral** = the microscopic organic constituent of coal, analogous to a "mineral" in a rock.

- **Cannel Coal** is *sporinite-rich* (often >50% of macerals by volume), and exhibits under the reflected-light microscope a structureless "groundmass" of fine micrinite with discrete, flattened spore/pollen exines.
- **Boghead Coal** is *alginite-rich*; the alginite occurs as yellow-orange fluorescing globules or lamellar masses under blue-light excitation — a diagnostic feature.
- The two types frequently **grade laterally into one another** in the same seam; mixed varieties are called *Cannel-Boghead* or *Torbanite* (when algal-rich and oil-shale-like).

### 2.3 Chemical Characteristics & Significance

The high hydrogen content drives the following industrial advantages:
- **High Volatile Matter (VM):** Yields a long, luminous, smoky flame — ideal for **illuminating gas** manufacture.
- **High Tar Yield:** Upon low-temperature carbonization (LTC, ~500–700 °C), they yield **20–35% primary tar** (vs. 8–15% for humic coal), an excellent crude-oil substitute.
- **High Calorific Value:** Gross calorific value of **7,000 – 8,500 kcal/kg** (daf), often exceeding ordinary bituminous coal.
- **Low Caking Power:** A disadvantage for metallurgical coke, but an advantage for **smokeless domestic briquettes** (e.g., the famous "Coalite" and "Phurnacite" processes historically used Cannel-Boghead blends).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these expressions — they are the most-tested items in KTU 2014/2024 Scheme ESE.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{H/C Atomic Ratio} &= \frac{\text{H (wt\%)}}{\text{C (wt\%)}} \times \frac{12}{1} = 12 \cdot \frac{\%H}{\%C}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fuel Ratio (FR)} &= \frac{\% \text{Fixed Carbon (FC)}}{\% \text{Volatile Matter (VM)}} \;\; \text{(on dry basis)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VM}_{\text{dry}} &= \text{VM}_{\text{as received}} \times \frac{100}{100 - M}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ash}_{\text{dry}} &= \text{Ash}_{\text{ar}} \times \frac{100}{100 - M}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV (kcal/kg, daf)} &\approx 81.4 \cdot C + 342.5 \cdot \left( H - \tfrac{O}{8} \right) + 22.5 \cdot S \quad \text{(Dulong's Eqn.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Boundary Conditions & Practical Rules:**

| Rule / Constant | Value | Note |
|---|---|---|
| ASTM rank of typical Cannel | High-Volatile Bituminous C | **Caking index (FSI) < 1.5** |
| ASTM rank of typical Boghead | Sub-bituminous A to Bituminous | Often classified with **oil shales** |
| **H/C ratio demarcation** | < 0.8 → Humic ; **≥ 0.8 → Sapropelic** | Used as a screening index |
| Torbanite (type Boghead) | H/C ≈ 1.5 | Classic Scottish locality |
| Cannel oil yield (Fischer assay) | 25 – 200 L/tonne | Comparable to oil shales |
| Maceral fluorescence colour | Yellow-orange (Boghead) | Under UV / blue-light excitation |
| Moisture, M | 1 – 3 % | Cannel and Boghead are *low-moisture* fuels |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufactured Gas Industry (historical):** Cannel coal was the preferred gas-making coal in 19th-century London and Paris gasworks because its high VM gave high gas yield (≥ 350 m³/tonne).
- **Coal-to-Liquids (CTL) & Hydrogenation:** Bergius' original 1913–1925 hydrogenation experiments used **Boghead/Torbanite** as the parent feedstock to produce synthetic gasoline.
- **Domestic Smokeless Fuel:** Non-caking Cannel is a premium smokeless fuel under UK and EU "Smoke Control Areas" legislation.
- **Petroleum Source-Rock Analogue:** Boghead coals are studied by petroleum geochemists as **Type-I kerogen source rocks**, the most prolific oil-generating organofacies.
- **Carbon Research:** Their high aliphatic (–CH₂–) content makes them excellent precursors for **activated carbon** and **carbon molecular sieves**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation — Computing H/C Atomic Ratio of a Boghead Coal Sample

> **Problem:** A Boghead coal sample (dry, ash-free basis) has the following ultimate analysis: C = 80.0 wt %, H = 9.0 wt %, O = 5.0 wt %, N = 1.5 wt %, S = 4.5 wt %. Verify that it qualifies as a **sapropelic** coal, and compute the fuel ratio if VM = 65 % and FC = 35 % (dry basis).

**Step 1: Recall the formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{H}{C}_{\text{atomic}} = 12 \cdot \frac{\%H_{\text{daf}}}{\%C_{\text{daf}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the daf values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{H}{C}_{\text{atomic}} = 12 \cdot \frac{9.0}{80.0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Perform the division.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{9.0}{80.0} = 0.1125
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Multiply by 12.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{H}{C}_{\text{atomic}} = 12 \times 0.1125 = 1.35
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Classify.**

Since 1.35 > 0.80 (the humic-sapropelic demarcation), the sample is **confirmed sapropelic** — consistent with a Boghead coal.

**Step 6: Compute the Fuel Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FR = \frac{FC}{VM} = \frac{35}{65} = 0.538
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Interpret.** A fuel ratio of ~0.54 is far below the coking range (1.0 – 1.7), confirming the **non-caking** nature of Boghead coal.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation — Dulong's GCV for Cannel Coal

> **Problem:** A Cannel coal has (daf): C = 84.0 %, H = 6.0 %, O = 7.0 %, S = 0.8 %, N = 2.2 %. Calculate the Gross Calorific Value (GCV) in kcal/kg.

**Step 1: Apply Dulong's equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV = 81.4\,C + 342.5\left(H - \tfrac{O}{8}\right) + 22.5\,S
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute each term with its numerical value.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV &= 81.4 \times 84.0 \;+\; 342.5 \times \left(6.0 - \tfrac{7.0}{8}\right) \;+\; 22.5 \times 0.8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the "available hydrogen" bracket.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(6.0 - \tfrac{7.0}{8}\right) = 6.0 - 0.875 = 5.125
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute each product term.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
81.4 \times 84.0 &= 6837.6 \\
342.5 \times 5.125 &= 1755.31 \\
22.5 \times 0.8 &= 18.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Sum the three contributions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV &= 6837.6 + 1755.31 + 18.0 \\
GCV &= 8610.91 \;\; \text{kcal/kg (daf)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Converting to MJ/kg (1 kcal = 4.1868 kJ).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV &= \frac{8610.91}{1000} \times 4.1868 = 36.05 \;\; \text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Validation.** This value lies in the **8,400 – 8,800 kcal/kg band** typical of high-grade Cannel coals. ✔

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cannel vs. Boghead Head-to-Head

| Discriminating Property | Cannel Coal | Boghead Coal | KTU Verification Logic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Maceral identity** | Sporinite (spores, pollen) | Alginite (algal remains) | Petrological thin-section / fluorescence |
| **Geological setting** | Shallow lake margins, deltas | Lagoonal, boggy peat swamps | Paleogeographic reconstruction |
| **H/C atomic ratio** | 0.8 – 1.0 | 1.0 – 1.5 | Ultimate analysis + 12×(H/C) |
| **VM (daf, %)** | 40 – 55 | 50 – 80 | Proximate analysis |
| **Primary tar yield (LTC, % wt)** | 15 – 25 | 25 – 40 | Fischer assay at 500 °C |
| **Famous locality** | Westphalia, Kentucky | Torbane Hill (Scotland), Autun (France) | Field trip / case study |
| **Industrial use (modern)** | Domestic smokeless fuel | CTL feed, hydrogenation feedstock | Engineering economics |
| **Type of kerogen equivalent** | Type II (oil-prone) | Type I (highly oil-prone) | Geochemical classification |
| **Diagnostic fluorescence** | Dull brown sporinite | Bright yellow-orange alginite | Blue-light reflected-light microscopy |
| **Coking behaviour** | Non-caking | Non-caking | Roga Index / FSI test |

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Mineral Matter Correction

> **Problem:** A Cannel coal as-received contains 4.0 % moisture and 4.0 % ash. The dry-ash-free VM is 50 %. Convert to (a) dry basis, (b) dry-ash-free basis confirmation, and (c) as-received basis.

**Step 1: Convert VM to dry basis.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VM_{\text{dry}} = VM_{\text{daf}} \times \frac{100 - A_{\text{dry}}}{100}
\end{aligned}
$$

But we first need ash on dry basis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{dry}} = A_{\text{ar}} \times \frac{100}{100 - M} = 4.0 \times \frac{100}{100 - 4.0} = 4.0 \times 1.0417 = 4.167\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute dry VM.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VM_{\text{dry}} = 50 \times \frac{100 - 4.167}{100} = 50 \times 0.9583 = 47.92\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Fixed carbon (dry) by difference.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FC_{\text{dry}} = 100 - VM_{\text{dry}} - A_{\text{dry}} = 100 - 47.92 - 4.167 = 47.92\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Convert back to as-received for full report.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VM_{\text{ar}} = VM_{\text{dry}} \times \frac{100 - M}{100} = 47.92 \times 0.96 = 45.99\,\% \\
FC_{\text{ar}} = 47.92 \times 0.96 = 45.99\,\% \\
A_{\text{ar}} = 4.0\,\% \;\;(given) \\
M_{\text{ar}} = 4.0\,\% \;\;(given) \\
\text{Total} = 45.99 + 45.99 + 4.0 + 4.0 \approx 100\,\% \;\; \text{✔}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Maceral-Level Architecture of Sapropelic Coals

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the *genetic-architecture* relationship between depositional environment, precursor material, maceral assemblage, and end-product coal type. Use this as a revision block-diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Stagnant Lake or Lagoon Anoxic Bottom Water"] --> B1["Accumulation of Sapropel Organic Ooze"]
    A2["Swampy Bog or Peat Flat"] --> B2["Algal Mat Bloom Botryococcus Pila"]
    A3["Deltaic Margin Spore Rich"] --> B3["Spore Pollen Exine Debris"]
    B1 --> C1["Bituminisation Diagenesis"]
    C1 --> D1{"Dominant Precursor"}
    D1 -- "Alginite" --> E1["Boghead Coal Torbanite H/C greater than 1.0"]
    D1 -- "Sporinite" --> E2["Cannel Coal H/C 0.8 to 1.0"]
    D1 -- "Mixed" --> E3["Cannel Boghead Blend Transitional Seam"]
    E1 --> F1["High Tar Yield Smokeless Fuel CTL Feedstock"]
    E2 --> F2["Town Gas High VM Smokeless Briquettes"]
    E3 --> F3["Domestic Heating Industrial Combustion"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Follow the central *vertical* spine from depositional environment → sapropel accumulation → diagenesis → maceral-controlled branching → end-use. The three terminal leaves (F1, F2, F3) correspond to the three primary industrial applications of sapropelic coals.

### 4.2 Comparative Property Heat-Map

The following block-flow schematic compares the *intensity* of key fuel-engineering properties across Cannel, Boghead, and ordinary Humic coal. The "arrow magnitude" represents relative intensity, expressed as a structural block architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Humic["HUMIC COAL Reference"]
        H1["Hydrogen Content LOW"]
        H2["Volatile Matter MODERATE"]
        H3["Caking Power HIGH"]
        H4["Tar Yield LOW"]
    end
    subgraph Cannel["CANNEL COAL"]
        C1["Hydrogen Content MEDIUM HIGH"]
        C2["Volatile Matter HIGH"]
        C3["Caking Power NIL"]
        C4["Tar Yield HIGH"]
    end
    subgraph Boghead["BOGHEAD COAL"]
        B1["Hydrogen Content VERY HIGH"]
        B2["Volatile Matter VERY HIGH"]
        B3["Caking Power NIL"]
        B4["Tar Yield VERY HIGH"]
    end
    H1 --- C1
    C1 --- B1
    H2 --- C2
    C2 --- B2
    H3 --- C3
    C3 --- B3
    H4 --- C4
    C4 --- B4
```

### 4.3 Industrial Application Map (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Feedstock["Sapropelic Coal Feed Cannel and Boghead"] --> P1["Crushing and Sizing"]
    P1 --> P2{"Process Route Selection"}
    P2 -- "Low Temperature Carbonisation 500 to 700 C" --> R1["Smokeless Domestic Fuel Char"]
    R1 --> R1A["Coalite Phurnacite Type Briquettes"]
    P2 -- "High Temperature Carbonisation 1000 C plus" --> R2["High Gas Yield Town Gas"]
    R2 --> R2A["Historical 19th Century Urban Gasworks"]
    P2 -- "Hydrogenation Bergius Process" --> R3["Synthetic Liquid Fuels CTL"]
    R3 --> R3A["Pre WWII Fuel Security Programme"]
    P2 -- "Direct Combustion Power Plant" --> R4["Thermal Energy Steam Generation"]
    R4 --> R4A["Long Smoky Flame High Luminance"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Set B]**
*Distinguish between Cannel coal and Boghead coal on the basis of (i) parent material, (ii) dominant maceral, and (iii) industrial application.*

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Parent material:** Cannel coal originates from *spores, pollen grains, and cuticular debris* of higher plants. Boghead coal originates from *colonial and unicellular algae* (e.g., *Botryococcus*, *Pila*).

(ii) **Dominant maceral:** Cannel = **Sporinite** (>50% of macerals). Boghead = **Alginite** (algal bodies showing yellow-orange fluorescence under blue light).

(iii) **Industrial application:** Cannel was historically used for **illuminating gas production** and **smokeless domestic fuel**. Boghead was the preferred feedstock for **Bergius hydrogenation** to yield synthetic petroleum and is studied as a **Type-I kerogen** source rock analogue.

> 3 marks split: 1 mark for each sub-part with crisp key word.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]**
*State Dulong's formula for calculating the GCV of a coal and explain why the H/C atomic ratio is a critical indicator of sapropelic character.*

**Model Answer:**

Dulong's empirical formula is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV\ (\text{kcal/kg, daf}) = 81.4\,C + 342.5\left(H - \tfrac{O}{8}\right) + 22.5\,S
\end{aligned}
$$

where C, H, O, S are mass percentages on dry-ash-free basis. The H/C atomic ratio is computed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{H}{C}_{\text{atomic}} = 12 \cdot \frac{\%H}{\%C}
\end{aligned}
$$

A **threshold of H/C ≥ 0.8** is the diagnostic criterion for sapropelic coal. Ordinary humic coal has H/C ≈ 0.5–0.7, while Cannel (0.8–1.0) and Boghead (1.0–1.5) exceed this threshold, indicating high aliphatic (–CH₂–) content, the molecular origin of their high volatile matter and oil yield.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice**: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries sub-parts of 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **origin, depositional environment, and petrographic composition** of Cannel coal. Add a note on its **proximate analysis** characteristics.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis (daf basis): C = 82.5 %, H = 7.2 %, O = 6.8 %, S = 1.5 %, N = 2.0 %. Calculate its **GCV using Dulong's formula** and **H/C atomic ratio**. Classify it as humic or sapropelic.

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Origin & Depositional Environment** *(2 marks)*: Cannel coal is formed from the accumulation of *spores, pollen grains, and other lipid-rich plant debris* in **stagnant, oxygen-deficient water bodies** such as shallow lakes, lagoon margins, and deltaic back-swamps. Anoxic conditions prevent aerobic decay, allowing the lipid-rich sapropel to accumulate.

2. **Petrographic Composition** *(3 marks)*: Under reflected-light microscopy, Cannel coal shows a *fine, structureless groundmass* of micrinite and clay-sized mineral matter in which are embedded:
   - **Sporinite** (flattened, lens-shaped, often with visible ornamentation)
   - Smaller amounts of **vitrinite** and **inertinite**
   - **Resinite** and **cutinite** (minor)
   The maceral sporinite is the diagnostic feature, typically exceeding 50 % of the maceral assemblage by volume.

3. **Proximate Analysis Characteristics** *(2 marks)*: Typical ranges:
   - Moisture: 1 – 3 %
   - Volatile Matter: 40 – 55 % (daf)
   - Ash: 2 – 10 %
   - Fixed Carbon: 40 – 50 % (daf)
   The **Fuel Ratio (FC/VM) < 1** confirms its non-caking nature.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Substitute into Dulong's formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV &= 81.4 \times 82.5 + 342.5 \times \left(7.2 - \tfrac{6.8}{8}\right) + 22.5 \times 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Simplify the available hydrogen.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(7.2 - \tfrac{6.8}{8}\right) = 7.2 - 0.85 = 6.35
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute each product.

$$
\begin{aligned}
81.4 \times 82.5 &= 6715.5 \\
342.5 \times 6.35 &= 2174.875 \\
22.5 \times 1.5 &= 33.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Sum to obtain GCV.

$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV &= 6715.5 + 2174.875 + 33.75 = 8924.125 \;\; \text{kcal/kg (daf)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute H/C ratio.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{H}{C} = 12 \times \frac{7.2}{82.5} = 12 \times 0.08727 = 1.047
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Classify.** H/C = 1.047 > 0.8 → **Sapropelic coal** (most likely Boghead, given H/C > 1.0). GCV ≈ 8,924 kcal/kg (daf) — extremely high-grade, consistent with sapropelic character.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Cannel and Boghead coals** in terms of: parent material, hydrogen content, volatile matter, caking property, primary tar yield, and industrial applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **petrographic significance of fluorescence microscopy** in identifying Boghead coal. Why is fluorescence absent or weak in Cannel coal?

**Model Solution for (a):** *(Suggested tabulated answer — 1 mark per row, with crisp justification)*

| Parameter | Cannel Coal | Boghead Coal |
|---|---|---|
| Parent material | Spores, pollen, cuticles | Algae (Botryococcus, Pila) |
| H/C atomic ratio | 0.8 – 1.0 | 1.0 – 1.5 |
| VM (daf) | 40 – 55 % | 50 – 80 % |
| Caking property | Non-caking / weakly caking | Non-caking |
| Primary tar yield | 15 – 25 wt % | 25 – 40 wt % |
| Industrial use | Town gas, smokeless fuel | CTL, hydrogenation, kerogen analogue |

*(Conclusion: 1 mark)* → Both belong to the sapropelic class, but Boghead is the *more oil-prone*, the *more aliphatic*, and the *more hydrogen-rich* of the two.

**Model Solution for (b):**

1. **Fluorescence Phenomenon** *(2 marks)*: Under blue-light excitation in a reflected-light microscope, **alginite** (the diagnostic maceral of Boghead coal) fluoresces in **bright yellow-orange to brown** tones. This is because alginite retains abundant **aliphatic, lipid-rich molecular structures** that absorb the incident blue light and re-emit longer-wavelength visible light (Stokes' shift).

2. **Diagnostic for Boghead** *(2 marks)*: Fluorescence is the *defining petrographic criterion* for distinguishing alginite-bearing Boghead from humic coals under the microscope. Maceral percentages and H/C ratio can be ambiguous; fluorescence is unambiguous.

3. **Why Cannel does not fluoresce strongly** *(3 marks)*: Sporinite, the dominant maceral in Cannel, has *thicker, more aromatic, more carbonised* spore-exine walls. The aliphatic content is lower than in alginite, and the molecular structure absorbs incident blue light without re-emission, or re-emits in the *invisible* longer-wavelength (red) range that the eye cannot readily detect. Hence Cannel appears **dull to weakly fluorescent** in contrast to the brilliant fluorescence of Boghead.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Board Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing Cannel with Boghead** — Students often write "Cannel and Boghead are the same." They are *NOT*: different parent material, different maceral, different H/C ratio. Deduct 2 marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the daf correction in Dulong's formula** — Dulong's formula is valid *only* on a dry-ash-free basis. Plugging in as-received C, H, O, S values will yield an inflated GCV. Always state the basis explicitly.
> 3. **Omitting the H/C atomic demarcation** — Just stating "Cannel has high hydrogen" is worth ½ a mark. Stating the *numerical* demarcation of 0.8 carries full credit.
> 4. **Mixing up Coke vs. Smokeless Fuel** — Cannel and Boghead are *not* coking coals. Writing that they are used for metallurgical coke will lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Not defining "sapropel"** — In a 7-mark sub-question, the first sentence must define the parent material term. Examiners look for this.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this section as your last-day revision card.**

- ☐ **Cannel Coal** = *sporinite-rich* sapropelic coal from spores/pollen; H/C = 0.8 – 1.0.
- ☐ **Boghead Coal** = *alginite-rich* sapropelic coal from algae; H/C = 1.0 – 1.5.
- ☐ **Sapropel** = anaerobic, hydrogen-rich organic ooze deposited in stagnant water.
- ☐ Both are **non-caking** and have **high volatile matter**.
- ☐ **Fluorescence microscopy** is the *diagnostic* tool for alginite.
- ☐ Boghead = Type-I kerogen analogue; Cannel = Type-II kerogen analogue.
- ☐ **Dulong's formula** (daf basis only) gives GCV in kcal/kg.
- ☐ **H/C = 12 × (%H / %C)** is the **key** numerical index.
- ☐ Threshold **H/C ≥ 0.8** confirms sapropelic character.
- ☐ Famous Torbanite (Boghead) locality: **Torbane Hill, Scotland**.
- ☐ Famous Cannel locality: **Westphalia (Germany)**, **Kentucky (USA)**.
- ☐ Primary industrial uses: **town gas, smokeless fuel, CTL feedstock, source-rock analogue**.
- ☐ **Fuel Ratio (FC/VM) < 1** → confirms non-caking nature.
- ☐ Higher tar yield of Boghead (25–40%) makes it the *preferred* CTL feed over Cannel.
- ☐ ASTM rank: both fall in the **bituminous** window; Cannel is non-caking (FSI < 1.5).
- ☐ Always convert proximate values to a stated basis (ar / dry / daf) before any calculation.
- ☐ Conchoidal fracture and uniform dull lustre are *hand-specimen* identifiers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Properties of Coal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physical Properties of Coal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Coal? (KTU Formal Definition)

**Coal** is a naturally occurring, sedimentary, organic combustible rock of predominantly biogenic origin, formed from the accumulation, compaction, and thermal maturation of plant debris (lignin, cellulose, and other macerals) over geological time scales of millions of years under elevated temperature and pressure conditions — a process termed **coalification** or **carbonization**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Coal is classified under **Category I – Solid Fuels** in fuel engineering. It is the most abundant fossil fuel globally and forms the baseline reference for studying all other solid, liquid, and gaseous fuels.

### 1.2 Physical Properties — Overview

The **physical properties of coal** are the measurable, observable, and intrinsic characteristics that govern its **handling, storage, pulverization, combustion behavior, and classification** in industrial and power-plant applications. Unlike chemical properties (proximate/ultimate analysis), physical properties do not alter the elemental composition but describe the bulk macroscopic and microscopic nature of the coal mass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** Physical properties of coal are **engineering (bulk) properties** — they affect **handling, sizing, washing, storage, and combustion equipment design**, while **chemical (analytical) properties** determine **calorific value, combustion chemistry, and emission behavior**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Coal as a Sponge-Cake"

Imagine a **sponge-cake** baking in an oven over millions of years:

- The **flour and sugar** (ancient plant cellulose/lignin) → become the **carbon matrix** of coal.
- The **air pockets** inside the cake → become the **pore spaces** in coal (**porosity**).
- The **moisture** in the batter → becomes the **inherent moisture** trapped inside coal.
- The **hardness of the crust** → represents the **rank, hardness, and friability** of coal.
- The **density of the cake** (mass per unit volume) → represents the **bulk density / true density** of coal.
- The **color and shine of the crust** → represents **luster, streak, and color** of coal.

Just as a baker judges a cake by touch, look, and weight, a fuel engineer judges coal by its **physical appearance, hardness, density, porosity, and grindability**.

### 1.4 Standard Reference Constants & Metrics

The following universally accepted engineering parameters are used to quantify coal physical behavior:

- **True (skeletal) density of coal: 1.2 – 1.8 g/cm³**
- **Apparent (bulk) density: 0.6 – 0.9 g/cm³** (loose), **0.8 – 1.0 g/cm³** (compacted)
- **Hardness (Mohs Scale): 2 – 4** (peat to anthracite range)
- **Reflectance (Vitrinite %Ro,max): 0.2 – 6.0%** (rank indicator)
- **Grindability Index (HGI): 30 – 110** (Hardgrove)
- **Standard Reference: ISO 11760 (Classification of Coals)**, **ASTM D388 (US Standard)**, **IS 770 (BIS India)**

### 1.5 Primary Physical Properties — Master List (KTU Module 1 Syllabus)

| S.No. | Physical Property | Engineering Significance |
|-------|------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Color & Luster | Visual rank assessment |
| 2 | Hardness & Friability | Grindability, pulverizer selection |
| 3 | Density (True & Bulk) | Storage volume, transport |
| 4 | Porosity | Combustion reactivity, drying |
| 5 | Specific Gravity | Washability, float-sink tests |
| 6 | Moisture Content (Inherent) | CV penalty, handling |
| 7 | Reflectance (Vitrinite) | Rank & maturation index |
| 8 | Grindability (HGI) | Pulverizer energy demand |
| 9 | Free Swelling Index (FSI) | Combustion behavior indicator |
| 10 | Friability Index | Degradation during handling |
| 11 | Thermal Conductivity | Heat transfer in stockpiles |
| 12 | Specific Heat | Combustion process modeling |
| 13 | Electrical Resistivity | Spontaneous combustion risk |
| 14 | Angle of Repose | Stockpile design |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown of Each Physical Property

#### A. Color and Luster

- **Mechanism:** As coal rank increases, volatile matter decreases and carbon content increases, causing progressive darkening from **brown (lignite)** → **black with woody texture (sub-bituminous)** → **glossy black (bituminous)** → **metallic / submetallic luster (anthracite)**.
- **Engineering Utility:** Field geologists and fuel inspectors use color and luster for **preliminary rank assessment** before laboratory analysis.
- **Diagnostic value:** Anthracite shows **bright, conchoidal, semi-metallic luster**, while lignite shows **dull, earthy brown appearance**.

#### B. Hardness and Friability

- **Hardness:** Resistance to scratching (Mohs scale) and indentation (Vickers/Rockwell).
- **Friability:** Tendency of coal to break into smaller fragments under impact, abrasion, or pressure.
- **Mechanism:** Depends on **maceral composition** (vitrinite is harder; inertinite is friable), **moisture content** (drier = more friable), and **rank** (low-rank lignite is soft; anthracite is hardest but also brittle).
- **Engineering Utility:** Determines **selection of crushers, mills, and pulverizers** in coal-fired power plants.

#### C. Density (True, Apparent, Bulk)

Three distinct density definitions are critical:

1. **True Density (ρₜ)**: Mass per unit volume of coal excluding pore spaces, i.e., density of the **solid carbonaceous matrix** alone.
2. **Apparent Density (ρₐ)**: Mass per unit volume of coal including sealed/closed pores but excluding open pores accessible to fluids.
3. **Bulk Density (ρᵦ)**: Mass per unit volume of coal in **lump, granular, or compacted form**, including inter-particle voids.

- **Why three definitions?** Because a coal particle has:
  - Solid carbon skeleton
  - Closed (sealed) micro-pores
  - Open (accessible) macro-pores
  - Inter-particle void spaces in bulk mass

#### D. Porosity (ε)

- **Definition:** Fraction of coal volume occupied by pore spaces (open + closed).
- **Engineering Utility:** Higher porosity = higher **reactivity** (oxygen can penetrate), higher **drying rate**, higher **methane adsorption** (important in coal-bed methane).
- **Mechanism:** Low-rank coals (lignite) have **high porosity (~30–40%)**; high-rank coals (anthracite) have **low porosity (~5–10%)** due to compaction during coalification.

#### E. Specific Gravity (G)

- **Definition:** Ratio of coal density to density of water at standard temperature (usually 4 °C or 25 °C).
- **Engineering Utility:** Critical for **float-sink washability tests** — separation of ash-rich shale/pyrite from clean coal in beneficiation plants using dense-media separation (DMS).

#### F. Moisture Content (Inherent / Bed Moisture)

- **Definition:** Water retained within the pore structure of coal in its natural state, distinct from surface moisture.
- **Mechanism:** Adsorbed in micro-pores via capillary condensation and surface adsorption.
- **Engineering Utility:** High moisture → **reduced calorific value, increased transport cost, risk of freezing in winter, spontaneous combustion resistance**.

#### G. Vitrinite Reflectance (%Ro)

- **Definition:** Percentage of incident light reflected from the polished surface of **vitrinite macerals** under oil immersion in a reflected-light microscope.
- **Mechanism:** As rank increases, vitrinite molecular structure becomes more aromatic and ordered, increasing reflectivity.
- **Engineering Utility:** Most reliable **rank indicator**, used in oil & gas exploration to assess source rock maturity.

#### H. Hardgrove Grindability Index (HGI)

- **Definition:** Relative ease of pulverizing coal compared to a standard coal (HGI = 100).
- **Mechanism:** Test involves grinding 50 g of coal (−1.18 mm) in a ball mill under standard conditions, then sieving. The mass passing 75 µm is the HGI.
- **Engineering Utility:** Direct input to **pulverizer capacity, power consumption, and fineness selection** in thermal power stations.

#### I. Free Swelling Index (FSI) / Crucible Swelling Number (CSN)

- **Definition:** Visual size of coke button produced when 1 g of coal is heated rapidly in a standardized crucible.
- **Scale:** 0 (non-coking) to 9 (highly swelling).
- **Engineering Utility:** Indicates **coking behavior** — used to classify coal for metallurgical (coke-making) applications.

#### J. Friability Index

- **Definition:** Percentage reduction in average particle size after a standardized tumbling/impact test.
- **Engineering Utility:** Predicts **degradation, dust generation, and handling losses** during transport and stockpiling.

#### K. Thermal Conductivity (k)

- **Definition:** Ability of coal to conduct heat.
- **Range:** **0.15 – 0.45 W/(m·K)** — increases with rank and ash content.
- **Engineering Utility:** Critical in **stockpile self-heating analysis** and **combustion chamber design**.

#### L. Specific Heat (cp)

- **Definition:** Heat required to raise the temperature of unit mass of coal by 1 °C.
- **Range:** **1.0 – 1.5 kJ/(kg·K)** (dry basis).
- **Engineering Utility:** Used in **combustion calculations, furnace heat balance, and thermodynamic modeling**.

#### M. Electrical Resistivity

- **Definition:** Resistance to electrical current flow.
- **Trend:** Decreases sharply with rank — anthracite is a good conductor, lignite is a near-insulator.
- **Engineering Utility:** Indicator of **spontaneous combustion susceptibility** and used in **electrostatic precipitator design** for fly ash.

#### N. Angle of Repose

- **Definition:** Maximum angle of stable slope of a coal pile relative to horizontal.
- **Range:** **27° – 45°** depending on moisture, particle size, and compaction.
- **Engineering Utility:** **Stockpile geometry design, silo/bunker design, conveyor system design**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\rho_t = \dfrac{m}{V_{solid}}$ | True density (pycnometer method) | g/cm³ or kg/m³ |
| 2 | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V_{bulk}}$ | Bulk density (loose or tapped) | g/cm³ or kg/m³ |
| 3 | $G = \dfrac{\rho_{coal}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Specific gravity (dimensionless) | — |
| 4 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_{pore}}{V_{total}} = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}$ | Porosity (open + closed) | fraction (0–1) or % |
| 5 | $M_{inh} = \dfrac{m_{wet} - m_{dry}}{m_{dry}} \times 100$ | Inherent moisture (% dry basis) | % |
| 6 | $HGI = 13 + 6.93 \times W$ where $W$ = mass passing 75 µm (g) | Hardgrove Grindability Index | dimensionless |
| 7 | $CV_{net} = CV_{gross} - 9 \times H \times (2.467 - M)$ | Net CV from gross CV (Seyler's eqn) | kcal/kg |
| 8 | $FSI = \dfrac{d_{coke} - d_{coal}}{d_{coal}} \times 100$ | Free Swelling Index (% change in profile diameter) | % |
| 9 | $\phi_{max} = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{h}{r}\right)$ | Angle of Repose from cone geometry | degrees (°) |
| 10 | $k_{coal} = 0.32 + 0.0025 \times C_{daf}$ | Thermal conductivity vs carbon content | W/(m·K) |
| 11 | $c_p = 1.0 + 0.0064 \times M + 0.001 \times A$ | Specific heat (approx., dry basis) | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 12 | $R_v = 0.018 \times \ln(V) + 0.72$ (approx.) | Vitrinite reflectance vs VM (Meyers eqn) | % Ro |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In numerical problems, always quote answers in **SI units (kg/m³)** for density and **% (by mass)** for moisture, porosity, and reflectance — partial marking is awarded for unit correctness even if the numerical value is slightly off.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry / Field | Application of Physical Properties |
|------------------|-------------------------------------|
| **Thermal Power Plants** | Bulk density → bunker sizing; HGI → mill selection; angle of repose → coal yard design |
| **Steel & Metallurgy** | FSI / CSN → coking coal selection; reflectance → blending strategy |
| **Coal Beneficiation** | Specific gravity → washability curves; density → dense-media separator design |
| **Spontaneous Combustion Control** | Thermal conductivity, resistivity, moisture → stockpile management |
| **CBM & Underground Mining** | Porosity & reflectance → methane adsorption capacity, outburst risk |
| **Combustion Modeling** | Specific heat, thermal conductivity → CFD input for furnace simulation |
| **Transportation & Logistics** | Bulk density → stowage factor for ships/wagons; angle of repose → silo design |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between True Density, Bulk Density, and Porosity

**Given:**
- Let $V_{total}$ = total volume of a coal lump including all pores
- Let $V_{solid}$ = volume of the solid carbonaceous skeleton (excluding all pores)
- Let $V_{pore}$ = total volume of pores (open + closed)
- $V_{total} = V_{solid} + V_{pore}$

**Step 1: Define true density**

$$\rho_t = \dfrac{m}{V_{solid}}$$

**Step 2: Define bulk (apparent) density**

$$\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V_{total}} = \dfrac{m}{V_{solid} + V_{pore}}$$

**Step 3: Express porosity definition**

Porosity $\varepsilon$ is the fraction of total volume occupied by pores:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_{pore}}{V_{total}}$$

**Step 4: Substitute and rearrange**

From Step 1, $V_{solid} = \dfrac{m}{\rho_t}$.
From Step 2, $V_{total} = \dfrac{m}{\rho_b}$.

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_{pore}}{V_{total}} = \dfrac{V_{total} - V_{solid}}{V_{total}} = 1 - \dfrac{V_{solid}}{V_{total}}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the density expressions**

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{m/\rho_t}{m/\rho_b} = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Master Formula:**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_t} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \rho_b = \rho_t(1 - \varepsilon)}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Gravity and Washability Application

**Problem Logic:**
A float-sink test in a coal beneficiation plant uses a dense medium of specific gravity $G_m$. Coal particles with specific gravity $G_p$ behave as follows:

- If $G_p < G_m$ → particle **floats** (clean coal product)
- If $G_p > G_m$ → particle **sinks** (reject/shale)

**Step 1:** Force balance on a particle immersed in dense medium:

$$F_{buoyancy} = \rho_m \cdot V \cdot g$$
$$F_{gravity} = \rho_p \cdot V \cdot g$$

**Step 2:** Net downward force per unit volume:

$$F_{net} = (\rho_p - \rho_m) \cdot g$$

**Step 3:** The particle floats if net force is negative, i.e., $\rho_p < \rho_m$:

$$G_p = \dfrac{\rho_p}{\rho_{water}} < \dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho_{water}} = G_m$$

**Final Result:** The dividing specific gravity $G_d = G_m$ is the **washability cut-point**, used to construct the **Mayer curve (washability curve)** in coal preparation plants.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hardgrove Grindability Index (HGI) Empirical Formula

The standard HGI test yields the mass $W$ (in grams) of coal passing through a 75 µm sieve after the standard grinding procedure. The empirical relationship is:

$$HGI = 13 + 6.93 \cdot W$$

**Step-by-step justification:**
- When $W = 12.6$ g → HGI = 100 (standard reference coal)
- When $W = 0$ g → HGI = 13 (theoretical minimum, hardest to grind)
- When $W = 14$ g → HGI ≈ 110 (theoretical maximum, easiest to grind)

**Engineering Use:** HGI is used in Bond's law for pulverizer power estimation:

$$P = 10 \cdot W_i \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right)$$

where $W_i$ (Work Index) for coal correlates inversely with HGI as:

$$W_i \approx \dfrac{435}{HGI^{0.91}} \quad (\text{kWh/short ton})$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Net Calorific Value Penalty Due to Moisture

The gross calorific value (GCV) of coal is measured in a bomb calorimeter with all water in liquid form. In real boiler operation, water leaves as steam, incurring a latent heat penalty. The latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C is **2260 kJ/kg = 540 kcal/kg**.

**Step 1: Hydrogen in coal produces water**

1 kg of hydrogen produces 9 kg of water (since $2H_2 + O_2 \to 2H_2O$, molecular weights $2 \to 18$).

**Step 2: Latent heat loss per kg of coal**

$$L_H = 9 \times H \times 540 \;\; \text{kcal/kg of coal}$$

**Step 3: Inherent moisture vaporization loss**

$$L_M = M \times 540 \;\; \text{kcal/kg of coal}$$

**Step 4: Net calorific value**

$$NCV = GCV - (9H + M) \times 540 \;\; \text{kcal/kg}$$

**Seyler's empirical form:**

$$NCV = GCV - 9H(2.467) - M(0.6) \;\; \text{(kJ/kg basis, simplified)}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Coal Rank on Physical Properties

| Property | Peat | Lignite | Sub-bituminous | Bituminous | Anthracite |
|----------|------|---------|----------------|------------|------------|
| Color | Brown | Brown-black | Dull black | Black | Black with metallic luster |
| Hardness (Mohs) | <1 | ~1.5 | ~2 | 2–2.5 | 3.5–4 |
| True Density (g/cm³) | 0.7–1.0 | 1.1–1.3 | 1.25–1.4 | 1.3–1.5 | 1.5–1.8 |
| Porosity (%) | 50–60 | 30–40 | 15–25 | 5–15 | 3–8 |
| Moisture (%) | 70–90 | 30–60 | 15–30 | 5–15 | 2–5 |
| Reflectance %Ro | <0.2 | 0.2–0.4 | 0.4–0.6 | 0.6–2.0 | 2.0–6.0 |
| HGI | <30 | 30–50 | 40–60 | 50–90 | 40–70 |
| FSI | 0 | 0–1 | 1–2 | 2–9 | 1–2 |
| Thermal Cond. (W/mK) | 0.10 | 0.15 | 0.20 | 0.25 | 0.40 |
| Electrical Resistivity | Insulator | Insulator | Semi | Semi-conductor | Conductor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** As rank increases, density, hardness, reflectance, and thermal conductivity **increase**, while porosity, moisture, and friability **decrease**. This monotonic trend is the fundamental basis of coal classification by physical properties.

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Porosity Calculation

**Problem:** A coal sample has:
- Mass $m = 50$ g
- True density $\rho_t = 1.45$ g/cm³
- Bulk density $\rho_b = 1.05$ g/cm³

**Find:** Porosity $\varepsilon$

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply the master formula:

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}$$

Step 2 — Substitute values:

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{1.05}{1.45} = 1 - 0.7241$$

Step 3 — Compute:

$$\varepsilon = 0.2759 \approx 27.6\%$$

**Answer:** Porosity of the coal sample is **27.6%**, indicating it is a **medium-rank sub-bituminous/bituminous coal**.

---

### 3.7 Worked Numerical Example — HGI from Sieve Analysis

**Problem:** In a Hardgrove test on 50 g of coal, the mass passing through a 75 µm sieve after grinding is $W = 8.5$ g. Determine HGI and classify the coal.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply the empirical formula:

$$HGI = 13 + 6.93 \times W = 13 + 6.93 \times 8.5$$

Step 2 — Compute:

$$HGI = 13 + 58.91 = 91.91 \approx 92$$

**Answer:** $HGI = 92$, indicating **soft coal (easy to grind)** — typical of bituminous coal. Such coal is suitable for **ball mills and bowl mills** in thermal power plants.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Coal Physical Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[COAL PHYSICAL PROPERTIES] --> B[VISUAL PROPERTIES]
    A --> C[MECHANICAL PROPERTIES]
    A --> D[THERMAL PROPERTIES]
    A --> E[ELECTRICAL PROPERTIES]
    A --> F[TRANSPORT PROPERTIES]

    B --> B1[Color]
    B --> B2[Luster]
    B --> B3[Streak]
    B --> B4[Reflectance Ro]

    C --> C1[Hardness Mohs]
    C --> C2[Friability Index]
    C --> C3[Hardgrove HGI]
    C --> C4[FSI CSN]
    C --> C5[True Density]
    C --> C6[Specific Gravity]

    D --> D1[Thermal Conductivity k]
    D --> D2[Specific Heat cp]
    D --> D3[Moisture Content]
    D --> D4[Porosity epsilon]

    E --> E1[Electrical Resistivity]
    E --> E2[Dielectric Constant]

    F --> F1[Bulk Density]
    F --> F2[Angle of Repose]
    F --> F3[Compressibility]
    F --> F4[Cohesiveness]

    C5 --> C5a[Used in: Storage Design]
    C5a --> C5b[Used in: Washability Tests]
    C5a --> C5c[Used in: Combustion Modeling]
    D4 --> D4a[Used in: Drying]
    D4a --> D4b[Used in: Reactivity]
    D4b --> D4c[Used in: CBM Recovery]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Physical Property Testing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Coal Sample] --> B[Sampling and Division]
    B --> C[Physical Property Tests]
    C --> D[Visual Tests]
    C --> E[Density Tests]
    C --> F[Mechanical Tests]
    C --> G[Thermal Tests]
    C --> H[Optical Tests]

    D --> D1[Color Luster Streak]
    E --> E1[Pycnometer Method]
    E --> E2[Float Sink Test]
    F --> F1[Mohs Hardness]
    F --> F2[Hardgrove HGI]
    F --> F3[Drop Test Friability]
    G --> G1[Thermal Conductivity Probe]
    G --> G2[Calorimeter Specific Heat]
    H --> H1[Microscope Reflectance]

    D1 --> X[Classification Report]
    E1 --> X
    E2 --> X
    F1 --> X
    F2 --> X
    F3 --> X
    G1 --> X
    G2 --> X
    H1 --> X

    X --> Y[Rank and Grade Assignment]
    Y --> Z[End Use: Power Metallurgy Beneficiation]
```

### 4.3 Porosity & Density Relationship — Conceptual Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Coal Lump Volume V_total] --> B[Solid Matrix V_solid]
    A --> C[Pore Space V_pore]
    B --> B1[True Density rho_t]
    C --> C1[Open Pores]
    C --> C2[Closed Pores]
    C1 --> C1a[Accessible to Air Water]
    C1a --> C1b[Affect Drying and Reactivity]
    C2 --> C2a[Sealed Within Matrix]
    C2a --> C2b[Affect Apparent Density]
    B1 --> D[Bulk Density rho_b]
    C1b --> D
    C2b --> D
    D --> E[Porosity epsilon = 1 - rho_b/rho_t]
    B1 --> E
```

### 4.4 Hardgrove Grindability Index Test — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Coal Sample Minus 1.18 mm] --> B[50 g Charge in Bowl Mill]
    B --> C[Standard 60 Revolutions at 20 kgf Load]
    C --> D[Sieve Through 75 Micron]
    D --> E[Mass Passing = W grams]
    E --> F[Apply HGI = 13 + 6.93 x W]
    F --> G{HGI Value}
    G -->|Below 40| H[Hard Coal Anthracite]
    G -->|40 to 70| I[Medium Coal Bituminous]
    G -->|Above 70| J[Soft Coal Lignite Subbituminous]
    H --> K[Pulverizer Selection]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Porosity. How is it related to True and Bulk Density?

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Porosity ($\varepsilon$)** is defined as the ratio of the total volume of pores (both open and closed) within a coal sample to the total volume of the coal sample, expressed as a fraction or percentage.

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_{pore}}{V_{total}}$$

**Relationship with Density:**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}}$$

where $\rho_b$ = bulk density (mass per unit total volume) and $\rho_t$ = true density (mass per unit solid volume). Higher $\varepsilon$ indicates greater pore volume and typically corresponds to **lower-rank, more reactive coals** such as lignite.

---

#### Question 2: List Any Six Physical Properties of Coal Used in Industry for Its Quality Assessment.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Six key physical properties of coal used in industry are:

1. **Hardness (Mohs Scale)** — indicates ease of pulverization.
2. **Friability** — measures breakage during handling.
3. **True and Bulk Density** — required for storage and transport sizing.
4. **Specific Gravity** — used in washability and float-sink tests.
5. **Hardgrove Grindability Index (HGI)** — direct input to pulverizer design.
6. **Free Swelling Index (FSI)** — indicates coking behavior for metallurgical use.

*(Valid alternatives: porosity, reflectance, angle of repose, moisture content, thermal conductivity.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | 14 Marks]**

**Q. (a) Define the following physical properties of coal and state their engineering significance: (i) Hardgrove Grindability Index, (ii) Free Swelling Index, (iii) Vitrinite Reflectance, (iv) Angle of Repose.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Hardgrove Grindability Index (HGI):**

It is a measure of the relative ease of pulverizing a coal sample compared to a standard reference coal (HGI = 100). The test grinds 50 g of coal (−1.18 mm) in a ball mill under a 20 kgf load for 60 revolutions. The mass passing through a 75 µm sieve is recorded as $W$, and:

$$HGI = 13 + 6.93 \times W$$

**Significance:** Direct indicator for **pulverizer selection and power consumption** in thermal power plants. Low HGI (<40) means harder coal requiring more crushing energy.

**(ii) Free Swelling Index (FSI) / Crucible Swelling Number (CSN):**

A visual index (0 to 9) measuring the size and profile of the coke button produced when 1 g of coal is heated rapidly in a standardized covered crucible to 800 °C.

**Significance:** Predicts **coking behavior** of coal; essential for **metallurgical coke-making** in the steel industry. FSI = 0 means non-coking; FSI = 8–9 indicates excellent coking.

**(iii) Vitrinite Reflectance (%Ro):**

The percentage of incident light reflected from a polished surface of vitrinite maceral under oil immersion (RI = 1.518) using a reflected-light microscope.

**Significance:** Most reliable **rank parameter** for coal. Increases monotonically from 0.2% (peat) to 6.0% (anthracite). Used in coal-bed methane evaluation and coal classification.

**(iv) Angle of Repose:**

The maximum angle (in degrees) that a conical pile of coal makes with the horizontal plane under natural (loose) piling conditions.

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{h}{r}\right)$$

**Significance:** Used in **stockpile geometry design, bunker/silo design, and conveyor belt inclinations** in coal-handling plants.

---

**Q. (b) A coal sample has true density 1.42 g/cm³ and bulk density 1.10 g/cm³. Calculate the porosity. If the same coal has mass 200 g in a 250 mL measuring cylinder, comment on the consistency of the measurement.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Part (i) Porosity Calculation:**

Given: $\rho_t = 1.42$ g/cm³, $\rho_b = 1.10$ g/cm³

Apply the master formula:

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_t} = 1 - \dfrac{1.10}{1.42} = 1 - 0.7746 = 0.2254$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 22.54\%}$$

**Part (ii) Consistency Check:**

The measured bulk density from the cylinder:

$$\rho_{measured} = \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{200 \text{ g}}{250 \text{ cm}^3} = 0.80 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Comment:** The measured bulk density (0.80 g/cm³) is **lower** than the given bulk density (1.10 g/cm³). This indicates that the coal in the measuring cylinder is in a **loose, loosely-packed state** (with more inter-particle void space), whereas the given value of 1.10 g/cm³ corresponds to a **tapped/compacted bulk density**. The measurement is therefore **internally consistent** if interpreted as loose bulk density.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | 14 Marks]**

**Q. (a) Explain in detail the physical properties of coal that govern (i) its pulverization behavior, and (ii) its coking behavior. List the standard test methods and the typical numerical ranges for each property.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Properties Governing Pulverization Behavior:**

| Property | Test Standard | Typical Range | Effect on Pulverization |
|----------|--------------|---------------|-------------------------|
| Hardness | Mohs Scale (IS 1438) | 2 – 4 | Higher hardness → more energy |
| HGI | ASTM D409 | 30 – 110 | Higher HGI → easier to grind |
| Friability | ISO 4699 | 10 – 80% | High friability → more fines |
| Moisture | IS 1350 | 2 – 60% | High moisture → clogging, mill choking |
| Specific Gravity | IS 1438 | 1.2 – 1.8 | Used in dense-media pre-separation |

The **Hardgrove Grindability Index (HGI)** is the most direct and universally used indicator. For thermal power plants, **HGI > 50 is preferred** for ball/ring-roller mills.

**(ii) Properties Governing Coking Behavior:**

| Property | Test Standard | Typical Range | Effect on Coke |
|----------|--------------|---------------|---------------|
| Free Swelling Index (FSI) | ASTM D720 | 1 – 9 | Higher FSI → better coke strength |
| Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro) | ISO 7404 | 0.6 – 1.5% | Optimal coking: 0.9 – 1.3% |
| Maceral Composition | ISO 7404 | Vitrinite-rich | More vitrinite → better coke |
| Rheological Properties (Gieseler) | ASTM D2639 | Max fluidity 0 – 35 ddpm | Indicates plastic stage |

**Coking coal** is a blend of **prime coking** (FSI 7–9), **medium coking** (FSI 4–6), and **semi-coking** (FSI 2–3) coals, with the optimal vitrinite reflectance between 0.9% and 1.3% to produce strong metallurgical coke.

---

**Q. (b) A coal seam is being evaluated for a coal-bed methane (CBM) extraction project. As a fuel engineer, which physical property would you prioritize for assessment? Justify your answer with relevant numerical ranges and their engineering implications.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Property to Prioritize: Porosity (with Vitrinite Reflectance as a co-indicator)**

**Justification:**

CBM extraction depends on the coal's ability to **adsorb and desorb methane** from its internal pore structure. Porosity directly governs the **storage capacity**, while vitrinite reflectance indicates the **thermal maturity** for methane generation.

**Numerical Analysis:**

| Rank | Porosity (%) | %Ro | CBM Potential |
|------|-------------|-----|---------------|
| Lignite | 30 – 40 | 0.2 – 0.4 | Low – high moisture, immature |
| Sub-bituminous | 15 – 25 | 0.4 – 0.6 | Moderate – developing fractures |
| High-Volatile Bituminous | 5 – 15 | 0.6 – 1.0 | High – optimum for CBM |
| Low-Volatile Bituminous | 5 – 10 | 1.0 – 2.0 | Good – well-developed cleat system |
| Anthracite | 3 – 8 | 2.0 – 6.0 | Variable – low porosity, but dry gas |

**Engineering Implication:**

For optimum CBM, target coal with:
- **Porosity: 5–15%** (balanced storage and permeability)
- **%Ro: 0.8–1.5%** (thermally mature, methane-generating window)
- **High density of cleats and fractures** (permeability)

A coal seam with porosity < 5% is uneconomical for CBM regardless of reflectance, while porosity > 25% suggests low-rank coal with high adsorbed water competing with methane adsorption.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Physical Properties of Coal Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Writing porosity as 0.22 instead of 22% — *always convert and clearly state the unit*.
> 2. **Confusing True vs Bulk Density:** True density **excludes** all pores; bulk density **includes** inter-particle voids. Using the wrong one in the porosity formula gives wrong answers.
> 3. **HGI Misinterpretation:** Higher HGI = **softer** coal (easier to grind), NOT harder. This is the most common conceptual error.
> 4. **Reflectance Direction:** Higher %Ro = **higher rank** (anthracite), NOT lower. Students often invert the trend.
> 5. **Skipping the Empirical Constant:** Writing HGI = $W$ alone is incomplete; the standard formula is $HGI = 13 + 6.93W$.
> 6. **Mixing Up FSI and Coking:** FSI = 0 means **non-coking**, not weakly coking. The scale is 0–9 with 0 = no swelling at all.
> 7. **Ignoring the Base (dry/as-received):** Moisture is reported on **dry basis (db) or as-received (ar) basis** — failing to specify loses 1 mark in 14-mark numericals.
> 8. **Neglecting Engineering Significance:** Just defining a property without explaining "**why it matters to the engineer**" loses up to 2 marks in long answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] Coal is a **sedimentary, biogenic, combustible rock** formed by coalification of plant matter.
- [ ] Physical properties are **engineering/bulk** in nature — they govern handling, sizing, and equipment design.
- [ ] **True density** = solid matrix only; **Bulk density** = total volume (including pores and inter-particle voids).
- [ ] **Porosity formula:** $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_b/\rho_t$ — a must-remember derivation for KTU.
- [ ] **Specific gravity** drives **float-sink washability tests** in coal beneficiation.
- [ ] **HGI** = 13 + 6.93W; higher HGI = softer coal = easier pulverization.
- [ ] **Vitrinite reflectance (%Ro)** is the most reliable **rank parameter** (range 0.2% to 6.0%).
- [ ] **FSI scale 0–9**: 0 = non-coking, 8–9 = excellent coking for metallurgical use.
- [ ] **Angle of repose** governs stockpile, bunker, and silo design geometry.
- [ ] **Rank trends:** As rank ↑ → density ↑, hardness ↑, reflectance ↑, porosity ↓, moisture ↓.
- [ ] **Thermal conductivity** increases with rank; critical for stockpile self-heating analysis.
- [ ] **Electrical resistivity** decreases with rank — anthracite is a conductor, lignite is an insulator.
- [ ] **Specific heat** ~ 1.0–1.5 kJ/(kg·K) — used in furnace heat balance calculations.
- [ ] Always specify the **basis (dry, as-received, daf)** for moisture, ash, and CV values.
- [ ] Remember the **three density definitions** (true, apparent, bulk) and their physical meaning.
- [ ] For CBM assessment, prioritize **porosity (5–15%)** and **reflectance (0.8–1.5% Ro)**.
- [ ] **Units checklist:** density in kg/m³ (SI) or g/cm³; porosity in %; HGI dimensionless; angle in degrees.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proximate and Ultimate Analysis of Coal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL engineering
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Proximate and Ultimate Analysis of Coal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 - Proximate and Ultimate Analysis of Coal

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Coal Analysis** is the systematic determination of the physical and chemical properties of coal, expressed in terms of its constituent components. It is broadly classified into two complementary methods: **Proximate Analysis** and **Ultimate Analysis**. Together, these analyses form the foundational characterisation protocol for any solid, liquid, or gaseous fuel prior to its industrial utilisation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proximate Analysis** determines four operational parameters: **Moisture (M)**, **Volatile Matter (VM)**, **Ash (A)**, and **Fixed Carbon (FC)**. The summation of these four components equals **100%** on an air-dry basis.
>
> **Ultimate Analysis** determines the elemental composition: **Carbon (C)**, **Hydrogen (H)**, **Nitrogen (N)**, **Sulphur (S)**, and **Oxygen (O)**, along with Ash and Moisture. The summation also equals **100%**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy and Intuition

Imagine you purchase a packet of mixed dry fruits. Before you consume it, you want to know three things:

1. **How much water is trapped inside it?** *(Moisture)* — If there is too much, it will rot and lose heating value.
2. **How much non-edible shell/husk is present?** *(Ash + Inerts)* — This is useless weight that you pay for.
3. **What is the actual fat/protein content that gives you energy?** *(Fixed Carbon / Combustible elements)* — This is the real "fuel" inside.

**Proximate Analysis** is like the first two checks — it gives the **operational/engineering** view (how the coal will behave in a furnace, how much smoke, how much residue). **Ultimate Analysis** is like opening the packet and doing a **chemical breakdown** of the actual elemental nutrients (C, H, N, S, O) that produce heat upon combustion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Standards:**
> - **IS 1350 Series** — Indian Standard for Coal Analysis
> - **ASTM D3172** — Standard Practice for Proximate Analysis
> - **ASTM D3176** — Standard Practice for Ultimate Analysis
> - **Bomb Calorimeter Standard:** **ISO 1928**

### 1.3 Why These Analyses Are Mandatory in KTU Syllabus

| Parameter | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| Moisture | Reduces calorific value; causes handling issues |
| Volatile Matter | Governs flame stability and combustion kinetics |
| Ash | Causes slagging, fouling, and disposal problems |
| Fixed Carbon | Primary heat-generating solid residue |
| C, H, N, S, O | Determines stoichiometric air requirement and emission profile |

### 1.4 Classification of Coal by Rank

Coal is broadly classified based on **carbon content** and **age** (degree of metamorphism):

1. **Peat** — C ≈ 55%; high moisture
2. **Lignite** — C ≈ 60-70%; brown coal
3. **Bituminous** — C ≈ 78-90%; most widely used in power plants
4. **Anthracite** — C ≈ 92-98%; highest rank, cleanest burning
5. **Coke** — Derived residue after destructive distillation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis and KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Proximate Analysis — Step-by-Step Logic

The proximate analysis is a **sequential thermal decomposition** experiment, where approximately **1 gram of air-dried coal** (finely powdered, -212 µm / 72 mesh) is subjected to controlled heating in a **silica crucible** inside a **muffle furnace**.

### Step A: Determination of Moisture (M)

- Coal sample is heated in an **oven at 105 °C – 110 °C for 1 hour**.
- The loss in weight represents **moisture content**.
- Crucible is cooled in a **desiccator** to prevent re-absorption of atmospheric moisture.

$$M\,(\%) = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100$$

Where:
- $W_1$ = Weight of coal sample taken (g)
- $W_2$ = Weight of coal after oven drying (g)

### Step B: Determination of Volatile Matter (VM)

- The dried sample from Step A is heated in a **muffle furnace at 925 °C ± 20 °C for exactly 7 minutes** with the crucible **covered** (to prevent air ingress).
- The loss in weight (excluding moisture already removed) gives the **volatile matter**.
- Volatile matter includes **hydrocarbons, H₂, CO, CO₂, CH₄, tar vapours, etc.**

$$VM\,(\%) = \frac{W_2 - W_3}{W_1} \times 100$$

Where:
- $W_3$ = Weight of residue after volatile matter expulsion (g)

### Step C: Determination of Ash (A)

- The residue from Step B is heated in the **same crucible (lid removed)** at **700–750 °C until constant weight** is obtained.
- This burns off all combustible matter, leaving only **incombustible mineral matter (ash)**.

$$A\,(\%) = \frac{W_4}{W_1} \times 100$$

Where:
- $W_4$ = Weight of ash residue (g)

### Step D: Determination of Fixed Carbon (FC) — By Difference

$$FC\,(\%) = 100 - (M + VM + A)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Summing Rule:** $M + VM + A + FC = 100\%$ (always on the as-determined / air-dry basis)

---

## 2.2 Ultimate Analysis — Elemental Determination Logic

The ultimate analysis determines the **chemical elements** present in coal.

### Step A: Carbon and Hydrogen (Liebig Combustion Method)

- A known mass of coal (≈ 1–2 g) is burnt in a **stream of pure oxygen** in a combustion tube.
- **Carbon** is converted to **CO₂** and absorbed in a **soda-lime or KOH tube** (weight gain = mass of CO₂).
- **Hydrogen** is converted to **H₂O** and absorbed in a **magnesium perchlorate or anhydrous CaCl₂ tube** (weight gain = mass of H₂O).
- A **copper gauge** is used to absorb any free sulphur oxides.

$$C\,(\%) = \frac{12}{44} \times \frac{\text{Mass of CO}_2 \text{ absorbed}}{\text{Mass of coal}} \times 100$$

$$H\,(\%) = \frac{2}{18} \times \frac{\text{Mass of H}_2\text{O absorbed}}{\text{Mass of coal}} \times 100$$

### Step B: Nitrogen (Kjeldahl Method or Dumas Method)

- Coal is digested with **concentrated H₂SO₄** in the presence of a catalyst (K₂SO₄ + CuSO₄).
- Nitrogen is converted to **ammonium sulphate**.
- **NH₃ is liberated** by adding excess NaOH, distilled into a known volume of standard H₂SO₄ or HCl, and back-titrated.

$$N\,(\%) = \frac{1.4 \times N_a \times (V_b - V_s)}{W}$$

Where:
- $N_a$ = Normality of acid
- $V_b$ = Volume of acid for blank (mL)
- $V_s$ = Volume of acid for sample (mL)
- $W$ = Weight of coal (g)

### Step C: Sulphur (Eschka Method)

- Coal is fused with **Eschka mixture (MgO + Na₂CO₃)** in a muffle furnace.
- Sulphur is converted to **sulphates**, leached with hot water, and precipitated as **BaSO₄** by adding BaCl₂.
- The precipitate is filtered, washed, dried, and weighed.

$$S\,(\%) = \frac{32}{233} \times \frac{\text{Mass of BaSO}_4}{W} \times 100$$

### Step D: Oxygen — By Difference

$$O\,(\%) = 100 - (C + H + N + S + A + M)$$

> [!NOTE]
> Oxygen is **never determined directly**; it is always obtained by subtraction because direct determination is technically complex and prone to error.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture (M) | $\frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ | % | Proximate analysis |
| Volatile Matter (VM) | $\frac{W_2 - W_3}{W_1} \times 100$ | % | Proximate analysis |
| Ash (A) | $\frac{W_4}{W_1} \times 100$ | % | Proximate analysis |
| Fixed Carbon (FC) | $100 - (M + VM + A)$ | % | By difference |
| Carbon (C) | $\frac{12}{44} \times \frac{m_{CO_2}}{W} \times 100$ | % | Liebig method |
| Hydrogen (H) | $\frac{2}{18} \times \frac{m_{H_2O}}{W} \times 100$ | % | Liebig method |
| Nitrogen (N) | $\frac{1.4 \times N_a \times (V_b - V_s)}{W}$ | % | Kjeldahl method |
| Sulphur (S) | $\frac{32}{233} \times \frac{m_{BaSO_4}}{W} \times 100$ | % | Eschka method |
| Oxygen (O) | $100 - (C + H + N + S + A + M)$ | % | By difference |
| Dulong's GCV | $\frac{8080\,C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240\,S}{100}$ | kcal/kg | Calorific value |
| NCV | $GCV - \frac{9\,H \times 587}{100}$ | kcal/kg | Net calorific value |
| Air Required (O₂) | $\frac{32C + 8H + 32S - 8O}{12 \times W}$ | kg/kg fuel | Stoichiometry |
| Theoretical Air | $\frac{100}{23} \times O_2$ | kg/kg fuel | 23% O₂ by mass in air |

### 2.4 Stoichiometric Oxygen & Air Requirement Derivation Logic

For combustion of fuel: C + O₂ → CO₂ ; H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O ; S + O₂ → SO₂

- 12 kg C requires 32 kg O₂
- 4 kg H requires 32 kg O₂
- 32 kg S requires 32 kg O₂
- 32 kg O releases 32 kg O₂ (already in fuel)

$$O_2 \text{ required (kg/kg fuel)} = \frac{32\,C + 8\,H + 32\,S - 8\,O}{100 \times W_{fuel}}$$

Since air contains **23% O₂ by mass** and **21% by volume**:

$$\text{Theoretical Air} = \frac{O_2 \text{ required}}{0.23} \text{ kg/kg fuel}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Real furnaces operate at **20–50% excess air** to ensure complete combustion.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants:** Selection of coal grade for thermal power stations based on GCV, ash, and VM.
- **Coke Making (Metallurgy):** Bituminous coal with low VM, low ash, and high FC is preferred.
- **Pollution Control:** Sulphur and nitrogen data guide flue gas desulphurisation (FGD) and De-NOx system design.
- **Combustion Stoichiometry:** Ultimate analysis data is mandatory for designing burners, air fans, and chimneys.
- **Cement Industry:** High-ash coal is preferred as ash becomes part of the clinker (Portland cement raw mix).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations of Key Empirical Formulas

### Derivation 1: Dulong's Formula for Gross Calorific Value (GCV)

Dulong's empirical formula combines the heat released by each combustible element in coal.

**Starting Equation (Empirical):**

$$GCV = 8080\,C + 34500\,(H - \frac{O}{8}) + 2240\,S \;\;\text{kcal/kg}$$

**Logic Behind Each Coefficient:**

- **8080 kcal/kg C:** The heat released when 1 kg of pure carbon burns to CO₂.
- **34500 kcal/kg H:** Heat released when 1 kg of hydrogen burns to H₂O.
- **$H - \frac{O}{8}$:** The available hydrogen after subtracting the hydrogen needed to combine with the fuel's intrinsic oxygen (since $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \to H_2O$, i.e., 2 kg H needs 16 kg O, so 1 kg H needs 8 kg O).
- **2240 kcal/kg S:** Heat released when 1 kg of sulphur burns to SO₂.

**All percentages in the formula are expressed on a mass basis (kg of element per 100 kg of coal).** This is why we divide by 100 at the end.

**Algebraic Expansion for a Sample Calculation:**

Let $C = 70\%$, $H = 5\%$, $S = 1\%$, $O = 10\%$, $A = 8\%$, $M = 6\%$ (on a dry basis).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV} &= \frac{8080 \times 70 + 34500 \times \left(5 - \frac{10}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 1}{100} \\
&= \frac{565600 + 34500 \times 3.75 + 2240}{100} \\
&= \frac{565600 + 129375 + 2240}{100} \\
&= \frac{697215}{100} \\
&= 6972.15 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion to MJ/kg:** $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.186 \text{ J}$, so:

$$GCV = 6972.15 \times 4.186 \times 10^{-3} = 29.18 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

---

### Derivation 2: Net Calorific Value (NCV) from GCV

Hydrogen in fuel, upon combustion, forms water. The latent heat of vaporisation of this water is **not recovered** in NCV.

- 1 kg of H produces 9 kg of H₂O (since $2H_2 + O_2 \to 2H_2O$; 4 kg H produces 36 kg H₂O; 1 kg H produces 9 kg H₂O).
- Latent heat of vaporisation of water = **587 kcal/kg**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
NCV &= GCV - \text{Latent heat lost} \\
&= GCV - \frac{9\,H \times 587}{100} \\
&= GCV - 52.83\,H
\end{aligned}
$$

For the same example: $H = 5\%$

$$
\begin{aligned}
NCV &= 6972.15 - \frac{9 \times 5 \times 587}{100} \\
&= 6972.15 - 264.15 \\
&= 6708.00 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Derivation 3: Stoichiometric Air Required for Combustion

Combustion reactions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C + O_2 &\to CO_2 \quad \text{(12 kg C needs 32 kg O}_2\text{)} \\
H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 &\to H_2O \quad \text{(4 kg H needs 32 kg O}_2\text{)} \\
S + O_2 &\to SO_2 \quad \text{(32 kg S needs 32 kg O}_2\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For 100 kg of fuel containing C, H, S, O (in kg):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O}_2 \text{ required} &= \frac{32\,C}{12} + \frac{32\,H}{4} + \frac{32\,S}{32} - \frac{32\,O}{32} \\
&= 2.667\,C + 8\,H + S - O \;\;\text{(kg of O}_2 \text{ per 100 kg fuel)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The minus O term accounts for the fact that intrinsic oxygen in coal can partially oxidise hydrogen and carbon, so less external air is required.

Since air is **23% O₂ by mass**:

$$\text{Theoretical Air} = \frac{2.667\,C + 8\,H + S - O}{23} \text{ kg/kg fuel}$$

**Sample Calculation (continuing above example):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_2 \text{ required} &= 2.667 \times 70 + 8 \times 5 + 1 - 10 \\
&= 186.69 + 40 + 1 - 10 \\
&= 217.69 \text{ kg per 100 kg fuel} \\
&= 2.177 \text{ kg/kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Theoretical Air} &= \frac{2.177}{0.23} = 9.465 \text{ kg/kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Conversion Between Bases (Critical for KTU Problems)

Coal analysis results can be reported on different bases. Converting between them is a frequent KTU exam topic.

| Basis | Definition | Conversion Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **As Received (ar)** | Coal as it arrives (with total moisture) | Reference (default in trade) |
| **Air-Dried (ad)** | Coal exposed to air until surface dry | Excludes surface moisture |
| **Dry Basis (db)** | Moisture-free basis | $X_{db} = X_{ad} \times \frac{100}{100 - M_{ad}}$ |
| **Dry Ash-Free (daf)** | Excludes moisture and ash | $X_{daf} = X_{ad} \times \frac{100}{100 - (M_{ad} + A_{ad})}$ |

**Example Conversion:** A coal has $A_{ad} = 12\%$, $M_{ad} = 4\%$. Find ash on dry basis.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{db} &= A_{ad} \times \frac{100}{100 - M_{ad}} \\
&= 12 \times \frac{100}{100 - 4} \\
&= 12 \times 1.0417 \\
&= 12.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Comprehensive KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A coal sample gave the following data:
- Mass of empty crucible = 18.42 g
- Mass of crucible + coal = 19.42 g (1 g coal)
- Mass after drying at 110 °C = 19.34 g
- Mass after heating at 925 °C (covered) = 18.86 g
- Mass after complete ashing = 18.49 g

**Calculate: (a) Moisture (b) Volatile Matter (c) Ash (d) Fixed Carbon**

**Step 1: Identify Weights**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 &= 1.00 \text{ g (coal taken)} \\
W_2 &= 19.34 - 18.42 = 0.92 \text{ g} \\
W_3 &= 18.86 - 18.42 = 0.44 \text{ g} \\
W_4 &= 18.49 - 18.42 = 0.07 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Moisture**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \frac{1.00 - 0.92}{1.00} \times 100 = 8.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Volatile Matter**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VM &= \frac{0.92 - 0.44}{1.00} \times 100 = 48.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Ash**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{0.07}{1.00} \times 100 = 7.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Fixed Carbon**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FC &= 100 - (8.00 + 48.00 + 7.00) = 37.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Inference:** High VM (48%) and moderate FC (37%) indicates this is a **bituminous coal** (possibly a coking-type coal).

---

## 3.4 Numerical on Dulong's Formula

**Problem:** The ultimate analysis of a coal is: C = 80%, H = 5%, N = 1.5%, S = 0.5%, O = 8%, Ash = 5%. Calculate the GCV and NCV using Dulong's formula.

**Step 1: GCV Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV &= \frac{8080 \times 80 + 34500 \times \left(5 - \frac{8}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 0.5}{100} \\
&= \frac{646400 + 34500 \times 4 + 1120}{100} \\
&= \frac{646400 + 138000 + 1120}{100} \\
&= \frac{785520}{100} \\
&= 7855.20 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: NCV Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NCV &= GCV - \frac{9 \times H \times 587}{100} \\
&= 7855.20 - \frac{9 \times 5 \times 587}{100} \\
&= 7855.20 - 264.15 \\
&= 7591.05 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Stoichiometric Air**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_2 \text{ required} &= 2.667 \times 80 + 8 \times 5 + 0.5 - 8 \\
&= 213.36 + 40 + 0.5 - 8 \\
&= 245.86 \text{ kg per 100 kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Theoretical Air} &= \frac{2.4586}{0.23} = 10.69 \text{ kg/kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
