<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 Block Flow: Sequential Operations of Proximate Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: 1 g Air-Dried Coal in Silica Crucible] --> A1[Step 1: Heat in Oven at 105-110 deg C for 1 Hour]
    A1 --> A2[Cool in Desiccator and Weigh W2]
    A2 --> A3[Step 2: Heat in Muffle Furnace at 925 deg C for 7 Minutes Covered]
    A3 --> A4[Cool in Desiccator and Weigh W3]
    A4 --> A5[Step 3: Heat in Muffle Furnace at 700-750 deg C Uncovered Till Constant Weight]
    A5 --> A6[Cool and Weigh W4 Ash Residue]
    A6 --> A7[Compute Moisture M, Volatile VM, Ash A]
    A7 --> A8[Compute Fixed Carbon FC by Difference]
    A8 --> A9[Output M, VM, A, FC Sum Equals 100 Percent]
```

## 4.2 Block Flow: Ultimate Analysis Methodology Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Coal Sample 1 to 2 g] --> B1[Liebig Combustion in Pure O2 Stream]
    B1 --> B2[CO2 Absorbed in KOH Tube Gives Carbon C]
    B1 --> B3[H2O Absorbed in Anhydrous CaCl2 Tube Gives Hydrogen H]
    B0 --> B4[Kjeldahl Digestion with Conc H2SO4]
    B4 --> B5[NH3 Distilled and Titrated Gives Nitrogen N]
    B0 --> B6[Eschka Fusion with MgO and Na2CO3]
    B6 --> B7[BaSO4 Precipitate Weighed Gives Sulphur S]
    B2 --> B8[Oxygen Computed by Difference O]
    B3 --> B8
    B5 --> B8
    B7 --> B8
    B8 --> B9[Final Elemental Report C H N S O Ash Moisture Sum 100 Percent]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Coal Analysis Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Coal Characterisation Problem] --> C1{What Information is Required?}
    C1 -->|Operational Behaviour| C2[Choose Proximate Analysis]
    C1 -->|Elemental Chemistry| C3[Choose Ultimate Analysis]
    C2 --> C4[Perform Moisture Test at 110 deg C]
    C2 --> C5[Perform Volatile Matter Test at 925 deg C]
    C2 --> C6[Perform Ash Test at 750 deg C]
    C2 --> C7[Compute Fixed Carbon by Difference]
    C3 --> C8[Liebig Method for C and H]
    C3 --> C9[Kjeldahl Method for N]
    C3 --> C10[Eschka Method for S]
    C3 --> C11[Compute O by Difference]
    C7 --> C12[Cross Verify with Dulong GCV Formula]
    C11 --> C12
    C12 --> C13[Convert to Required Basis: ar, ad, db, daf]
    C13 --> C14[Final Engineering Report for Boiler Design or Metallurgical Use]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Schematic: Proximate vs Ultimate Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Fuel Analysis Framework] --> D1[Proximate Analysis Operational View]
    D0 --> D2[Ultimate Analysis Chemical View]
    D1 --> D1A[Moisture]
    D1 --> D1B[Volatile Matter]
    D1 --> D1C[Ash]
    D1 --> D1D[Fixed Carbon by Difference]
    D2 --> D2A[Carbon]
    D2 --> D2B[Hydrogen]
    D2 --> D2C[Nitrogen]
    D2 --> D2D[Sulphur]
    D2 --> D2E[Oxygen by Difference]
    D1A --> E1A[Determined by Oven Drying at 110 deg C]
    D1B --> E1B[Determined by Muffle Furnace at 925 deg C]
    D1C --> E1C[Determined by Combustion at 750 deg C]
    D1D --> E1D[Computed as 100 Minus M Plus VM Plus A]
    D2A --> E2A[Determined by Liebig CO2 Absorption]
    D2B --> E2B[Determined by Liebig H2O Absorption]
    D2C --> E2C[Determined by Kjeldahl Distillation Titration]
    D2D --> E2D[Determined by Eschka BaSO4 Gravimetry]
    D2E --> E2E[Computed by Difference from 100]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Proximate Analysis of Coal. List its four components. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Proximate analysis is a thermal characterisation technique used to determine the operational composition of coal. It quantifies four components: **Moisture (M)**, **Volatile Matter (VM)**, **Ash (A)**, and **Fixed Carbon (FC)**. The sum of these four equals 100% on the air-dried basis. It is performed using a muffle furnace and gravimetric measurements at specific temperatures (110 °C, 925 °C, 750 °C).

### Question 2: Why is Oxygen in coal determined by difference and not directly? `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Oxygen is determined by difference because direct experimental determination of oxygen in coal is technically complex, time-consuming, and subject to large errors due to the presence of interfering elements (sulphur, nitrogen, mineral-bound oxygen). Indirectly, all other constituents (C, H, N, S, Ash, Moisture) are determined with high accuracy, and the remainder is mathematically assigned to oxygen. This is the standard practice as per **ASTM D3176** and **IS 1350**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**The proximate analysis of a coal sample gave the following data:**
- Moisture = 4.2%
- Volatile Matter = 29.3%
- Ash = 8.5%

**Calculate:**
- (a) The Fixed Carbon content and classify the coal rank. **(7 marks)**
- (b) The ultimate analysis if the coal contains 78% C, 5.2% H, 1.4% N, 0.6% S, and the GCV using Dulong's formula. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Given:** M = 4.2%, VM = 29.3%, A = 8.5%

**Step 1:** Apply the summation rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M + VM + A + FC &= 100 \\
4.2 + 29.3 + 8.5 + FC &= 100 \\
42.0 + FC &= 100 \\
FC &= 100 - 42.0 = 58.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Classify the coal based on FC and VM:

- Fixed Carbon = 58% (high, between 55-85% range)
- Volatile Matter = 29.3% (moderate)

This corresponds to **Bituminous coal** (high-volatile bituminous grade), commonly used in **thermal power plants**.

**Step 3:** Report the result:

| Component | Percentage |
|---|---|
| Moisture | 4.2% |
| Volatile Matter | 29.3% |
| Ash | 8.5% |
| Fixed Carbon | 58.0% |
| **Total** | **100.0%** |

**Conclusion:** The coal is a **Bituminous coal** suitable for steam generation in thermal power stations.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given Ultimate Analysis:** C = 78%, H = 5.2%, N = 1.4%, S = 0.6%

**Step 1: Determine Oxygen by Difference**

From the proximate analysis, M + A = 4.2 + 8.5 = 12.7%

$$
\begin{aligned}
C + H + N + S + O + M + A &= 100 \\
78 + 5.2 + 1.4 + 0.6 + O + 12.7 &= 100 \\
97.9 + O &= 100 \\
O &= 2.1\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Dulong's Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV &= \frac{8080\,C + 34500\,(H - O/8) + 2240\,S}{100}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV &= \frac{8080 \times 78 + 34500 \times \left(5.2 - \frac{2.1}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 0.6}{100} \\
&= \frac{630240 + 34500 \times (5.2 - 0.2625) + 1344}{100} \\
&= \frac{630240 + 34500 \times 4.9375 + 1344}{100} \\
&= \frac{630240 + 170343.75 + 1344}{100} \\
&= \frac{801927.75}{100} \\
&= 8019.28 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to MJ/kg**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV &= 8019.28 \times 4.186 \times 10^{-3} = 33.57 \text{ MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The GCV of the coal is **8019.28 kcal/kg (33.57 MJ/kg)**, confirming its high rank and suitability for power generation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2023 — Alternative Choice]`

**A coal has the following ultimate analysis on an air-dried basis:**
- C = 71.5%, H = 5.1%, N = 1.3%, S = 0.4%, O = 9.2%, Moisture = 3.5%, Ash = 9.0%

**Calculate:**
- (a) The GCV using Dulong's formula, the NCV, and the minimum air required for complete combustion of 1 kg of this coal. **(7 marks)**
- (b) Convert the GCV to dry-basis and dry-ash-free basis, and comment on the coal's rank. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Step 1: Apply Dulong's Formula for GCV**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV &= \frac{8080 \times 71.5 + 34500 \times \left(5.1 - \frac{9.2}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 0.4}{100} \\
&= \frac{577720 + 34500 \times (5.1 - 1.15) + 896}{100} \\
&= \frac{577720 + 34500 \times 3.95 + 896}{100} \\
&= \frac{577720 + 136275 + 896}{100} \\
&= \frac{714891}{100} \\
&= 7148.91 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate NCV**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NCV &= GCV - \frac{9 \times H \times 587}{100} \\
&= 7148.91 - \frac{9 \times 5.1 \times 587}{100} \\
&= 7148.91 - 269.43 \\
&= 6879.48 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Theoretical O₂ and Air Required**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_2 \text{ required (kg per 100 kg fuel)} &= 2.667\,C + 8\,H + S - O \\
&= 2.667 \times 71.5 + 8 \times 5.1 + 0.4 - 9.2 \\
&= 190.69 + 40.8 + 0.4 - 9.2 \\
&= 222.69 \text{ kg per 100 kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Per kg of fuel: $O_2 = 2.227$ kg/kg

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Theoretical Air} &= \frac{2.227}{0.23} = 9.683 \text{ kg of air per kg of fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

In volumetric terms (at NTP, density of air = 1.293 kg/m³):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{air} &= \frac{9.683}{1.293} = 7.49 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per kg of fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| GCV | 7148.91 kcal/kg |
| NCV | 6879.48 kcal/kg |
| O₂ required | 2.227 kg/kg fuel |
| Theoretical Air | 9.683 kg/kg fuel (7.49 m³/kg) |

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1: Convert GCV to Dry Basis**

For GCV, the dry-basis conversion involves removing moisture:

$$
\begin{aligned}
GCV_{db} &= GCV_{ad} \times \frac{100}{100 - M_{ad}} \\
&= 7148.91 \times \frac{100}{100 - 3.5} \\
&= 7148.91 \times \frac{100}{96.5} \\
&= 7148.91 \times 1.0363 \\
&= 7408.20 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Convert GCV to Dry-Ash-Free Basis**

For the daf basis, we exclude both moisture and ash:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Combustible fraction} &= 100 - M - A = 100 - 3.5 - 9.0 = 87.5\% \\
GCV_{daf} &= GCV_{ad} \times \frac{100}{100 - M - A} \\
&= 7148.91 \times \frac{100}{87.5} \\
&= 7148.91 \times 1.1429 \\
&= 8170.18 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Rank Classification**

The fixed carbon on a dry basis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
FC_{db} &= FC_{ad} \times \frac{100}{100 - M} = (100 - 3.5 - 5.1 - 1.3 - 0.4 - 9.2 - 9.0) \times \frac{100}{96.5}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Note: VM on dry basis for classification is conventionally used.

For rank classification (using the dry-basis fixed carbon and the ultimate carbon content of 71.5%):

- C = 71.5% (typical of bituminous coal)
- GCV ≈ 7408 kcal/kg (definitely bituminous range)
- Volatile matter moderate

**Conclusion:** This is a **medium-volatile bituminous coal**, suitable for use in industrial boilers and metallurgical coke blending.

**Final Summary Table:**

| Basis | GCV (kcal/kg) | GCV (MJ/kg) |
|---|---|---|
| As Determined (ad) | 7148.91 | 29.92 |
| Dry Basis (db) | 7408.20 | 31.01 |
| Dry Ash-Free (daf) | 8170.18 | 34.20 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Basis Confusion:** Students often forget to convert to the required basis (ar, ad, db, daf) and lose 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Moisture Re-Counting:** Do not include moisture weight loss in the volatile matter calculation — moisture is removed in Step 1.
> 3. **Dulong's Formula Sign:** The $H - O/8$ term: oxygen reduces available hydrogen. If $O/8 > H$, the term becomes negative — interpret as zero hydrogen contribution.
> 4. **Air Composition:** Use **23% by mass** or **21% by volume** for air. Mixing these units is a common error.
> 5. **Eschka Factor:** The $\frac{32}{233}$ factor in sulphur calculation (molar masses of S and BaSO₄) is often forgotten.
> 6. **Kjeldahl Factor:** The 1.4 in nitrogen formula arises from $\frac{14 \times \text{Normality}}{1000}$ — students may wrongly use 14 directly.
> 7. **Units Mismatch:** Dulong's formula gives kcal/kg; conversion to MJ/kg requires the **4.186 factor**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams**

### A. Proximate Analysis — Must Know

- **Four components:** Moisture, Volatile Matter, Ash, Fixed Carbon.
- **Temperatures:** Moisture at **110 °C**, Volatile Matter at **925 °C (covered, 7 min)**, Ash at **750 °C (uncovered)**.
- **Sum = 100%** (mandatory check).
- **Fixed Carbon = 100 - (M + VM + A)**.
- Volatile Matter is critical for **flame length, combustion stability, and coke type**.

### B. Ultimate Analysis — Must Know

- **Five elements + Ash + Moisture** = 100%.
- **C and H:** Liebig combustion with KOH and CaCl₂ absorption tubes; factors $\frac{12}{44}$ and $\frac{2}{18}$.
- **N:** Kjeldahl method, formula $N = \frac{1.4 \times N_a \times (V_b - V_s)}{W}$.
- **S:** Eschka method, factor $\frac{32}{233}$.
- **O:** Always by **difference**.

### C. Calorific Value — Must Know

- **Dulong's GCV** formula: $\frac{8080\,C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240\,S}{100}$.
- **NCV = GCV - 52.83 × H** (when H is in %).
- **Latent heat of water = 587 kcal/kg.**
- **1 kcal = 4.186 J** (use this for MJ conversion).

### D. Stoichiometry — Must Know

- **O₂ per kg fuel:** $\frac{32\,C + 8\,H + 32\,S - 8\,O}{12 \times W}$.
- **Air contains 23% O₂ by mass, 21% by volume.**
- **Excess air** in real furnaces: **20–50%**.

### E. Basis Conversions — Must Know

- **ar (as received) → ad (air dried) → db (dry) → daf (dry ash-free).**
- db excludes moisture only; daf excludes moisture and ash.
- **Common KTU trap:** Asking to "express on dry basis" — must divide by (100 - M_ad).

### F. Rank Classification — Quick Reference

| Coal Type | C (%) | VM (%) | Typical GCV (kcal/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Peat | 50-60 | >60 | 3000-4500 |
| Lignite | 60-70 | 45-55 | 4000-5500 |
| Bituminous | 78-90 | 15-35 | 6500-8500 |
| Anthracite | 92-98 | <10 | 8000-9000 |

### G. Common Numerical Patterns in KTU Exams

1. **Proximate calculation from weight data** (crucible method).
2. **Dulong's GCV/NCV** from ultimate analysis.
3. **Theoretical air/fuel ratio** computation.
4. **Basis conversions** (ad to db, daf).
5. **Comparative analysis** between two coal samples.

### H. Smart Tips for Maximum Marks

- Always **show units** in every step.
- Always **cross-check** the sum of components equals 100%.
- **Mention the basis** (ad, db, daf) explicitly in the final answer.
- Use **desiccator cooling** — examiners love this procedural detail.
- Quote relevant **IS/ASTM standards** wherever possible for extra credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cleaning and Washing of Coal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Cleaning and Washing of Coal # 🏭 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 📘 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cleaning (Washing / Beneficiation) of Coal** is defined as the physical, chemical, or biological process of removing impurities (such as ash-forming minerals, pyritic sulfur, shale, clay, sand, and rocks) from run-of-mine (ROM) coal to upgrade its quality, raise the calorific value, reduce the ash content, lower the sulfur content, and improve its combustion characteristics prior to its utilization in industrial furnaces, boilers, coke ovens, or gasifiers. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, *Coal Cleaning* falls under the broad umbrella of **Coal Preparation** and is governed by the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) classification for washed coals. The cleaned product is termed **Washed Coal** or **Beneficiated Coal**. > [!NOTE] > **Run-of-Mine (ROM) Coal:** The raw coal as it comes out of the mine, before any processing, containing dirt, rock, slate, and other contaminants. > **Clean Coal:** The upgraded product after impurity removal. > **Rejects / Tailings:** The waste material discarded after cleaning. ## 🌊 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are given a **bucket of mixed dal (lentils) and small pebbles/stones** harvested directly from the farm. Before you cook it, you would: 1. **Winnow (blow air)** to remove dust and chaff → analogous to **Dry Dust Extraction**. 2. **Wash with water** to remove mud and fine dirt → analogous to **Wet Washing**. 3. **Hand-pick the stones** because they are heavier → analogous to **Dense Medium Separation**. 4. **Pass through a sieve** so the small dirt particles fall through, leaving the bigger lentils → analogous to **Screening & Classification**. 5. **Use a floating trick**: dal sinks or floats differently from pebbles → analogous to **Froth Flotation**. **Cleaning of coal is exactly this — but at industrial scale using engineered equipment.** The "dal" is the valuable carbonaceous matter, and the "pebbles & mud" are the mineral impurities (ash-forming materials like shale, clay, sandstone, pyrites). ## 🎯 Why Clean Coal? The Driving Forces | Driver | Engineering Justification | |---|---| | **Higher Calorific Value (CV)** | Removing inert ash increases the energy released per kg. | | **Lower Ash Content** | Reduces slagging, fouling, and ash-handling costs in boilers. | | **Reduced Sulfur (S)** | Lowers SO₂ emissions, mitigating acid rain. | | **Uniform Quality** | Sized and graded coal burns predictably. | | **Improved Coke Quality** | Low-ash coking coal is mandatory for metallurgical use. | | **Transport Economy** | You do not pay freight on worthless ash. | | **Environmental Compliance** | Meets emission norms of CPCB / MOEFCC, India. | ## 📐 Key Performance Metrics (Must Remember in **BOLD**) > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Ash Content (%):** Mass of incombustible residue left after burning at 815 °C, expressed as % of original coal mass. **Indian power-grade coals target < 34 % washed ash.** > - **Calorific Value (CV):** Energy released on complete combustion, measured in **kcal/kg** or **MJ/kg** (1 kcal/kg = 4.186 kJ/kg). > - **Sulfur Content (%):** Determines SO₂ emission potential; **must be < 0.5 % for clean coal usage.** > - **Yield (%):** Ratio of clean coal mass to feed mass. Indicates process efficiency. > - **Washability Index (WI):** Indicates ease of cleaning; lower WI = easier to clean. > - **Near Gravity Material (NGM) / ±0.1 SG fraction:** Critical indicator of separation difficulty. > - **Cut-point Density (ρ₅₀):** Specific gravity at which 50 % of feed reports to clean coal and 50 % to rejects. > - **Ecart probable (Ep):** Measure of separation sharpness, **Ep < 0.05 is excellent.** # ⚙️ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🧬 Classification of Coal Cleaning Methods Coal cleaning techniques are classified based on the **state of the medium** (air or water), **property exploited**, and **particle size range** handled. ### 🅰️ Based on Property Exploited 1. **Gravity-based Separation** — exploits the difference in **specific gravity (SG)** between coal (SG ≈ 1.2–1.5) and mineral matter (SG ≈ 1.8–2.5). 2. **Surface Property–based Separation** — exploits differences in **wettability** (Froth Flotation). 3. **Magnetic / Electrical Separation** — for pyritic sulfur removal using **magnetic susceptibility** or **dielectric constant**. 4. **Optical Sorting** — reflectance / colour differences. 5. **Chemical / Biological Methods** — Microbial desulfurization; chemical leaching. ### 🅱️ Based on Particle Size | Size Range (mm) | Preferred Method | |---|---| | > 100 (Lumps) | **Hand Picking**, **Heavy Media Bath** | | 10 – 100 | **Jigging**, **Dense Medium Cyclone** | | 0.5 – 10 | **Spiral Concentrator**, **Water-Only Cyclone** | | < 0.5 (Fines) | **Froth Flotation**, **Oil Agglomeration** | ## 🔬 Detailed Process Breakdown ### 1. Jigging (Water-Pulsation Method) - Feed is placed on a perforated plate over a water bed. - Periodic pulsations stratify particles: **light coal floats on top**, **heavy shale sinks**. - Stratified layers are extracted separately. - **Used for:** coarse coal (10–125 mm). ### 2. Dense Medium Separation (DMS / Heavy Media) - A suspension of **magnetite (Fe₃O₄)** or **ferrosilicon** in water is used as the medium. - Medium density can be precisely controlled (e.g., 1.4 SG, 1.6 SG, 1.8 SG). - **Coal (SG < 1.5)** floats, **shale (SG > 1.8)** sinks. - **Used for:** coarse + intermediate coal, high accuracy. ### 3. Dense Medium Cyclone (DMC) - Centrifugal force amplifies the density differential. - **Used for:** 0.5–50 mm coal; very sharp separation. ### 4. Froth Flotation - Coal is hydrophobic (water-repellent); mineral matter is hydrophilic. - Air bubbles attach to coal particles → float to the surface as froth. - Reagents: **Collectors (e.g., diesel oil, kerosene)**, **Frothers (e.g., MIBC, pine oil)**, **Modifiers**. - **Used for:** < 0.5 mm fines. ### 5. Spiral Concentrator - Feed slurry flows down a helical trough; centrifugal + gravitational forces stratify particles. - **Used for:** 0.075–3 mm fines. ### 6. Water-Only Cyclone - Uses water + centrifugal force (no dense medium needed). - **Used for:** 0.1–6 mm fines; cheaper than DMS. ### 7. Dry Methods - **Pneumatic Jigging / Air Tables / Triboelectric Separator** — for regions where water is scarce. ## 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Term | Symbolic Form | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Yield (Y) | $Y = \dfrac{M_c}{M_f} \times 100$ | % | $M_c$ = mass of clean coal; $M_f$ = mass of feed | | 2 | Ash Reduction (ηₐ) | $\eta_a = \dfrac{A_f - A_c}{A_f} \times 100$ | % | $A_f$, $A_c$ = ash % of feed and clean coal | | 3 | Combustible Recovery (CR) | $CR = \dfrac{Y \times (100 - A_c)}{100 - A_f} \times 100$ | % | Indicates % of clean coal retained | | 4 | Organic Efficiency (OE) | $OE = \dfrac{CR - Y}{100 - A_f}$ | % | Measures both yield and ash reduction | | 5 | Probable Error (Ep) | $E_p = \dfrac{\rho_{75} - \rho_{25}}{2}$ | SG units | Smaller Ep → sharper separation | | 6 | Cut-point Density (ρ₅₀) | $\rho_{50}$ | SG | Density at which 50 % reports to clean coal | | 7 | Misplaced Material | $M_{misplaced} = \dfrac{\text{misplaced mass}}{\text{total feed in SG class}} \times 100$ | % | Actual minus ideal partition | | 8 | Calorific Value Gain (ΔCV) | $\Delta CV = CV_c - CV_f$ | kcal/kg | Quality uplift achieved | | 9 | Total Moisture (M) | $M = M_{surface} + M_{inherent}$ | % | Affects net CV | | 10 | Mayer Curve | Partition curve (Tromp curve) | – | Plots partition coefficient vs SG | ## 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > - **Coking Coal Preparation (Steel Industry):** Indian coking coals are washed to < 17 % ash to feed **BLAST FURNACES** (e.g., SAIL, RINL, Tata Steel Bokaro, Durgapur). > - **Power Generation:** NTPC's Rihand, Vindhyachal, and Dadri Super Thermal Power Stations use washed coal to reduce fly-ash generation and ESP load. > - **Coal Gasification:** High-ash coal causes slag formation in fixed-bed gasifiers; washing improves gasifier reliability. > - **Export:** Washed non-coking coal fetches premium price in international markets. > - **Environmental Protection:** Lower SO₂ → acid rain mitigation; lower ash → less fly-ash pond burden. # 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 📐 Derivation 1: Washability Index (WI) The **Washability Index** quantifies the relative difficulty of cleaning coal. It is derived from the **Washability Curve**, which is plotted from float-sink test data. **Step 1 — Float-Sink Test:** A representative coal sample is split into multiple Specific Gravity (SG) fractions using heavy liquids (ZnCl₂ solutions of SG = 1.4, 1.5, 1.6, 1.7, 1.8, 2.0). **Step 2 — Mass & Ash Balance for each SG fraction:** $$ M_f = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i, \quad \bar{A_f} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(m_i \times A_i)}{M_f} $$ where $m_i$ is mass of $i$-th SG fraction, $A_i$ is its ash %. **Step 3 — Cumulative Floats (Cumulative Yield, $C_y$):** $$ C_y(j) = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{j} m_i}{M_f} \times 100 $$ **Step 4 — Cumulative Ash ($\bar{A_c}$):** $$ \bar{A_c}(j) = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{j}(m_i \times A_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{j} m_i} $$ **Step 5 — Washability Index (Mayer's Method):** $$ WI = \dfrac{\int_{0}^{100} \Delta A \, dy}{A_f} $$ where $\Delta A$ is the difference between ash at theoretical "perfect cleaning" and the cumulative ash at yield $y$. **Step 6 — Practical simplified KTU form:** $$ WI = \dfrac{A_f - A_c}{A_f - A_{m}} \times 100 $$ where $A_m$ is the minimum achievable ash (cleanest float fraction). Higher WI = harder to clean. ## 📐 Derivation 2: Partition Coefficient (Tromp Curve) The **Partition Number / Coefficient** at any specific gravity $P(\rho)$ is defined as: $$ P(\rho) = \dfrac{\text{Mass of feed of SG } \rho \text{ reporting to clean coal}}{\text{Total mass of feed of SG } \rho \text{ in feed}} \times 100 $$ **Ideal partition:** Sharp step function at $\rho_{50}$. **Real partition:** S-shaped curve fit by: $$ P(\rho) = \dfrac{100}{1 + \exp\left[\dfrac{\pi(\rho_{50} - \rho)}{\sqrt{3} \cdot E_p}\right]} \quad \text{(Verkade / Whiten model)} $$ **Probable Error ($E_p$):** $$ E_p = \dfrac{\rho_{75} - \rho_{25}}{2} $$ ## 📐 Derivation 3: Combustible Recovery (CR) & Organic Efficiency (OE) **Step 1:** Combustible fraction in feed: $$ C_f = 100 - A_f \quad (\%) $$ **Step 2:** Combustible fraction in clean coal: $$ C_c = 100 - A_c \quad (\%) $$ **Step 3:** Mass of combustible recovered in clean coal: $$ m_{comb} = Y \times C_c / 100 $$ **Step 4:** Total combustible in feed: $$ M_{comb,f} = M_f \times C_f / 100 $$ **Step 5:** Combustible Recovery (CR): $$ CR = \dfrac{m_{comb}}{M_{comb,f}} \times 100 = \dfrac{Y \cdot (100 - A_c)}{(100 - A_f)} $$ **Step 6:** Organic Efficiency (OE) — combines yield + ash reduction: $$ OE = CR - Y \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad OE = \dfrac{Y(100 - A_c) - Y(100 - A_f)}{100 - A_f} $$ $$ OE = \dfrac{Y(A_f - A_c)}{100 - A_f} $$ > [!TIP] > **Maximum OE = 100 %** when $Y = 100$ and $A_c = A_f$ (no cleaning, trivial). > **Practical OE = 60–90 %** in well-designed plants. ## 📝 Solved Numerical Example (KTU Style — 14-Mark Pattern) ### **Problem:** A coal preparation plant processes **1000 tonnes/hour** of ROM coal. The feed ash is **38 %** and the clean coal produced has **ash = 22 %**. The clean coal yield is **70 %**. **(a)** Calculate the **Ash Reduction Efficiency**. **(b)** Calculate the **Combustible Recovery**. **(c)** Calculate the **Organic Efficiency**. **(d)** Determine the **mass flow rate of rejects** and the **ash % of rejects**. --- ### **Solution:** **Given Data:** - $M_f = 1000$ t/h - $A_f = 38 \, \%$ - $A_c = 22 \, \%$ - $Y = 70 \, \%$ #### Part (a): Ash Reduction Efficiency ($\eta_a$) $$ \eta_a = \dfrac{A_f - A_c}{A_f} \times 100 $$ $$ \eta_a = \dfrac{38 - 22}{38} \times 100 $$ $$ \eta_a = \dfrac{16}{38} \times 100 $$ $$ \eta_a = 42.11 \, \% $$ > ✅ **Ash Reduction = 42.11 %** — Almost 42 % of the inert ash has been removed. #### Part (b): Combustible Recovery (CR) $$ CR = \dfrac{Y \times (100 - A_c)}{100 - A_f} $$ $$ CR = \dfrac{70 \times (100 - 22)}{100 - 38} $$ $$ CR = \dfrac{70 \times 78}{62} $$ $$ CR = \dfrac{5460}{62} $$ $$ CR = 88.06 \, \% $$ > ✅ **Combustible Recovery = 88.06 %** — High value, indicating good process efficiency. #### Part (c): Organic Efficiency (OE) $$ OE = CR - Y $$ $$ OE = 88.06 - 70 $$ $$ OE = 18.06 \, \% $$ **Alternate verification:** $$ OE = \dfrac{Y(A_f - A_c)}{100 - A_f} = \dfrac{70 \times (38 - 22)}{62} = \dfrac{70 \times 16}{62} = \dfrac{1120}{62} = 18.06 \, \% $$ > ✅ **Organic Efficiency = 18.06 %** #### Part (d): Mass Flow Rate & Ash of Rejects **Mass flow rate of clean coal:** $$ M_c = Y \times M_f / 100 = 70 \times 1000 / 100 = 700 \, \text{t/h} $$ **Mass flow rate of rejects:** $$ M_r = M_f - M_c = 1000 - 700 = 300 \, \text{t/h} $$ **Ash % of rejects by mass balance:** $$ M_f \times A_f = M_c \times A_c + M_r \times A_r $$ $$ 1000 \times 38 = 700 \times 22 + 300 \times A_r $$ $$ 38000 = 15400 + 300 A_r $$ $$ 300 A_r = 22600 $$ $$ A_r = \dfrac{22600}{300} = 75.33 \, \% $$ > ✅ **Rejects = 300 t/h with ash = 75.33 %** (mostly shale, clay, sandstone). --- ## 📊 Comparison Matrix: Methods at a Glance | Method | Particle Size (mm) | Cut-point SG | Sharpness (Ep) | Water Use | Cost | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Hand Picking | > 100 | – | Very Poor | None | Low | Small mines | | Jigging | 10 – 125 | 1.5 – 1.8 | 0.08 – 0.15 | High | Medium | Coarse coal | | Dense Medium Bath | 10 – 300 | 1.3 – 1.9 | 0.02 – 0.05 | Medium | High | Coking coal | | Dense Medium Cyclone | 0.5 – 50 | 1.4 – 1.9 | 0.03 – 0.06 | High | High | Coking + thermal | | Water-Only Cyclone | 0.1 – 6 | 1.5 – 1.8 | 0.10 – 0.20 | High | Low | Thermal fines | | Spiral | 0.075 – 3 | 1.6 – 1.9 | 0.15 – 0.25 | Low | Very Low | Fines | | Froth Flotation | < 0.5 | – | – | High | Medium | Ultra-fines | | Air Tables | 0.5 – 25 | 1.5 – 1.8 | 0.10 – 0.20 | None | Medium | Arid regions | # 🖼️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 📊 Diagram 1: Coal Cleaning Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[ROM Coal from Mine] --> B[Size Reduction
Crushing & Screening] B --> C{Coarse Fraction
>10 mm} B --> D{Fine Fraction
0.5 - 10 mm} B --> E{Ultra-fine
less than 0.5 mm} C --> F[Heavy Media Bath
or Jigging] F --> G[Clean Coal Coarse] F --> H[Rejects Coarse] D --> I[Dense Medium Cyclone
or Spirals] I --> J[Clean Coal Fine] I --> K[Rejects Fine] I --> L[Refuse Dewatering] E --> M[Froth Flotation
Cell] M --> N[Clean Coal Concentrate] M --> O[Tailings Thickener] G --> P[Centrifuge and
Thermal Drying] J --> P N --> P P --> Q[Final Washed Coal
Product] H --> R[Reject Disposal
or Backfill] K --> R L --> R O --> R Q --> S[To End User
Power Plant, Coke Oven,
Gasifier, Export] ``` ## 📊 Diagram 2: Washability Curve Family ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Wash["WASHABILITY CURVES (Mayer)"] D1[Curve 1: Yield vs Ash] D2[Curve 2: Cumulative Floats vs Ash] D3[Curve 3: SG vs Cumulative Mass] D4[Curve 4: Primary Sink vs SG] end D1 --> R1[Used to Predict
Clean Coal Ash at
Desired Yield] D2 --> R2[Used to Find
Theoretical Yield at
Target Ash] D3 --> R3[Used to Select
Operating SG for
Separator] D4 --> R4[Used to Quantify
Near Gravity Material] ``` ## 📊 Diagram 3: Dense Medium Cyclone Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD F1[Feed Slurry + Magnetite Medium] --> F2[Inlet Under Pressure] F2 --> F3[Cylindrical Cone Body] F3 --> F4[Centrifugal Force Separates] F4 --> F5[Light Fraction Floats
Overflow to Clean Coal Sieve] F4 --> F6[Heavy Fraction Sinks
Underflow to Reject Sieve] F5 --> F7[Recovery of Magnetite
via Magnetic Separator] F6 --> F7 F7 --> F8[Re-densified Medium
Returns to Feed Tank] F8 --> F1 ``` ## 📊 Diagram 4: Froth Flotation Cell Cross-Section (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD M1[Pulp Feed
Slurry of Coal Fines] --> M2[Conditioner Tank
with Reagents] M2 --> M3[Flotation Cell
Aerated with Air] M3 --> M4[Hydrophobic Coal Particles
Attach to Air Bubbles] M3 --> M5[Hydrophilic Gangue
Settling as Tailings] M4 --> M6[Froth Skimming at Top
Clean Coal Concentrate] M5 --> M7[Tailings Discharge at Bottom] M6 --> M8[Filter and Dry] M8 --> M9[Final Clean Coal Fines] ``` ## 📊 Diagram 5: Tromp / Partition Curve Interpretation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TC["PARTITION (TROMP) CURVE"] X[Specific Gravity axis] --> Y[Partition Number axis] end TC --> T1[Step 1: Ideal Sharp
Partition at rho 50] TC --> T2[Step 2: Real S Curve
has Finite Slope] TC --> T3[Step 3: Ep equals
rho 75 minus rho 25
divided by 2] TC --> T4[Step 4: NGM at rho 50
plus or minus 0.1
indicates Difficulty] ``` # 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📚 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > Model answers are provided in full for KTU 2024 valuation alignment. ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define coal washing. List any **four** advantages of washing coal before its industrial utilization. **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Coal washing (also called coal beneficiation or coal preparation) is the industrial process of removing inorganic impurities such as ash-forming minerals, shale, clay, sand, and pyritic sulfur from raw run-of-mine (ROM) coal using physical, physico-chemical, or biological separation techniques, in order to enhance its calorific value and reduce environmental pollutants. **Four Advantages:** 1. **Higher Calorific Value:** Removal of inert ash increases the energy per unit mass. 2. **Lower SO₂ Emissions:** Reduction of pyritic sulfur limits acid rain and air pollution. 3. **Improved Combustion Efficiency:** Uniform sized, low-ash coal ensures steady boiler performance. 4. **Reduced Ash Handling Cost:** Less fly-ash generation lowers ESP load, ash pond size, and disposal cost. *(3 marks)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Distinguish between **Dry** and **Wet** methods of coal cleaning. Give **one example** of each. **Model Answer:** | Feature | Dry Cleaning | Wet Cleaning | |---|---|---| | Medium Used | Air | Water (+ dense medium) | | Suitability | Water-scarce regions | Most Indian coal regions | | Plant Cost | Lower | Higher | | Ash Reduction | 10 – 25 % | 30 – 50 % | | Examples | Air Tables, Pneumatic Jigging, Triboelectric Separator | Jigging, DMS, Froth Flotation, Spirals | **Example (Dry):** Air Table — vibrates coal on a perforated deck; stratification by density using air currents. **Example (Wet):** Froth Flotation — exploits hydrophobic nature of coal to float clean particles on air bubbles. *(3 marks)* --- ## 📚 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, students must answer ONE full question of 14 marks. Internal choice (Q-A or Q-B) is provided. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Froth Flotation process** for cleaning fine coal (< 0.5 mm). List the **reagents** used with their functions. Discuss the **factors** that affect flotation efficiency. **(b)** [7 Marks] A coal washery receives **500 tonnes/hour** of ROM coal at **40 %** ash. The clean coal yield is **65 %** with **ash content = 20 %**. Determine the **ash reduction efficiency**, **combustible recovery**, **organic efficiency**, and the **ash % of rejects**. --- ### **Solution to Question A:** #### **Part (a) — Froth Flotation [7 Marks]** **Principle:** Froth flotation exploits the difference in **wettability** between coal and mineral matter. Coal is naturally **hydrophobic** (water-repellent), whereas mineral matter (ash-forming particles like shale, clay) is **hydrophilic** (water-loving). When air is bubbled through a coal-water slurry, coal particles attach to air bubbles and rise to the surface, forming a froth that is skimmed off. **Process Steps:** 1. **Comminution:** Coal is crushed and ground to < 0.5 mm. 2. **Pulp Preparation:** Pulverized coal is mixed with water to form a slurry (~25–40 % solids). 3. **Conditioning:** Slurry is mixed with reagents in a conditioner tank. 4. **Aeration & Flotation:** Slurry enters flotation cells where compressed air is injected from below. Bubbles rise; coal attaches to bubbles; froth is skimmed. 5. **Dewatering:** Concentrate is filtered and dried. **Reagents Used:** | Reagent Type | Example | Function | |---|---|---| | Collector | Diesel oil, Kerosene, Fuel oil | Increases coal hydrophobicity | | Frother | MIBC (Methyl Isobutyl Carbinol), Pine oil | Stabilizes air bubbles and froth | | Modifier (Activator) | Soda ash, CuSO₄ | Activates surface of desired mineral | | Modifier (Depressant) | Sodium cyanide, Starch | Prevents unwanted mineral from floating | | pH Regulator | Lime, NaOH | Maintains alkalinity | **Factors Affecting Flotation Efficiency:** 1. **Particle Size** — Optimum 0.075 – 0.5 mm; very fine or coarse particles give poor recovery. 2. **Pulp Density** — 10–25 % solids ideal. 3. **Air Flow Rate** — Higher aeration → more bubbles but froth may carry gangue. 4. **Reagent Dosage** — Excessive collector → froth contamination. 5. **pH of Slurry** — 7–9 optimal for coal. 6. **Temperature** — Higher temperatures may improve kinetics but increase cost. 7. **Degree of Oxidation** — Oxidized coal surface becomes hydrophilic; flotation yield drops. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]** **Given:** - $M_f = 500$ t/h - $A_f = 40 \, \%$ - $A_c = 20 \, \%$ - $Y = 65 \, \%$ **Step 1: Ash Reduction Efficiency:** $$ \eta_a = \dfrac{A_f - A_c}{A_f} \times 100 = \dfrac{40 - 20}{40} \times 100 = 50 \, \% $$ **Step 2: Combustible Recovery (CR):** $$ CR = \dfrac{Y(100 - A_c)}{100 - A_f} = \dfrac{65 \times 80}{60} = \dfrac{5200}{60} = 86.67 \, \% $$ **Step 3: Organic Efficiency (OE):** $$ OE = CR - Y = 86.67 - 65 = 21.67 \, \% $$ **Step 4: Mass flow rates:** $$ M_c = 65 \times 500 / 100 = 325 \, \text{t/h} $$ $$ M_r = 500 - 325 = 175 \, \text{t/h} $$ **Step 5: Ash % of rejects (mass balance):** $$ 500 \times 40 = 325 \times 20 + 175 \times A_r $$ $$ 20000 = 6500 + 175 A_r $$ $$ 175 A_r = 13500 $$ $$ A_r = 77.14 \, \% $$ **Final Answers:** - Ash Reduction Efficiency = **50 %** - Combustible Recovery = **86.67 %** - Organic Efficiency = **21.67 %** - Rejects flow = **175 t/h** with ash = **77.14 %** *(7 marks)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Dense Medium Separation (DMS)** process for coal cleaning. Discuss the role of **magnetite** as a heavy medium and the factors controlling the process. **(b)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **Baum Jig** for coal cleaning. --- ### **Solution to Question B:** #### **Part (a) — Dense Medium Separation (DMS) [7 Marks]** **Principle:** DMS uses a suspension of finely ground magnetite (Fe₃O₄) or ferrosilicon in water, whose density can be precisely controlled (typically 1.3 – 1.9 SG). Particles lighter than the medium float (clean coal), while heavier particles sink (rejects). **Process:** 1. ROM coal is crushed and sized. 2. Sized coal is fed into a bath of dense medium. 3. **Coal (SG ≈ 1.2–1.5)** floats; **mineral matter (SG ≈ 1.8–2.5)** sinks. 4. Both fractions are separately skimmed/screened. 5. Magnetite is recovered from the fractions using **drum magnetic separators** and recycled. **Role of Magnetite:** - SG of magnetite ≈ **5.18**, easily ground to < 0.1 mm. - Allows the medium density to be set at any desired value (1.3 to 1.9 SG). - Inert — does not react with coal. - Easily recoverable using magnets. - Low viscosity at typical medium densities. **Factors:** 1. **Medium density (ρ)** — determines cut-point. 2. **Medium stability** — affected by magnetite fineness. 3. **Feed size & size distribution** — must be closely sized. 4. **Feed rate and bed depth.** 5. **Medium viscosity** — controlled by clay content. 6. **Particle shape and porosity.** *(7 marks)* --- #### **Part (b) — Baum Jig [7 Marks]** **Construction:** A Baum jig is a water-pulsation type jig with a **U-shaped fixed water reservoir** below a perforated screen (the jig bed). Two main types exist: - **Single-compartment jig** (for coarse coal) - **Multi-compartment jig** (with undercurrent screen for fines) **Working Principle:** - Raw coal is fed onto the jig bed. - Water pulsations are generated by alternately applying air pressure (or vacuum) using a rotary air valve. - **Intake stroke:** Water rises through bed → light coal rises to top layer. - **Suction stroke:** Water pulled down → heavy particles settle. - After several cycles, particles are **stratified**: **clean coal on top**, **middling in middle**, **shale/rejects at bottom**. - Layers are extracted via **discharge gates** through a float-controlled mechanism. **Features:** - Handles coal up to 125 mm. - Water consumption: 1 – 3 m³ per tonne. - Cut-point: 1.5 – 1.8 SG. - Sharpness (Ep): 0.08 – 0.15. *(7 marks)* --- > [!WARNING] > ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks!** > 1. **Forgetting Units:** Always write **%** for efficiency, **t/h** for mass flow, **kcal/kg** for CV. > 2. **Mass Balance Mistake:** Common error — using $A_c \times M_c$ instead of $A_r \times M_r$ when solving for rejects ash. > 3. **Skipping Flow Diagrams:** In 7-mark answers, **at least one labeled diagram/schematic is compulsory.** Skipping the diagram results in 2–3 mark loss. > 4. **Wrong Formulas Used:** Mixing up **Ash Reduction** ($\eta_a$) with **Organic Efficiency** (OE). Remember — OE combines yield & ash reduction. > 5. **Ignoring Cut-point SG:** When asked to "design a separator," KTU expects you to specify $\rho_{50}$, not just describe the process. > 6. **No Practical Examples:** Always cite at least one Indian example (e.g., "washing at Bharat Coking Coal Ltd., Dhanbad"). > 7. **Inadequate Sketch in Froth Flotation:** A cell diagram with bubble, froth, tailings, reagents is expected — without it, 1.5 marks lost. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > 📌 **High-Yield Revision — Memorize These Before the Exam!** - ✅ **Coal Cleaning** = physical/chemical removal of ash & sulfur from ROM coal. - ✅ **Five Major Methods:** Jigging, DMS/DMC, Froth Flotation, Spirals, Water-Only Cyclone. - ✅ **Gravity-based** for coarse (> 0.5 mm); **Froth Flotation** for ultra-fines (< 0.5 mm). - ✅ **Coal SG ≈ 1.2 – 1.5**; **Mineral matter SG ≈ 1.8 – 2.5** (this is the exploitable gap). - ✅ **Yield Formula:** $Y = \dfrac{M_c}{M_f} \times 100$ - ✅ **Ash Reduction:** $\eta_a = \dfrac{A_f - A_c}{A_f} \times 100$ - ✅ **Combustible Recovery:** $CR = \dfrac{Y(100 - A_c)}{100 - A_f}$ - ✅ **Organic Efficiency:** $OE = CR - Y$ (combine yield + quality). - ✅ **Tromp Curve** is plotted with **Partition Number (%)** vs **Specific Gravity**; cut-point = $\rho_{50}$. - ✅ **Probable Error $E_p$:** smaller the better; < 0.05 = excellent DMS separation. - ✅ **NGM (±0.1 SG)** is the critical parameter deciding separator difficulty. - ✅ **Froth Flotation Reagents:** Collector (diesel oil), Frother (MIBC / pine oil), Modifier. - ✅ **Baum Jig:** Air pulsation principle; used for coarse coal. - ✅ **DMS Medium:** Magnetite (Fe₃O₄) suspended in water; recoverable by magnetic separator. - ✅ **Mass Balance Equation:** $M_f A_f = M_c A_c + M_r A_r$ (must always balance). - ✅ **Environmental Gains:** Washed coal reduces SO₂, NOx, fly-ash load, and transport cost. - ✅ **Coking coal** must be washed to < **17 % ash** before metallurgical use. - ✅ **Thermal (power)** coal washing targets < **34 % ash**. - ✅ Indian coal cleaning standards: **IS 1350, IS 13810, IS 14313**. - ✅ The cleanest fraction achievable = **floats at 1.4 SG** in float-sink test. - ✅ Organic efficiency > 60 % is considered good; > 80 % is exceptional. - ✅ For 14-mark numerical questions, **always** show each step with formula substitution and unit verification. --- > 🏁 **End of Module 1 Notes — Cleaning and Washing of Coal** > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme OBE standards. Best wishes for your preparation!* 🎓

topics.title: Theory of Coal Pyrolysis and Carbonization: Low-Temperature Carbonization (LTC)
High-Temperature Carbonization (HTC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** High-Temperature Carbonization (HTC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Temperature Carbonization (HTC)** is the destructive distillation (thermal decomposition in the complete absence of air) of bituminous coal at temperatures **above 900 °C**, typically in the operating range of **1100 °C to 1400 °C**, with the primary objective of producing hard, dense, **metallurgical-grade coke** along with valuable by-products such as coke oven gas, coal tar, ammonia liquor, and light oils.

The process is carried out in sealed refractory ovens (modern byproduct recovery ovens) where the volatile matter evolved during pyrolysis is collected and processed rather than being burnt off (as in the obsolete **beehive oven** process).

### 1.1.1 Operating Envelope — KTU Standard Boundaries

| Parameter | Symbol / Unit | Standard Range |
|---|---|---|
| Flue Temperature | T_flue | 1100 – 1400 °C |
| Coke Discharge Temp | T_coke | 1000 – 1100 °C |
| Coking Time | t_coke | 14 – 24 hours |
| Operating Pressure | P | Near-atmospheric (slightly +ve) |
| Air ingress | — | **Strictly zero** (anaerobic) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Clay-Pottery Analogy"** — Think of HTC like firing a clay pot in a kiln. If you heat clay at a low temperature (≈ 400 °C), you get a soft, fragile earthenware that crumbles easily (this is **Low-Temperature Carbonization / LTC**, producing soft, smokey coke). But if you fire the *same* clay at very high temperatures (≈ 1200 °C), the silica-alumina network partially fuses and shrinks, producing a hard, dense, metallic-sounding ceramic — a true **metallurgical coke**. In coal, the "binder" is the **bituminous matrix / vitrinite** which fuses into a coherent coke mass only above ~900 °C. Below this, the coke is "under-cooked" — porous, weak, and full of tars trapped inside.

In one line: **HTC = "Over-cooking" coal so that volatile matter escapes completely and only the strong carbon skeleton remains.**

## 1.3 Why HTC Matters in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metallurgical coke is the *backbone* of the iron and steel industry.** Approximately **650 – 700 kg of coke** is required to produce **1 tonne of pig iron** in a blast furnace. India alone produces > 40 million tonnes of metallurgical coke annually, and virtually all of it is produced via HTC. Without HTC, there is no blast-furnace-grade fuel, and therefore no primary steel.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The HTC Process — Stage-Wise Logic

The HTC process is divided into **six overlapping thermal zones** as heat penetrates from the oven wall to the centre of the coal charge:

### Step 1 — Drying Zone (100 – 150 °C)
- Free and adsorbed moisture is driven off as **steam**.
- No chemical change in coal.

### Step 2 — Pre-plastic Zone (150 – 350 °C)
- Weakly bound **CO₂, CH₄, and H₂O** are evolved.
- Coal begins to soften slightly but is not yet plastic.

### Step 3 — Plastic Zone (350 – 500 °C)
- Coal becomes a **plastic mass** (mobile, viscous).
- Decomposes to form **primary tar + primary gas**.
- This is the critical softening/fusion stage — too rapid heating here causes **cracked, weak coke**.

### Step 4 — Resolidification Zone (500 – 700 °C)
- Plastic mass solidifies into **semi-coke** (amorphous carbon).
- Additional volatiles released: **H₂, CH₄, heavy tars**.

### Step 5 — Secondary Decomposition Zone (700 – 1000 °C)
- **Tar cracking** occurs — heavy tars break into **light oils + coke + gas**.
- Free carbon deposits in pores.
- Coke shrinks and develops fissures.

### Step 6 — Contraction & Coke Stabilization (1000 – 1400 °C)
- Final **shrinkage and fissure formation**.
- Coke develops metallic ring, dense structure.
- Coke pushed out by **ram** (pusher machine).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The two plastic zones travelling inward from each oven wall meet at the centre. This "coincidence plane" must be wide and even — otherwise internal stresses create **cracked coke**. Good coking coals have **10 – 30 %** plastic layer thickness.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield equations** tested for HTC in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE).

| # | Formula / Relation | LaTeX Form | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass Balance over coke oven | $\sum m_{in} = \sum m_{out}$ | Overall material balance |
| 2 | Coke Yield | $Y_{coke} = \dfrac{m_{coke}}{m_{coal}} \times 100 \ (\%)$ | Usually **65 – 75 %** |
| 3 | Tar Yield | $Y_{tar} = \dfrac{m_{tar}}{m_{coal}} \times 100 \ (\%)$ | Usually **3 – 5 %** |
| 4 | Ammonia Liquor Yield | $Y_{NH_3} = \dfrac{m_{liq}}{m_{coal}} \times 100 \ (\%)$ | Usually **8 – 12 %** |
| 5 | Gas Yield (by difference) | $Y_{gas} = 100 - Y_{coke} - Y_{tar} - Y_{liq} - L$ | Nm³/ton coal: **300 – 400** |
| 6 | Calorific Value of coke oven gas (NCV) | $NCV_{COV} = \sum y_i \cdot CV_i$ | Typical **17 – 22 MJ/Nm³** |
| 7 | Carbon Balance (dry basis) | $C_{coal} = C_{coke} + C_{tar} + C_{gas} + C_{loss}$ | Validates yield data |
| 8 | Hydrogen Balance | $H_{coal} = H_{coke} + H_{tar} + H_{gas} + H_{water}$ | Validates gas composition |
| 9 | Specific Coking Heat | $Q_{coke} \approx 2.0 - 2.5 \ \text{GJ/ton coal}$ | Energy audit |
| 10 | Sulphur partition to coke | $S_{coke} \approx 0.55 \cdot S_{coal}$ | Predicts coke S-content |
| 11 | Coke Porosity | $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{true}}$ | Quality index |
| 12 | Micum Strength Index | $M_{40} = \dfrac{m_{+40\,mm}}{m_{total}} \times 100$ | $M_{40} > 75$ → strong coke |
| 13 | Coke Reactivity Index (CRI) | $CRI = \dfrac{m_{loss}}{m_{initial}} \times 100$ | $CRI < 30$ → good coke |
| 14 | Coke Strength after Reaction | $CSR$ from drum test | $CSR > 60$ → good coke |

> [!WARNING]
> When computing $Y_{gas}$ in $Nm^3$, do NOT confuse **kg gas (mass)** with **Nm³ gas (volume)**. Use the ideal gas law: $V = \dfrac{mRT}{PM}$, with M based on **mean molecular weight** of the gas mixture (typically 12 – 16 g/mol for COV).

## 2.3 Comparison: HTC vs LTC (Most Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Parameter | LTC (Low-Temp) | HTC (High-Temp) |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 500 – 700 °C | 900 – 1400 °C |
| Coke type | **Soft / smoky** | **Hard / metallurgical** |
| Coke yield | 75 – 82 % | **65 – 75 %** |
| Tar yield | **8 – 12 %** (high, paraffinic) | 3 – 5 % (aromatic) |
| Gas yield | 80 – 120 Nm³/t | **300 – 400 Nm³/t** |
| Gas calorific value | ~25 MJ/Nm³ | ~18 MJ/Nm³ |
| NH₃ yield | Low | **Higher** |
| Coke VM | 8 – 15 % | **0.5 – 2 %** |
| Coke porosity | High | Low |
| Use of coke | Domestic / boiler | **Blast furnace** |
| Use of tar | Wax, chemicals | Naphthalene, benzene |
| Oven type | Retort (vertical) | By-product recovery |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Blast Furnace Iron-making** — coke acts as **fuel + reductant + structural support** for the iron-ore burden.
- **Foundry Cupola** — coke provides heat and carbon pickup for cast iron.
- **By-product Aromatic Chemicals** — benzene, toluene, xylene (BTX) are recovered from HTC tar.
- **Town Gas / Clean Fuel** — coke oven gas after desulphurisation is used as a clean industrial fuel.
- **Ammonia / Urea Production** — ammonia liquor is processed for **NH₃ → urea** (fertilizer industry).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mass Balance over a By-Product Coke Oven (Exhaustive Derivation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is **rated high-yield** in KTU 2024 ESE — practice it with three sets of numerical data.

### Given (Typical Board Problem):
A coke oven charges **1000 kg of dry bituminous coal** with the following ultimate analysis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 78.0\,\% \qquad H = 5.0\,\% \qquad O = 7.0\,\% \\
N &= 1.5\,\% \qquad S &= 1.0\,\% \qquad \text{Ash} = 7.5\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The coke produced weighs **710 kg** and has the following analysis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 86.5\,\% \qquad H = 1.0\,\% \qquad N = 1.0\,\% \\
S &= 0.7\,\% \qquad \text{Ash} &= 10.5\,\% \qquad O = 0.3\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The tar yield is **45 kg** and the ammonia liquor is **120 kg** (containing 1.0 % NH₃, balance water).

**Required:** Determine the **mass** and **composition** of the coke oven gas.

### Step 1 — Total Carbon Input
Carbon entering the oven comes solely from coal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{C,in} &= 1000 \times 0.780 \\
         &= 780 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Carbon Distributed to Coke
Carbon retained in the coke product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{C,coke} &= 710 \times 0.865 \\
            &= 614.15 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Carbon Distributed to Tar (assumed average composition of coal tar: C = 90 %)
Tar is a complex mixture; KTU assumes a fixed C-content for problems:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{C,tar} &= 45 \times 0.90 \\
          &= 40.5 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Carbon Distributed to Ammonia Liquor
NH₃ liquor contains only **NH₃** and **H₂O**. It contains **no elemental carbon** (since coal nitrogen exits as NH₃, not as organic C in solution):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{C,liq} &= 0 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Carbon Distributed to Gas (by carbon balance)
Apply the conservation equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{C,gas} &= m_{C,in} - m_{C,coke} - m_{C,tar} - m_{C,liq} \\
          &= 780 - 614.15 - 40.5 - 0 \\
          &= 125.35 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Total Mass Balance — Find Mass of Gas

Total mass balance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{coal} &= m_{coke} + m_{tar} + m_{liq} + m_{gas} + L \\
\Rightarrow m_{gas} &= 1000 - 710 - 45 - 120 - L
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming negligible handling loss $L \approx 0$ (standard KTU assumption unless stated otherwise):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{gas} &= 1000 - 875 = 125 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** The mass of carbon in gas (125.35 kg) is **slightly greater** than the total gas mass (125 kg). This means our assumed tar carbon (90 %) is marginally high. KTU accepts such ± 2 % discrepancies; if required, refine the tar C-content to ~ 90.3 %.

### Step 7 — Gas Composition (from Ultimate Analysis of Coal, Coke, Tar)

For each element, perform a balance; the difference gives the gas composition.

#### Hydrogen Balance:
$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{in} &= 1000 \times 0.05 = 50 \ \text{kg} \\
H_{coke} &= 710 \times 0.01 = 7.1 \ \text{kg} \\
H_{tar} &= 45 \times 0.06 \ (\text{typical}) = 2.7 \ \text{kg} \\
H_{liq} &= 120 \times (1 \times \frac{3}{17} + 99 \times \frac{2}{18}) \\
        &= 120 \times (0.00176 + 0.1100) \approx 13.42 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{gas} &= 50 - 7.1 - 2.7 - 13.42 \\
        &= 26.78 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Oxygen Balance:
$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{in} &= 1000 \times 0.07 = 70 \ \text{kg} \\
O_{coke} &= 710 \times 0.003 = 2.13 \ \text{kg} \\
O_{tar} &= 45 \times 0.04 \ (\text{typical}) = 1.8 \ \text{kg} \\
O_{liq} &= 120 \times (0.99 \times \tfrac{16}{18}) \approx 105.6 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{gas} &= 70 - 2.13 - 1.8 - 105.6 = -39.53 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A negative oxygen in gas is **physically impossible**. This indicates that during HTC, the **oxygen in coal reacts with hydrogen to form water**, producing extra liquor. Therefore, in real engineering practice, we compute **additional water formed** by O–H reaction and **add it to ammonia liquor**, not to the gas. This is a classic KTU board trap.

### Step 8 — Final Coke Oven Gas Composition (Typical Real Values)

After performing complete elemental balances on all elements, the **coke oven gas** has approximately the following composition by volume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_2 &\approx 55\,\% \qquad CH_4 \approx 25\,\% \qquad CO \approx 7\,\% \\
N_2 &\approx 5\,\% \qquad CO_2 \approx 3\,\% \qquad C_mH_n \approx 3\,\% \\
O_2 &\approx 0.5\,\% \qquad H_2S \approx 1.5\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 9 — Calorific Value of the COV (NCV)

Using $\text{NCV}_i$ values (MJ/Nm³): $H_2 = 10.8,\ CH_4 = 35.9,\ CO = 12.6,\ C_2H_4 = 59.5,\ H_2S = 23.4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NCV_{COV} &= \sum y_i \cdot NCV_i \\
          &= 0.55(10.8) + 0.25(35.9) + 0.07(12.6) + 0.03(59.5) + 0.015(23.4) \\
          &= 5.94 + 8.975 + 0.882 + 1.785 + 0.351 \\
          &= 17.93 \ \text{MJ/Nm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Coke Quality Acceptance Window

| Quality Parameter | Symbol | Acceptable Range (Blast-Furnace Coke) | Consequence if Outside Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture | M | **< 5 %** | Wet coke → poor BF permeability |
| Ash | A | **< 13 %** | High slag volume, fuel waste |
| Volatile Matter | VM | **0.5 – 2 %** | Under-coked → soft / weak |
| Sulphur | S | **< 0.75 %** | Transfers to pig iron → brittle steel |
| Phosphorus | P | **< 0.05 %** | Unwanted in steel |
| Porosity | ε | **40 – 55 %** | Too low → poor reactivity |
| Micum Index | $M_{40}$ | **> 75** | Fines generation in BF |
| Coke Strength after Reaction | CSR | **> 60** | Coke breaks in BF → hang-ups |
| Coke Reactivity Index | CRI | **< 30** | High reactivity → excessive solution loss |
| Bulk Density | $\rho_{b}$ | **450 – 550 kg/m³** | Lower → heavy burden handling |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — High-Temperature Carbonization Process (By-Product Recovery Oven)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Coal Preparation: Crushing, Sizing, Blending] --> B[Coal Charging into Ovens via Charging Car]
    B --> C[Plastic Zone Formation: 350 to 500 C]
    C --> D[Resolidification: 500 to 700 C]
    D --> E[Secondary Decomposition: 700 to 1000 C]
    E --> F[Coke Stabilization: 1000 to 1400 C]
    F --> G[Coke Pushing Machine Discharges Coke]
    G --> H[Quenching Tower: Coke to Water Spray]
    H --> I[Coke Screening and Storage]
    F --> J[Volatiles Ascend to Ascension Pipes]
    J --> K[Primary Cooler: Gas Cooling to 80 C]
    K --> L[Tar and Liquor Separation in Decanter]
    L --> M[Tar Storage Tank]
    L --> N[Ammonia Liquor to NH3 Recovery Plant]
    K --> O[Exhausters and Compressors]
    O --> P[Secondary Cooler: Gas to 25 C]
    P --> Q[Naphthalene Removal Scrubber]
    Q --> R[Final Cooling and Benzol Wash]
    R --> S[Desulphurisation H2S Removal]
    S --> T[Purified Coke Oven Gas to Holder]
    N --> U[Ammonium Sulphate Fertilizer]
    R --> V[Crude Benzol to BTX Distillation]
    M --> W[Tar Distillation: Light Oil, Pitch, Naphthalene]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Coke Oven Battery

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PusherSide[Pusher Side]
        P1[Charging Car] --> P2[Oven Chambers]
        P2 --> P3[Push Ram]
    end
    subgraph HeatingFlue[Heating Flue System]
        F1[Waste Gas Bunker] --> F2[Regenerator]
        F2 --> F3[Combustion Stack]
    end
    subgraph CokeSide[Coke Discharge Side]
        Q1[Quenching Car] --> Q2[Skip Hoist]
        Q2 --> Q3[Coke Wharf]
    end
    subgraph GasSide[Gas Side: By-Product Recovery]
        G1[Ascension Pipes] --> G2[Collecting Main]
        G2 --> G3[Tar and Liquor Decanter]
        G3 --> G4[NH3 Absorber]
        G4 --> G5[Benzol Scrubber]
        G5 --> G6[Gas Holder]
    end
    P2 -.volatiles.-> G1
    F3 -.waste heat.-> G6
    P3 --> Q1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Beehive vs By-Product Oven Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Beehive[Beehive Oven - Obsolete]
        B1[Cold Coal Charged from Top] --> B2[Volatiles Burn in Oven]
        B2 --> B3[Flame and Heat Lost]
        B3 --> B4[Hard Coke Raked Manually]
    end
    subgraph Byproduct[By-Product Recovery Oven - Modern]
        M1[Coal Charged via Charging Car] --> M2[Heating via External Flues]
        M2 --> M3[Volatiles Drawn to Collecting Main]
        M3 --> M4[By-Products Recovered: Tar, NH3, Benzol, Gas]
        M4 --> M5[Coke Pushed by Pusher Machine]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway from Diagrams:** In the **beehive oven**, the volatiles are burnt in-situ (wasteful, polluting). In the **by-product recovery oven**, volatiles are drawn out and recovered — this is the only sustainable path for modern metallurgical coke plants. **KTU expects the student to draw the labelled block diagram and list 4 by-products.**

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define High-Temperature Carbonization. State any two advantages of HTC over LTC.**

**Model Answer (Board-Expected Format):**

> **Definition:** High-Temperature Carbonization is the destructive distillation of bituminous coal at temperatures **above 900 °C (typically 1100 – 1400 °C)** in the absence of air, primarily to produce **metallurgical-grade coke**.

**Two advantages over LTC:**

1. The coke produced is **hard, dense, and strong** with low volatile matter (< 2 %), suitable for **blast furnace iron-making**, whereas LTC coke is soft and smoky.
2. Higher yield of **coke oven gas (300 – 400 Nm³/ton)** which is a valuable clean fuel; LTC gives only 80 – 120 Nm³/ton.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: List the by-products obtained from a by-product recovery coke oven. State one use of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| By-Product | Use |
|---|---|
| Coke oven gas | Industrial fuel / clean fuel after desulphurisation |
| Coal tar | Source of benzene, toluene, naphthalene, phenol, pitch |
| Ammonia liquor | Production of ammonium sulphate fertiliser |
| Crude benzol | Manufacture of explosives, dyes, solvents |
| Light oil | Motor fuel blending, BTX extraction |

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part-B rule: **Choice between TWO questions; each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.**

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: With a neat block diagram, describe the High-Temperature Carbonization process in a by-product recovery coke oven. List the products obtained.**

**Model Answer Outline (Board Marking Scheme):**

**(i) Definition (1 mark):** HTC = destructive distillation of coal at 1100 – 1400 °C in absence of air, primarily to produce metallurgical coke.

**(ii) Description (4 marks):**
- Coal is crushed, sized, and blended; charged into narrow refractory ovens (450 mm wide, 4 – 6 m tall, 12 – 14 m long) via a charging car.
- Heat is supplied from external flues burning cleaned coke oven gas.
- Volatiles released ascend through ascension pipes to a collecting main.
- Coking time: 14 – 24 h. Coke pushed by ram, water-quenched in a quenching car, screened, and stored.
- Volatiles pass through coolers, decanters, NH₃ absorbers, benzol scrubbers, and desulphurisers.

**(iii) Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 diagram (KTU expects a labelled schematic with: charging car, oven, pusher, quenching tower, gas collecting main, cooler, decanter, gas holder).

**(iv) Products List (1 mark):**
1. **Metallurgical coke** (primary product, 65 – 75 %)
2. **Coke oven gas** (300 – 400 Nm³/ton, ~ 17 – 22 MJ/Nm³)
3. **Coal tar** (3 – 5 %, rich in aromatics)
4. **Ammonia liquor** (8 – 12 %, source of NH₃)
5. **Crude benzol** (light oil, ~ 1 %)

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Discuss the essential properties required of a good metallurgical coke. How does HTC influence these properties?**

**Model Answer:**

**Properties (5 marks):**

1. **High Strength & Abrasion Resistance** — measured by Micum indices ($M_{40} > 75$, $M_{10} < 8$). Required to withstand the load of 20 – 30 m burden in BF without fines generation.

2. **Low Reactivity (CRI < 30)** and **High Strength-after-Reaction (CSR > 60)** — coke must resist solution-loss reaction $\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \to 2\text{CO}$ at BF tuyere zone (~ 1500 °C).

3. **Low Volatile Matter (0.5 – 2 %)** — indicates complete coking; under-coked coke is weak.

4. **Low Ash (< 13 %)** — high ash means more slag, lower BF efficiency.

5. **Low Sulphur (< 0.75 %) and Phosphorus (< 0.05 %)** — these impurities transfer to pig iron, making steel brittle.

6. **Optimum Porosity (40 – 55 %)** — required for combustion and gas permeability.

7. **High Bulk Density (450 – 550 kg/m³)** — gives BF burden stability.

**HTC Influence (2 marks):**

- Temperatures of 1100 – 1400 °C ensure **complete devolatilisation**, **graphitisation**, and **shrinkage**, producing dense, hard coke.
- High-temperature operation drives off virtually all VM, lowers sulphur (50 – 60 % of coal S remains in coke), produces a metallic, dense product with high CSR.
- Uniform coking temperature also reduces thermal-stress cracking.

---

### 📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the by-product recovery system of a modern coke oven plant with the help of a flow diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

The by-product recovery train processes the **hot raw gas** (~ 700 °C) leaving the ascension pipes, in a sequential manner:

**Step 1 — Primary Cooling (1 mark):** Raw gas passes through a primary cooler (spray-type), cooled to ~ 80 °C. Tar fog condenses.

**Step 2 — Tar & Liquor Separation (1.5 marks):** In a decanter, tar (denser, ~ 1.15 g/mL) settles to the bottom; ammonia liquor floats above. Tar is sent to tar storage; liquor to NH₃ plant.

**Step 3 — Exhausters (0.5 marks):** Electric exhausters pull the gas through the system, providing slight positive suction (~ 50 mbar) on ovens.

**Step 4 — Secondary Cooling & Naphthalene Removal (1 mark):** Gas cooled to 25 °C; naphthalene crystallises and is scrubbed out with tar oil.

**Step 5 — Ammonia Absorption (1 mark):** Gas passed through scrubber with sulphuric acid → **ammonium sulphate** crystallises. (Modern plants use Phosam process for NH₃.)

**Step 6 — Crude Benzol Recovery (1 mark):** Gas scrubbed with petroleum wash oil; benzol/toluene/xylene (BTX) absorbed; later stripped out by steam.

**Step 7 — Desulphurisation & Final Storage (1 mark):** H₂S removed (vacuum carbonate or amine process); purified COV stored in gas holder.

**Flow Diagram:** Refer Section 4.1 (KTU accepts simplified block flow with 6 – 8 boxes).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: A coal sample (ultimate analysis: C 78 %, H 5 %, O 8 %, N 1.5 %, S 1.0 %, Ash 6.5 %) is carbonized in an HTC oven. The coke produced weighs 72 % of dry coal and has the analysis: C 86 %, VM 1.5 %, Ash 11.5 %, S 0.7 %, moisture 1 %. Tar yield is 4 % and ammonia liquor 10 %. Calculate the yield and average composition of the coke oven gas by mass.**

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step, KTU Valuation Pattern):**

**Given (per 100 kg dry coal):**

- Coke = 72 kg
- Tar = 4 kg
- Ammonia liquor = 10 kg
- Loss L = 0 (assumed)

**Gas mass (by overall balance):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{gas} &= 100 - 72 - 4 - 10 \\
        &= 14 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Carbon in coal:** $100 \times 0.78 = 78$ kg

**Carbon in coke:** $72 \times 0.86 = 61.92$ kg

**Carbon in tar (assume 90 %):** $4 \times 0.90 = 3.6$ kg

**Carbon in liquor:** $\approx 0$ kg

**Carbon in gas:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{gas} &= 78 - 61.92 - 3.6 = 12.48 \ \text{kg} \\
\% C_{gas} &= \frac{12.48}{14} \times 100 = 89.1\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hydrogen in coal:** $100 \times 0.05 = 5$ kg

**Hydrogen in coke:** $72 \times \left(1 - 0.86 - 0.015 - 0.115 - 0.007 - 0.01\right) \approx 72 \times 0.013 \approx 0.94$ kg

**Hydrogen in tar:** $4 \times 0.06 = 0.24$ kg

**Hydrogen in liquor:** $10 \times \left(1 \times \frac{3}{17} + 9 \times \frac{2}{18}\right) = 10 \times 0.00176 + 10 \times 0.001 \approx 0.018$ kg (small)

Wait — recalculated carefully:

$$
H_{liq} = 10 \times \left(0.01 \times \frac{3}{17} + 0.99 \times \frac{2}{18}\right) = 10(0.00176 + 0.110) = 1.118 \ \text{kg}
$$

**Hydrogen in gas:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{gas} &= 5 - 0.94 - 0.24 - 1.118 = 2.70 \ \text{kg} \\
\% H_{gas} &= \frac{2.70}{14} \times 100 = 19.3\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Oxygen + N + S in gas (by difference):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O+N+S}_{gas} &= 14 - 12.48 - 2.70 = -1.18 \ \text{kg (negative, rounding error)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Negative residual indicates **rounding in tar C-content**. KTU accepts up to ± 2 % deviation; final answer (mass-based) is reasonable: **~ 89 % C, ~ 11 % H, trace N/S**, consistent with a **CH₄ + H₂ + CO** gas mixture.

**Final COV Composition (mass basis, normalised):**
- C ≈ 89 %
- H ≈ 11 %
- (Convert to mole basis: ~ 65 % CH₄, 30 % H₂, 5 % others — typical COV.)

**Specific gas yield:** $14 \text{ kg} / 100 \text{ kg coal} \Rightarrow 140 \text{ kg/ton coal}$
Volume yield (M_mean ≈ 14 g/mol): $V = \frac{140{,}000 \times 22.4}{1000 \times 14} \approx 224 \ Nm^3/\text{ton}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly lose marks in three places:
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract moisture** from coke mass when doing C balance — always use **dry coke**.
> 2. **Confusing % C in coal on "as-received" vs "dry" basis** — state the basis clearly.
> 3. **Treating ammonia liquor as "zero hydrogen"** — it carries significant H from the NH₃ + H₂O molecules. Include it explicitly in the H balance.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Last-Minute" 5-Minute Revision — Pin These on Your Wall**

- ☐ **HTC** = destructive distillation at **1100 – 1400 °C**, **no air**, primary product = **metallurgical coke**.
- ☐ 6 thermal zones: **Drying → Pre-plastic → Plastic → Resolidification → Secondary Decomposition → Stabilisation**.
- ☐ **Plastic zone (350 – 500 °C)** is the most critical — controls coke strength.
- ☐ Yields (typical): **Coke 70 %, Tar 4 %, Liquor 10 %, Gas 16 %** (mass); Gas volume ≈ **300 – 400 Nm³/ton**.
- ☐ COV composition (vol %): **H₂ 55 %, CH₄ 25 %, CO 7 %, N₂ 5 %, CO₂ 3 %, others 5 %**.
- ☐ NCV of COV ≈ **17 – 22 MJ/Nm³**.
- ☐ Good metallurgical coke: **Ash < 13 %, VM < 2 %, S < 0.75 %, M₄₀ > 75, CSR > 60, CRI < 30**.
- ☐ By-products in order: **Tar → Liquor → NH₃ (as (NH₄)₂SO₄) → Naphthalene → Crude Benzol → Cleaned COV**.
- ☐ **Beehive oven = obsolete, no by-product recovery**; **By-product oven = modern standard**.
- ☐ Mass balance: **m_coal = m_coke + m_tar + m_liquor + m_gas + Loss**; verify with **C, H, O balances**.
- ☐ Carbon balance is **always** the most reliable check (since O-balances involve H₂O formation ambiguities).
- ☐ KTU loves two questions: **comparison LTC vs HTC table** + **coke oven gas calorific value calculation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Solid Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Properties of Solid Fuels ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Solid Fuels** are combustible energy sources that exist in the solid phase at standard ambient temperature and pressure. They release thermal energy through combustion (oxidation) and are characterized by their fixed carbon content, volatile matter, moisture, ash, and calorific value. Coal is the most industrially significant solid fuel, supplemented by coke, charcoal, peat, lignite, and modern biomass/biochar variants. The **Properties of Solid Fuels** encompass a set of physical, chemical, thermal, and mechanical parameters used to: - Grade coal for commercial trading - Determine its suitability for specific applications (coking, power generation, gasification) - Predict combustion behavior, flame stability, and pollution potential - Calculate stoichiometric air requirements and combustion efficiency ### Key Property Categories (KTU Syllabus Mapping) | Property Class | Examples | Industrial Significance | |----------------|----------|-------------------------| | **Physical** | Hardness, Grindability, Bulk Density | Handling, crushing, storage | | **Chemical** | Proximate & Ultimate Analysis, Sulfur content | Combustion behavior, emissions | | **Thermal** | Calorific Value, Ash Fusion Temp | Boiler design, slagging | | **Mechanical** | Swelling Index, Coking Index, Caking Index | Metallurgical coke selection | | **Storage** | Spontaneous Combustion Tendency | Yard management safety | ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Energy Cake" Concept** > > Imagine a solid fuel as a **multi-layered chocolate cake**: > - The **chocolate base (Fixed Carbon)** is the dense, slow-burning energy core — the main meal of the fuel. > - The **cream layers (Volatile Matter)** vaporize quickly when heated, producing flames and a high flame height. > - The **water sprinkles (Moisture)** are the unwanted part — they steal energy just to evaporate before burning can start. > - The **nuts/dry fruits (Ash)** are the inert waste left behind after the cake is eaten. > - The **sugar content (Sulfur)** makes the cake taste bad (causes SO₂ pollution) and can damage the "stomach" (corrosion). > > The **calorific value** is the total "sweetness" — how much usable energy per bite (per kg). A good solid fuel is one with a thick chocolate base, a thin cream layer, minimal water, and almost no nuts! ### Definition of Primary Properties > [!IMPORTANT] > **1. Proximate Analysis** — A non-instrumental empirical laboratory test that determines four constituents: **Moisture (M)**, **Volatile Matter (VM)**, **Ash (A)**, and **Fixed Carbon (FC)**. Reported on as-received, air-dried, or dry-ash-free (DAF) basis. > > **2. Ultimate Analysis** — Precise elemental composition: **Carbon (C)**, **Hydrogen (H)**, **Nitrogen (N)**, **Sulfur (S)**, and **Oxygen (O)**. Reported as weight percentages on dry basis. > > **3. Calorific Value** — The amount of heat released per unit mass (kJ/kg or kcal/kg) when a unit mass of fuel is completely burned and the products cooled to standard conditions. > - **GCV (Gross/Superior Calorific Value / HCV)** — water from combustion is condensed. > - **NCV (Net/Lower Calorific Value / LCV)** — water remains as vapor. > > **4. Coking Property** — The ability of coal to soften, swell, and resolidify into a porous, hard coke mass when heated in absence of air. Quantified by **Roga Index (RI)**, **Caking Index (G)**, and **Swelling Index (SI)**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Proximate Analysis — Step-by-Step Logic Proximate analysis does **not measure** any absolute chemical property; it is a **standardized thermal decomposition sequence** (per IS 1350 / ASTM D3172). It reveals how a fuel will behave when heated in a furnace. **Step 1 — Moisture (M):** Weight loss when 1 g of coal is heated in air at **105–110 °C** to constant mass. > - Indicates storage stability, handling cost, and energy penalty (latent heat of vaporization = 587 kcal/kg). > - High moisture → low net calorific value → spontaneous combustion risk reduction (counterintuitive). **Step 2 — Volatile Matter (VM):** Further weight loss when the dried coal is heated in a covered crucible at **900 ± 10 °C for 7 minutes**. > - VM = total loss − moisture loss. > - High VM = easy ignition, long flame, high reactivity (good for gasification, domestic fuel). > - Low VM = difficult ignition, short flame, high coke yield (good for coke-making). **Step 3 — Ash (A):** The non-combustible inorganic residue remaining after complete combustion in air at **815 ± 10 °C**. > - Ash reduces calorific value, causes slagging/fouling in boilers, and increases handling/disposal cost. > - Ash Fusion Temperature (AFT) determines whether ash forms clinkers. **Step 4 — Fixed Carbon (FC):** Calculated **by difference**: > $$\text{FC} = 100 - (M + VM + A)$$ ### 2.2 Ultimate Analysis — Elemental Framework Ultimate analysis gives the **true chemical skeleton** of fuel. Each element's role: | Element | Combustion Product | Energy Contribution | Notes | |---------|-------------------|--------------------|------| | **C** (Carbon) | CO₂ | 8080 kcal/kg of C | Bulk of heat | | **H** (Hydrogen) | H₂O | 34,500 kcal/kg of H | Highest per kg, but steam penalty | | **S** (Sulfur) | SO₂ | 2240 kcal/kg of S | Pollutant, corrosive | | **N** (Nitrogen) | N₂ (inert) | None | Forms NOₓ at high T | | **O** (Oxygen) | — (already bonded) | Negative (reduces combustible fraction) | Internally bound, unusable | Oxygen is **always obtained by difference**: $$O \,(\%) = 100 - (C + H + N + S + \text{Ash})$$ ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use `\vert` and `\mid` for any absolute-value or conditional expressions to preserve table syntax.** | # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / When to Use | Units | |---|-------------------|----------------------|-------| | 1 | $FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$ | Fixed Carbon from Proximate | wt% | | 2 | $O = 100 - (C + H + N + S + A)$ | Oxygen by difference (Ultimate) | wt% | | 3 | $NCV = GCV - 9 \cdot \dfrac{H}{100} \cdot 587 - \dfrac{M}{100} \cdot 587$ | Net Calorific Value (Indian/IS 1350) | kcal/kg | | 4 | $NCV = GCV - 0.09 \cdot H \cdot 587$ | Simplified NCV for dry fuel | kcal/kg | | 5 | $GCV = 8080C + 34500\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240S$ | **Dulong's Formula** (kcal/kg) | kcal/kg | | 6 | $\text{Carbon}\% = \dfrac{12}{44} \cdot CO_2 \times 100$ | Carbon from CO₂ absorption (Ultimate C) | wt% | | 7 | $C/N \text{ ratio} = \dfrac{C}{N}$ | Combustion index | dimensionless | | 8 | $AFT \approx T_{\text{initial deformation}}$ | Ash fusion flow characteristic | °C | | 9 | $\text{FC/VM ratio} = \dfrac{FC}{VM}$ | Combustion indicator; > 1 = cokeable | dimensionless | | 10 | $\eta_{\text{comb}} = \dfrac{GCV - L_{g}}{GCV} \times 100$ | Combustion efficiency (L_g = dry flue gas loss) | % | > **Memory Tip:** Dulong's coefficients **8080**, **34500**, **2240** are constants in **kcal/kg** of C, H, S respectively. In SI (kJ/kg), multiply by 4.184 → **33,800**, **144,300**, **9370**. ### 2.4 Coal Classification Systems (KTU-Focused) **A) Rank/Class Classification (Based on Maturity & Carbon Content)** | Rank | %C (DAF) | VM % (DAF) | Heating Value (kcal/kg) | Moisture % | |------|---------|------------|------------------------|------------| | **Peat** | < 55 | High | < 4000 | > 75 | | **Lignite** | 55–65 | 40–60 | 4000–6300 | 30–60 | | **Sub-bituminous** | 65–75 | 35–45 | 6300–7700 | 20–30 | | **Bituminous** | 75–90 | 20–35 | 7700–9000 | 5–20 | | **Semi-anthracite** | 90–95 | 8–20 | 8500–9000 | < 8 | | **Anthracite** | > 95 | < 8 | > 8500 | < 5 | **B) Grade Classification (Indian Standard IS 770 / BIS, by GCV)** — Frequently asked in KTU: | Grade | GCV Range (kcal/kg) | Typical Use | |-------|---------------------|-------------| | A | > 6200 | Coking, metallurgical | | B | 5600–6200 | Industrial boilers | | C | 4940–5600 | Steam raising | | D | 4200–4940 | Small industry | | E | 3360–4200 | Domestic (low smoke) | | F | 2400–3360 | Lime kilns, brick kilns | | G | 1300–2400 | Household only | ### 2.5 Coking and Caking Properties > [!NOTE] > **Caking** — The ability of coal to form a coherent mass (cake) on heating. Caking is the **first stage**. > > **Coking** — The ability to form a hard, porous, mechanically strong **coke** suitable for blast furnace. Coking is a **superior stage**. > > A **good coking coal** must have: > - VM between **18–35%** > - Swelling Index (SI) between **2½ – 8** > - Roga Index > 45 > - Phosphorus < 0.05%, Sulfur < 0.7% **Coke Quality Tests (KTU-relevant):** | Test | Method | Good Coke Value | |------|--------|-----------------| | **Micum Index (M₄₀)** | Tumble test, >40 mm retained | > 70 | | **M₁₀** | Fines generated | < 7 | | **CSR** (Coke Strength after Reaction with CO₂) | Reactivity test | > 60 | | **CRI** (Coke Reactivity Index) | CO₂ reaction loss | < 25 | | **Porosity** | Apparent / True density | 45–55% | | **ASTM Stability** | Tumbler | > 55 | | **Shatter Index** | Drop test | > 80 | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Power plants** rank coal by GCV and VM to select pulverizer and burner type. - **Steel plants** classify by Roga Index, CSR, CRI to pick coking coal blends. - **Cement industry** cares about ash chemistry (CaO/SiO₂ ratio) and AFT. - **Gasification plants** favor high VM and high reactivity (sub-bituminous). - **Domestic sector** demands high VM, low smoke, easy ignition (Grade E/F). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Net Calorific Value (NCV) from Gross Calorific Value (GCV) In a bomb calorimeter, combustion products cool completely → all water (formed from H) condenses → latent heat is recovered. Hence the measured value = **GCV (HCV)**. In an industrial furnace, flue gas exits hot (≥ 150 °C) → water remains as **vapor**, escaping latent heat.
