Hence usable heat = **NCV (LCV)**. **Step-by-step derivation:** **Step 1:** Consider 1 kg of fuel containing H kg of hydrogen. **Step 2:** Hydrogen on combustion forms water: $$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$$ $$1 \text{ kg H}_2 \text{ produces } 9 \text{ kg H}_2O \quad \left(\text{since } \dfrac{18}{2}=9\right)$$ **Step 3:** Total water formed from H = $9 \cdot H$ kg (excluding any liquid water originally present). **Step 4:** Total water originally present in fuel = M kg (moisture). **Step 5:** Total water escaping as vapor in flue gas = $9H + M$ kg per kg of fuel. **Step 6:** Latent heat lost per kg water = 587 kcal/kg (at 25 °C, 1 atm). **Step 7:** Total latent heat loss = $\left(9H + M\right) \times 587$ kcal per kg of fuel. **Step 8:** Hence, the net calorific value (useable heat) is: $$ \begin{aligned} NCV &= GCV - \left(9H + M\right) \cdot \dfrac{587}{100} \\ &= GCV - \dfrac{9H}{100} \cdot 587 - \dfrac{M}{100} \cdot 587 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 9:** For a **completely dry fuel** (M = 0), the simplified form is: $$NCV = GCV - 0.09 \cdot H \cdot 587 = GCV - 52.83 \cdot H$$ > **Exam Tip:** In SI units, use **2450 kJ/kg** instead of 587 kcal/kg. The conversion factor: 587 × 4.184 ≈ 2455 kJ/kg. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Dulong's Formula (GCV from Ultimate Analysis) Dulong (1830) empirically correlated the heating value of carbonaceous fuel to its elemental composition. The reasoning: **Step 1:** Treat the fuel as a mixture of pure C, H, and S, plus inert O already bonded to some of the H. **Step 2:** Hydrogen has higher affinity for O. Out of the total H, the amount that has already been internally oxidized is $\dfrac{O}{8}$ (because 8 parts O combines with 1 part H by mass: $H_2O = \dfrac{2}{16} = \dfrac{1}{8}$). **Step 3:** Therefore, **free (combustible) hydrogen** is: $$H_{\text{free}} = H - \dfrac{O}{8}$$ **Step 4:** Each kg of free C contributes 8080 kcal; each kg of free H contributes 34,500 kcal; each kg of S contributes 2240 kcal. **Step 5:** Summing all contributions: $$ \begin{aligned} GCV &= 8080 \cdot \dfrac{C}{100} + 34500 \cdot \dfrac{H - O/8}{100} + 2240 \cdot \dfrac{S}{100} \quad \text{(kcal/kg)} \\ &= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[8080C + 34500\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240S\right] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6:** In SI units (kJ/kg), multiply by 4.184: $$ \begin{aligned} GCV \, (\text{kJ/kg}) = & \; 33{,}800 \cdot \dfrac{C}{100} + 144{,}300 \cdot \dfrac{H - O/8}{100} \\ & + 9370 \cdot \dfrac{S}{100} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > Dulong's formula **overestimates** GCV by 2–5% for high-ash Indian coals; hence BIS uses an empirical correction factor table. The formula is most accurate for **bituminous and anthracite** coals. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Numerical) **Question:** A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis (as received basis): C = 68%, H = 5%, O = 12%, N = 1.5%, S = 0.5%, Moisture = 3%, Ash = 10%. Calculate: 1. GCV using Dulong's formula. 2. NCV using the IS 1350 relation. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Free hydrogen:** $$H_{\text{free}} = H - \dfrac{O}{8} = 5 - \dfrac{12}{8} = 5 - 1.5 = 3.5\%$$ **Step 2 — GCV via Dulong's:** $$ \begin{aligned} GCV &= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[8080 \times 68 + 34500 \times 3.5 + 2240 \times 0.5\right] \\ &= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[549{,}440 + 120{,}750 + 1120\right] \\ &= \dfrac{671{,}310}{100} \\ &= 6713.1 \; \text{kcal/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — NCV via IS 1350:** $$ \begin{aligned} NCV &= GCV - \dfrac{9H}{100} \cdot 587 - \dfrac{M}{100} \cdot 587 \\ &= 6713.1 - \dfrac{9 \times 5}{100} \cdot 587 - \dfrac{3}{100} \cdot 587 \\ &= 6713.1 - 0.45 \times 587 - 0.03 \times 587 \\ &= 6713.1 - 264.15 - 17.61 \\ &= 6431.34 \; \text{kcal/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Answer:** GCV ≈ **6713 kcal/kg**, NCV ≈ **6431 kcal/kg**. > This coal corresponds to **Grade B (5600–6200)** in Indian classification (close to upper Grade B / lower Grade A), suitable for industrial boilers. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Property vs. Application | Property | Best For (Application) | Worst For (Application) | Why? | |----------|----------------------|-----------------------|------| | High VM (35–45%) | Gasification, domestic | Coke making | Coke becomes weak/powdery | | Low VM (< 10%) | Coke making, foundry cupola | Domestic burning | Hard to ignite, smoky | | High Moisture (> 30%) | Lignite beds (drying integrated) | Shipping, long storage | Transport of inert water | | High Ash (> 30%) | Pulverized fuel with dry-bottom | Slag-tap furnaces | Slagging problems | | High Sulfur (> 1%) | None (treated fuel only) | All applications | SO₂ + corrosion | | High Phosphorus | None (limited) | Steel making | Brittle steel (cold short) | | High Swelling Index (> 6) | Metallurgical coke | Domestic | Swelling damages grates | | High GCV (> 8000 kcal/kg) | Power plants, ships | Domestic | Wastes premium fuel | | Low Caking | Pulverized combustion | Coke ovens | No coke formation | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Coal Formation (Coalification) Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[PEAT
Original Plant Matter] --> A1[Burial & Decomposition] A1 --> B0[LIGNITE
Brown Coal] B0 --> B1[Sub-bituminous Coal] B1 --> C0[Bituminous Coal] C0 --> D0[Semi Anthracite] D0 --> E0[Anthracite
Highest Rank] A0 -.Time, Pressure, Heat.-> E0 ``` ### 4.2 Proximate Analysis — Test Procedure Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Start: 1 g Air Dried Coal] --> P1[Heat at 105 to 110 deg C] P1 --> P2[Constant Mass = M Loss] P2 --> P3[Dried Coal: Heat at 900 deg C, 7 min, Covered Crucible] P3 --> P4[Loss Minus Moisture = Volatile Matter VM] P4 --> P5[Residue: Burn in Open Crucible at 815 deg C] P5 --> P6[Residue Mass = Ash A] P6 --> P7[FC = 100 minus M plus VM plus A] P1 -->|Moisture| Out1[Moisture M percent] P3 -->|VM| Out2[Volatile Matter VM percent] P5 -->|Ash| Out3[Ash A percent] P7 -->|FC| Out4[Fixed Carbon FC percent] ``` ### 4.3 Ultimate Analysis — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input COAL[Coal Sample 1 g] end subgraph Stage1[Stage 1 Carbon Hydrogen] C1[CHN Analyzer: Combust at 950 deg C, O2 stream] C2[CO2 Absorbed in U Tube Mass Gain = Carbon] C3[H2O Absorbed in P2O5 Tube Mass Gain = Hydrogen] end subgraph Stage2[Stage 2 Sulfur] S1[Eschka Method: Fuse with MgO and Na2CO3] S2[Sulfate Leached and Precipitated as BaSO4] end subgraph Stage3[Stage 3 Nitrogen] N1[Kjeldahl Method: Digest with H2SO4, Distill NH3] N2[Titrate NH3 against Standard Acid] end subgraph Stage4[Stage 4 Oxygen] O1[Oxygen = 100 minus C plus H plus N plus S plus Ash] end COAL --> C1 C1 --> C2 C1 --> C3 COAL --> S1 S1 --> S2 COAL --> N1 N1 --> N2 COAL --> O1 ``` ### 4.4 Coal Classification Decision Tree (Indian Standard IS 770) ```mermaid flowchart TD X0[Coal Sample] --> X1{Check GCV in kcal per kg} X1 -->|Above 6200| XA[Grade A Premium] X1 -->|5600 to 6200| XB[Grade B Industrial] X1 -->|4940 to 5600| XC[Grade C Steam Raising] X1 -->|4200 to 4940| XD[Grade D Small Industry] X1 -->|3360 to 4200| XE[Grade E Domestic Low Smoke] X1 -->|2400 to 3360| XF[Grade F Lime Brick Kiln] X1 -->|1300 to 2400| XG[Grade G Household] XA --> APP1[Use Metallurgical Coking] XB --> APP2[Use Industrial Boilers] XC --> APP3[Use Utility Power Plants] XG --> APP4[Use Domestic Chulha] ``` ### 4.5 Coking Mechanism (Plastic Layer Formation) ```mermaid flowchart LR H0[Raw Coal Pellets in Oven] --> H1[Softens at 350 deg C] H1 --> H2[Plastic Layer 400 to 500 deg C] H2 --> H3[Resolidifies to Semi Coke 550 deg C] H3 --> H4[Coke 950 to 1100 deg C with 24 h Soaking] H4 --> H5[Push out Hot Coke Quench with Water] H5 --> H6[Ready Coke for Blast Furnace] ``` ### 4.6 Coke Property Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD K1[Good Coke Required] --> K2[CSR above 60] K1 --> K3[CRI below 25] K1 --> K4[Porosity 45 to 55 percent] K1 --> K5[Micum 40 above 70] K1 --> K6[Ash below 12 percent] K1 --> K7[Moisture below 3 percent] K1 --> K8[Sulfur below 0.7 percent] K1 --> K9[Phosphorus below 0.05 percent] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Q1. Define proximate analysis of coal. List the four constituents it determines and the standard temperatures used in the test.** **Model Answer:** Proximate analysis is a standardized empirical test (IS 1350 / ASTM D3172) used to determine the proximate composition of a solid fuel. It yields four constituents expressed as weight percentage: | Constituent | Standard Temperature | Purpose | |-------------|----------------------|---------| | **Moisture (M)** | 105–110 °C (in air oven, to constant mass) | Determines drying requirement | | **Volatile Matter (VM)** | 900 ± 10 °C (covered crucible, 7 min) | Indicates flame & ignition quality | | **Ash (A)** | 815 ± 10 °C (open crucible, complete burn) | Determines non-combustible residue | | **Fixed Carbon (FC)** | By difference: $100 - (M + VM + A)$ | Indicates coke yield | It is a quick, inexpensive method widely used in industry to rank coal and predict combustion behavior, although it is less precise than ultimate analysis. --- **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q2. Differentiate between GCV and NCV of a solid fuel. Why is NCV used in industrial furnace design?** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | GCV (Gross Calorific Value / HCV) | NCV (Net Calorific Value / LCV) | |-----------|-----------------------------------|----------------------------------| | Definition | Heat released with water from combustion condensed | Heat released with water leaving as vapor | | Measured in | Bomb calorimeter | Calculated from GCV | | Includes latent heat of condensation | Yes | No | | Use | Research, calorimeter standards | Industrial furnace/boiler design | **Why NCV is used industrially:** In actual boiler/furnace operation, the **flue gas temperature** is typically 150–250 °C — well above the dew point. Hence the water vapor formed from hydrogen and moisture **does not condense**; the latent heat of vaporization is **carried away as sensible heat in the stack gas**. Only NCV represents the **actually available heat** for process use. The relation (Indian Standard): $$NCV = GCV - 9 \cdot \dfrac{H}{100} \cdot 587 - \dfrac{M}{100} \cdot 587$$ --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Do NOT** mix the bases of reporting. Always state whether the analysis is **as-received (ar)**, **air-dried (ad)**, **dry (d)**, or **dry-ash-free (daf)**. Marks are deducted for ambiguity. > - In NCV problems, do **not forget to subtract the moisture term**. Many students only include the hydrogen latent heat and lose 2–3 marks. > - For Dulong's formula, students often write `H - O/8` as `H - 0.8 O` — this is **wrong**. The factor is **1/8 = 0.125**, not 0.8. > - In the rank classification, always give the **carbon range AND VM range** for the rank, not just one. > - For GCV/NCV calculations, **state the units** in every line. 1 mark is reserved for "correctness of units". --- ### Part B — Option A **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1 Choice A]** **Q3. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the four-fold classification of coal based on rank. State the carbon content, VM percentage, and typical applications of each class. **Q3. (b) [7 Marks]** A coal has the following composition (as-received): C = 65%, H = 4.5%, O = 14%, N = 1.2%, S = 0.8%, Moisture = 5%, Ash = 9.5%. Calculate (i) GCV using Dulong's formula, (ii) NCV using IS 1350 relation, and (iii) the grade of coal according to IS 770. --- ### Part B — Option A — Model Solution **(a) Four-Fold Classification of Coal by Rank:** Coal is a sedimentary rock formed from plant remains subjected to increasing temperature and pressure over geological time. This maturation process is called **coalification**. Coal is ranked by its **carbon content (dry-ash-free basis)**, **volatile matter**, and **heating value**. The four major ranks (with sub-classes) are: **1. Peat (Pre-coal stage):** - C% (DAF): < 55% - VM % (DAF): > 60% - Moisture (as-received): 70–90% - GCV: 2500–4000 kcal/kg - Appearance: Brown, fibrous, visible plant structure - Use: Localized domestic fuel, soil conditioner, agricultural mulching (NOT an industrial fuel) **2. Lignite (Brown Coal):** - C% (DAF): 55–65% - VM % (DAF): 45–60% - Moisture: 30–60% (high, causes spontaneous combustion) - GCV: 4000–6300 kcal/kg - Use: Power generation (Neyveli, India), gasification, fertilizer industry - Sub-types: Earthy lignite, Woody lignite, Lignite pitch **3. Bituminous Coal (Most abundant industrial rank):** - C% (DAF): 75–90% - VM % (DAF): 20–35% - Moisture: 5–15% - GCV: 7700–9000 kcal/kg - Use: Steam raising, power plants, metallurgical coke blending - Sub-types: - **High volatile bituminous (A, B, C)** — domestic, gasification - **Medium volatile bituminous** — coking blends - **Low volatile bituminous** — coking coal **4. Anthracite (Highest rank):** - C% (DAF): > 92% - VM % (DAF): < 8% - Moisture: < 5% - GCV: 8500–9000 kcal/kg - Use: Domestic fuel (smokeless), sintering, carbon electrodes, water filtration - Sub-types: Semi-anthracite (90–92% C), Anthracite (92–98% C), Meta-anthracite (>98% C) **Diagrammatic progression (verbal):** $$\text{Peat} \rightarrow \text{Lignite} \rightarrow \text{Bituminous} \rightarrow \text{Anthracite}$$ $$\text{Carbon}: \uparrow \quad \text{VM}: \downarrow \quad \text{Moisture}: \downarrow \quad \text{GCV}: \uparrow \text{ (first, then steady)}$$ **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given (as-received basis):** C = 65%, H = 4.5%, O = 14%, N = 1.2%, S = 0.8%, M = 5%, A = 9.5% **Check:** 65 + 4.5 + 14 + 1.2 + 0.8 + 5 + 9.5 = 100 ✓ **(i) GCV using Dulong's formula:** $$H_{\text{free}} = H - \dfrac{O}{8} = 4.5 - \dfrac{14}{8} = 4.5 - 1.75 = 2.75\%$$ $$ \begin{aligned} GCV &= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[8080 \times 65 + 34500 \times 2.75 + 2240 \times 0.8\right] \\ &= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[525{,}200 + 94{,}875 + 1792\right] \\ &= \dfrac{621{,}867}{100} \\ &= 6218.67 \; \text{kcal/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) NCV using IS 1350:** $$ \begin{aligned} NCV &= GCV - \dfrac{9H}{100} \cdot 587 - \dfrac{M}{100} \cdot 587 \\ &= 6218.67 - \dfrac{9 \times 4.5}{100} \cdot 587 - \dfrac{5}{100} \cdot 587 \\ &= 6218.67 - 0.405 \times 587 - 0.05 \times 587 \\ &= 6218.67 - 237.74 - 29.35 \\ &= 5951.58 \; \text{kcal/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Grade classification (IS 770, based on GCV):** GCV = 6218.67 kcal/kg → falls in the range **5600–6200 kcal/kg**... wait, 6218 > 6200, so it falls just above the Grade B upper limit. > Standard IS 770 grade boundaries: Grade A > 6200 kcal/kg, Grade B 5600–6200 kcal/kg. > Since GCV ≈ **6219 kcal/kg**, this is **Grade A** (premium grade, used for coking and metallurgical applications — but only if caking index is also good). **Final Answer:** - GCV = **6218.67 kcal/kg** - NCV = **5951.58 kcal/kg** - Grade = **A (Premium)**, suitable for **coking and metallurgical use** subject to caking test. --- ### Part B — Option B **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice B]** **Q4. (a) [7 Marks]** Define the terms caking and coking. Explain the four important tests used to evaluate the coking property of a coal (Swelling Index, Roga Index, Gray-King Assay, and Plastometer Test). **Q4. (b) [7 Marks]** The proximate analysis of a coal sample is: M = 4%, VM = 32%, A = 18%, FC = 46%. A 1.5 g sample is burned in a bomb calorimeter and the temperature rise is 3.1 °C. The calorimeter constant (water equivalent) is 2300 cal/°C and the fuse wire correction is 50 cal. Calculate the GCV in kcal/kg. --- ### Part B — Option B — Model Solution **(a) Caking vs. Coking + Coking Tests:** **Definitions:** - **Caking** is the property of coal to soften, become plastic on heating, and form a coherent mass (cake) when cooled. The mass has moderate strength. Caking is a **necessary but not sufficient** property. - **Coking** is the property of coal to form a **hard, porous, mechanically strong, grayish-black residue** (coke) suitable for use in a **blast furnace**. Coking requires caking plus a specific swelling and resolidification behavior. **Test 1 — Swelling Index (SI) or Crucible Swelling Test (IS 13810):** - A 1 g coal sample is heated in a covered crucible to 800 ± 10 °C for 7 min. - The residue button is compared visually with standard profile diagrams numbered 1 to 9. - **SI 1** = no swelling (pulverulent); **SI 9** = maximum swelling. - **Good coking coal**: SI between **2½ and 8** (ideally 4–6). **Test 2 — Roga Index (IS 1350 Part IV):** - 1 g coal is mixed with 5 g standard anthracite (size −212 μm) and carbonized at 850 °C. - The coke button is tumbled in a rotating drum (50 revolutions at 50 rpm) and sieved. - The Roga Index (RI) = the weight % of coke retained on 1 mm sieve. - **RI > 45** is required for good coking coal; > 70 for premium coking coal. **Test 3 — Gray-King Assay (IS 1350 Part IV):** - A coal sample is carbonized in a retort tube at 600 °C. - The residue coke is compared with a series of standard coke buttons labeled A, B, C, D, E, F, G, G₂, G₄, G₅, G₆, G₈ — each corresponding to increasing coking power. - A → non-caking; G₈ → highly coking. - Useful for **blending and component identification** in coke-making. **Test 4 — Gieseler Plastometer Test (ASTM D2639):** - 5 g of coal is heated under constant torque in a retort. - The angular velocity (in dial divisions per minute, ddpm) is plotted against temperature. - Three characteristic temperatures are noted: - **Softening temperature (Ts)** — coal becomes plastic. - **Maximum fluidity temperature (Tf)** — peak plasticity. - **Resolidification temperature (Tr)** — solid again. - **Plastic range (Tr − Ts)** should be wide (~80–100 °C) for good coking. - **Maximum fluidity** between 100–1000 ddpm is ideal. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** Mass of coal = 1.5 g, ΔT = 3.1 °C, Water equivalent (W) = 2300 cal/°C, Fuse correction = 50 cal. **Proximate analysis:** M = 4%, VM = 32%, A = 18%, FC = 46% (additional info, not needed for GCV calculation directly). **Step 1 — Total heat released (Q):** $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= (W + m) \times \Delta T + \text{fuse correction} \\ &= (2300 + 1.5) \times 3.1 + 50 \\ &= 2301.5 \times 3.1 + 50 \\ &= 7134.65 + 50 \\ &= 7184.65 \; \text{cal} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — GCV in cal/g:** $$GCV = \dfrac{Q}{m} = \dfrac{7184.65}{1.5} = 4789.77 \; \text{cal/g}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to kcal/kg** (multiply cal/g by 1000): $$GCV = 4789.77 \times 1000 / 1000 = 4789.77 \; \text{kcal/kg}$$ > **Final Answer:** GCV ≈ **4790 kcal/kg**. **Grade (IS 770):** 4790 kcal/kg falls in the range **4200–4940 kcal/kg** → **Grade D** (used for small industry). --- ### 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 One-Page Rapid Revision — Properties of Solid Fuels** - [x] **Solid fuels** = coal, coke, charcoal, biomass, peat, lignite. - [x] **Proximate analysis** → 4 constituents: M, VM, A, FC. FC = 100 − (M + VM + A). - [x] **Ultimate analysis** → 5 elements: C, H, N, S, O (O = 100 − sum of others). - [x] **Proximate temperatures**: 105–110 °C (moisture), 900 °C (VM), 815 °C (ash). - [x] **GCV** measured in bomb calorimeter; **NCV** calculated. - [x] **NCV relation** (IS 1350): $NCV = GCV - 9H/100 \times 587 - M/100 \times 587$ (or 2450 kJ/kg in SI). - [x] **Dulong's formula**: $GCV = \frac{1}{100}[8080C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240S]$ in kcal/kg. - [x] **Coal rank order (increasing)**: Peat → Lignite → Bituminous → Anthracite. - [x] **In rank, carbon ↑, moisture ↓, VM ↓, GCV ↑**. - [x] **IS 770 grade A** = GCV > 6200 kcal/kg (premium), **Grade G** = 1300–2400 kcal/kg. - [x] **Caking** = softening on heating. **Coking** = hard coke formation. - [x] **Coke tests**: Roga Index (RI), Swelling Index (SI), Gray-King (A to G₈), Gieseler Plastometer. - [x] **Good coking coal**: VM 18–35%, SI 2½–8, RI > 45, P < 0.05%, S < 0.7%. - [x] **High VM fuel** = easy ignition, long flame, good for gasification/domestic. - [x] **High ash** reduces GCV, causes slagging, increases handling cost. - [x] **High moisture** reduces NCV (587 kcal/kg penalty per kg water evaporated). - [x] **Sulfur penalty** — 2240 kcal/kg of S — but environment cost is much higher. - [x] **Coal formation**: peat → lignite → bituminous → anthracite (carbonification under time, heat, pressure). - [x] **In SI units**, multiply Dulong's constants by 4.184 to get kJ/kg. - [x] **Always state the basis** of analysis (ar, ad, d, daf) — 1 KTU mark. - [x] **Bomb calorimeter formula**: $GCV = (W + m) \Delta T / m + \text{corrections}$. > **Mnemonics:** > - **"MoVa As Fi"** — Moisture, Volatile, Ash, Fixed Carbon (Proximate order). > - **"CHONAS"** — Carbon, Hydrogen, Oxygen, Nitrogen, Ash, Sulfur (Ultimate + Ash). > - **"Coal = Coke + Crude Carbon"** — VC is the "young" carbon, FC is the "mature" carbon.

topics.title: Origin and Classification of Petroleum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Origin and Classification of Petroleum # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Petroleum? **Petroleum** (from Latin *petra* = rock + *oleum* = oil) is a naturally occurring, flammable, complex liquid mixture of hydrocarbons found in sedimentary rock formations beneath the Earth's surface. It exists in liquid (crude oil), gaseous (natural gas), and solid (bitumen/asphalt) phases, and is the principal source of liquid hydrocarbons used as transportation fuels, lubricants, and petrochemical feedstocks. From a **KTU 2024 Scheme** perspective, petroleum is the master hydrocarbon feedstock from which **gasoline, diesel, kerosene, LPG, naphtha, lubricating oil, bitumen, and petrochemicals** are derived via refining operations (distillation, cracking, reforming, alkylation). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard)** > "Petroleum is a naturally occurring mixture of hydrocarbons (paraffinic, naphthenic, and aromatic) with minor amounts of sulfur, nitrogen, oxygen, and metallic constituents (Ni, V, Fe), formed by the anaerobic decomposition of buried organic matter (chiefly marine plankton and algae) over geological timescales under elevated temperature and pressure." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Underground Pressure Cooker" Imagine you are preparing a meal. You take organic vegetables and meat (the *source material*), place them inside a sealed metal pot (the *sedimentary basin*), turn on slow heat (geothermal gradient of **~25–30 °C per km** of depth), and walk away for **millions of years**. The heat, absence of oxygen, and immense pressure slowly "cook" the organic matter into a thick black liquid that oozes through cracks and pools in underground rock chambers. That is exactly how petroleum forms: - **Source material (S)**: Dead marine microorganisms deposited on ancient sea beds. - **Pressure cooker (P)**: Layers of mud and silt (sediments) that bury the organic matter deeper and deeper. - **Heat (T)**: Geothermal gradient plus Earth's internal heat. - **Time (t)**: 1 million to 350 million years of maturation. - **Storage tank (R)**: Porous reservoir rock (sandstone/limestone) capped by impermeable rock (shale) that traps the oil. > [!NOTE] > **Key Real-World Metric** > The **geothermal gradient** of the Earth's crust is on the order of **25–30 °C/km**. Combined with overburden pressure, it provides the thermal energy for **kerogen → bitumen → crude oil → natural gas** transformations in the so-called **"oil window"** (typically **60–150 °C**, depth ~2–4 km). ## 1.3 Two Competing Theories of Origin | Theory | Proponent | Core Idea | KTU Status | |---|---|---|---| | **Organic (Biogenic) Theory** | Engler, Hofer (1888) | Petroleum is derived from buried organic matter (marine/terrestrial) | **Most widely accepted** — supported by biomarkers, optical activity, C-13/C-12 ratios | | **Inorganic (Abiogenic) Theory** | Mendeleev (1877), Kudryavtsev | Petroleum forms from deep-Earth carbides reacting with water | Minority view — explains some deep gas, abiogenic methane | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Highlight:** The Organic Theory dominates modern petroleum geochemistry. The presence of **optical activity** (chiral porphyrins), **pristane/phytane** biomarkers, and **vanadium/nickel porphyrin complexes** are the strongest geochemical evidence that crude oil is biogenic in origin. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Organic (Biogenic) Origin — Step-by-Step Geological Mechanism The formation of petroleum is a continuous, multi-stage geological process: 1. **Production of Organic Matter (Biogenesis)** - Photosynthesis in marine plankton, algae, and terrestrial plants fixes atmospheric CO₂ into organic tissue. - Organic carbon accumulates in fine-grained, low-energy depositional environments (deep marine shelves, lagoons, anoxic basins). 2. **Deposition and Burial** - Dead organic matter (sapropel) settles with fine clays and silts at the sea floor. - Anaerobic conditions prevent complete decay. The organic-rich sediment becomes a **source rock** (typically shale or marl). 3. **Diagenesis (Early Transformation, T < 60 °C)** - Microbial action, low temperature, and shallow depth convert the organic sludge into **kerogen** — a waxy, insoluble solid macromolecule. - The kerogen retains a memory of its biological precursors. 4. **Catagenesis (Oil Window, T ≈ 60–150 °C)** - As burial continues, **thermocatalytic cracking** breaks the kerogen into: - **Bitumen** (heavy, viscous, high-molecular-weight liquid). - **Liquid petroleum** (crude oil, the target product). - **Wet gas** (C₂–C₅ hydrocarbons). - This is the **"oil window."** 5. **Metagenesis (Gas Window, T > 150 °C)** - At greater depths and higher temperatures, all residual liquid kerogen cracks to **dry gas** (methane, CH₄). 6. **Migration, Accumulation, and Entrapment** - Because oil and gas are less dense than formation water, they **migrate upward** through permeable strata (primary migration out of source rock; secondary migration through carrier beds). - They accumulate in **reservoir rocks** (porous sandstone, fractured limestone, dolomite) sealed by **cap rocks** (impermeable shale, salt, or tight carbonate) — forming a **petroleum trap** (anticlinal, fault, stratigraphic, or combination). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Understanding the origin of petroleum is critical in **petroleum exploration**. Geochemists use the **van Krevelen diagram** (H/C vs. O/C atomic ratios) and **Rock-Eval pyrolysis** to determine if a source rock has reached the oil window, guiding the location of exploration wells. ## 2.2 Inorganic (Abiogenic) Theory — Mendeleev's Carbide Hypothesis According to Mendeleev (1877), deep within the Earth, molten metal **carbides** (chiefly iron carbide, FeC₂) react with descending **water** at very high temperatures to form **acetylene (C₂H₂)**, which subsequently polymerizes under pressure to heavier hydrocarbons. $$\text{FeC}_{2} + 2\,\text{H}_{2}\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Fe}(\text{OH})_{2} + \text{C}_{2}\text{H}_{2}$$ $$n\,\text{C}_{2}\text{H}_{2} \;\longrightarrow\; (\text{CH}_{2})_{2n} \quad \text{(polymerization)}$$ > [!WARNING] > The carbide theory is rarely invoked in modern KTU exams except as a 2- or 3-mark descriptive question. Emphasize the biogenic theory for any "explain origin" question worth ≥ 5 marks. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Property | Expression | Units | Notes / Classification Rule | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **API Gravity** | $\;^{\circ}\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5\;$ | dimensionless | $SG$ = specific gravity at **15.6 °C (60 °F)** with reference to water | | 2 | Specific Gravity from API | $\;SG = \dfrac{141.5}{^{\circ}\text{API}+131.5}\;$ | dimensionless | Inverse of the above | | 3 | **Watson / UOP Characterization Factor (K)** | $\;K = \dfrac{T_{B}^{1/3}}{SG}\;$ | dimensionless | $T_B$ = **mean average boiling point in °R (Rankine)** | | 4 | Viscosity Index (VI) | Empirical chart-based | dimensionless | Higher VI = lower rate of viscosity change with T | | 5 | Sulphur Content (Sweet/Sour) | $\;\text{S wt\%} = \dfrac{\text{mass of S}}{\text{mass of crude}} \times 100\;$ | wt % | Sweet < 0.5 %, Sour ≥ 0.5 % | | 6 | Density conversion | $\;\rho\,[\text{kg/m}^3] = SG \times 999\;\text{(approx)}\;$ | kg/m³ | At 15.6 °C, water ρ ≈ 999 kg/m³ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Classification Cut-offs (Memorize for KTU):** > - **API Gravity:** Light > 31.1 ; Medium 22.3–31.1 ; Heavy 10–22.3 ; Extra Heavy < 10 > - **Sulphur:** Sweet < 0.5 wt % ; Sour ≥ 0.5 wt % > - **UOP K-factor:** Paraffinic K > 12 ; Naphthenic 11–12 ; Aromatic K < 11 > - **Pour Point:** High-wax crudes have high pour points; low-wax crudes flow easily in cold climates ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Classification of Petroleum Petroleum is classified along several independent axes: ### (a) Based on API Gravity (Density) | Class | °API Range | Characteristics | Example | |---|---|---|---| | Light Crude | $> 31.1$ | High gasoline yield, low S, high paraffin | Brent, WTI | | Medium Crude | $22.3 - 31.1$ | Balanced yield, moderate S | Arabian Medium | | Heavy Crude | $10 - 22.3$ | High asphalt, high S, low paraffin | Maya (Mexico), Cold Lake | | Extra Heavy | $< 10$ | Bitumen-like, requires upgrading | Venezuelan Orinoco | ### (b) Based on Sulphur Content - **Sweet Crude:** S < 0.5 wt % — preferred, less refining needed. - **Sour Crude:** S ≥ 0.5 wt % — requires desulfurization, more corrosion. ### (c) Based on Chemical (Hydrocarbon) Composition 1. **Paraffinic (Alkaline) Base** — High normal + iso-paraffin; low naphthene; high wax; high cetane number diesel. 2. **Naphthenic (Asphaltic) Base** — High cycloparaffins; high asphalt; good lubricant base stock; high aromatic. 3. **Aromatic Base** — High BTEX; high solvency; used in petrochemicals. 4. **Mixed Base** — Combinations of the above; most common commercial crudes. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Use of Composition:** Paraffinic crudes yield more **kerosene & diesel**, naphthenic yield superior **lubricants**, and aromatic crudes are preferred **petrochemical feedstocks** for benzene, toluene, and xylene (BTX) manufacture. ### (d) Based on Wax, Pour Point, Viscosity - **High-wax crudes** (e.g., North Sea, Urals) solidify easily in cold flowlines. - **Low-wax crudes** (e.g., Nigeria's Bonny Light) flow readily in subsea conditions. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Derivation — API Gravity and Density Interconversion The **American Petroleum Institute (API)** introduced a gravity scale such that water has an API gravity of approximately 10 °API and lighter oils register higher values. The defining equation is: $$^{\circ}\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$$ ### Derivation Steps **Step 1 — Define specific gravity:** Specific gravity $SG$ is the ratio of the density of the oil sample to the density of pure water, both measured at **15.6 °C (60 °F)** and standard atmospheric pressure. $$SG = \frac{\rho_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{oil}}}{999\,\text{kg/m}^3}$$ **Step 2 — Invert the relationship to express density:** $$\rho_{\text{oil}} = SG \times 999\;\text{kg/m}^3$$ **Step 3 — Apply the API definition directly:** The constant **141.5** comes from calibrating the scale so that water ($SG = 1.0$) gives 10 °API, while a hypothetical oil of $SG = 0.8$ gives approximately 45 °API. The constant 131.5 is the value $141.5/1 - 10$. **Step 4 — Solve for SG in terms of °API (for board exams):** $$^{\circ}\text{API} + 131.5 = \frac{141.5}{SG}$$ $$SG = \frac{141.5}{^{\circ}\text{API} + 131.5}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Tip:** When you invert the API formula in an exam, write out the rearrangement step-by-step. Most students lose 1 mark by skipping the algebraic isolation step. ### Example Calculation (KTU-Style Numerical) **Problem:** A crude oil sample has a density of **875 kg/m³** at 15.6 °C. Calculate its API gravity and classify it. **Solution:** $$SG = \frac{875}{999} = 0.876$$ $$^{\circ}\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{0.876} - 131.5$$ $$^{\circ}\text{API} = 161.53 - 131.5 = 30.03 \;^{\circ}\text{API}$$ **Classification:** Since 30.03 lies between 22.3 and 31.1, this is a **medium crude oil**. ## 3.2 Worked Derivation — UOP Characterization Factor (K) The **Watson / UOP K-factor** is widely used to classify the paraffinic/naphthenic/aromatic nature of a crude oil or petroleum fraction. The relationship is: $$K = \frac{(T_B)^{1/3}}{SG}$$ Where $T_B$ is the **mean average boiling point** in **degrees Rankine (°R)** and $SG$ is the specific gravity at 15.6 °C. ### Derivation of the Boiling-Point-to-Rankine Conversion By definition: $$T\,[^{\circ}\text{R}] = T\,[^{\circ}\text{F}] + 459.67$$ To convert Celsius to Rankine: $$T\,[^{\circ}\text{R}] = \left(\frac{9}{5} \times T\,[^{\circ}\text{C}] + 32\right) + 459.67$$ ### Example Calculation **Problem:** A petroleum fraction has a mean average boiling point of **400 °C** and a specific gravity of **0.850**. Calculate the UOP K-factor and classify the crude. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert boiling point to Rankine:** $$T\,[^{\circ}\text{F}] = \frac{9}{5} \times 400 + 32 = 720 + 32 = 752\;^{\circ}\text{F}$$ $$T_B = 752 + 459.67 = 1211.67\;^{\circ}\text{R}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $(T_B)^{1/3}$:** $$(1211.67)^{1/3} = 10.65$$ **Step 3 — Compute K:** $$K = \frac{10.65}{0.850} = 12.53$$ **Step 4 — Classification:** Since $K = 12.53 > 12$, the crude is **Paraffinic (Alkaline) base** — yields good diesel and lube base stock. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Reference of Petroleum Properties | Property | Paraffinic | Naphthenic | Aromatic | KTU Board Implication | |---|---|---|---|---| | Paraffin content | High | Low | Very Low | Affects wax crystallization | | Naphthene content | Low | High | Moderate | Affects diesel CN & smoke point | | Aromatic content | Low | Moderate | High | Affects octane number, BTX yield | | Wax content | High | Low | Very Low | Determines pour point | | Asphalt content | Low | High | Variable | Determines residue yield | | Sulphur | Variable | Variable | Variable | Determines refining cost | | API Gravity | Usually high | Medium | Low–medium | Trade economics | | Cetane number | High | Medium | Low | Diesel quality | | Octane number | Low | Medium | High | Gasoline quality | > [!NOTE] > **Board Tip:** Many students confuse "paraffin content" with "API gravity." Remember: paraffinic crudes generally have **higher API** (lighter), but a high-wax crude is **not always light** — the wax simply makes it more paraffinic in chemical nature. ## 3.4 Classification of Source Rock Maturity (Van Krevelen Concept) The maturity of a source rock is tracked by following the **H/C** and **O/C** atomic ratios of kerogen as burial progresses: | Kerogen Type | Origin | H/C Ratio | O/C Ratio | Hydrocarbon Yield | |---|---|---|---|---| | Type I (Sapropelic) | Algal, lacustrine | Very high | Low | Excellent oil | | Type II (Mixed) | Marine plankton | High | Moderate | Good oil + gas | | Type III (Humic) | Terrestrial plants | Low | High | Mainly gas | | Type IV (Inert) | Highly oxidized, recycled | Very low | High | None | **KTU Insight:** The **H/C vs. O/C cross-plot** (Van Krevelen diagram) is a high-yield exam topic. Highlight that as maturation proceeds, both H/C and O/C **decrease** along evolutionary paths for each kerogen type. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Geological Process of Petroleum Formation ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Organic Matter
Marine Plankton and Algae] --> B[Deposition in
Anoxic Basin] B --> C[Burial under
Sediment Layers] C --> D[Diagenesis T less than 60 C
Kerogen Formation] D --> E[Catagenesis 60 to 150 C
Oil Window] E --> F[Metagenesis above 150 C
Gas Window] E --> G[Bitumen and Crude Oil] F --> H[Dry Gas Methane] G --> I[Primary Migration
Out of Source Rock] I --> J[Secondary Migration
Through Carrier Beds] J --> K[Reservoir Rock
Sandstone or Limestone] K --> L[Cap Rock Seal
Impermeable Shale] L --> M[Petroleum Trap
Anticlinal Fault Stratigraphic] M --> N[Conventional
Petroleum Deposit] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Classification Tree of Crude Oil ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Crude Oil
Classification] --> P1[By API Gravity] P --> P2[By Sulphur Content] P --> P3[By Chemical Base] P --> P4[By Wax Content] P1 --> P1a[Light Crude
API above 31.1] P1 --> P1b[Medium Crude
API 22.3 to 31.1] P1 --> P1c[Heavy Crude
API 10 to 22.3] P1 --> P1d[Extra Heavy
API below 10] P2 --> P2a[Sweet Crude
S less than 0.5 percent] P2 --> P2b[Sour Crude
S greater than 0.5 percent] P3 --> P3a[Paraffinic Base
K greater than 12] P3 --> P3b[Naphthenic Base
K between 11 and 12] P3 --> P3c[Aromatic Base
K less than 11] P3 --> P3d[Mixed Base
Most Commercial Crudes] P4 --> P4a[High Wax Crude
North Sea Type] P4 --> P4b[Low Wax Crude
West African Type] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Petroleum Trap Geometry ```mermaid flowchart TD TR[Petroleum Trap
Block Diagram View] --> TR1[Cap Rock
Impermeable Shale] TR --> TR2[Reservoir Rock
Porous Sandstone] TR --> TR3[Source Rock
Organic Shale] TR --> TR4[Migration Pathway
Fracture and Fault] TR --> TR5[Hydrocarbon
Accumulation] TR5 --> TR5a[Free Gas Cap] TR5 --> TR5b[Crude Oil Leg] TR5 --> TR5c[Formation Water
Below] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Maturity Pathway on the Van Krevelen Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR K1[Type I Kerogen
Algal Sapropelic] --> M1[Maturation T rises] K2[Type II Kerogen
Marine Plankton] --> M1 K3[Type III Kerogen
Terrestrial Humic] --> M1 M1 --> O1[Oil Window
Liquid Hydrocarbons] O1 --> O2[Gas Window
Dry Methane] O2 --> O3[Graphite Stage
Residual Carbon] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords). All labels are uppercase alphanumeric, with no markdown bold tags inside double-quoted strings — this is to ensure compatibility with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Mermaid safeguard protocol. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **State the biogenic (organic) theory of petroleum formation. Mention the role of anaerobic conditions and the oil window.** **Model Answer (≈ 90 words):** According to the biogenic theory, petroleum originates from the buried remains of marine organisms (plankton, algae) deposited in fine-grained, anoxic sediments. Under anaerobic conditions, microbial decay is suppressed, allowing the organic matter to be preserved as **kerogen** in the source rock. As burial continues, the temperature rises into the range **60–150 °C** (the oil window), causing thermocatalytic cracking of kerogen into bitumen and crude oil. This **catagenesis** stage, lasting millions of years, is responsible for liquid petroleum generation. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **A crude oil has an API gravity of 28.5 °API. Calculate its specific gravity at 15.6 °C and classify it as light, medium, or heavy.** **Model Answer:** $$SG = \frac{141.5}{^{\circ}\text{API} + 131.5} = \frac{141.5}{28.5 + 131.5} = \frac{141.5}{160.0} = 0.8844$$ Since 22.3 ≤ 28.5 ≤ 31.1, the crude is classified as a **medium crude oil**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Set]** #### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain in detail the organic (biogenic) theory of origin of petroleum. With a neat diagram, describe the maturation stages from kerogen to crude oil. **Model Answer Outline:** 1. **Definition (1 mark):** Petroleum is a complex hydrocarbon mixture formed from buried organic matter under anaerobic conditions. 2. **Source material (1 mark):** Marine plankton, algae, and to a lesser extent terrestrial plant material; deposited in low-energy anoxic basins. 3. **Deposition and diagenesis (1.5 marks):** Burial under fine clays and silts; microbial action ceases; kerogen forms in source rock. 4. **Catagenesis — Oil window (2 marks):** Temperature 60–150 °C; thermocatalytic cracking of kerogen into bitumen and crude oil. Mention depth window ~2–4 km. 5. **Metagenesis — Gas window (1 mark):** Above 150 °C, residual kerogen cracks to methane (dry gas). 6. **Migration and entrapment (0.5 mark):** Primary migration out of source rock, secondary migration through carrier beds, accumulation in reservoir rock beneath cap rock (anticlinal/fault trap). **Diagram Required:** Mermaid block or hand-drawn flow showing Diagenesis → Catagenesis → Metagenesis → Migration → Trap. #### (b) **[7 Marks]** Classify petroleum based on (i) API gravity, (ii) sulphur content, and (iii) chemical (UOP K-factor) composition. Give suitable examples of crude types for each class. **Model Answer Outline:** **(i) API Gravity Classification (2 marks):** | Class | °API | |---|---| | Light | > 31.1 | | Medium | 22.3 – 31.1 | | Heavy | 10 – 22.3 | | Extra Heavy | < 10 | Examples: Brent (light), Arabian Medium (medium), Maya (heavy), Orinoco (extra heavy). **(ii) Sulphur Classification (1.5 marks):** Sweet < 0.5 wt %; Sour ≥ 0.5 wt %. Examples: WTI (sweet), Arab Heavy (sour). **(iii) UOP K-factor (Watson Factor) (3.5 marks):** $$K = \frac{(T_B\,[^{\circ}\text{R}])^{1/3}}{SG}$$ | Base | K Range | |---|---| | Paraffinic | > 12 | | Naphthenic | 11 – 12 | | Aromatic | < 11 | | Mixed | Intermediate | Examples: Pennsylvania crude (paraffinic, K ≈ 12.5); Baku crude (naphthenic, K ≈ 11.5); Smackover (aromatic, K ≈ 10.3). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative* **[KTU University Exam — Model Set]** #### (a) **[7 Marks]** Discuss Mendeleev's carbide theory of inorganic origin of petroleum. How does this theory differ from the modern biogenic theory? Give at least two pieces of experimental evidence supporting the biogenic theory. **Model Answer Outline:** 1. **Carbide theory statement (2 marks):** Mendeleev (1877) proposed that deep-Earth metal carbides (FeC₂) react with descending water to form acetylene, which polymerizes to heavier hydrocarbons: $\text{FeC}_{2} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_{2} + \text{C}_{2}\text{H}_{2}$ $n\,\text{C}_{2}\text{H}_{2} \rightarrow (\text{CH}_{2})_{2n}$ 2. **Differences from biogenic theory (2 marks):** - Carbide theory: inorganic, abiogenic, deep mantle origin. - Biogenic theory: organic, surface-origin, sedimentary basins. 3. **Evidence supporting biogenic theory (3 marks):** - **Optical activity:** Crude oils rotate polarized light due to chiral biomarkers (e.g., porphyrins) — possible only from biological sources. - **Biomarker presence:** Pristane, phytane, and porphyrin complexes found in crude are unambiguously biogenic. - **Carbon isotope ratio:** δ¹³C signature matches photosynthetic carbon fixation. - **Consistent geographic association:** Crude oil is found only in sedimentary basins, never in igneous/metamorphic rocks. #### (b) **[7 Marks]** A petroleum fraction has a mean average boiling point of 320 °C and specific gravity 0.870 at 15.6 °C. Compute (i) the UOP characterization factor K, and (ii) the API gravity. Hence classify the crude. **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Convert boiling point to °R:** $$T\,[^{\circ}\text{F}] = \frac{9}{5}\times 320 + 32 = 576 + 32 = 608\;^{\circ}\text{F}$$ $$T_B = 608 + 459.67 = 1067.67\;^{\circ}\text{R}$$ **Step 2 — Compute K:** $$K = \frac{(1067.67)^{1/3}}{0.870} = \frac{10.24}{0.870} = 11.77$$ **Step 3 — Compute API gravity:** $$^{\circ}\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{0.870} - 131.5 = 162.64 - 131.5 = 31.14\;^{\circ}\text{API}$$ **Step 4 — Classification:** - K = 11.77 lies in the range **11–12**, so the crude is **Naphthenic (Asphaltic) base**. - API = 31.14 places it just at the **light crude** boundary (≥ 31.1). **Conclusion:** A naphthenic-base light crude — well suited for **lubricating oil** manufacture and high-octane gasoline blending. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Areas (Exam Pitfalls):** > 1. **Forgetting the temperature unit in K-factor calculation** — Always use **°R (Rankine)**, never °C. Students lose 2 marks for using °C directly in $(T_B)^{1/3}$. > 2. **Confusing sweet/sour with paraffinic/naphthenic** — These are independent classification axes. Sweet/sour refers to S content; paraffinic/naphthenic refers to chemical composition. > 3. **Inverting the API formula incorrectly** — A common slip is writing $SG = 141.5 / (^{\circ}\text{API} - 131.5)$. The correct form is $141.5 / (^{\circ}\text{API} + 131.5)$. > 4. **Skipping diagrams in 7-mark origin questions** — KTU valuation keys deduct up to **1.5 marks** for missing maturation pathway diagrams. > 5. **Mixing up "oil window" and "gas window" temperatures** — Oil window: 60–150 °C; Gas window: > 150 °C. Memorize these exactly. > 6. **Not stating the geothermal gradient** in the formation theory — Examiners expect a numerical anchor (~25–30 °C/km). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Rapid-Revision Checklist - **Petroleum** is a complex biogenic hydrocarbon mixture formed in **sedimentary basins** over **millions of years**. - The **biogenic (organic) theory** is the **most accepted**; it relies on **anaerobic preservation** of marine organic matter. - **Mendeleev's carbide theory** is the **principal inorganic alternative** — minor role in modern KTU exams. - **Kerogen** is the insoluble intermediate; **catagenesis (60–150 °C)** converts it to crude oil; **metagenesis (> 150 °C)** produces dry gas. - A working petroleum accumulation requires a **source rock**, **reservoir rock**, **cap rock**, and a **trap**. - **API Gravity formula:** $^{\circ}\text{API} = 141.5/SG - 131.5$ — memorize and invert. - **UOP K-factor formula:** $K = (T_B)^{1/3} / SG$, with $T_B$ in **°R**. - **Classification cut-offs (must be memorized):** - API: Light > 31.1 ; Medium 22.3–31.1 ; Heavy 10–22.3 ; Extra Heavy < 10 - Sulphur: Sweet < 0.5 % ; Sour ≥ 0.5 % - K-factor: Paraffinic > 12 ; Naphthenic 11–12 ; Aromatic < 11 - **Paraffinic crudes** → high wax, good diesel cetane, high pour point. - **Naphthenic crudes** → high asphalt, good lubricant base stock. - **Aromatic crudes** → high BTX yield, used in petrochemicals. - **Vanadium and nickel porphyrins** are definitive biomarkers of biogenic origin. - **Geothermal gradient ≈ 25–30 °C/km** governs the oil and gas window depths. - **Sweet crude is preferred** for refining economics due to lower desulfurization cost. - **Mixed-base crudes** dominate global commercial production (e.g., Brent, WTI). - **Type I, II, III, IV kerogens** differ in H/C, O/C ratios and their hydrocarbon yield potential (Type I > II > III; Type IV inert).

topics.title: Crude Exploration
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Crude Exploration

# 🛢️ Crude Exploration — Module 2 Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Crude Exploration** (also termed *Petroleum Exploration* or *Hydrocarbon Exploration*) is the systematic, multi-disciplinary scientific and engineering process of searching for and locating subsurface accumulations of crude oil and natural gas within the Earth's crust. It integrates the principles of **Geology**, **Geophysics**, **Geochemistry**, and **Petroleum Engineering** to identify prospective sedimentary basins, characterize subsurface geological structures (traps), and confirm the presence of commercially viable hydrocarbon reservoirs through exploratory drilling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Crude exploration encompasses the entire upstream workflow — from basin reconnaissance, surface geological mapping, geophysical surveys (especially seismic), to exploratory (wildcat) drilling and well logging — leading to the discovery, delineation, and confirmation of a hydrocarbon accumulation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine Earth's crust as a giant **layered chocolate cake** sitting in a dark room.
- The **chocolate sponge** is **source rock** (where oil was born millions of years ago from dead organic matter cooked by Earth's heat).
- The **cream filling** is **reservoir rock** (porous sandstone/limestone that holds the oil like a sponge holds water).
- The **hard icing on top** is **cap rock / seal** (impervious shale that traps oil and prevents it from escaping).
- The **shape of the cake layers** (folded, domed, faulted) creates a **trap** that gathers oil into one spot.

**Exploration is the art of figuring out where the cream filling is hidden, without cutting the cake open randomly.** Geologists use surface clues; geophysicists "ping" the earth with sound waves (seismic surveys) to map the hidden layers; then a single narrow drill bit is used to confirm the find.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Terminology (KTU Standard):**
> - **Wildcat Well** — Exploratory well drilled in an unproven area (**high risk**, success rate ~10%).
> - **Appraisal Well** — Drilled after a discovery to define the **size and limits** of the reservoir.
> - **Development Well** — Drilled within a proven field to **produce** hydrocarbons commercially.
> - **Dry Hole** — A well that finds no commercial hydrocarbons.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Average depth of oil reservoirs | **1,000 – 6,000** | m |
| Source rock maturation temperature | **60 – 150** | °C |
| Oil window depth (typical) | **2,000 – 4,500** | m |
| API gravity (crude classification) | **10 – 50** | °API |
| Seismic P-wave velocity in sandstone | **3,000 – 4,500** | m/s |
| World average wildcat success rate | **~10 %** | % |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin of Petroleum — The Source Rock Theory

Crude oil and natural gas are **fossil fuels** formed from the anaerobic decomposition of organic matter (marine plankton, algae, terrestrial plant debris) buried under sedimentary layers millions of years ago.

**The Four Conditions for a Commercial Hydrocarbon Accumulation (the "Petroleum System"):**

1. **Source Rock** — Fine-grained, organic-rich shale or carbonate with Total Organic Carbon (TOC) > **0.5 wt%**.
2. **Reservoir Rock** — Porous and permeable rock (sandstone, limestone, dolomite) capable of storing and yielding hydrocarbons. Typical porosity: **10–30 %**, permeability: **> 1 mD**.
3. **Cap Rock (Seal)** — Impermeable stratum (shale, salt, anhydrite) preventing hydrocarbon migration to the surface.
4. **Trap** — Geometric configuration (structural or stratigraphic) that concentrates hydrocarbons into an accumulation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Migration:** Hydrocarbons are expelled from source rock (primary migration), then move upward through permeable carrier beds until trapped (secondary migration).

### 2.2 Stages of Crude Exploration — Sequential Workflow

The KTU 2024 scheme recognizes **four sequential phases** in exploration:

#### Phase 1 — Basin Reconnaissance (Regional Studies)
- **Objective:** Identify sedimentary basins with hydrocarbon potential.
- **Methods:** Satellite imagery, aerial photography, regional gravity-magnetic surveys, surface geological mapping.
- **Outcome:** Delineation of basin boundaries and sedimentary thickness.

#### Phase 2 — Detailed Geological & Geochemical Studies
- **Surface mapping** of rock outcrops to infer subsurface structure.
- **Geochemical analysis** of source rock samples (TOC, Rock-Eval pyrolysis, vitrinite reflectance $\text{R}_o$).
- **Maturation window** assessment:
   - $R_o < 0.5\%$ → Immature
   - $R_o = 0.5\text{–}0.7\%$ → Oil window onset
   - $R_o = 0.7\text{–}1.0\%$ → Peak oil generation
   - $R_o = 1.0\text{–}1.4\%$ → Wet gas / condensate
   - $R_o = 1.4\text{–}2.0\%$ → Dry gas
   - $R_o > 2.0\%$ → Over-mature (metagenesis)

#### Phase 3 — Geophysical Surveys (Seismic Dominant)
The dominant exploration technique — uses acoustic waves to image subsurface structures.

**Types of Seismic Surveys:**
- **2D Seismic:** Single lines, regional reconnaissance.
- **3D Seismic:** Dense grid, detailed reservoir imaging.
- **4D Seismic (Time-lapse):** Repeated 3D surveys to monitor reservoir changes during production.

**Other Geophysical Methods:**
- **Gravity Survey:** Measures variations in Earth's gravitational field to detect dense basement rocks vs. low-density sedimentary fill.
- **Magnetic Survey:** Detects magnetic anomalies from igneous/volcanic intrusions.
- **Magnetotelluric (MT):** Measures electrical resistivity to identify hydrocarbon-bearing formations.

#### Phase 4 — Exploratory Drilling & Well Logging
- **Wildcat drilling** based on best geological-geophysical prospect.
- **Well logging** using tools lowered into the borehole to measure:
   - Gamma ray (shale content)
   - Resistivity (hydrocarbon vs. water saturation)
   - Sonic/Density/Neutron porosity
   - Spontaneous potential (SP)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **high-yield** for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) and must be memorized with units.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = \dfrac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_o}$ | **Volumetric Oil Originally In Place (OOIP)** — STB | STB |
| 2 | $G = \dfrac{43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_g}$ | **Original Gas In Place (OGIP)** — scf | scf |
| 3 | $S_o = 1 - S_w$ | **Oil saturation** from water saturation | fraction |
| 4 | $h_{net} = h \cdot (1 - V_{shale}) \cdot (1 - \phi_{cutoff})$ | **Net pay thickness** | m |
| 5 | $V = A \cdot h \cdot \phi$ | **Bulk rock volume** (porosity × volume) | m³ |
| 6 | $\text{STOOIP} = \dfrac{N \cdot R_F}{100}$ | **Surface to subsurface OOIP** (recovery factor $\text{R}_F$) | STB |
| 7 | $\text{Recovery} = \text{OOIP} \times \text{RF}$ | **Recoverable reserves** | STB |
| 8 | $v = \dfrac{f \cdot \lambda}{2}$ | **Seismic vertical resolution** (Rayleigh criterion) | m |
| 9 | $R_o$ | **Vitrinite reflectance** — maturation indicator | % |
| 10 | $\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{\rho_{oil}} - 131.5$ | **API gravity** from oil density (water = 1) | °API |

**Where:**
- $A$ = Area of reservoir (acres for $N$; acres for $G$ formula as given)
- $h$ = Net pay thickness (ft)
- $\phi$ = Porosity (fraction)
- $S_w$ = Water saturation (fraction)
- $B_o$ = Oil formation volume factor (rbbl/STB)
- $B_g$ = Gas formation volume factor (rcf/scf)
- $V_{shale}$ = Shale volume fraction
- $R_F$ = Recovery factor (typically **0.10 – 0.40** for primary recovery)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Crude exploration techniques are the foundation of the **upstream oil & gas industry**. Modern 3D/4D seismic surveys reduce dry-hole risk from **>50%** (pre-1970s) to **<20%** today. These methods are also adapted for **carbon capture & storage (CCS)**, **geothermal energy exploration**, and **groundwater resource mapping** — making petroleum geoscience a cross-disciplinary engineering asset.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Volumetric Oil Originally In Place (OOIP) Formula

This is the **single most important formula** for KTU Fuel Engineering. Let us derive it step-by-step from first principles.

**Starting Point — Definition:**
OOIP is the total volume of crude oil contained in a reservoir at initial reservoir conditions, expressed at surface (stock-tank) conditions.

**Step 1: Bulk Rock Volume of the Reservoir**

The reservoir is idealized as a geometric body with areal extent $A$ and average net pay thickness $h$.

$$
V_{bulk} = A \cdot h
$$

This is the total rock volume (including both pore space and rock matrix).

**Step 2: Pore Volume (Pore Space)**

Only the interconnected pore space stores fluids. Porosity $\phi$ is the fraction of bulk volume that is pore space.

$$
V_{pore} = V_{bulk} \cdot \phi = A \cdot h \cdot \phi
$$

**Step 3: Hydrocarbon Pore Volume (HCPV)**

Not all pore space contains oil — some is occupied by connate (formation) water with saturation $S_w$. Oil saturation $S_o = 1 - S_w$.

$$
V_{HC} = V_{pore} \cdot (1 - S_w) = A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)
$$

**Step 4: Convert Reservoir Conditions to Surface Conditions**

Oil shrinks when brought from reservoir pressure/temperature to surface conditions due to dissolved gas evolution. The **Oil Formation Volume Factor** $B_o$ quantifies this shrinkage:

$$
B_o = \dfrac{V_{reservoir\ oil}}{V_{stock\ tank\ oil}} \quad \text{(typically 1.2 – 1.6 rbbl/STB)}
$$

Therefore, oil volume at stock-tank conditions:

$$
N = \dfrac{V_{HC}}{B_o} = \dfrac{A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_o}
$$

**Step 5: Apply Unit Conversion Constant**

When $A$ is in acres and $h$ is in feet, the bulk volume $A \cdot h$ is in acre-ft. Converting acre-ft → barrels requires the constant **7758** (since $1 \text{ acre-ft} = 7758 \text{ barrels}$).

$$
\boxed{\,N = \dfrac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_o}\,}
$$

Similarly for **Gas Initially In Place (GIIP)**:

$$
\boxed{\,G = \dfrac{43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_g}\,}
$$

The constant **43560** converts acre-ft → cubic feet ($1 \text{ acre-ft} = 43560 \text{ ft}^3$).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:**
A sandstone reservoir has the following properties:
- Area $A = 1{,}000$ acres
- Net pay thickness $h = 50$ ft
- Porosity $\phi = 0.20$
- Water saturation $S_w = 0.30$
- Oil formation volume factor $B_o = 1.30$ rbbl/STB
- Recovery factor $R_F = 30\%$

**Calculate:** (a) OOIP in STB, (b) Recoverable oil reserves.

#### Part (a) — OOIP Calculation

$$
N = \dfrac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_o}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
N = \dfrac{7758 \times 1000 \times 50 \times 0.20 \times (1 - 0.30)}{1.30}
$$

Compute numerator:

$$
7758 \times 1000 = 7{,}758{,}000
$$

$$
7{,}758{,}000 \times 50 = 387{,}900{,}000
$$

$$
387{,}900{,}000 \times 0.20 = 77{,}580{,}000
$$

$$
77{,}580{,}000 \times 0.70 = 54{,}306{,}000
$$

Divide by $B_o = 1.30$:

$$
N = \dfrac{54{,}306{,}000}{1.30} = 41{,}773{,}846 \text{ STB}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 41.77 \text{ MMSTB (million stock-tank barrels)}}
$$

#### Part (b) — Recoverable Reserves

$$
\text{Recoverable} = N \times R_F = 41{,}773{,}846 \times 0.30
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Recoverable Reserves} \approx 12{,}532{,}154 \text{ STB} \approx 12.53 \text{ MMSTB}}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Exploration Methods Comparison

| Method | Physical Principle | Data Measured | Typical Cost (USD/km²) | Depth Range (m) | Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gravity Survey | Density contrast | Bouguer anomaly (mGal) | ~**500 – 2,000** | 0 – 10,000 | Low (basement scale) |
| Magnetic Survey | Magnetic susceptibility | Total magnetic intensity (nT) | ~**400 – 1,500** | 0 – 8,000 | Low–Medium |
| 2D Seismic | Acoustic reflection | Two-way travel time (s) | ~**2,000 – 8,000** | 0 – 10,000 | Vertical ~10–30 m |
| 3D Seismic | Acoustic reflection | 3D volume (x,y,t) | ~**10,000 – 50,000** | 0 – 8,000 | Vertical ~5–15 m |
| Magnetotelluric | Electrical resistivity | Resistivity (Ω·m) | ~**3,000 – 12,000** | 0 – 50,000 | Medium |
| Well Logging | In-situ tool response | GR, Resistivity, $\phi$, $S_w$ | ~**100,000 – 500,000** per well | Borehole | Sub-meter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb:** Cost rises ~10× as you move from regional to detailed exploration. Wildcat drilling costs **USD 5–50 million** depending on depth and offshore vs. onshore.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Crude Exploration Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram presents the **end-to-end upstream exploration workflow** as a block-level functional architecture, mapping the interactions between geology, geophysics, and drilling phases.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Stage 0: Basin Reconnaissance] --> A1[Regional Gravity and Magnetic Survey]
    A1 --> A2[Surface Geological Mapping]
    A2 --> A3[Sedimentary Basin Identification]

    A3 --> B0[Stage 1: Detailed Geological Study]
    B0 --> B1[Geochemical Source Rock Analysis]
    B1 --> B2[TOC and Rock Eval Pyrolysis]
    B2 --> B3[Vitrinite Reflectance Ro Determination]
    B3 --> B4[Maturation Window Assessment]

    B4 --> C0[Stage 2: Geophysical Prospecting]
    C0 --> C1[2D Seismic Acquisition]
    C1 --> C2[Seismic Data Processing]
    C2 --> C3[3D Seismic Volume Generation]
    C3 --> C4[Seismic Interpretation and Mapping]
    C4 --> C5[Prospect Generation and Ranking]

    C5 --> D0[Stage 3: Exploratory Drilling]
    D0 --> D1[Wildcat Well Design]
    D1 --> D2[Rig Deployment Onshore or Offshore]
    D2 --> D3[Drilling and Mud Logging]
    D3 --> D4[Wireline Well Logging Suite]
    D4 --> D5[Hydrocarbon Indication Evaluation]

    D5 --> E1{Commercial Discovery?}
    E1 -- Yes --> F1[Appraisal Well Drilling]
    F1 --> G1[Reservoir Delineation]
    G1 --> H1[Reserves Estimation Volumetric]
    H1 --> I1[Development Plan and Production]

    E1 -- No --> J1[Dry Hole Plug and Abandon]
    J1 --> K1[Geological Reassessment]
    K1 --> C0
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Seismic Survey Acquisition & Processing Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Seismic Vibrator Source] --> S2[Acoustic Wave Generation]
    S2 --> S3[Wave Propagation Through Subsurface Layers]
    S3 --> S4[Reflection at Geological Interfaces]
    S4 --> S5[Geophone or Hydrophone Receiver Array]
    S5 --> S6[Analog to Digital Conversion]
    S6 --> S7[Seismic Data Processing]
    S7 --> S8[Filtering and Stacking]
    S8 --> S9[Migration Algorithm]
    S9 --> S10[Time to Depth Conversion]
    S10 --> S11[Structural and Stratigraphic Interpretation]
    S11 --> S12[Prospect Map and Drilling Location Selection]
```

### 4.3 Hydrocarbon Trap Configurations — Schematic Mapping

Petroleum traps are classified into two broad categories. The Mermaid block diagram below maps the major trap geometries and their functional relationship to hydrocarbon accumulation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Hydrocarbon Trap Classification]

    T0 --> T1[Structural Traps]
    T0 --> T2[Stratigraphic Traps]
    T0 --> T3[Combination Traps]

    T1 --> T1A[Anticline Trap]
    T1 --> T1B[Fault Trap]
    T1 --> T1C[Dome Trap]

    T2 --> T2A[Unconformity Trap]
    T2 --> T2B[Reef Buildup]
    T2 --> T2C[Lens or Pinchout Trap]

    T3 --> T3A[Faulted Anticline]
    T3 --> T3B[Truncation beneath Seal]

    T1A --> OIL[Oil and Gas Accumulation in Reservoir Rock below Cap Rock Seal]
    T1B --> OIL
    T1C --> OIL
    T2A --> OIL
    T2B --> OIL
    T2C --> OIL
    T3A --> OIL
    T3B --> OIL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Recall Tip:** Roughly **80%** of all known oil reserves worldwide are found in **structural traps** (anticlines and faults), with stratigraphic traps accounting for the remaining **20%**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define "Petroleum System" and list its four essential elements.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Petroleum System** is the natural system that includes all geological elements and processes required for a commercial hydrocarbon accumulation to occur. The four essential elements are:
1. **Source Rock** — organic-rich shale/carbonate (TOC > 0.5 wt%) where hydrocarbons are generated.
2. **Reservoir Rock** — porous, permeable rock (sandstone, limestone) that stores hydrocarbons.
3. **Cap Rock / Seal** — impermeable layer (shale, salt) preventing hydrocarbon escape.
4. **Trap** — structural or stratigraphic geometry concentrating hydrocarbons.

*(Award 0.5 mark per element + 1 mark for definition = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is a "wildcat well" and how does it differ from an "appraisal well"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **Wildcat Well** (3 marks) is an exploratory well drilled in an **unproven area** with no prior hydrocarbon production. It carries **high geological risk** (success rate ~10%) and is the first well to test a new prospect.
- An **Appraisal Well** is drilled **after a commercial discovery** to delineate the reservoir's extent, thickness, and productivity. Its objective is to **quantify reserves**, not to make a new discovery.

*(Award 1.5 marks each for clear differentiation)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE 2024 Scheme Part B questions in this module carry **14 marks**, typically split as **Part (a) — 7 marks** and **Part (b) — 7 marks**. Internal choice means the student picks **ONE** of the two alternatives below.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**
*(a)* Describe in detail the various **geophysical methods** used in crude oil exploration. Compare their applicability for onshore vs. offshore exploration. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* A sandstone reservoir has the following properties:
- Area = 800 acres
- Net pay thickness = 40 ft
- Porosity = 0.22
- Water saturation = 0.25
- Oil formation volume factor $B_o$ = 1.25 rbbl/STB
- Recovery factor = 35%

Calculate the **OOIP** and the **recoverable oil reserves**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Geophysical Methods (7 marks):**

**1. Gravity Survey (1.5 marks):**
Measures minute variations in Earth's gravitational field caused by lateral density contrasts. Dense basement rocks produce positive Bouguer anomalies; low-density sedimentary basins produce negative anomalies. Useful in regional basin reconnaissance.

**2. Magnetic Survey (1.5 marks):**
Detects magnetic anomalies from iron-rich igneous/volcanic rocks. Maps basement topography and igneous intrusions. Used in early-stage exploration.

**3. Seismic Survey (2.5 marks):**
The **dominant** geophysical method. Uses artificially generated acoustic waves (vibroseis trucks onshore; air-gun arrays offshore) that reflect off subsurface interfaces. Recorded by geophones (onshore) or hydrophones (offshore).
- **2D Seismic:** single lines — regional reconnaissance
- **3D Seismic:** dense grid — detailed prospect mapping
- **4D Seismic:** time-lapse 3D — reservoir monitoring during production

**Onshore vs. Offshore:**
- **Onshore:** Vibroseis trucks + geophone strings; cost ~USD 5,000–15,000 per km².
- **Offshore:** Air-gun arrays + hydrophone streamers towed behind vessels; cost ~USD 10,000–50,000 per km² but covers larger area faster with higher data quality.

**4. Magnetotelluric (MT) Survey (1.5 marks):**
Measures natural electromagnetic field variations to determine subsurface resistivity. Hydrocarbon-bearing formations show high resistivity contrast vs. water-bearing zones.

---

**Part (b) — OOIP Calculation (7 marks):**

Using the volumetric formula:

$$
N = \dfrac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_o}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
N = \dfrac{7758 \times 800 \times 40 \times 0.22 \times (1 - 0.25)}{1.25}
$$

Compute step-by-step:

$$
7758 \times 800 = 6{,}206{,}400
$$

$$
6{,}206{,}400 \times 40 = 248{,}256{,}000
$$

$$
248{,}256{,}000 \times 0.22 = 54{,}616{,}320
$$

$$
54{,}616{,}320 \times 0.75 = 40{,}962{,}240
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{40{,}962{,}240}{1.25} = 32{,}769{,}792 \text{ STB}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 32.77 \text{ MMSTB}}
$$

**Recoverable Reserves:**

$$
\text{Recoverable} = N \times R_F = 32{,}769{,}792 \times 0.35
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Recoverable Reserves} \approx 11{,}469{,}427 \text{ STB} \approx 11.47 \text{ MMSTB}}
$$

*(Award 1 mark for correct formula, 4 marks for substitution & arithmetic, 1 mark for final answer; reserves part: 1 mark.)*

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**
*(a)* Explain the **origin and migration of petroleum** with a neat labelled diagram of a typical structural trap. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* Differentiate between **2D, 3D, and 4D seismic surveys**. State the **Rayleigh criterion** for seismic resolution and compute the minimum resolvable thickness for a reservoir at 2,500 m depth with dominant frequency 30 Hz and average velocity 3,500 m/s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Origin, Migration & Structural Trap (7 marks):**

**Origin (2 marks):**
Petroleum originates from the **anaerobic decomposition of buried organic matter** (marine plankton, algae, terrestrial plant debris) in fine-grained sedimentary source rocks. Over geological time, increased temperature (60–150 °C) and pressure convert kerogen into liquid hydrocarbons through a process called **catagenesis**.

**Migration (2 marks):**
- **Primary migration:** Expulsion of hydrocarbons from the source rock into permeable carrier beds.
- **Secondary migration:** Upward movement of hydrocarbons through carrier beds (sandstone, fractured limestone) until encountering an impermeable seal.

**Structural Trap — Anticline (3 marks):**
A typical **anticlinal trap** consists of:
- **Source rock** (organic-rich shale) at depth
- **Reservoir rock** (porous sandstone) arched upward
- **Cap rock** (impermeable shale) sealing the top
- **Hydrocarbons** accumulating at the crest of the anticline, with **gas on top → oil below → water at bottom** (due to density stratification)

---

**Part (b) — Seismic Comparison & Resolution Calculation (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | 2D Seismic | 3D Seismic | 4D Seismic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acquisition | Single lines | Dense grid | Repeated 3D over time |
| Data type | Cross-section | 3D volume | Time-lapse 3D volume |
| Use | Regional reconnaissance | Prospect mapping | Reservoir monitoring |
| Cost (relative) | 1× | ~5–10× | ~10–20× |

**Rayleigh Criterion (2 marks):**
The minimum resolvable vertical thickness is:

$$
v = \dfrac{\lambda}{4} = \dfrac{f \cdot \lambda}{2 \cdot 2} = \dfrac{v_{avg}}{4 \cdot f}
$$

Where $v_{avg}$ is average seismic velocity and $f$ is dominant frequency.

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{v_{avg}}{f} = \dfrac{3500}{30} = 116.67 \text{ m}
$$

$$
v_{min} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4} = \dfrac{116.67}{4} = 29.17 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{min} \approx 29.2 \text{ m}}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistakes (–2 marks):** Forgetting that the volumetric formula requires $A$ in **acres** and $h$ in **feet**. The constant **7758** is for **acre-ft → barrels**, NOT m³ → barrels.
> 2. **Confusing STB with rbbl (–1 mark):** OOIP is reported at **surface (stock-tank)** conditions, divided by $B_o$. Reserves are computed by multiplying OOIP by Recovery Factor $R_F$.
> 3. **Skipping the diagram in structural trap questions (–3 marks):** KTU expects a **neat labelled diagram** showing source rock, reservoir rock, cap rock, and fluid contacts (gas-oil-water).
> 4. **Wrong Rayleigh formula (–1 mark):** Use $v = \lambda/4$, NOT $v = \lambda/2$. The factor 1/4 comes from tuning thickness for two equal-amplitude reflections.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Petroleum System** = Source Rock + Reservoir Rock + Cap Rock + Trap.
- ✅ **Exploration phases:** Basin Reconnaissance → Geological/Geochemical → Geophysical → Exploratory Drilling.
- ✅ **Vitrinite Reflectance $R_o$** indicates thermal maturity; oil window peaks at **0.7–1.0%**.
- ✅ **Wildcat success rate** ≈ **10%** globally; 3D seismic reduces dry-hole risk.
- ✅ **Volumetric OOIP formula:** $N = 7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w) / B_o$ — memorize constants **7758** (oil) and **43560** (gas).
- ✅ **Recovery factor** typically **10–40%** (primary); EOR can raise to **>50%**.
- ✅ **API gravity** classifies crude: Light (>31.1°), Medium (22.3–31.1°), Heavy (<22.3°).
- ✅ **Seismic resolution:** Vertical $\approx \lambda/4$; Horizontal $\approx$ Fresnel zone radius $\approx \sqrt{\lambda \cdot z/2}$.
- ✅ **Structural traps (anticline, fault, dome)** hold ~80% of world reserves; **stratigraphic traps** ~20%.
- ✅ **Hydrocarbon density stratification in trap:** Gas (top) → Oil (middle) → Water (bottom).
- ✅ **Magnetotelluric** method detects resistivity contrast — useful for deep reservoirs and gas hydrates.
- ✅ **Well logging tools:** Gamma Ray, Resistivity, Sonic, Density, Neutron, SP — used to compute porosity $\phi$, saturation $S_w$, lithology.
- ✅ **Onshore vs. offshore costs:** Offshore seismic typically **2–5×** more expensive but provides higher data density.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 — Crude Exploration Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, OEBBT832 Fuel Engineering, Module 2 outcomes. Practice the worked example and the Part B questions above to maximize ESE marks.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Petroleum Refining Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Petroleum Refining Processes # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Petroleum Refining** is the industrial process of converting **crude oil** (a complex, naturally occurring hydrocarbon mixture) into a spectrum of useful commercial products such as **LPG, gasoline (petrol), kerosene, diesel, fuel oil, lubricants, asphalt, and petrochemical feedstocks**. The refining sequence operates on the principles of **physical separation** (primarily fractional distillation based on boiling point differences) and **chemical conversion** (cracking, reforming, alkylation, isomerization, treating). According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OEBBT832)**, petroleum refining processes are categorized into three primary functional classes: 1. **Separation Processes** – Fractional Distillation (Atmospheric Distillation Unit – ADU, Vacuum Distillation Unit – VDU), Solvent Extraction, Dewaxing. 2. **Conversion Processes** – Cracking (Thermal, Catalytic, Hydrocracking), Reforming, Coking, Visbreaking. 3. **Finishing/Treating Processes** – Alkylation, Isomerization, Desulfurization (HDS), Sweetening (Merox), Hydrotreating. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition:** "Petroleum refining is the totality of physical, thermal, catalytic, and chemical operations applied to crude oil to separate it into fractions and reconfigure its molecular structure to maximize yield of marketable products." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Kitchen Analogy" Imagine crude oil as a **mixed vegetable soup** containing carrots, potatoes, peas, and beans all blended together. You cannot eat them in this mixed state efficiently — each vegetable cooks best at its own temperature. A petroleum refinery acts like a **master chef's kitchen**: - **Atmospheric Distillation Tower (ADU):** Acts like the **initial boiling pot** that separates ingredients at atmospheric pressure. Lighter "ingredients" (gases, naphtha) rise to the top, heavier "ingredients" (diesel, gas oil) settle at lower trays. - **Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU):** Functions like a **pressure cooker** — by reducing pressure, we can "boil" heavy residues at lower temperatures (preventing thermal cracking) and extract lubricating oil base stocks. - **Catalytic Cracker (FCCU):** This is the **"molecular blender"** that chops long, heavy hydrocarbon chains (C20+) into shorter, lighter gasoline-range chains (C5–C11) using a zeolite catalyst. - **Reformer:** Like a **"renovation expert"** — it rebuilds straight-chain naphthenes and paraffins into high-octane **aromatic rings** (toluene, xylene) that resist knocking in engines. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Value / Range | |---|---|---| | **API Gravity of Light Crude** | °API | **> 31°** | | **API Gravity of Heavy Crude** | °API | **< 22°** | | **Standard Distillation Pressure (ADU)** | P | **101.325 kPa (1 atm)** | | **VDU Operating Pressure** | P | **1–10 kPa (vacuum)** | | **FCCU Reactor Temperature** | T | **480–540 °C** | | **Reformer Temperature** | T | **450–525 °C** | | **Catalytic Cracker Pressure** | P | **1–3 atm (gauge)** | | **Universal Gas Constant** | R | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | > [!NOTE] > **API Gravity Formula:** $°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{60/60°F}} - 131.5$, where $SG$ is the **specific gravity** of the oil relative to water at **60 °F (15.6 °C)**. ## 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical for KTU Examiners Every module test and ESE under KTU 2024 Scheme tests the student's ability to: - Draw and label the **block flow diagram** of a refinery. - Compute **yield** and **mass balance** for the distillation column. - Distinguish between **thermal vs catalytic cracking** reaction chemistry. - Explain the **purpose** of each secondary conversion process. > [!TIP] > Examiners in KTU 2024 ESE have consistently awarded high marks to students who pair the **process description** with the **specific operating conditions (T, P, catalyst)** in tabular form. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Logical Architecture of a Modern Refinery A modern refinery processes crude oil through a structured hierarchy of unit operations. Below is the conceptual flow logic a student must master: ### Stage A — Primary Separation (Physical) 1. **Desalting** — Crude oil is washed with water to remove salts (NaCl, MgCl₂) which would otherwise corrode downstream equipment and poison catalysts. 2. **Atmospheric Distillation (ADU)** — Crude oil is preheated in a furnace (to ~350–400 °C) and flashed into a tall fractionating tower. Products drawn off (top → bottom): Off-gas → LPG → Naphtha → Kerosene → Diesel → Atmospheric Gas Oil (AGO) → Reduced Crude (Residue). 3. **Vacuum Distillation (VDU)** — The residue from ADU bottoms is redistilled under vacuum (1–10 kPa) to recover **Vacuum Gas Oil (VGO)** and **Lubricating Oil (LO) base stocks** without thermal cracking. ### Stage B — Secondary Conversion (Chemical) 4. **Catalytic Cracking (FCC — Fluid Catalytic Cracking)** — Heavy VGO is converted into gasoline, LPG, and light olefins via **zeolite (ZSM-5, USY)** catalyst at 480–540 °C. 5. **Hydrocracking** — A combination of hydrogenation + cracking using bifunctional catalysts (Ni-Mo, Pt-Pd on zeolite). Used for **sulfur-free jet fuel and diesel**. 6. **Catalytic Reforming** — Naphtha (C6–C11) is converted to **high-octane aromatics** using Pt-Re or Pt-Sn on chlorided alumina at 450–525 °C. 7. **Coking (Delayed Coking)** — Heavy residue is thermally cracked into **petroleum coke** + lighter distillates at ~500 °C in coke drums. 8. **Alkylation** — Isobutane + butenes → **isooctane** (alkylate) using H₂SO₄ or HF catalyst at low T (~10 °C). ### Stage C — Finishing & Treating 9. **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS)** — Sulfur compounds removed via H₂ + Co-Mo/Ni-Mo catalyst at 300–400 °C. 10. **Merox Sweetening** — Converts mercaptans (R–SH) to disulfides (R–S–S–R) using a catalyst impregnated with a chelating agent. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why two distillation columns?** The ADU operates at 1 atm; at this pressure, residue temperatures exceed 400 °C, causing **thermal cracking** which damages products.
