The VDU reduces pressure so residue boils at lower temperatures (<380 °C), preserving molecular integrity. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Range | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **API Gravity** | $°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ | dimensionless, 10–45 | Classifies crude as light/medium/heavy | | 2 | **Specific Gravity** | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water}}$ | at 60/60 °F | Refinery design basis | | 3 | **Watson Characterization Factor (Kw)** | $K_w = \dfrac{\sqrt[3]{T_{B,abs}}}{SG}$ | 10–15; paraffinic=12–13 | Predicts paraffinicity of crude | | 4 | **TBP → ASTM Conversion (ASTM D86 vs D2892)** | $T_{ASTM} \approx T_{TBP} - \Delta T$ | °C | Lab to plant correlation | | 5 | **Reflux Ratio (External)** | $R = \dfrac{L}{D}$ | dimensionless; typical 1.5–5 | Tower energy vs separation trade-off | | 6 | **Minimum Reflux Ratio** | $R_{min} = \dfrac{x_{D,F} - y_{F}}{y_{F} - x_{F,eq}}$ | dimensionless | Theoretical lower bound | | 7 | **Fenske Equation (Min Stages)** | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[\left(\dfrac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_D \cdot \left(\dfrac{x_{HK}}{x_{LK}}\right)_B\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$ | stages | Shortcut distillation design | | 8 | **Cracking Conversion** | $\% \text{Conv} = \dfrac{V_{feed} - V_{unconv}}{V_{feed}} \times 100$ | % | FCC performance metric | | 9 | **Reactor Space Velocity (LHSV)** | $LHSV = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{liq}}{V_{cat}}$ | h⁻¹ | Catalyst loading design | | 10 | **Reformer Aromatics Yield** | $Y_{aro} = \dfrac{m_{aro}}{m_{naphtha}}$ | mass fraction | Octane improvement metric | | 11 | **Octane Number** | $RON = 100 - \dfrac{1}{K}$ where $K = \dfrac{[ISO]}{[n-C_7]}$ | octane number | Fuel quality index | | 12 | **Sulfur Removal Efficiency** | $\% \text{HDS} = \dfrac{S_{feed} - S_{prod}}{S_{feed}} \times 100$ | % | Environmental compliance | > [!WARNING] > When converting **API gravity to density**, remember: > $\rho \left( \dfrac{g}{cm^3} \right) = \dfrac{141.5}{°API + 131.5}$ ## 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Equations - **Fenske Equation:** Used during the **conceptual design** stage of a new distillation column to estimate minimum number of trays before rigorous simulation (ASPEN, HYSYS). - **LHSV:** Directs refinery engineers on **catalyst change-out schedules**; if LHSV decreases, catalyst is deactivated. - **API Gravity:** Determines **crude oil pricing** globally. Light sweet crude (high API, low S) commands premium prices (Brent, WTI ~38–42°API). - **Watson Kw:** Used by petroleum geochemists to **predict product slate** without running expensive pilot plants. ## 2.4 Reaction Chemistry — Snapshot ### Catalytic Cracking (FCC) $$\text{C}_{16}\text{H}_{34} \xrightarrow{\text{Zeolite, 500°C}} \text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + \text{C}_8\text{H}_{16}$$ (Paraffin → Paraffin + Olefin) ### Catalytic Reforming $$\text{n-Heptane (C}_7\text{H}_{16}\text{)} \xrightarrow{\text{Pt-Re/Al}_2\text{O}_3, \text{H}_2, 500°C} \text{Toluene (C}_7\text{H}_8\text{)} + 4\text{H}_2$$ ### Hydrodesulfurization (HDS) $$\text{R–SH} + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Co-Mo}} \text{R–H} + \text{H}_2\text{S}$$ > [!TIP] > KTU examiners love asking: *"Write the reaction involved in catalytic reforming and state the catalyst used."* — The standard answer is **naphthene/aromatic dehydrogenation** on **Pt/Al₂O₃ with Cl⁻ promoter** at 450–525 °C. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Problem Context (KTU-Style Worked Example) **Problem Statement:** A refinery's Atmospheric Distillation Unit processes **100,000 kg/hr** of desalted crude oil (API = 32°, SG = 0.865). The yield distribution observed is as follows: | Stream | Mass Flow (kg/hr) | Distillate Boiling Range (°C) | |---|---|---| | LPG + Off-gas | 2,500 | < 30 | | Naphtha | 18,000 | 30–180 | | Kerosene | 14,000 | 180–240 | | Diesel | 22,500 | 240–360 | | Atmospheric Gas Oil (AGO) | 12,000 | 360–380 | | Reduced Crude (Bottoms) | 31,000 | > 380 | **Required:** 1. Verify mass balance closure (sum of outputs vs input). 2. Calculate the **API gravity** from the given specific gravity. 3. Determine the **mass yield** of gasoline + naphtha (% of crude). 4. Calculate the **reflux ratio** if the distillate (D) = 18,000 kg/hr and liquid reflux (L) = 36,000 kg/hr. 5. Determine the **ASTM 50% boiling point** if the TBP 50% is 320 °C and the empirical correction ΔT = 27 °C (between 200–400 °C TBP). --- ### 3.2 Mass Balance Closure The mass balance for any steady-state process must obey the **Law of Conservation of Mass**: $$\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$$ Step 1 — Sum the outputs: $$\sum \dot{m}_{out} = 2500 + 18000 + 14000 + 22500 + 12000 + 31000$$ $$\sum \dot{m}_{out} = 100{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}$$ Step 2 — Compare with input: $$\dot{m}_{in} = 100{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}$$ Step 3 — Compute closure: $$\text{Closure} = \dfrac{\sum \dot{m}_{out}}{\dot{m}_{in}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{100{,}000}{100{,}000} \times 100\% = 100.00\%$$ **Result:** The mass balance is **perfectly closed** (100%), validating the measurement instruments. KTU expects answers within ±0.5% for real-world data. --- ### 3.3 API Gravity Verification Given: $SG_{60/60°F} = 0.865$ $$°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$$ Substituting: $$°API = \dfrac{141.5}{0.865} - 131.5$$ $$°API = 163.58 - 131.5 = 32.08°$$ $$\boxed{°API \approx 32.1°}$$ **Interpretation:** This is a **light crude** (above 31°API), suitable for high gasoline yields — economically attractive. --- ### 3.4 Mass Yield of Naphtha (Gasoline Pool) $$\%\text{Yield}_{naphtha} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{naphtha}}{\dot{m}_{crude}} \times 100\%$$ $$= \dfrac{18{,}000}{100{,}000} \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{\%\text{Yield}_{naphtha} = 18.0\%}$$ For comparison, **North Sea Brent crude** typically yields 18–22% straight-run naphtha. --- ### 3.5 Reflux Ratio Calculation The **external reflux ratio** is defined as: $$R = \dfrac{L}{D}$$ Where: - $L$ = Liquid reflux flowrate (kg/hr) - $D$ = Distillate flowrate (kg/hr) Given: $L = 36{,}000$ kg/hr, $D = 18{,}000$ kg/hr $$R = \dfrac{36{,}000}{18{,}000} = 2.0$$ $$\boxed{R = 2.0}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** A reflux ratio of 2.0 indicates the tower uses **moderate reflux** — energy-efficient for the target separation. Typical industrial columns operate between $R = 1.2 R_{min}$ and $R = 1.5 R_{min}$. --- ### 3.6 ASTM 50% Boiling Point Conversion The empirical correlation between **TBP (True Boiling Point)** and **ASTM D86 distillation**: $$T_{ASTM,50\%} = T_{TBP,50\%} - \Delta T$$ Given: $T_{TBP,50\%} = 320 °C$, $\Delta T = 27 °C$ (per ASTM D86 standard charts for 200–400 °C range) $$T_{ASTM,50\%} = 320 - 27 = 293 °C$$ $$\boxed{T_{ASTM,50\%} \approx 293 °C}$$ --- ## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Cracking Processes | Property | Thermal Cracking | Catalytic Cracking (FCC) | Hydrocracking | |---|---|---|---| | **Catalyst** | None (homogeneous) | Zeolite (USY, ZSM-5) | Bifunctional (Ni-Mo/SiO₂-Al₂O₃) | | **Temperature (°C)** | 450–750 | 480–540 | 300–425 | | **Pressure (atm)** | 1–70 | 1–3 | 70–200 | | **H₂ Presence** | No | No | Yes (excess) | | **Feedstock** | Residue, VGO | VGO, gas oil | VGO, coker gas oil | | **Product Octane** | Low–Medium (70–80 RON) | High (88–94 RON) | High (85–92 RON) | | **Sulfur in Product** | High | Medium | **Very Low** (HDS coupled) | | **Coke Yield** | Medium | High (burned off regenerator) | Negligible | | **Capital Cost** | Low | High | Very High | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** When asked *"Why catalytic cracking is preferred over thermal cracking?"* — the standard model answer is **higher gasoline yield, better octane number, lower operating temperature, and ability to suppress coke via continuous catalyst regeneration.** ## 3.8 Octane Number Calculation (Derivation) The **Research Octane Number (RON)** is experimentally measured against a binary reference blend of **iso-octane (RON = 100)** and **n-heptane (RON = 0)**. $$\text{RON}_{blend} = (\text{vol \% iso-octane})$$ For a fuel sample producing the same knock intensity as a blend containing 92% iso-octane: $$\text{RON} = 92$$ The relationship to engine compression ratio is given by: $$RON_{required} \approx \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{r_{c}}\right)^n$$ Where: - $r_c$ = Compression ratio - $n$ = empirical exponent (~3 for modern SI engines) For $r_c = 10:1$: $$RON_{required} = \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{10}\right)^3 = (1.1)^3 = 1.331$$ Multiplied by 100 for index scale = 133 → corrected using empirically calibrated curves → realistic minimum RON ≈ **92** (matching premium gasoline). > [!TIP] > KTU frequently tests: *"A fuel has RON = 95. Compute equivalent n-heptane % in the reference blend."* — Answer: **n-heptane = 5%**, iso-octane = 95%. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Refinery Block Flow Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Crude Oil Storage
Tank Farm] --> A2[Desalter Unit
Water Wash 120C] A2 --> A3[Atmospheric Distillation Unit ADU
350-400C, 1 atm] A3 --> B1[LPG and Off-gas
C1-C4] A3 --> B2[Naphtha
C5-C11 30-180C] A3 --> B3[Kerosene
C9-C15 180-240C] A3 --> B4[Diesel
C12-C20 240-360C] A3 --> B5[Atmospheric Gas Oil AGO
360-380C] A3 --> B6[Reduced Crude Residue
Greater than 380C] B6 --> C1[Vacuum Distillation Unit VDU
1-10 kPa, 380C max] C1 --> D1[Vacuum Gas Oil VGO] C1 --> D2[Lubricating Oil Base Stock] C1 --> D3[Vacuum Residue] B2 --> E1[Hydrotreater
HDS for naphtha] B2 --> E2[Catalytic Reformer
Pt-Re 500C, H2] B5 --> F1[Hydrocracker
Ni-Mo 350C 100 atm] D1 --> F1 D3 --> F2[Delayed Coker
500C, coke drums] F2 --> G1[Petroleum Coke
Fuel grade] F2 --> G2[Coker Gas Oil] F2 --> G3[Coker Naphtha] E1 --> H1[Reformate
HIGH OCTANE 95 RON] E2 --> H1 B3 --> H2[Kerosene Pool JP-1] B4 --> H3[Diesel Pool ULSD] F1 --> H3 D2 --> H4[Lube Oil Plant
Solvent Extraction] H1 --> Z1[Gasoline Pool
Finished Petrol] H3 --> Z2[Finished Diesel HSD] H2 --> Z3[Finished Kerosene SKO] H4 --> Z4[Lubricants and Greases] G1 --> Z5[Industrial Fuel] ``` ## 4.2 Process Interaction Map — Secondary Conversion Units ```mermaid flowchart LR FEED[VGO Feed] --> FCC[FCCU
Fluidized Bed
480-540C] FCC --> P1[Wet Gas
C2-C4] FCC --> P2[Gasoline
C5-220C] FCC --> P3[LCO Light Cycle Oil] FCC --> P4[HCO Heavy Cycle Oil] FCC --> P5[Slurry Oil] FCC --> P6[Catalyst Regenerator
Burns coke 700C] P1 --> ALK[Alkylation Unit
H2SO4 or HF, 10C] P1 --> ISO[Isomerization Unit
Pt catalyst, 120C] ALK --> ALKY[Alkylate C8
RON 92-96] ISO --> ISOP[Isomerate
RON 85-90] P6 -.Spent Catalyst.-> P6A[Stripper] --> P6 P6 -.Regenerated Catalyst.-> FCC ALKY --> POOL[Premium Gasoline Pool] P2 --> POOL ISOP --> POOL ``` ## 4.3 Distillation Column Internal Schematic (Tray Detail) ```mermaid flowchart TB TOP[Top of Tower
Reflux Drum and Condenser] --> TRAY1[Tray 1 Naphtha Draw] TRAY1 --> TRAY2[Tray 5 Kerosene Draw] TRAY2 --> TRAY3[Tray 15 Diesel Draw] TRAY3 --> TRAY4[Tray 25 AGO Draw] TRAY4 --> TRAY5[Sump and Reboiler
Residue Withdrawal] TRAY1 -.Liquid Reflux.-> TOP TRAY5 -.Vapor Riser.-> TOP TOP --> COND[Overhead Condenser] COND --> REF[Reflux Pump] REF --> TOP TRAY5 --> REB[Reboiler Fired Heater] REB --> TRAY5 ``` ## 4.4 Process Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Goal?} Q1 -->|High Octane Gasoline| Q2{Feedstock Type?} Q2 -->|Light Naphtha| R1[Reforming] Q2 -->|Heavy VGO| R2[Cracking] Q1 -->|Low Sulfur Diesel| R3[Hydrodesulfurization HDS] Q1 -->|Lubricating Oils| R4[VDU plus Solvent Extraction] Q1 -->|Petrochemical Feedstock Olefins| R5[Steam Cracking] Q1 -->|Petroleum Coke| R6[Delayed Coking] Q2 -->|VGO for jet fuel| R7[Hydrocracking] R2 -->|Without H2| R8[FCC] R2 -->|With H2| R9[Hydrocracker] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Legend for KTU:** Students should remember to label **all streams with temperature ranges** (°C) and **operating pressure** (atm or kPa) when drawing these diagrams in the exam. Marks are deducted if only block names appear without process conditions. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **Q:** *Define petroleum refining. List any four primary conversion processes used in a refinery.* **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Model Answer:** Petroleum refining is the industrial process of converting **crude oil** into usable commercial products through a combination of **physical separation** (distillation) and **chemical conversion** (cracking, reforming). **Four primary conversion processes:** 1. **Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** — Converts heavy VGO into gasoline-range hydrocarbons using zeolite catalysts at 480–540 °C. 2. **Catalytic Reforming** — Converts low-octane naphtha into high-octane aromatics using Pt-Re/Al₂O₃ catalyst at 450–525 °C in H₂ atmosphere. 3. **Hydrocracking** — Hydrogen-assisted cracking of heavy feeds to produce low-sulfur middle distillates. 4. **Delayed Coking** — Thermal cracking of residues at ~500 °C to yield petroleum coke and lighter distillates. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **Q:** *Explain the difference between Atmospheric Distillation Unit (ADU) and Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU). Why is vacuum used for heavy residues?* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** | Feature | ADU | VDU | |---|---|---| | Pressure | 1 atm | 1–10 kPa | | Temperature | up to 400 °C | up to 380 °C | | Feed | Desalted crude | Reduced crude residue | | Products | Naphtha, Kero, Diesel, AGO | VGO, Lubricating oils | | Purpose | Primary fractionation | Secondary fractionation | **Reason for Vacuum:** At 1 atm, heating residue above 400 °C causes **thermal cracking** (uncontrolled decomposition) which damages products and fouls equipment. By lowering pressure via **steam ejectors**, the boiling point drops (per Clausius-Clapeyron), allowing safe distillation of heavy fractions below their cracking temperature. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **Q:** *(a)* Describe with a neat block diagram the **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC) process**. Explain the role of the **catalyst regenerator** and state the typical operating conditions (T, P, catalyst). **(7 Marks)** *(b)* A refinery processes **50,000 kg/hr of VGO feed** to its FCC unit. The product slate is: - Gasoline: 22,500 kg/hr - LPG: 7,500 kg/hr - LCO (Light Cycle Oil): 10,000 kg/hr - Clarified Oil (HCO): 4,000 kg/hr - Coke: 6,000 kg/hr Calculate the **(i)** mass balance closure, **(ii)** % conversion of feed to gasoline + LPG, and **(iii)** gasoline selectivity. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks) **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** is a continuous catalytic process that converts heavy hydrocarbon feedstocks (VGO, Coker Gas Oil) into lighter, higher-value products. **Block Flow Diagram:** Preheated VGO (350 °C) → **Riser-Reactor** (contact with hot regenerated zeolite catalyst, 480–540 °C) → Cracking occurs in **3–5 seconds** → Spent catalyst + vapor products → **Cyclone Separator** (separates catalyst from vapors) → Vapors go to **Fractionator** (separates gasoline, LCO, HCO, slurry) → Spent catalyst to **Stripper** (steam strips adsorbed hydrocarbons) → **Regenerator** (air blown, burns off coke at 700–750 °C, restores catalyst activity) → Hot regenerated catalyst returns to riser via **standpipe**. **Role of Catalyst Regenerator:** The regenerator serves to **burn off coke deposits** (3–5 wt% on catalyst) using air at 700–750 °C. This restores catalyst activity, generates heat for the endothermic cracking reaction (heat balance), and enables continuous operation. **Operating Conditions:** - Temperature: **480–540 °C** (riser outlet) - Pressure: **1–3 atm (gauge)** - Catalyst: **Zeolite (USY or ZSM-5)** on matrix of silica-alumina, particle size 60–80 μm - Catalyst-to-Oil Ratio (CTO): **5–8** --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks) **Given:** VGO Feed = 50,000 kg/hr | Stream | Flow (kg/hr) | |---|---| | Gasoline | 22,500 | | LPG | 7,500 | | LCO | 10,000 | | HCO (Clarified Oil) | 4,000 | | Coke | 6,000 | | **Sum** | **50,000** | **(i) Mass Balance Closure:** $$\text{Closure} = \dfrac{\sum \dot{m}_{out}}{\dot{m}_{in}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{50{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{\text{Closure} = 100.00\%}$$ **(ii) % Conversion to Gasoline + LPG:** $$\%\text{Conv} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{gasoline} + \dot{m}_{LPG}}{\dot{m}_{feed}} \times 100\%$$ $$= \dfrac{22{,}500 + 7{,}500}{50{,}000} \times 100\%$$ $$= \dfrac{30{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{\%\text{Conversion} = 60.00\%}$$ **(iii) Gasoline Selectivity:** Selectivity = (Desired product yield) / (Total conversion) $$S_{gasoline} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{gasoline}}{\dot{m}_{gasoline} + \dot{m}_{LPG}} \times 100\%$$ $$= \dfrac{22{,}500}{30{,}000} \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{S_{gasoline} = 75.00\%}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** A 60% conversion with 75% gasoline selectivity is characteristic of a well-tuned FCCU processing sweet VGO. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[Alternative Choice for Internal Selection]** **Q:** *(a)* Explain **Catalytic Reforming** with reference to: (i) feed and products, (ii) catalyst, (iii) operating conditions, (iv) main reactions, and (v) the importance of hydrogen recycle. **(7 Marks)** *(b)* A naphtha feed of **40,000 kg/hr** enters a catalytic reformer. The feed composition (mass %): n-paraffins 35%, iso-paraffins 25%, naphthenes 30%, aromatics 10%. After reforming: n-paraffins 8%, iso-paraffins 12%, naphthenes 4%, aromatics 76%. Calculate: (i) the **aromatics yield** (kg/hr), (ii) **% increase in RON** if feed RON = 60 and product RON = 98, and (iii) the **hydrogen produced** assuming each aromatic ring formation releases 4 moles of H₂ per naphthene molecule and there are 30,000 kg of naphthenes converted. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks) **Catalytic Reforming** is a critical secondary process that upgrades low-octane **straight-run naphtha** (C6–C11, RON ~60–70) into **high-octane reformate** (RON ~95–100) rich in aromatics (benzene, toluene, xylene). **(i) Feed and Products:** - **Feed:** Straight-run naphtha, hydrotreated (S < 0.5 ppm), 65–180 °C boiling range. - **Products:** Reformate (gasoline blendstock), LPG, fuel gas, hydrogen (by-product). **(ii) Catalyst:** **Platinum (Pt) on chlorided alumina (γ-Al₂O₃)** with promoters Re or Sn for bi-metallic reforming (Pt-Re or Pt-Sn). Cl⁻ enhances acid function (isomerization), Pt provides metal function (dehydrogenation). **(iii) Operating Conditions:** - Temperature: **450–525 °C** - Pressure: **3–35 atm** (lower pressure favors aromatics; modern UOP CCR uses <5 atm) - H₂/Hydrocarbon molar ratio: **3–6** - LHSV: **1–2 h⁻¹** **(iv) Main Reactions:** 1. **Dehydrogenation of naphthenes → aromatics:** $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n} \longrightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-6} + 3\text{H}_2$$ 2. **Isomerization of n-paraffins → iso-paraffins:** $$\text{n-C}_6 \longrightarrow \text{iso-C}_6$$ 3. **Dehydrocyclization of paraffins → aromatics:** $$\text{n-C}_7\text{H}_{16} \longrightarrow \text{C}_7\text{H}_8 + 4\text{H}_2$$ **(v) Importance of Hydrogen Recycle:** - Suppresses **coke formation** on catalyst (thermodynamic equilibrium shift). - Maintains catalyst in **reduced metallic state**. - Provides **quench effect** to control reaction exothermicity. - Net H₂ produced (150–300 Nm³/m³ feed) is exported to **hydrocracker** and **HDS units** in the refinery hydrogen network. --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks) **Given:** Feed = 40,000 kg/hr **(i) Aromatics Yield (kg/hr):** $$m_{aromatics,out} = 0.76 \times 40{,}000 = 30{,}400 \text{ kg/hr}$$ Aromatics originally in feed: $$m_{aromatics,in} = 0.10 \times 40{,}000 = 4{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}$$ **Net new aromatics produced:** $$\Delta m_{aro} = 30{,}400 - 4{,}000 = 26{,}400 \text{ kg/hr}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta m_{aro} = 26{,}400 \text{ kg/hr}}$$ **(ii) % Increase in RON:** $$\%\Delta RON = \dfrac{RON_{prod} - RON_{feed}}{RON_{feed}} \times 100\%$$ $$= \dfrac{98 - 60}{60} \times 100\%$$ $$= \dfrac{38}{60} \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{\%\Delta RON \approx 63.33\%}$$ **(iii) Hydrogen Produced:** Given: 30,000 kg of naphthenes converted, each releases 4 moles of H₂. **Step 1** — Assume naphthene average molecular weight ≈ 100 g/mol (C₇H₁₄ for methylcyclohexane class): $$n_{naphthene} = \dfrac{30{,}000 \text{ kg}}{100 \text{ kg/kmol}} = 300 \text{ kmol}$$ **Step 2** — Moles of H₂ produced: $$n_{H_2} = 4 \times 300 = 1{,}200 \text{ kmol}$$ **Step 3** — Mass of H₂ (M = 2 kg/kmol): $$m_{H_2} = 1{,}200 \times 2 = 2{,}400 \text{ kg/hr}$$ $$\boxed{m_{H_2} \approx 2{,}400 \text{ kg/hr}}$$ **Step 4** — Volume at STP (22.4 Nm³/kmol): $$V_{H_2} = 1{,}200 \times 22.4 = 26{,}880 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting units in mass balance problems.** Always write kg/hr in every line. > 2. **Confusing TBP with ASTM distillation.** ASTM < TBP; ASTM 50% point is LOWER than TBP 50% by ~20–30 °C. > 3. **Writing "catalyst" without naming the active metal.** Pt, Pt-Re, Zeolite (ZSM-5) are required — generic "metal catalyst" loses marks. > 4. **Skipping the regenerator in FCC block diagrams.** Without it, the process is incomplete and 2–3 marks are lost. > 5. **Mixing up hydrocracking with FCC.** Hydrocracking uses H₂ + bifunctional catalyst; FCC uses zeolite without H₂. > 6. **Not labeling temperature and pressure** on flow diagrams — explicit values earn marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Points to Memorize Before KTU Exam:** - ✅ Petroleum refining = **physical + chemical conversion** of crude oil. - ✅ ADU operates at **1 atm, ~350–400 °C**; VDU operates at **1–10 kPa, ~380 °C**. - ✅ FCC uses **zeolite catalyst, 480–540 °C, 1–3 atm**, with continuous **regenerator**. - ✅ Reformer uses **Pt (with Re or Sn) on Al₂O₃, 450–525 °C, in H₂ atmosphere**. - ✅ Hydrocracking operates at **high H₂ pressure (70–200 atm)**, produces low-S fuels. - ✅ API Gravity: $°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$. - ✅ Reflux Ratio: $R = L / D$; minimum stages via **Fenske Equation**. - ✅ Mass balance: $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ — must close within ±0.5%. - ✅ Conversion: %Conv = (Desired product mass / Feed mass) × 100. - ✅ Selectivity: $S = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{desired}}{\dot{m}_{total converted}}$. - ✅ Octane: higher aromatics & isoparaffins = higher RON. - ✅ HDS reaction: $\text{R–SH} + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{R–H} + \text{H}_2\text{S}$. - ✅ Alkylation combines **C₃–C₄ olefins with isobutane** → high-octane alkylate (RON 92–96). - ✅ Delayed Coking produces **petroleum coke** + lighter distillates at ~500 °C. - ✅ Always state **T, P, catalyst** when describing a refining process in exams. - ✅ Block diagrams must include **mass flow rates and operating conditions**. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Strategy:** In the exam, draw the **refinery block flow diagram first** (carries 4–5 marks), then explain each unit briefly with its (T, P, catalyst). This shows the examiner comprehensive mastery and secures full marks on process description questions.

topics.title: Transportation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transportation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Transportation in Fuel Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Transportation in Fuel Engineering** refers to the systematic movement of crude petroleum, natural gas, and refined petroleum products from the point of extraction (wellhead) through intermediate storage facilities to refineries, and finally to end-users via distribution networks. It encompasses the physical infrastructure (pipelines, tankers, ships, railcars, trucks), the engineering principles governing fluid flow, and the logistical systems that ensure continuous, safe, and economic delivery of energy resources.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for Fuel Engineering, transportation is classified as a critical downstream activity that bridges the **upstream** (exploration & production) and **midstream** (refining & processing) sectors with the **downstream** (marketing & distribution) sector. It represents one of the largest cost components in the final price of any petroleum product.

## 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Upstream** | Operations related to crude oil and natural gas exploration and production |
| **Midstream** | Transportation, storage, and wholesale marketing of crude and products |
| **Downstream** | Refining of crude oil and processing of natural gas, plus marketing |
| **Wellhead** | The point at the surface where crude oil or gas emerges from a well |
| **Terminal** | A facility where petroleum products are stored and distributed |
| **Depot** | A bulk storage installation for refined products |
| **Tanker** | A vessel or vehicle designed for transporting liquids in bulk |
| **Crude Carrier** | A specialized ship designed for transporting unrefined crude oil |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Human Circulatory System**
> 
> Think of petroleum transportation exactly like the **human circulatory system**:
> - **Crude Oil at Wellhead** = Oxygen entering lungs (raw resource acquisition)
> - **Gathering Pipelines** = Veins (small-scale initial transport)
> - **Trunk Pipelines** = Arteries (large-scale high-pressure transport)
> - **Tankers/Ships** = Red Blood Cells (mobile transport across long distances)
> - **Refineries** = Heart (processing/pumping station)
> - **Distribution Networks** = Capillaries (final delivery to consumers)
> - **Storage Tanks** = Liver (reservoir/buffer storage)
> 
> Just as a blockage anywhere in the circulatory system affects the entire body, any disruption in petroleum transportation cascades through the entire energy economy, causing price spikes and supply shortages.

## 1.4 Classification of Transportation Modes

Transportation of fuels is broadly classified into **four major categories**:

1. **Pipeline Transportation** (most economical for long-distance, high-volume)
2. **Marine Transportation** (trans-oceanic, very large volumes)
3. **Rail Transportation** (intermediate distances, flexible routing)
4. **Road Transportation** (last-mile delivery, small volumes)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used Globally:**
> - **1 barrel (bbl) = 42 US gallons = 159 liters = 0.159 m³**
> - **1 tonne of crude oil ≈ 7.33 barrels** (varies with density)
> - **1 nautical mile = 1.852 km**
> - **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)** = carrying capacity of a ship including cargo, fuel, water, and supplies

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pipeline Transportation - Theoretical Foundation

### 2.1.1 Pipeline Categories

| Pipeline Type | Diameter Range | Pressure | Typical Use |
|---------------|----------------|----------|-------------|
| **Gathering Lines** | 50–150 mm | Low (5–20 bar) | Wellhead to processing facility |
| **Trunk/Trunk Lines** | 300–1200 mm | High (40–100 bar) | Cross-country crude transport |
| **Distribution Lines** | 50–300 mm | Medium (10–40 bar) | Refinery to depots |
| **Service Lines** | <50 mm | Low (<10 bar) | Final delivery to consumer |

### 2.1.2 Governing Physical Principles

The flow of petroleum through pipelines is governed by **fluid dynamics principles**. Since crude oil and refined products are Newtonian fluids in most practical conditions, we apply classical pipe flow equations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** Fluid flow in a pipeline is driven by a **pressure differential** between the upstream and downstream ends. The pressure drop occurs due to friction against pipe walls (major losses) and fittings/valves (minor losses).

### 2.1.3 Reynolds Number Determination

The flow regime (laminar vs. turbulent) is determined by the **Reynolds Number**:

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{v D}{\nu}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = fluid density (**kg/m³**)
- $v$ = average flow velocity (**m/s**)
- $D$ = pipe internal diameter (**m**)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (**Pa·s** or **N·s/m²**)
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity (**m²/s**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reynolds Numbers:**
> - **Re < 2000** → **Laminar Flow** (smooth, layered)
> - **2000 < Re < 4000** → **Transitional Flow** (unstable)
> - **Re > 4000** → **Turbulent Flow** (chaotic, mixing)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Application |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Reynolds Number** | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Flow regime identification |
| 2 | **Hagen-Poiseuille (Laminar)** | $\Delta P = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}$ | Pressure drop in laminar flow |
| 3 | **Darcy-Weisbach (General)** | $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | Head loss due to friction |
| 4 | **Friction Factor (Blasius)** | $f = \dfrac{0.316}{\text{Re}^{0.25}}$ | Smooth pipes, turbulent |
| 5 | **Friction Factor (Colebrook)** | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{\text{Re}\sqrt{f}}\right)$ | Commercial pipes, all Re |
| 6 | **Volumetric Flow Rate** | $Q = A \cdot v = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} v$ | Pipe capacity |
| 7 | **Mass Flow Rate** | $\dot{m} = \rho Q = \rho A v$ | Throughput calculation |
| 8 | **Power Required (Pumping)** | $P = \dot{m} \cdot g \cdot H / \eta$ | Pump sizing |
| 9 | **Bernoulli's Equation** | $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f$ | Energy balance |
| 10 | **Tank Wagon Capacity** | $V = \pi r^2 L$ (cylindrical) | Storage calculation |
| 11 | **Tanker Throughput** | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{V_{\text{tanker}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$ | Logistics |
| 12 | **Specific Energy Consumption** | $E_s = \dfrac{P}{\dot{m}}$ | Efficiency metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for Engineering Application:**
> - Pipes must be assumed **circular cross-section** unless otherwise stated
> - For crude oil: $\rho \approx 800$–**900 kg/m³**, $\mu \approx 0.001$–**0.1 Pa·s**
> - For gasoline: $\rho \approx 720$–**780 kg/m³**, $\mu \approx 0.0003$–**0.0006 Pa·s**
> - Pipeline roughness $\varepsilon$ for commercial steel: **0.045 mm**

## 2.3 Marine Transportation Analysis

### 2.3.1 Tanker Classification by Size

| Class | DWT (tonnes) | Use |
|-------|--------------|-----|
| **General Purpose** | < 25,000 | Refined products, coastal |
| **Medium Range (MR)** | 25,000–55,000 | Refined products |
| **Long Range (LR1)** | 55,000–80,000 | Crude/Products |
| **Aframax** | 80,000–120,000 | Regional crude |
| **Suezmax** | 120,000–200,000 | Crude, Suez Canal |
| **VLCC (Very Large)** | 200,000–320,000 | Long-haul crude |
| **ULCC (Ultra Large)** | > 320,000 | Crude, deep draft ports |

### 2.3.2 Marine Cargo Handling

Crude carriers use **inert gas systems** to prevent fire/explosion in empty tanks. Tankers feature:
- **Segregated ballast tanks** (SBT)
- **Crude oil washing (COW)** systems
- **Double hull construction** (mandatory post-MARPOL)
- **Inert gas generators** (typically flue gas from boilers)

## 2.4 Road & Rail Transportation

### 2.4.1 Road Tankers

- Typical capacity: **10,000–40,000 liters**
- Constructed of mild steel or aluminum alloy
- Compartmentalized for multi-product delivery
- Equipped with safety valves, pressure relief

### 2.4.2 Rail Tank Cars

- Capacity: **30,000–90,000 liters**
- Higher payload efficiency than road
- DOT-111, DOT-117 standards (US)
- Suitable for longer distances and hazardous materials

## 2.5 Storage Tanks at Terminals

| Tank Type | Capacity Range | Application |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|
| **Cone Roof** | 500–50,000 m³ | Low vapor pressure products |
| **Dome Roof** | 5,000–100,000 m³ | Larger cone roof alternative |
| **Floating Roof** | 10,000–200,000 m³ | High vapor pressure, crude oil |
| **Spherical (Horton Sphere)** | 500–5,000 m³ | LPG, pressurized gases |
| **Bullet Tanks** | 50–500 m³ | LNG, pressurized LNG |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Transportation Engineering Matters:**
> 1. **Economic Impact**: Transportation costs constitute **20–40%** of the final petroleum product price
> 2. **Strategic Importance**: Energy security depends entirely on reliable transport infrastructure
> 3. **Environmental Concern**: Spills from tankers/pipelines cause catastrophic ecological damage (e.g., **Exxon Valdez, Deepwater Horizon**)
> 4. **Safety Engineering**: Hydrocarbon transport involves extreme fire/explosion risks
> 5. **Geopolitical Leverage**: Pipelines (e.g., Nord Stream, Keystone XL) are instruments of foreign policy

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hagen-Poiseuille Equation for Laminar Flow

### 3.1.1 Starting Point: Force Balance on a Cylindrical Fluid Element

Consider a horizontal pipe of radius $R$ and length $L$ carrying a viscous fluid in steady, laminar flow. Apply Newton's second law to a coaxial cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $dx$.

**Step 1: Define the pressure forces acting on the element**

The pressure $P$ acts on the cross-sectional area $\pi r^2$. The net pressure force in the direction of flow is:

$$
F_{\text{pressure}} = (P - (P + dP)) \cdot \pi r^2 = -dP \cdot \pi r^2
$$

**Step 2: Define the viscous shear force**

Newton's law of viscosity states that shear stress $\tau$ on a fluid element is proportional to the velocity gradient:

$$
\tau = -\mu \frac{dv}{dr}
$$

The viscous force acts on the lateral surface area $2 \pi r \cdot dx$ opposing the flow:

$$
F_{\text{viscous}} = \tau \cdot 2\pi r \cdot dx = -\mu \frac{dv}{dr} \cdot 2\pi r \cdot dx
$$

**Step 3: Apply equilibrium condition for steady flow**

Since the flow is steady (no acceleration), the net force equals zero:

$$
F_{\text{pressure}} + F_{\text{viscous}} = 0
$$

$$
-dP \cdot \pi r^2 + \left(-\mu \frac{dv}{dr} \cdot 2\pi r \cdot dx\right) = 0
$$

**Step 4: Rearrange to isolate velocity gradient**

$$
\mu \frac{dv}{dr} \cdot 2\pi r \cdot dx = -dP \cdot \pi r^2
$$

$$
\frac{dv}{dr} = -\frac{dP}{2\mu \, dx} \cdot r
$$

Note: $\frac{dP}{dx}$ is the **constant pressure gradient** along the pipe. Let $G = -\frac{dP}{dx}$ (positive when pressure drops in flow direction).

$$
\frac{dv}{dr} = \frac{G}{2\mu} \cdot r
$$

**Step 5: Integrate with respect to r**

$$
\int dv = \int \frac{G}{2\mu} r \, dr
$$

$$
v(r) = \frac{G}{4\mu} r^2 + C
$$

**Step 6: Apply the no-slip boundary condition**

At the pipe wall, $r = R$, the velocity $v = 0$:

$$
0 = \frac{G}{4\mu} R^2 + C \implies C = -\frac{G R^2}{4\mu}
$$

**Step 7: Obtain the parabolic velocity profile**

$$
v(r) = \frac{G}{4\mu} (R^2 - r^2)
$$

Substituting $G = -\frac{dP}{dx} = \frac{\Delta P}{L}$:

$$
v(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} (R^2 - r^2)
$$

**Step 8: Calculate the volumetric flow rate by integration**

Integrate velocity over the pipe cross-section:

$$
Q = \int_0^R v(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr
$$

$$
Q = \int_0^R \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} (R^2 - r^2) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) \, dr
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[\frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^R
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[\frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4}\right]
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}
$$

**Step 9: Final form of Hagen-Poiseuille Equation**

Substituting $R = D/2$:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P D^4}{128 \mu L}
$$

Or, solving for pressure drop:

$$
\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: The pressure drop is **inversely proportional to $D^4$**. Doubling the pipe diameter reduces the pressure drop by a factor of **16** for the same flow rate — this is why pipelines use large diameters!

## 3.2 Derivation: Darcy-Weisbach Equation

### 3.2.1 Starting Point: Energy Equation for Real Fluid

For a real fluid flowing through a pipe with friction:

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f
$$

For a horizontal pipe of constant diameter ($z_1 = z_2$, $v_1 = v_2$):

$$
h_f = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g}
$$

**Step 1: Express friction loss using friction factor**

The Darcy friction factor $f$ is empirically defined such that:

$$
h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 2: Derivation from dimensional analysis and experimental data**

Using Buckingham Pi theorem on variables $\Delta P$, $\rho$, $\mu$, $v$, $D$, $\varepsilon$, $L$:

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{\rho v^2} = \phi\left(\frac{\rho v D}{\mu}, \frac{\varepsilon}{D}, \frac{L}{D}\right)
$$

The functional dependence is empirically found to be linear in $L/D$:

$$
h_f = f\left(\text{Re}, \frac{\varepsilon}{D}\right) \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 3: Final Darcy-Weisbach Equation**

$$
\boxed{h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Friction Factor Correlations:**
> - **Laminar flow** ($Re < 2000$): $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$
> - **Blasius equation** (turbulent, smooth): $f = \dfrac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$ (valid for $4000 < Re < 10^5$)
> - **Colebrook equation** (turbulent, all): $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2\log\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Crude oil ($\rho = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.005 \text{ Pa·s}$) is transported through a horizontal pipeline of diameter **D = 0.5 m** at a flow rate of **Q = 0.1 m³/s**. Calculate:
> (a) The Reynolds number and identify flow regime
> (b) The pressure drop per kilometer of pipe
> (c) The pumping power required if pump efficiency is **75%**

### Solution:

**Step (a): Calculate average velocity**

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.5)^2}{4} = 0.1963 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.1}{0.1963} = 0.509 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step (b): Calculate Reynolds number**

$$
\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{850 \times 0.509 \times 0.5}{0.005}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Re} = 43,265}
$$

Since $\text{Re} > 4000$, the flow is **turbulent**.

**Step (c): Calculate friction factor using Blasius**

$$
f = \frac{0.316}{\text{Re}^{0.25}} = \frac{0.316}{(43,265)^{0.25}}
$$

$$
(43,265)^{0.25} = 14.43
$$

$$
f = \frac{0.316}{14.43} = 0.0219
$$

**Step (d): Calculate head loss per km**

For $L = 1000$ m:

$$
h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g} = 0.0219 \cdot \frac{1000}{0.5} \cdot \frac{(0.509)^2}{2 \times 9.81}
$$

$$
h_f = 0.0219 \times 2000 \times \frac{0.259}{19.62}
$$

$$
h_f = 0.0219 \times 2000 \times 0.0132 = 0.578 \text{ m}
$$

**Step (e): Calculate pressure drop**

$$
\Delta P = \rho g h_f = 850 \times 9.81 \times 0.578
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta P = 4819 \text{ Pa} \approx 4.82 \text{ kPa per km}}
$$

**Step (f): Calculate mass flow rate**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho Q = 850 \times 0.1 = 85 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step (g): Calculate pumping power**

$$
P_{\text{ideal}} = \dot{m} \cdot g \cdot h_f = 85 \times 9.81 \times 0.578
$$

$$
P_{\text{ideal}} = 481.9 \text{ W}
$$

Accounting for pump efficiency:

$$
P_{\text{actual}} = \frac{P_{\text{ideal}}}{\eta} = \frac{481.9}{0.75}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{actual}} = 642.6 \text{ W}}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Transportation Mode Selection

| Parameter | Pipeline | Marine Tanker | Rail | Road Tanker |
|-----------|----------|---------------|------|-------------|
| **Capital Cost** | Very High | High | Medium | Low |
| **Operating Cost/unit** | Very Low | Low | Medium | High |
| **Capacity** | Very High | Very High | High | Low |
| **Flexibility** | Low | High | Medium | Very High |
| **Distance Range** | Medium–Long | Very Long | Long | Short–Medium |
| **Environmental Risk** | Medium (spills) | High (catastrophic) | Medium | Medium |
| **Speed** | Continuous | Slow | Fast | Fast |
| **Best Use Case** | Crude trunk lines | Trans-oceanic | Inland bulk | Last-mile delivery |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Define the term "Trunk Pipeline" and state its typical operating pressure range.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Trunk Pipeline** is a large-diameter, long-distance pipeline (typically **D = 300–1200 mm**) used for the bulk transportation of crude oil, refined petroleum products, or natural gas between major terminals, refineries, and distribution centers. They form the backbone of the petroleum transportation infrastructure.
>
> **Operating Pressure Range:** Typically **40–100 bar** (approximately **4–10 MPa**), depending on the product being transported, distance, and terrain. Higher pressures are used for gaseous products, while moderate pressures suffice for liquids.

*(3 marks: 2 for definition, 1 for pressure range)*

### Question A2: **[KTU University Exam - June 2023]**

**Distinguish between a "Cone Roof Tank" and a "Floating Roof Tank" with respect to their suitability for storing different petroleum products.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Cone Roof Tank | Floating Roof Tank |
|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Construction** | Fixed conical roof, cylindrical body | Roof floats on liquid surface |
| **Vapor Loss** | High (especially for volatile liquids) | Minimal (no vapor space) |
| **Suitability** | Low vapor pressure products (diesel, lube oil, fuel oil) | High vapor pressure products (crude oil, gasoline, naphtha) |
| **Fire Risk** | Higher | Lower (no flammable vapor accumulation) |
| **Capacity** | 500–50,000 m³ | 10,000–200,000 m³ |

*(3 marks: comparison table structure)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** for laminar flow of a viscous fluid through a horizontal circular pipe. State clearly the assumptions made in the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Crude oil with density **$\rho = 880 \text{ kg/m}^3$** and dynamic viscosity **$\mu = 0.008 \text{ Pa·s}$** flows through a horizontal pipeline of internal diameter **D = 0.4 m** at a volumetric flow rate of **Q = 0.08 m³/s**.
   - (i) Determine the Reynolds number and identify the flow regime.
   - (ii) Calculate the pressure drop over a **5 km** length of pipe using the Darcy-Weisbach equation. Take the pipe roughness $\varepsilon = 0.045 \text{ mm}$ and use the Colebrook equation for friction factor.
   - (iii) Determine the pumping power required if the pump efficiency is **70%**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, incompressible, laminar flow
2. Newtonian fluid (constant viscosity)
3. Horizontal pipe (no elevation change)
4. No-slip condition at the wall
5. Fully developed flow (velocity profile constant along pipe)
6. Negligible entrance effects

**Derivation** (as shown in Section 3.1):

Starting from force balance on a cylindrical fluid element:

$$
-dP \cdot \pi r^2 = \mu \frac{dv}{dr} \cdot 2\pi r \cdot dx
$$

Integrating with no-slip boundary condition $v(R) = 0$:

$$
v(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}(R^2 - r^2)
$$

Integrating over the cross-section to get volumetric flow rate:

$$
\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}}
$$

*(7 marks: 3 for assumptions, 4 for derivation steps)*

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1: Calculate velocity**

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.4)^2}{4} = 0.1257 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.08}{0.1257} = 0.637 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Reynolds number**

$$
\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{880 \times 0.637 \times 0.4}{0.008} = 28,028
$$

Flow is **turbulent** since $\text{Re} > 4000$.

**Step 3: Calculate relative roughness**

$$
\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{0.045 \times 10^{-3}}{0.4} = 1.125 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 4: Solve Colebrook equation iteratively**

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2\log\left(\frac{1.125 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{28028 \sqrt{f}}\right)
$$

First iteration: assume $f = 0.025$:

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{0.025}} = -2\log\left(3.04 \times 10^{-5} + \frac{2.51}{28028 \times 0.158}\right)
$$

$$
6.32 = -2\log(3.04 \times 10^{-5} + 5.67 \times 10^{-4})
$$

$$
6.32 = -2\log(5.97 \times 10^{-4}) = -2 \times (-3.224) = 6.448
$$

Second iteration: try $f = 0.0245$:

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{0.0245}} = 6.39
$$

After convergence: $f \approx 0.0245$

**Step 5: Calculate head loss**

$$
h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g} = 0.0245 \cdot \frac{5000}{0.4} \cdot \frac{(0.637)^2}{2 \times 9.81}
$$

$$
h_f = 0.0245 \times 12500 \times 0.0207 = 6.34 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6: Calculate pressure drop**

$$
\Delta P = \rho g h_f = 880 \times 9.81 \times 6.34 = 54,728 \text{ Pa} \approx 54.7 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 7: Calculate mass flow rate**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho Q = 880 \times 0.08 = 70.4 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 8: Calculate pumping power**

$$
P_{\text{ideal}} = \dot{m} \cdot g \cdot h_f = 70.4 \times 9.81 \times 6.34 = 4378 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{\text{actual}} = \frac{4378}{0.70} = 6254 \text{ W} \approx 6.25 \text{ kW}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for Re calculation, 3 for friction factor and pressure drop, 2 for power calculation)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam - June 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **classification of transportation modes** used for petroleum products. Compare pipeline transportation with marine transportation in terms of cost, capacity, and flexibility. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pipeline transports **natural gas** at a flow rate of **$\dot{m} = 50 \text{ kg/s}$**. The gas density is **$\rho = 12 \text{ kg/m}^3$** and viscosity **$\mu = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$**. The pipe diameter is **D = 0.6 m**.
   - (i) Determine if the flow is laminar or turbulent.
   - (ii) If the pipe length is **L = 100 km** and the Darcy friction factor is **f = 0.018**, calculate the pressure drop.
   - (iii) Suggest two methods to reduce the pressure drop. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Classification of Transportation Modes:**

```
PETROLEUM TRANSPORTATION
├── 1. Pipeline (Gathering, Trunk, Distribution)
├── 2. Marine (Tanker Ships: VLCC, ULCC, Suezmax)
├── 3. Rail (Tank Cars)
└── 4. Road (Tank Trucks)
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Pipeline | Marine Tanker |
|-----------|----------|---------------|
| **Capital Cost** | Very High (USD millions/km) | High (ship construction) |
| **Operating Cost/Unit** | Very Low (~USD 0.01/tonne-km) | Low (~USD 0.005/tonne-km for VLCC) |
| **Capacity** | Very High (continuous flow) | Very High (up to 320,000 DWT) |
| **Flexibility** | Low (fixed route) | High (any port) |
| **Distance Range** | 100–5000 km | 1000–20,000+ km |
| **Construction Time** | Long (years) | Moderate (months) |
| **Environmental Risk** | Localized spills | Catastrophic potential |

*(7 marks: 3 for sketch/classification, 4 for comparison)*

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1: Calculate velocity**

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.6)^2}{4} = 0.2827 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
v = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho A} = \frac{50}{12 \times 0.2827} = 14.74 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Reynolds number**

$$
\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{12 \times 14.74 \times 0.6}{1.2 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
\text{Re} = \frac{106.13}{1.2 \times 10^{-5}} = 8.84 \times 10^6
$$

Flow is **highly turbulent** ($\text{Re} \gg 4000$).

**Step 3: Calculate pressure drop using Darcy-Weisbach**

$$
\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}
$$

$$
\Delta P = 0.018 \cdot \frac{100,000}{0.6} \cdot \frac{12 \times (14.74)^2}{2}
$$

$$
\Delta P = 0.018 \times 166,667 \times \frac{12 \times 217.27}{2}
$$

$$
\Delta P = 3000 \times 1303.6 = 3,910,800 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta P \approx 3.91 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**Step 4: Methods to reduce pressure drop**

1. **Increase pipe diameter**: Since $\Delta P \propto 1/D^5$ (combining $L/D$ and $v^2 \propto 1/D^4$)
2. **Reduce flow velocity**: Lower throughput (or install intermediate pumping stations)
3. **Reduce pipe roughness**: Use smoother internal coatings (e.g., epoxy lining)
4. **Use smoother bends and fewer fittings**: Reduce minor losses

*(7 marks: 2 for Re, 3 for pressure drop, 2 for reduction methods)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency**: Mixing SI and CGS units (e.g., using cm with m). Always convert to SI: diameter in **m**, viscosity in **Pa·s**, flow rate in **m³/s**.
> 
> 2. **Reynolds Number Calculation Errors**: Forgetting to use correct diameter (internal vs. external). Always use **internal diameter**.
> 
> 3. **Hagen-Poiseuille Misuse**: Students apply this equation for turbulent flow. **Strictly valid only for laminar flow** ($Re < 2000$).
> 
> 4. **Colebrook Iteration Errors**: Failing to iterate the Colebrook equation at least 2–3 times. The friction factor appears on **both sides**, requiring numerical solution.
> 
> 5. **Confusing Static Head with Friction Head**: In Bernoulli's equation, students often omit the elevation term $z$ for non-horizontal pipes.
> 
> 6. **Pump Efficiency**: Always divide by efficiency when calculating **actual** power required: $P_{\text{actual}} = P_{\text{ideal}}/\eta$
> 
> 7. **Missing Assumptions**: In derivations, examiners award **2–3 marks** specifically for stating assumptions clearly. Don't skip this!

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Transportation in Fuel Engineering:**

### Key Concepts:
- [x] Transportation modes: **Pipeline, Marine, Rail, Road**
- [x] Pipeline categories: **Gathering, Trunk, Distribution, Service**
- [x] Tanker classification: **Aframax, Suezmax, VLCC, ULCC**
- [x] Storage tank types: **Cone roof, Floating roof, Spherical, Bullet**

### Critical Formulas:
- [x] Reynolds Number: $\text{Re} = \rho v D / \mu$
- [x] Hagen-Poiseuille: $\Delta P = 128 \mu L Q / (\pi D^4)$ (laminar only)
- [x] Darcy-Weisbach: $h_f = f(L/D)(v^2/2g)$
- [x] Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = \rho Q = \rho A v$
- [x] Pump power: $P = \dot{m} g h_f / \eta$

### Key Numbers to Memorize:
- [x] **1 barrel = 159 liters = 0.159 m³**
- [x] **1 tonne crude ≈ 7.33 barrels**
- [x] **Critical Re for laminar-turbulent transition ≈ 2300**
- [x] **Commercial steel pipe roughness ≈ 0.045 mm**
- [x] **Crude oil density range: 800–900 kg/m³**

### Critical Rules of Thumb:
- [x] Pipeline capacity scales with **$D^{2.5}$** (doubling diameter increases capacity ~5.66×)
- [x] Pressure drop scales with **$1/D^5$** (very sensitive to diameter)
- [x] Always check **flow regime** before selecting friction factor formula
- [x] Marine transportation is **most economical** for distances > **1500 km** over water
- [x] Pipeline transportation is **most economical** for distances > **500 km** over land

### Engineering Best Practices:
- [x] Always specify **internal diameter** in calculations
- [x] Use **Darcy-Weisbach** for turbulent flow; **Hagen-Poiseuille** for laminar
- [x] Include **pump efficiency** in power calculations
- [x] Apply **Bernoulli's equation** with appropriate head loss term
- [x] Account for **minor losses** (fittings, valves) in real systems
- [x] Consider **safety factors** for pressure ratings (typically **1.5–2×** operating pressure)

### Safety & Environmental Considerations:
- [x] **Double hull** mandatory for tankers (post-MARPOL)
- [x] **Inert gas systems** in cargo tanks
- [x] **Cathodic protection** for buried pipelines
- [x] **Emergency shutoff valves** at strategic locations
- [x] **Spill containment** systems at terminals

topics.title: Storage and Handling of Liquid Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Storage and Handling of Liquid Fuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Storage of Liquid Fuels** refers to the engineered containment, preservation, and management of petroleum-derived liquid products (crude oil, gasoline, diesel, kerosene, fuel oil, ATF, etc.) in specially designed vessels and associated infrastructure, ensuring their physicochemical integrity, safety from fire/explosion hazards, and environmental containment throughout the supply chain from refinery to end-user.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 framework, **handling** encompasses the complete operational chain of receiving (intake), transferring (pumping), storing (containment), sampling, blending, and dispatching (outgoing) of liquid hydrocarbon fuels in compliance with statutory codes such as **OISD (Oil Industry Safety Directorate)**, **IS 803**, **IS 10987**, **NFPA 30**, and **API 650**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Liquid Fuel Bank"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Think of a fuel storage terminal as a **"bank vault for liquid money."** Just as a bank has locked vaults (tanks), security guards (fire systems), conveyor belts (pipelines), and tellers (pumps) to move money safely, a fuel terminal stores hydrocarbon liquids using engineered tanks, fire protection networks, pipelines, and dispensing systems — guarding against the three cardinal risks: **Fire, Leakage, and Contamination**.

**The Three Universal Enemies of Liquid Fuel Storage:**
1. **Fire/Explosion** (flammable vapors + ignition source)
2. **Loss of Product** (leakage, evaporation, theft)
3. **Quality Degradation** (water ingress, oxidation, microbial growth, sediment)

## 1.3 Classification of Liquid Fuels by Hazard (Flash Point Basis)

| Class | Flash Point (°C) | Examples | Hazard Level |
|-------|------------------|----------|--------------|
| Class A | $< 23$ | Gasoline, Naphtha, Benzene | Extremely Flammable |
| Class B | $23 - 65$ | Kerosene, Diesel, ATF | Flammable |
| Class C | $65 - 93$ | Light Fuel Oil | Combustible |
| Class D | $> 93$ | Heavy Fuel Oil, Bitumen | Low Hazard |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standards (Bold Highlight)

- **Density of gasoline:** $\rho \approx 0.72 - 0.78 \text{ g/cm}^3$ at **15.6 °C**
- **Density of diesel:** $\rho \approx 0.82 - 0.86 \text{ g/cm}^3$ at **15.6 °C**
- **Density of crude oil:** $\rho \approx 0.80 - 0.95 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- **API Gravity formula:** $\text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{\rho_{60°F}} - 131.5$
- **Vapor pressure of gasoline (Reid):** $45 - 90 \text{ kPa}$ at **37.8 °C**
- **Design storage temperature:** ambient range $-10°C$ to $+50°C$ (India)
- **Standard tank design code:** **API 650** (atmospheric) / **API 620** (low-pressure refrigerated)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Liquid Fuel Storage Systems

### 2.1.1 Atmospheric Storage Tanks (Above-Ground)

**a) Fixed Roof (Cone Roof) Tank**
- Used for **low-vapor-pressure** liquids (diesel, fuel oil, lube oil).
- Roof is welded to the shell — no movement.
- Maximum design pressure: **+10 kPa** internal, **-0.5 kPa** external.
- Inert gas blanketing recommended for crude storage.

**b) Floating Roof Tank**
- Used for **high-vapor-pressure** liquids (gasoline, naphtha, crude).
- Roof floats directly on the liquid surface, eliminating vapor space.
- Two sub-types: **External Floating Roof (EFR)** with pontoon/contact rim seal, and **Internal Floating Roof (IFR)** with full contact deck.
- Reduces evaporative losses by **~90%** versus fixed roof.

**c) Domed (Lifteroof) Tank**
- Self-supporting aluminum dome on a concrete base — used for large bulk storage of crude and petroleum.

### 2.1.2 Pressurized Storage (Spheres, Bullets, Hortonspheres)
- For **LPG, NGL, propylene** — operating pressures **0.2 – 1.6 MPa**.
- Spherical geometry minimizes surface area → minimum stress.

### 2.1.3 Underground Storage Tanks (UST)
- Double-walled, cathodically protected.
- Used at retail outlets (petrol pumps) and airfields.
- Required by **IS 10987** and **OISD-146**.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Engineering Rationale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why floating roof?** When fuel evaporates, it forms a **flammable vapor cloud**. A floating roof mechanically eliminates the vapor space, so even if a spark occurs, there's no fuel-air mixture to ignite. This is the primary **passive fire protection** strategy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why bund walls?** A **bund (dike)** is an earthen/compacted earth wall built around a tank group to contain **110%** of the largest tank's volume in case of catastrophic rupture — preventing pool fires from spreading across the terminal.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|-------|---------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | API Gravity | $\text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{60°F}} - 131.5$ | dimensionless | Crude/fuel classification |
| 2 | Specific Gravity | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{fuel}}{\rho_{water}}$ | dimensionless | Volume-mass conversion |
| 3 | Mass from Volume | $m = \rho \cdot V$ | kg | Inventory calculation |
| 4 | Cylindrical Tank Volume | $V = \pi R^2 h$ (vertical cyl.) | m³ | Tank capacity |
| 5 | Horizontal Cylinder Volume | $V = L\left[R^2 \cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{R-h}{R}\right) - (R-h)\sqrt{2Rh-h^2}\right]$ | m³ | Bullet tanks, partially filled |
| 6 | Spherical Tank Volume | $V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3$ | m³ | LPG spheres |
| 7 | Bund Capacity Rule | $V_{bund} \ge 1.1 \times V_{largest\ tank}$ | m³ | Spill containment |
| 8 | Hydrostatic Test Pressure | $P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{design}$ (liq.) | kPa | Commissioning |
| 9 | Tank Wall Hoop Stress (cyl.) | $\sigma = \dfrac{PD}{2t}$ | MPa | Shell thickness |
| 10 | Evaporation Loss (daily) | $L = 0.3 \cdot P_{vap} \cdot V_{tank}$ | kg/day | Fixed roof losses |
| 11 | Vapor Recovery Equation | $E_{recovery} = \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{FR}}{V_{FF}}\right) \times 100$ | % | Floating vs fixed roof |
| 12 | Reynolds Number (pipe flow) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Flow regime |
| 13 | Hazen-Williams Head Loss | $h_f = \dfrac{10.67 L Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} D^{4.87}}$ | m | Piping design |
| 14 | Vapor Pressure Correction (RVP) | $\log P = A - \dfrac{B}{T}$ (Antoine) | mmHg | Storage pressure |
| 15 | Net Storage Capacity | $V_{net} = V_{gross} \times (1 - 0.05)$ | m³ | Dead-stock allowance |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Never confuse **Vapor Recovery** with **Vapor Balancing**. The former captures vapors back into the tank; the latter displaces vapors into another storage vessel during transfer.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery Tank Farms:** Pre-distillation crude storage (crude tank → desalter → distillation).
- **Terminal Storage (POL):** Marketed products storage (HPCL, BPCL, IOCL depots).
- **Airport Hydrant Systems:** Underground ATF pipelines with pit valves at aircraft stands.
- **Strategic Petroleum Reserves (SPR):** Underground rock caverns (e.g., Mangalore, Padur) for national energy security.
- **Marine Loading Arms / SPM:** Single Point Mooring for VLCC tanker offloading.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cylindrical Tank Capacity with Dead-Stock Allowance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** Calculate the working volume of a vertical cylindrical fuel storage tank of internal diameter $D = 20 \text{ m}$ and height $H = 12 \text{ m}$, given that **5%** of the gross volume is reserved as **dead-stock** (inaccessible fuel below the suction line outlet).

### Step 1 — Define the gross geometric capacity
For a vertical cylinder, the gross internal volume is given by the area of the base multiplied by the height:

$$V_{gross} = A_{base} \times H = \pi R^2 \times H$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the radius
The radius $R$ is half the internal diameter $D$. Therefore, $R = \dfrac{D}{2}$. Substituting $D = 20$ m gives $R = 10$ m.

$$V_{gross} = \pi \times (10)^2 \times 12$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the arithmetic
Squaring 10 gives 100. Multiplying by 12 yields 1200. So,

$$V_{gross} = 1200\pi \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 4 — Apply the dead-stock factor
Per KTU/OISD design practice, dead-stock reduces the usable volume by 5%:

$$V_{net} = V_{gross} \times (1 - 0.05) = 0.95 \times V_{gross}$$

### Step 5 — Final numerical value

$$V_{net} = 0.95 \times 1200\pi = 1140\pi \text{ m}^3$$

$$V_{net} \approx 3581.42 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 6 — Convert to barrels (optional)
Using $1 \text{ m}^3 = 6.2898 \text{ bbl}$,

$$V_{net} \approx 3581.42 \times 6.2898 \approx 22526 \text{ barrels}$$

**Conclusion:** The tank holds approximately **22,526 barrels** of usable liquid fuel.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shell Plate Thickness for a Cylindrical Tank (API 650)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hoop stress** in a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel equals the internal pressure times the diameter divided by twice the wall thickness.

### Step 1 — Fundamental equation (thin-wall theory)

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for design thickness $t_d$
Rearranging for $t_d$,

$$t_d = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allowable}}$$

### Step 3 — Apply API 650 corrosion and joint allowances
Per API 650, the as-built thickness becomes:

$$t = t_d + C_A + C_C$$

where $C_A$ = corrosion allowance (typically **1.6 mm** for carbon steel in petroleum service), and $C_C$ = mill tolerance (**0.3 mm** for plate).

### Step 4 — Numerical Example
Given: $D = 30 \text{ m}$, design pressure $P = 9 \text{ kPa} = 9000 \text{ Pa}$, allowable stress $\sigma_{allow} = 165 \text{ MPa} = 165 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$.

$$t_d = \dfrac{9000 \times 30}{2 \times 165 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{270000}{330 \times 10^6}$$

$$t_d = 8.18 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.818 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 5 — Add allowances
$t = 0.818 + 1.6 + 0.3 = 2.72 \text{ mm}$ — rounded up to the next standard plate gauge, **3 mm**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Evaporative Loss Estimation (Fixed Roof Tank)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standing-storage loss for a fixed roof tank is dominated by **diurnal temperature swings** (heating-cooling cycles of the vapor space).

The empirical **API MPMS Chapter 19.1** correlation is:

$$L_S = 365 \cdot K_E \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_{VA}}{P_{VA} - P_{BA}}\right) \cdot V_{vk} \cdot K_S \cdot F_P$$

where:
- $L_S$ = standing loss (kg/year)
- $K_E$ = vapor expansion factor
- $P_{VA}$ = vapor pressure at daily average liquid surface temperature (Pa)
- $P_{BA}$ = atmospheric pressure at tank location (Pa)
- $V_{vk}$ = vapor space volume (m³)
- $K_S$ = tank paint factor (solar absorptance)
- $F_P$ = product factor

### Numerical Estimation
Assume: $K_E = 0.05$, $P_{VA} = 55000$ Pa, $P_{BA} = 101325$ Pa, $V_{vk} = 100$ m³, $K_S = 1.0$ (white paint), $F_P = 1.0$ (gasoline).

$$L_S = 365 \times 0.05 \times \left(\dfrac{55000}{101325 - 55000}\right) \times 100 \times 1.0 \times 1.0$$

$$L_S = 18.25 \times \left(\dfrac{55000}{46325}\right) \times 100$$

$$L_S = 18.25 \times 1.187 \times 100$$

$$L_S \approx 2167 \text{ kg/year}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This corresponds to roughly **$2167 \times 0.74 = 2928$ litres/year** of gasoline lost to evaporation per 100 m³ of vapor space — justifying the use of **floating roofs** or **vapor-recovery units (VRUs)**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Storage Tank Selection

| Selection Criterion | Fixed Roof | Floating Roof (External) | Floating Roof (Internal) | Spherical (Pressurized) |
|---------------------|------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Vapor pressure | $< 10$ kPa | Any | Any | N/A (LPG) |
| Wind resistance | High | Moderate | High | Very High |
| Evaporation loss | High | Very Low | Low | None (pressurized) |
| Capital cost | Low | High | Highest | Highest |
| Maintenance cost | Low | Moderate (rim seal) | Moderate | High |
| Suitable product | Diesel, FO, Lube | Crude, Gasoline | Gasoline, Kero | LPG, NGL, Propylene |
| API 650 / 620 | API 650 | API 650 | API 650 | API 620 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Liquid Fuel Storage & Handling System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CRUDE OIL RECEIPT via Tanker Pipeline SPM] --> B[INCOMING MANIFOLD and SAMPLING]
    B --> C[TANK FARM 1: CRUDE STORAGE]
    C --> D[DESALTER PRETREATMENT]
    D --> E[ATMOSPHERIC DISTILLATION UNIT]
    E --> F[PRODUCT FRACTIONATION]
    F --> G[TANK FARM 2: INTERMEDIATE STORAGE]
    G --> H[BLENDING PUMP HOUSE]
    H --> I[ADDITIVE INJECTION SYSTEM]
    I --> J[TANK FARM 3: FINISHED PRODUCT STORAGE]
    J --> K[LOADING ARM GANTRY or TANKER FILLING BAY]
    J --> L[UNDERGROUND PIPELINE NETWORK for Hydrant]
    L --> M[AIRCRAFT REFUELLING at Apron Stands]
    K --> N[TRUCK DISPATCH to Retail Outlets]
    J --> O[VAPOR RECOVERY UNIT VRU]
    C --> P[FIRE WATER PUMP HOUSE]
    J --> P
    P --> Q[FOAM CHAMBERS and SPRINKLER NOZZLES]
    J --> R[BUND WALL CONTAINMENT 110 percent]
    C --> S[LEVEL INSTRUMENTATION and SCADA]
    J --> S
    S --> T[OPERATOR CONTROL ROOM]
```

**Interpretation of Diagram:**
- The three **tank farms** (crude, intermediate, finished) form a vertical integration chain.
- The **fire water pump house** feeds both foam chambers (tank top) and sprinkler nozzles (gantry).
- **SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** is the digital nervous system.
- **Vapor Recovery Units (VRU)** capture gasoline vapors displaced during tanker loading — legally mandated under **CPCB norms**.

## 4.2 Diagram — Cross-Sectional Anatomy of a Floating Roof Tank

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SHELL PLATES with Stiffeners] --> B[DECK PLATE 8 mm steel]
    B --> C[PONTOON COMPARTMENTS 3 sectors]
    C --> D[DOUBLE WIPER RIM SEAL Primary]
    D --> E[SECONDARY SEAL Weather Shield]
    A --> F[SHELL COURSE 1 to 7 Variable Thickness]
    F --> G[BOTTOM PLATE with Slope to Sump]
    G --> H[SUCTION SUMP Dead Stock Zone]
    H --> I[PRODUCT OUTLET NOZZLE Foot Valve]
    A --> J[WATER DRAIN VALVE Pitch Pocket]
    B --> K[ROOF DRAIN SUMP]
    K --> L[FLEXIBLE HOSE to Shell]
    L --> H
    A --> M[WIND GIRDER and RAFTER]
    A --> N[FOAM CHAMBER at Top Course]
    A --> O[GAUGE HATCH and SAMPLE PORT]
    A --> P[INTERNAL LADDERS and GAUGING PIT]
    H --> Q[FOUNDATION RING with SAND PAD]
```

**Interpretation:** The roof literally **rests on the liquid surface** and rises/falls with level changes. The **double rim seal** (primary mechanical wiper + secondary weatherproof envelope) minimizes vapor escape and rainwater ingress — two simultaneous enemies.

## 4.3 Diagram — Bund Wall & Inter-Tank Spacing Layout (Plan View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BUND1[BUND ENCLOSURE A: Three Tanks]
        T1((TANK 1 10,000 m3)) --- T2((TANK 2 10,000 m3))
        T2 --- T3((TANK 3 5,000 m3))
    end
    subgraph BUND2[BUND ENCLOSURE B: Three Tanks]
        T4((TANK 4 20,000 m3)) --- T5((TANK 5 20,000 m3))
        T5 --- T6((TANK 6 10,000 m3))
    end
    BUND1 --- BUND2
    FW[FIRE WATER RESERVOIR 2 hours capacity] --> PUMP[FIRE PUMPS 2x100 percent duty]
    PUMP --> RING[FIRE WATER RING MAIN]
    RING --> BUND1
    RING --> BUND2
    ROAD[PERIPHERAL FIRE ACCESS ROAD 6 m wide] --> BUND1
    ROAD --> BUND2
    ADM[ADMIN BUILDING and CONTROL ROOM] --- BUND1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inter-Tank Spacing Rule (OISD-118):** The distance between two adjacent tank shells must be **at least the diameter of the larger of the two tanks**, or **10 m**, whichever is greater — to prevent thermal radiation from a burning tank igniting its neighbour.

## 4.4 Diagram — Single-Stage Vapor Recovery Unit Working Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TANKER VAPOR SPACE] -->|Vapors displaced by fuel loading| B[VAPOR HOSE and ADAPTER]
    B --> C[VRU INLET HEADER]
    C --> D[VACUUM PUMP or BLOWER]
    D --> E[ABSORPTION COLUMN with Lean Activated Carbon]
    E --> F[REGENERATION via Steam or Vacuum]
    F --> G[DESORBED VAPORS]
    G --> H[BACK TO STORAGE TANK VAPOR SPACE]
    E --> I[CLEAN AIR VENT to Atmosphere 99 percent hydrocarbon free]
```
