# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a fixed roof tank and a floating roof tank. List any two petroleum products best suited for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Fixed Roof Tank | Floating Roof Tank |
|---------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Vapor space | **Large** (air+liquid surface) | **Negligible** (roof rests on liquid) |
| Evaporation loss | High | Very Low (~90% reduction) |
| Design pressure | Slight (a few kPa) | Atmospheric |
| Best-suited products | Diesel, Fuel Oil, Lube oil | Gasoline, Naphtha, Crude, ATF |

*(1 mark — vapor space explanation; 1 mark — product suitability; 1 mark — diagrammatic or formula basis)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define flash point. Why is flash point the most critical parameter for fuel storage safety?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Flash point** is the **lowest temperature at which the vapor above a liquid fuel, when mixed with air, becomes ignitable upon application of a flame source**, but the liquid itself does not sustain combustion (sustained combustion defines the **fire point**, which is 5–10 °C higher).

**Why critical for storage safety:**
1. Determines **Hazard Class A/B/C/D classification** of storage zones.
2. Sets the **minimum safety distance** between tanks and ignition sources.
3. Governs the **type of electrical fittings** (flameproof, intrinsically safe) used in the tank farm.

*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — fire point distinction; 1 mark — three safety applications)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**(a)** Describe with neat sketches the constructional features and working of an **External Floating Roof (EFR) Tank** for gasoline storage. Mention any **seven** essential components and explain how the **double seal** system reduces evaporative losses. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A horizontal cylindrical tank of diameter **3 m** and length **10 m** is filled to a depth of **2.4 m** with diesel ($SG = 0.86$). Calculate the **volume in litres** and the **mass of diesel** stored. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **External Floating Roof (EFR) tank** is a cylindrical steel tank whose roof is free to move vertically along the inside of the shell. It is used primarily for **volatile liquids** such as gasoline, naphtha, and crude oil.

**Seven Essential Components:**

1. **Shell** — Welded carbon steel courses (1 to 7) with progressively thinner plates moving upward (saves steel).
2. **Floating Deck** — Welded steel plate (typically **6–8 mm**) supported by **pontoon compartments** that provide buoyancy.
3. **Pontoon Compartments** — Hollow chambers welded to the deck perimeter that act as floatation collars; must be **leak-tested** for watertightness.
4. **Rim Seal System** — A two-stage seal arrangement:
   - **Primary Seal**: Metallic wiper blade in contact with the shell, blocking ~95% of vapor.
   - **Secondary Seal**: Rubber/fabric envelope with a wiper tip providing weather protection.
5. **Roof Drain Sump** — Collects rainwater; a flexible hose drains it down the shell to a siphon break at grade.
6. **Shoe Seal Support** — Secondary mechanical seal shoes press against the shell during high winds.
7. **Anti-Rotation Cable** — A wire rope attached to the deck and to a guide pole prevents the roof from spinning, which would break the rim seal.

**Working Principle:**
As fuel is pumped in, the roof floats up. As fuel is drawn out, the roof descends. Because there is **no fixed air gap**, vapors do not accumulate, and evaporative losses are reduced by approximately **85–95%** compared to fixed roof tanks. The rim seal accommodates the small annular gap between roof and shell.

*(1 mark per component with neat sketch — 7 marks total)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Given data**
- Diameter $D = 3 \text{ m}$ → radius $R = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- Length $L = 10 \text{ m}$
- Liquid depth $h = 2.4 \text{ m}$ (note: $h > R$, so liquid level is **above the centerline**)
- Specific gravity $SG = 0.86$
- Density of water $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 2 — Formula for partial cylindrical volume**
The volume of liquid in a horizontal cylinder filled to depth $h$ is:

$$V = L \left[ R^2 \cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{R-h}{R}\right) - (R-h)\sqrt{2Rh-h^2} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values**
Since $R - h = 1.5 - 2.4 = -0.9 \text{ m}$, and $2Rh - h^2 = 2(1.5)(2.4) - (2.4)^2 = 7.2 - 5.76 = 1.44$:

$$\dfrac{R-h}{R} = \dfrac{-0.9}{1.5} = -0.6$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the inverse cosine**
$\cos^{-1}(-0.6) = \pi - \cos^{-1}(0.6) = \pi - 0.9273 = 2.2143 \text{ rad}$

**Step 5 — Square root term**
$\sqrt{1.44} = 1.2 \text{ m}$

**Step 6 — Substitute into the volume equation**
$$V = 10 \left[ (1.5)^2 \times 2.2143 - (-0.9)(1.2) \right]$$

$$V = 10 \left[ 2.25 \times 2.2143 + 1.08 \right]$$

$$V = 10 \left[ 4.9822 + 1.08 \right]$$

$$V = 10 \times 6.0622 = 60.62 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 7 — Convert to litres**
$V = 60.62 \text{ m}^3 \times 1000 = 60620 \text{ litres}$

**Step 8 — Calculate mass**
Density of diesel: $\rho = 0.86 \times 1000 = 860 \text{ kg/m}^3$

$$m = \rho \times V = 860 \times 60.62 = 52133.2 \text{ kg} \approx 52.13 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Final Answer:** $V \approx 60{,}620 \text{ litres}$ and $m \approx 52{,}133 \text{ kg}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **Bund (Dike) Wall** in a tank farm. State the **OISD-118** design rules for bund capacity, inter-tank spacing, and number of tanks per bund. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An aviation fuel tank has a gross volume of **15,000 m³**. If the **dead-stock fraction is 4%** and the **product density is 0.79 g/cm³**, calculate the **net volume** in **barrels** and the **net mass** in **tonnes**. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Concept of a Bund Wall:**
A **bund (dike)** is an impervious embankment, masonry wall, or compacted earth barrier built around a group of storage tanks to contain **spilled or leaked fuel** in case of catastrophic tank failure. It is the **last line of defence** against uncontrolled pool fires and soil/groundwater contamination.

**OISD-118 Design Rules:**

1. **Bund Capacity Rule:** The effective volumetric capacity of the bund must be **at least 110%** of the volume of the **largest single tank** enclosed within the bund. For example, if the largest tank is 20,000 m³, the bund capacity $\ge 22{,}000$ m³.

2. **Number of Tanks per Bund:** No more than **6 tanks** of the largest size class, or **10 tanks** of smaller size class, are permitted in any single bund. The bund must be subdivided (compartmentalized) if this limit is exceeded.

3. **Inter-Tank Spacing:**
   - Minimum shell-to-shell distance = **diameter of the larger of the two adjacent tanks**, OR
   - **10 metres**, whichever is greater.
   - Between two tanks of equal diameter, spacing is therefore $D$ or 10 m, whichever is greater.

4. **Bund Wall Slope and Freeboard:** The internal face of an earthen bund must have a slope no steeper than **1 : 1.5 (vertical : horizontal)**; the top of the bund must be at least **600 mm above the calculated liquid level** of any spill.

5. **Bund Material and Impermeability:** The bund floor and wall must be **impermeable** to hydrocarbons — achieved by a **compacted clay lining** with a **geo-membrane** (HDPE) underlay, or **concrete** with a chemical-resistant coating.

*(1 mark — definition with diagram; 6 marks — 5 design rules ≈ 1.2 each)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Gross volume**
$$V_{gross} = 15000 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply dead-stock factor (4% deduction)**

$$V_{net} = V_{gross} \times (1 - 0.04) = 15000 \times 0.96$$

$$V_{net} = 14400 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Convert to barrels**
Using the standard conversion $1 \text{ m}^3 = 6.2898 \text{ bbl}$:

$$V_{net} = 14400 \times 6.2898 = 90573.12 \text{ barrels}$$

**Step 4 — Density conversion**
Given $\rho = 0.79 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 790 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Step 5 — Mass calculation**

$$m_{net} = \rho \times V_{net} = 790 \times 14400$$

$$m_{net} = 11{,}376{,}000 \text{ kg} = 11376 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Final Answer:** Net volume = **90,573 bbl**, Net mass = **11,376 tonnes**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Unit slip-ups:** Students often forget to convert m³ → litres (× 1000) or m³ → barrels (× 6.2898). A wrong conversion is treated as a **full-step error** and costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Dead-stock vs working stock:** Be explicit — the question is about *usable* (net) volume, not geometric gross volume.
> 3. **Bund capacity:** Saying "110% of the tank volume" is the rule of **the largest tank**, not the sum of all tanks — examiners specifically test this nuance.
> 4. **Floating roof components:** Students often forget the **anti-rotation cable** — losing 1 mark.
> 5. **Flash point vs Fire point:** Conflating these two is a common 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Storage is classified by vapor pressure of the fuel** — fixed roof for low-VP, floating roof for high-VP.
- **API 650** governs atmospheric tanks; **API 620** governs low-pressure refrigerated tanks.
- **Inter-tank spacing = diameter of larger tank or 10 m, whichever is greater** (OISD-118).
- **Bund capacity = 110% of largest tank's volume** within the bund.
- **Maximum 6 large or 10 small tanks per bund** (compartmentalization required beyond).
- **Dead-stock reserve = 5% of gross volume** in standard design.
- **Rim seal = primary (metallic wiper) + secondary (envelope)** = 95%+ vapor containment.
- **Foam chambers** at the top course of every floating roof tank rim — fed by fire water ring main.
- **Lightening protection** as per IS/IEC 62305 — every tank must have a **down-conductor and earth pit**.
- **Vapor Recovery Units (VRU)** are mandatory for gasoline loading bays per CPCB.
- **Strategic Petroleum Reserves** in India: Visakhapatnam, Mangalore, Padur (rock cavern type, 5.33 MMT).
- **Density ↔ Volume ↔ Mass** conversions are tested in **every** numerical question.
- **1 m³ of oil = 6.2898 barrels = 35.315 cubic feet**.
- **API Gravity > 10** floats on water; **API Gravity < 10** sinks — important for spill behaviour.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Storage and Handling of Liquid Fuels**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties and Testing of Petroleum Products
Liquid Fuel from Coal: Bergius and Fischer-Tropsch Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Liquid Fuel from Coal: Bergius and Fischer-Tropsch Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Liquid Fuels from Coal — Foundational Concept

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
Liquid fuels from coal refer to synthetic liquid hydrocarbons (gasoline, diesel, kerosene) produced by chemically converting solid coal through high-pressure hydrogenation or catalytic synthesis routes. Two industrially significant processes are the **Bergius Process** (direct hydrogenation) and the **Fischer-Tropsch Process** (indirect synthesis via syngas).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** India has limited petroleum reserves but abundant coal (~300 billion tonnes). Coal-to-liquid (CTL) technology is a strategic fuel security pathway studied under Module 2 of Fuel Engineering.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a hard, dark lump of coal that cannot power a car engine directly. Now picture two "kitchen recipes" to transform this coal into a smooth liquid fuel:

- **Recipe 1 — Bergius (Direct Hydrogenation):** Take coal, grind it into powder, mix it with heavy oil to form a thick paste (like making a curry paste), and then force **hydrogen gas** into it under extreme pressure (~250 atm) and heat (~480°C) inside a sealed reactor. Hydrogen atoms literally "crack open" the large coal molecules and "saturate" them into smaller, liquid hydrocarbon chains. Think of it as **pressure-cooking coal with hydrogen**.

- **Recipe 2 — Fischer-Tropsch (Indirect via Syngas):** First, "burn" the coal in a controlled way with steam and oxygen to convert it into a **gaseous mixture called synthesis gas (syngas)** — a blend of **CO** and **H₂**. Then, in a separate reactor, pass this syngas over a metallic catalyst (Iron or Cobalt) at moderate conditions. The CO and H₂ molecules "stitch together" like Lego blocks, growing one carbon atom at a time to build liquid hydrocarbon chains. Think of it as **assembling fuel molecule-by-molecule from building-block gases**.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Bergius Process | Fischer-Tropsch Process |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Temperature | **450–500 °C** | **200–350 °C** |
| Operating Pressure | **200–700 atm** | **1–40 atm** |
| Catalyst | **Fe₂O₃, Sn, Mo, Ni** | **Fe, Co, Ru** |
| Coal State Required | Powdered / paste | Gasified (syngas) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pressure Unit Reminder:** 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar. Bergius operates at pressures ~200× normal atmospheric pressure.

---

### 1.4 Strategic Importance

Both processes were commercialized in **Germany during the 1920s–1930s** to overcome petroleum shortages. Today, **South Africa (Sasol)**, **China (Shenhua)**, and **Qatar (Pearl GTL)** operate mega-plants using variants of these technologies, validating their industrial maturity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bergius Process — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Coal Preparation:** Bituminous coal is crushed and ground to a fine powder (typically <0.1 mm particle size) to maximize surface area.

**Step 2 — Paste Formation:** The powdered coal is mixed with **heavy recycle oil** (a process-derived heavy fraction) in a ratio of approximately **1:1 to 1:2 (coal:oil)** to form a pumpable paste.

**Step 3 — Hydrogenation Reactor:** The paste is pumped into a high-pressure hydrogenation reactor (Hydrogenation Unit). Pure **H₂ gas** at ~250 atm is introduced.

**Step 4 — Catalytic Conversion:** At **450–500 °C**, with **Fe₂O₃/Sn/Mo catalysts**, hydrogen atoms attack the aromatic and aliphatic structures of coal, breaking C–C and C=O bonds and saturating free valencies with H.

**Step 5 — Product Separation:** The reactor effluent is cooled and depressurized. Products are fractionated into:
- **Gasoline** (C₅–C₁₁)
- **Middle Oil / Diesel** (C₁₂–C₂₀)
- **Heavy Oil** (recycled back as paste medium)
- **Unreacted gases**

**Net Simplified Reaction:**

$$nC_{(coal)} + (n+1)H_2 \xrightarrow{Fe_2O_3,\, 480°C,\, 250\,atm} C_nH_{2n+2}$$

For aromatic coal structures, the reaction also liberates heteroatoms (S, N, O) as **H₂S, NH₃, H₂O**, which are removed.

---

### 2.2 The Fischer-Tropsch Process — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Coal Gasification (Syngas Generation):**
Coal reacts with steam and oxygen in a gasifier:

$$C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2 \quad \Delta H = +131.3\,kJ/mol$$

$$C + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO \quad \Delta H = -111\,kJ/mol$$

The resulting **syngas** is a mixture of **CO + H₂**, ideally in a **H₂:CO ratio of 2:1** for FT synthesis.

**Step 2 — Water-Gas Shift (WGS) for H₂/CO tuning:**

$$CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H = -41.2\,kJ/mol$$

This adjusts the H₂:CO ratio if needed and removes CO as CO₂.

**Step 3 — Gas Purification:** Sulfur compounds (H₂S, COS) are removed down to **<0.1 ppm** because sulfur is a **permanent poison** for FT catalysts.

**Step 4 — FT Synthesis Reactor:** Clean syngas passes over **Fe or Co catalyst** at **200–350 °C** and **10–40 atm**. Hydrocarbon chains grow via surface polymerization (Anderson-Schulz-Flory distribution).

**Step 5 — Product Workup:** Waxy products are hydrocracked to yield **gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, and waxes**.

**Net Reactions (Paraffin and Olefin):**

$$(2n+1)H_2 + nCO \rightarrow C_nH_{2n+2} + nH_2O \quad \text{(Paraffins)}$$

$$2nH_2 + nCO \rightarrow C_nH_{2n} + nH_2O \quad \text{(Olefins)}$$

$$2nH_2 + nCO \rightarrow C_nH_{2n+1}OH + (n-1)H_2O \quad \text{(Alcohols)}$$

---

### 2.3 Why These Reactions Work — The "How" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Bergius — Why high pressure?** Hydrogenation requires hydrogen to dissolve and react with coal's dense aromatic matrix. Le Chatelier's principle dictates that high H₂ pressure shifts equilibrium toward hydrogenated (liquid) products.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fischer-Tropsch — Why Fe or Co?** These transition metals have partially filled d-orbitals that chemisorb CO and H₂ dissociatively, enabling stepwise chain propagation (–CH₂– units).

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Process | Description | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| $nC + (n+1)H_2 \rightarrow C_nH_{2n+2}$ | Bergius | Direct hydrogenation | 450–500 °C, 200–700 atm, Fe₂O₃ cat. |
| $C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$ | FT Step 1 | Steam gasification | 800–1100 °C, endothermic |
| $C + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO$ | FT Step 1 | Partial oxidation | Exothermic, supplies heat |
| $CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2$ | FT Step 2 | Water-gas shift | 200–450 °C, Fe/Cu cat. |
| $(2n+1)H_2 + nCO \rightarrow C_nH_{2n+2} + nH_2O$ | FT Step 4 | Paraffin formation | 200–350 °C, 10–40 atm, Fe/Co |
| $2nH_2 + nCO \rightarrow C_nH_{2n} + nH_2O$ | FT Step 4 | Olefin formation | Same conditions |
| $W_n = (1-\alpha)^2 \cdot \alpha^{n-1}$ | FT distribution | Anderson-Schulz-Flory | Chain growth probability $\alpha$ |
| $H_2:CO = 2:1$ | FT feed | Stoichiometric ideal | Adjusted via WGS |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The Anderson-Schulz-Flory (ASF) distribution governs product selectivity in FT reactors. By tuning **temperature, pressure, H₂:CO ratio, and catalyst**, engineers can bias production toward gasoline ($\alpha \approx 0.7$) or diesel/wax ($\alpha \approx 0.9$).

---

### 2.5 Comparative Analysis — Bergius vs. Fischer-Tropsch

| Feature | Bergius | Fischer-Tropsch |
|---|---|---|
| Liquefaction Type | **Direct** | **Indirect (via syngas)** |
| Coal Requirement | Bituminous preferred | Any rank (lignite to anthracite) |
| Operating Pressure | Very high (200–700 atm) | Moderate (10–40 atm) |
| Operating Temperature | High (450–500 °C) | Moderate (200–350 °C) |
| Catalyst Cost | Moderate (Fe₂O₃) | High (Co preferred, but Fe cheaper) |
| Product Slate | Narrow (mostly gasoline/diesel) | Wide (gasoline, diesel, waxes, olefins) |
| Sulfur Tolerance | Lower | Very poor (strict <0.1 ppm) |
| Capital Cost | Very High | High |
| Major Plant | Germany (1940s), closed | **Sasol (South Africa), Pearl GTL (Qatar)** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Hydrogen Requirement for Bergius Process

**Starting Assumption:** Bituminous coal has the empirical formula **C₁₀₀H₆₀O₁₀** (typical moisture/ash-free).

**Step 1 — Identify target product:** Gasoline ≈ **C₈H₁₈** (octane).

**Step 2 — Set up overall hydrogenation reaction:**

$$C_{100}H_{60}O_{10} + a\,H_2 \rightarrow b\,C_8H_{18} + c\,H_2O$$

**Step 3 — Apply atomic balance for Carbon:**
$$100 = 8b \implies b = 12.5$$

**Step 4 — Apply atomic balance for Oxygen:**
$$10 = c \implies c = 10$$

**Step 5 — Apply atomic balance for Hydrogen:**
$$60 + 2a = 18b + 2c$$
$$60 + 2a = 18(12.5) + 2(10)$$
$$60 + 2a = 225 + 20 = 245$$
$$2a = 185 \implies a = 92.5$$

**Step 6 — Final stoichiometric equation:**

$$C_{100}H_{60}O_{10} + 92.5\,H_2 \rightarrow 12.5\,C_8H_{18} + 10\,H_2O$$

**Step 7 — Mass of H₂ required per kg of coal:**
$$M_{coal} = 100(12) + 60(1) + 10(16) = 1420\,g/mol$$
$$M_{H_2} = 92.5 \times 2 = 185\,g\,H_2/mol\,coal$$

$$\text{Mass ratio} = \frac{185}{1420} = 0.130$$

$$\boxed{\text{H}_2\,\text{required} \approx 0.13\,kg\,H_2\,\text{per kg of coal (MAF)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Industrial Bergius plants required ~130 kg of H₂ per 1000 kg of coal. Producing this H₂ by coal gasification consumed ~50% of plant throughput — a major economic drawback that led to the dominance of Fischer-Tropsch post-1945.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Ideal Syngas Ratio for FT Synthesis

**Step 1 — General paraffin synthesis:**
$$(2n+1)H_2 + nCO \rightarrow C_nH_{2n+2} + nH_2O$$

**Step 2 — Ratio of H₂ to CO required:**
$$\frac{H_2}{CO} = \frac{2n+1}{n} = 2 + \frac{1}{n}$$

**Step 3 — Limit behavior:**
- For **large n** (heavy waxes, e.g., n = 30):
$$\frac{H_2}{CO} \rightarrow 2$$
- For **small n** (light gases, e.g., n = 1, methane):
$$\frac{H_2}{CO} = 3$$

**Step 4 — Industrial design conclusion:** A practical syngas feed uses:

$$\boxed{\frac{H_2}{CO} \approx 2.0\text{–}2.3}$$

This is the **design ratio** for fixed-bed FT reactors in commercial Sasol plants.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Anderson-Schulz-Flory (ASF) Distribution

The ASF distribution gives the **mass fraction (Wₙ)** of hydrocarbon with carbon number **n**:

$$W_n = n(1-\alpha)^2 \alpha^{n-1}$$

**Where:**
- $\alpha$ = chain growth probability factor
- $n$ = carbon number
- $W_n$ = mass fraction of Cₙ product

**Derivation (logic, not full statistical mechanics):**

**Step 1 — Surface polymerization assumption:** A growing chain on the catalyst surface can either:
- Terminate with probability $(1-\alpha)$ → yields product Cₙ
- Continue growing with probability $\alpha$ → adds one CH₂ unit

**Step 2 — Probability of forming an n-carbon chain:**

The chain reaches length n if it grew n−1 times and then terminated once:

$$P_n = \alpha^{n-1}(1-\alpha)$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mole fraction:**

Total moles of product ≈ sum of all chain lengths (assuming long chains):

$$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} P_n = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \alpha^{n-1}(1-\alpha) = 1$$

**Step 4 — Mass fraction by multiplying Pₙ by n (molecular weight ~ n·14):**

$$\boxed{W_n = n(1-\alpha)^2 \alpha^{n-1}}$$

**Step 5 — Selectivity implications:**

Setting $\frac{dW_n}{dn} = 0$ yields the carbon number of maximum yield:

$$n_{max} = \frac{-1}{\ln \alpha}$$

| $\alpha$ value | $n_{max}$ | Dominant Product |
|---|---|---|
| 0.5 | 1.4 | CH₄ (gas) |
| 0.7 | 2.9 | Gasoline |
| 0.85 | 6.1 | Diesel |
| 0.95 | 19.5 | Waxes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common KTU question asks to compute Wₙ for a given α. Practice with α = 0.7 and n = 5, 8, 12.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Bergius Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Coal Crushing and Drying Unit] --> B[Heavy Oil Mixing Tank]
    B --> C[Coal-Oil Paste Pump]
    C --> D[Pre-Heater Furnace]
    D --> E[Hydrogenation Reactor at 480 C and 250 atm with Fe2O3 Catalyst]
    E --> F[Hot Separator]
    F --> G[Cooling and Pressure Reduction]
    G --> H[Distillation Column]
    H --> I[Gasoline Product C5 to C11]
    H --> J[Diesel Product C12 to C20]
    H --> K[Heavy Oil Recycle to Mixing Tank]
    H --> L[Unreacted Gas Stream]
    L --> M[H2 Purification and Recycle]
```

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fischer-Tropsch Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Coal Feed Hopper] --> B[Gasifier with O2 and Steam at 1000 C]
    B --> C[Raw Syngas CO plus H2 plus CO2 plus H2S]
    C --> D[Heat Recovery Boiler]
    D --> E[Water-Gas Shift Reactor]
    E --> F[Acid Gas Removal H2S and CO2 Scrubbing]
    F --> G[Syngas Compressor to 30 atm]
    G --> H[FT Synthesis Reactor with Fe or Co Catalyst at 250 C]
    H --> I[Product Condenser]
    I --> J[Three-Phase Separator]
    J --> K[Liquid Hydrocarbon Product]
    J --> L[Aqueous Phase Water and Alcohols]
    J --> M[Tail Gas Recycle]
    K --> N[Hydrocracker Unit]
    N --> O[Distillation Column]
    O --> P[Gasoline]
    O --> Q[Diesel]
    O --> R[Jet Fuel]
    O --> S[Waxes]
```

---

### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Bergius vs. Fischer-Tropsch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BergiusRoute [BERGIUS DIRECT ROUTE]
        B1[Coal] --> B2[Coal plus Heavy Oil Paste]
        B2 --> B3[Direct Hydrogenation at High Pressure]
        B3 --> B4[Liquid Fuels]
    end
    subgraph FischerRoute [FISCHER-TROPSCH INDIRECT ROUTE]
        F1[Coal] --> F2[Gasification to Syngas]
        F2 --> F3[Syngas Cleanup]
        F3 --> F4[Catalytic FT Synthesis]
        F4 --> F5[Liquid Fuels]
    end
```

---

### 4.4 Process Parameter Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Process Parameters Influencing Product Slate] --> T[Temperature]
    P --> Pr[Pressure]
    P --> R[H2 to CO Ratio]
    P --> Cat[Catalyst Type]
    P --> S[Space Velocity]
    T --> T1[Low T favors high molecular weight waxes]
    T --> T2[High T favors methane and light gases]
    Pr --> Pr1[High P favors liquid products]
    R --> R1[Low ratio favors olefins]
    R --> R2[High ratio favors paraffins]
    Cat --> Cat1[Fe favors olefins and WGS]
    Cat --> Cat2[Co favors paraffins higher activity]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Distinguish between direct and indirect coal liquefaction processes. Give one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Direct Liquefaction | Indirect Liquefaction |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Coal is directly reacted with hydrogen at high T and P | Coal is first gasified to syngas, then converted to liquids |
| Intermediate State | Solid/liquid (paste) | Gaseous (CO + H₂) |
| Example | **Bergius Process** | **Fischer-Tropsch Process** |
| Pressure Required | Very high (200–700 atm) | Moderate (10–40 atm) |

**Key contrast:** Direct route **skips** the gasification step, while indirect route **requires** syngas as an intermediate.

---

**Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*Write a short note on the role of the catalyst in the Fischer-Tropsch process.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The FT process uses transition metal catalysts — primarily **Iron (Fe)** and **Cobalt (Co)** — to convert syngas (CO + H₂) into liquid hydrocarbons. These metals possess partially filled d-orbitals that facilitate:
1. **Dissociative chemisorption** of H₂ and CO on the metal surface.
2. **Stepwise chain propagation** via surface –CH₂– units (polymerization mechanism).
3. **Product selectivity control:** Iron favors olefins and WGS activity; Cobalt gives higher activity and longer-chain paraffins.

Catalyst poisoning by **sulfur compounds** is severe — feed purification to <0.1 ppm H₂S is mandatory.

---

### PART B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Bergius process** for the manufacture of liquid fuels from coal. Discuss the role of temperature, pressure, and catalyst. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A coal sample has the empirical formula **C₁₀₀H₅₅O₈** (MAF basis). Calculate the mass of hydrogen required to convert 1 kg of this coal into gasoline (C₈H₁₈) by direct hydrogenation. Also calculate the mass of water produced. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Bergius Process Explanation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Coal Preparation (1 mark):**
Bituminous coal is crushed to <0.1 mm and dried to <2% moisture.

**Step 2 — Paste Formation (1 mark):**
Coal is mixed with **heavy recycle oil** (1:1 to 1:2 ratio) to form a pumpable slurry.

**Step 3 — Hydrogenation (2 marks):**
The paste is pumped with H₂ into a high-pressure reactor at:
- **Temperature:** 450–500 °C
- **Pressure:** 200–700 atm (typically 250 atm)
- **Catalyst:** Fe₂O₃, Sn, Mo compounds

**Net Reaction:**
$$C_{coal} + H_2 \xrightarrow{cat} \text{Liquid Hydrocarbons} + H_2O + H_2S + NH_3$$

**Step 4 — Product Recovery (2 marks):**
Distillation yields gasoline, diesel, and heavy oil (recycled).

**Step 5 — Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Set up reaction:**

$$C_{100}H_{55}O_8 + a\,H_2 \rightarrow b\,C_8H_{18} + c\,H_2O$$

**Step 2 — Carbon balance:**
$$100 = 8b \implies b = 12.5$$

**Step 3 — Oxygen balance:**
$$8 = c \implies c = 8$$

**Step 4 — Hydrogen balance:**
$$55 + 2a = 18b + 2c$$
$$55 + 2a = 18(12.5) + 2(8)$$
$$55 + 2a = 225 + 16 = 241$$
$$2a = 186 \implies a = 93$$

**Step 5 — Molar masses:**
$$M_{coal} = 100(12) + 55(1) + 8(16) = 1200 + 55 + 128 = 1383\,g/mol$$
$$M_{H_2} = 93 \times 2 = 186\,g$$
$$M_{H_2O} = 8 \times 18 = 144\,g$$

**Step 6 — Scale to 1 kg of coal:**
$$m_{H_2} = \frac{186}{1383} \times 1000 = \boxed{134.5\,g\,H_2\,\text{per kg coal}}$$

$$m_{H_2O} = \frac{144}{1383} \times 1000 = \boxed{104.1\,g\,H_2O\,\text{per kg coal}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **Fischer-Tropsch process** with a flow diagram for converting coal to liquid fuels. Discuss the role of syngas composition, catalyst, and operating conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Anderson-Schulz-Flory distribution, calculate the mass fraction of C₅, C₈, and C₁₂ hydrocarbons for a Fischer-Tropsch reactor operating at chain growth probability **α = 0.75**. Comment on which product dominates. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Fischer-Tropsch Process Explanation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Gasification (2 marks):**
Coal reacts with O₂ and H₂O at 800–1100 °C:
$$C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$$
$$C + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO$$

**Step 2 — Syngas Cleanup (1 mark):**
Water-gas shift adjusts H₂:CO ratio to ~2:1. Sulfur removal to <0.1 ppm.

**Step 3 — FT Synthesis (2 marks):**
- **Catalyst:** Fe or Co
- **Temperature:** 200–350 °C
- **Pressure:** 10–40 atm
- **Reaction:** $(2n+1)H_2 + nCO \rightarrow C_nH_{2n+2} + nH_2O$

**Step 4 — Product Workup (1 mark):**
Waxes hydrocracked; distillation to gasoline, diesel, jet fuel.

**Step 5 — Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.2 block diagram.

---

**Part (b) — ASF Calculation (7 marks):**

**Formula:**
$$W_n = n(1-\alpha)^2 \alpha^{n-1}$$

Given: $\alpha = 0.75 \implies (1-\alpha) = 0.25 \implies (1-\alpha)^2 = 0.0625$

**For n = 5 (C₅):**
$$W_5 = 5 \times 0.0625 \times (0.75)^4$$
$$(0.75)^4 = 0.3164$$
$$W_5 = 5 \times 0.0625 \times 0.3164 = 0.0989 \approx \boxed{9.89\%}$$

**For n = 8 (C₈):**
$$W_8 = 8 \times 0.0625 \times (0.75)^7$$
$$(0.75)^7 = 0.1335$$
$$W_8 = 8 \times 0.0625 \times 0.1335 = 0.0668 \approx \boxed{6.68\%}$$

**For n = 12 (C₁₂):**
$$W_{12} = 12 \times 0.0625 \times (0.75)^{11}$$
$$(0.75)^{11} = 0.04224$$
$$W_{12} = 12 \times 0.0625 \times 0.04224 = 0.0317 \approx \boxed{3.17\%}$$

**Step 5 — Locate the maximum:**
$$n_{max} = \frac{-1}{\ln(0.75)} = \frac{-1}{-0.2877} = 3.48$$

**Conclusion:** The peak product is around **C₃–C₄** (light gases). For **α = 0.75**, the distribution favors **lighter hydrocarbons**, indicating gasoline-range production is feasible but methane selectivity is also significant. Higher α (>0.85) would be needed to bias toward diesel/wax range.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing H₂:CO ratios:** For FT paraffin synthesis it is **(2n+1):n**, not 2:1 in general — the 2:1 ratio applies only in the **large n limit**.
> 2. **Confusing Bergius and FT pressures:** Many students write "high pressure" for both. Bergius is 200+ atm; FT is only 10–40 atm.
> 3. **Forgetting sulfur poisoning in FT:** Always mention syngas desulfurization for FT — this is a high-frequency KTU question.
> 4. **ASF formula misapplication:** Wₙ is the **mass fraction**, not mole fraction — students often confuse these.
> 5. **Catalyst name errors:** Bergius uses Fe₂O₃/Sn/Mo (not Fe metal); FT uses Fe or Co metal (not oxide).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- ⭐ **Bergius Process:** **Direct** hydrogenation of coal at **450–500 °C, 200–700 atm**, with **Fe₂O₃/Sn/Mo** catalyst. Requires huge H₂ (~130 kg H₂ per 1000 kg coal).
- ⭐ **Fischer-Tropsch Process:** **Indirect** route via **syngas (CO + H₂)**, with **Fe or Co** catalyst at **200–350 °C, 10–40 atm**.
- ⭐ **Syngas Generation:** C + H₂O → CO + H₂ (endothermic, primary route).
- ⭐ **Water-Gas Shift:** CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂ (used to tune H₂:CO ratio).
- ⭐ **Ideal H₂:CO Feed Ratio:** ≈ **2:1** (for high-n paraffins).
- ⭐ **ASF Distribution:** $W_n = n(1-\alpha)^2 \alpha^{n-1}$; **α controls product slate** (gasoline at α ≈ 0.7; diesel at α ≈ 0.85; wax at α ≈ 0.95).
- ⭐ **Sulfur is fatal** for FT catalysts — purification to **<0.1 ppm H₂S** mandatory.
- ⭐ **Major commercial plants:** Sasol (South Africa), Pearl GTL (Qatar — natural gas-based FT), Shenhua (China — direct coal liquefaction Bergius variant).
- ⭐ **Historical origin:** Bergius (1913, Nobel 1931); Fischer-Tropsch (1925, Germany).
- ⭐ **Engineering trade-off:** Bergius gives higher-quality gasoline directly but at extreme pressure; FT gives broader product range at milder conditions but requires gasifier.
- ⭐ **India relevance:** Coal reserves ~300 Bt; ~14 CTL projects under consideration as of 2024.

### Common KTU Keywords to Use in Answers

> "Sasol-type slurry-bed reactor," "chain growth probability (α)," "Anderson-Schulz-Flory distribution," "syngas," "water-gas shift," "high-pressure hydrogenation," "catalyst poisoning," "liquid hydrocarbon yield," "direct vs indirect liquefaction."

### Memory Hook for Bergius vs FT

> **B**ergius = **B**ig Pressure, **B**lended coal paste
> **F**ischer-Tropsch = **F**lowing gas (syngas), **F**riendly (lower) pressure

---

*End of KTU Module 2 Notes — Liquid Fuel from Coal: Bergius and Fischer-Tropsch Process*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Other Synthetic Liquid Fuels

## 1. Introduction & Core Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Synthetic liquid fuels are liquid hydrocarbons (or hydrocarbon-bearing liquids) manufactured from sources other than crude petroleum through chemical conversion processes such as hydrogenation, destructive distillation, fermentation, blending, or colloidal suspension. They supplement or substitute conventional petroleum fuels in IC engines, industrial furnaces, and boilers.

The five major "Other Synthetic Liquid Fuels" mandated by the KTU Fuel Engineering syllabus are:

| S.No | Synthetic Fuel | Source / Origin | Key Process |
|:----:|:---------------|:----------------|:------------|
| 1 | **Benzol** | Coal / Coke Oven Gas | Recovery of Benzene + Blending |
| 2 | **Shale Oil** | Oil Shale Rock | Destructive Distillation (Retorting) |
| 3 | **Gashol** | Gasoline + Alcohol | Physical Blending |
| 4 | **Power Alcohol** | Biomass (Sugars/Starch) | Fermentation + Denaturation |
| 5 | **Colloidal Fuel** | Coal + Fuel Oil | Colloidal Suspension |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
> Imagine your kitchen runs out of cooking gas (crude petroleum). You don't just sit hungry — you find *alternative* fuels: dried cow dung cakes (biomass → power alcohol), coal powder mixed with kerosene (colloidal fuel), or fermented sugar solution (ethanol → gashol). Synthetic liquid fuels are exactly this engineering mindset applied on an industrial scale: when crude oil reserves shrink, engineers *manufacture* usable liquid fuels from coal, rock, biomass, or even from coal dust suspended in oil.

---

### 1.1 Benzol — Definition

**Benzol** is a blended motor fuel consisting predominantly of **benzene (C₆H₆)** along with minor aromatic homologues (toluene, xylene) recovered as a by-product from **coke-oven gas** or **coal-gas** during the carbonisation of coal.

> [!NOTE]
> - Chemical formula of pure benzene: **C₆H₆**
> - Molecular weight: **78 g/mol**
> - Density: **0.876 g/cm³** at 20 °C
> - Boiling point: **80.1 °C**
> - Calorific value: approximately **40 200 kJ/kg**

A "Benzol blend" typically used as engine fuel contains:
- **Benzene**: 50–70 %
- **Toluene**: 10–25 %
- **Xylene**: 5–15 %
- **Higher aromatics / wash oil residues**: balance

### 1.2 Shale Oil — Definition

**Shale oil** is a crude liquid hydrocarbon obtained by the **destructive distillation (pyrolysis)** of fine-grained sedimentary rock called **oil shale**, which contains a solid organic matter known as **kerogen**.

> [!NOTE]
> - Source rock: **Oil shale** (marlstone-type sedimentary)
> - Organic content: **Kerogen** (insoluble solid bitumen precursor)
> - Calorific value of crude shale oil: **35 000 – 42 000 kJ/kg**
> - Density: **0.85 – 0.95 g/cm³**

### 1.3 Gashol — Definition

**Gashol** is a **petrol–alcohol blend** (typically gasoline blended with absolute ethanol in the ratio 90 : 10 or 80 : 20) used as an automotive spark-ignition engine fuel.

> [!NOTE]
> - Common commercial ratio: **Gasoline 90 % + Ethanol 10 %** (also called **E10**)
> - Ethanol used must be **anhydrous (absolute)**, i.e., 99.5 %+ purity
> - Octane rating boost: **+2 to +4 RON** over base gasoline

### 1.4 Power Alcohol — Definition

**Power alcohol** is **rectified/absolute ethyl alcohol (ethanol, C₂H₅OH)** denatured with a small percentage of pyridine, naphtha, or methanol to render it **unpotable**, and is used as a fuel in spark-ignition engines either alone or blended with gasoline.

> [!NOTE]
> - Chemical formula: **C₂H₅OH**
> - Molecular weight: **46 g/mol**
> - Calorific value: **29 700 kJ/kg** (roughly 65 % of gasoline)
> - Latent heat of vaporisation: **842 kJ/kg** (very high → good charge cooling)
> - Octane number: **108 RON** (very high)

### 1.5 Colloidal Fuel — Definition

**Colloidal fuel** is a fluid mixture in which **finely pulverised coal** (or sometimes coke) is suspended uniformly in **fuel oil** through the use of a chemical **stabiliser/surfactant**, so that the solid does not settle out under normal storage.

> [!NOTE]
> - Typical composition: **Coal powder 30–50 %** + **Fuel oil 50–70 %** + **Stabiliser ~1 %**
> - Particle size of coal: **less than 60 microns (μm)**
> - Calorific value: **35 000 – 42 000 kJ/kg**

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Benzol — Production, Properties & Applications

### 2.1.1 Source and Recovery

Benzol is recovered from **coke-oven gas** (produced during the destructive distillation of coal at **900–1100 °C** in coke ovens).

The recovery sequence is:

1. **Cooling** of hot coke-oven gas.
2. **Scrubbing with wash oil** (a high-boiling petroleum fraction, b.p. 230–300 °C) — this absorbs benzene, toluene, xylene (BTX).
3. **Stripping** of the rich wash oil by steam — releases crude benzol.
4. **Fractional distillation** of crude benzol → **90 % benzol**, **pure benzene**, **toluene**, **xylenes**.

### 2.1.2 Properties (High-Yield Table)

| Property | Value | Engineering Significance |
|:---------|:------|:--------------------------|
| Specific gravity | 0.876 | Lighter than water → floats during spills |
| Calorific value | 40 200 kJ/kg | Higher than gasoline (~44 000 kJ/kg comparable) |
| Octane number | ~108 RON | Excellent anti-knock |
| Flash point | ~12 °C (closed cup) | Highly flammable — safety hazard |
| Latent heat of vaporisation | ~394 kJ/kg | Lower than gasoline → easier cold start |
| Aromatic content | 99 %+ | High soot, high luminosity flame |

### 2.1.3 Uses

- **Aviation fuel** blending (during WWI & WWII; "pool petrol" of UK).
- High-octane petrol blending.
- Chemical feedstock for aniline, phenol, nylon.

---

## 2.2 Shale Oil — Production, Properties & Applications

### 2.2.1 Origin

Oil shale is sedimentary rock of Marlstone type containing **kerogen** — a solid, insoluble organic polymer. When heated in absence of air, kerogen decomposes into:

$$\text{Kerogen} \xrightarrow{\;\Delta\;(\text{absence of air})\;} \text{Shale Oil} + \text{Shale Gas} + \text{Carbon Residue}$$

### 2.2.2 Extraction Methods

**A. Surface Retorting (Above-Ground)**

The mined oil shale is crushed and heated in a retort at **450–550 °C**.

Main commercial processes:

1. **Kiviter Process** (Estonia) — internal combustion retort, 700 °C combustion zone.
2. **Petrosix Process** (Brazil) — vertical retort, yields ~10 % oil by weight.
3. **Tosco-II Process** (USA) — uses hot ceramic balls for heat transfer.

**B. In-Situ Retorting (Below-Ground)**

Underground heating without mining — used for deep deposits.
Examples: **Shell In-situ Conversion Process (ICP)**, **Chevron CRUSH**.

### 2.2.3 Properties of Crude Shale Oil

| Property | Shale Oil | Conventional Crude |
|:---------|:----------|:-------------------|
| Specific gravity | 0.85 – 0.95 | 0.80 – 0.95 |
| Sulphur content | 0.5 – 2 % | 0.1 – 3 % |
| Nitrogen content | 0.5 – 2 % (high) | 0.1 – 0.5 % |
| Pour point | 15 – 30 °C (high) | –30 to 0 °C |
| Calorific value | 38 000 – 42 000 kJ/kg | 42 000 – 45 000 kJ/kg |
| Wax / asphaltene | Variable | Variable |

> [!NOTE]
> Shale oil typically has **higher nitrogen and oxygen content** than petroleum crude. This necessitates **hydrotreating** before refining.

---

## 2.3 Gashol — Production, Properties & Applications

### 2.3.1 Production Principle

Gashol is made by **simple physical blending** of anhydrous ethanol with gasoline, accompanied by a small percentage of a **co-solvent** (often an aliphatic alcohol or ether such as **TBA — tert-butyl alcohol** or **MTBE**) to prevent **phase separation** in the presence of trace water.

### 2.3.2 Key Formulas & Concepts

**A. Octane Enhancement Formula**

$$\text{Octane Number of Blend (ON}_{\text{blend}}\text{)} = V_{\text{eth}} \cdot \text{ON}_{\text{eth}} + V_{\text{gas}} \cdot \text{ON}_{\text{gas}}$$

where $V_{\text{eth}}$ and $V_{\text{gas}}$ are the **volume fractions**.

**B. Calorific Value of Blend**

$$\text{CV}_{\text{blend}} = \frac{m_{\text{eth}} \cdot \text{CV}_{\text{eth}} + m_{\text{gas}} \cdot \text{CV}_{\text{gas}}}{m_{\text{eth}} + m_{\text{gas}}}$$

Because ethanol has only **~65 %** the calorific value of gasoline, fuel consumption rises roughly **3–7 %** for E10 blends.

### 2.3.3 Properties of Common Gashol Blends

| Property | E5 | E10 | E20 | E85 |
|:---------|:---|:----|:----|:----|
| Ethanol (% v/v) | 5 | 10 | 20 | 85 |
| RON | ~95 | ~96 | ~98 | ~105 |
| Calorific value (MJ/kg) | ~43 | ~42 | ~40 | ~31 |
| Vapour pressure (RVP) | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑ | moderate |

---

## 2.4 Power Alcohol — Production, Properties & Applications

### 2.4.1 Production by Fermentation

Sugar/starch → Glucose → Ethanol + CO₂

$$\text{C}_{6}\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_{6} \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase (Yeast)}} 2\,\text{C}_{2}\text{H}_{5}\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The theoretical yield of ethanol from glucose is **51.1 %** by mass. Industrial yields are **~95 % of theoretical** after distillation.

### 2.4.2 Distillation Sequence

| Step | Method | Ethanol Concentration |
|:-----|:-------|:-----------------------|
| 1 | Fermentation broth | 6 – 10 % |
| 2 | Simple distillation | ~40 % |
| 3 | Azeotropic distillation (benzene) | ~95 % (constant boiling mixture) |
| 4 | Molecular sieve / third component distillation | 99.5 – 100 % (absolute alcohol) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Azeotrope of ethanol-water boils at **78.2 °C** with composition **95.6 % ethanol + 4.4 % water** — pure distillation cannot cross this.

### 2.4.3 Denaturation

Because pure ethanol attracts excise duty (it is potable), it must be **denatured** for fuel use:

| Denaturant | Typical Dose |
|:-----------|:-------------|
| Pyridine bases | 0.5 – 1.0 % v/v |
| Naphtha | 1 – 2 % |
| Methanol (methylated spirit) | 5 – 10 % |

### 2.4.4 Properties of Pure Ethanol (Power Alcohol)

| Property | Value | Engineering Comment |
|:---------|:------|:--------------------|
| Calorific value | 29 700 kJ/kg | Low → ~67 % of gasoline |
| Octane number (RON) | 108 – 110 | Excellent |
| Latent heat of vaporisation | 842 kJ/kg | **Very high** → better volumetric efficiency |
| Density | 0.789 g/cm³ | Lighter than water |
| Auto-ignition temperature | 363 °C | High → resists knock |
| Flame velocity | 0.4 m/s | Lower than gasoline |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combustion Chemistry of Ethanol**
> $$\text{C}_{2}\text{H}_{5}\text{OH} + 3\,\text{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_{2} + 3\,\text{H}_{2}\text{O}$$
> Stoichiometric air–fuel ratio = **9.0 : 1** (mass basis), much leaner than gasoline (~14.7 : 1).

---

## 2.5 Colloidal Fuel — Production, Properties & Applications

### 2.5.1 Composition & Stabilisers

A colloidal fuel is a **heterogeneous two-phase system**:
- **Dispersed phase:** Pulverised coal (1 – 60 μm particle size)
- **Continuous phase:** Fuel oil (Bunker-C or heavy residual oil)
- **Stabiliser (peptising agent):** A surfactant like **soap, naphthenic acid, or an alkaline salt of sulphonated fatty acid** at ~0.5 – 1 % to prevent settling.

### 2.5.2 Viscosity–Composition Relationship (Approximate Empirical)

$$\mu_{\text{cf}} = \mu_{\text{oil}} \cdot \left(1 - \phi\right)^{-2.5}$$

where $\phi$ is the volume fraction of coal, and $\mu$ denotes viscosity (Pa·s).

> [!NOTE]
> Viscosity rises *very* rapidly as coal loading increases; hence coal fraction is practically capped at **~50 %** to keep the mixture pumpable.

### 2.5.3 Properties

| Property | Value |
|:---------|:------|
| Coal : Oil ratio | 30 : 70 to 50 : 50 |
| Viscosity at 50 °C | 100 – 500 cP |
| Calorific value | 33 000 – 42 000 kJ/kg |
| Stability shelf-life | 1 – 6 months |

### 2.5.4 Advantages

- Direct use of cheap pulverised coal.
- Eliminates pulverised-coal injection grinding at burner.
- Lower storage hazard than coal dust (oil matrix suppresses dust explosion).
- Can be pumped, atomised and burnt like oil.

### 2.5.5 Disadvantages

- High viscosity → needs preheating to 70–90 °C.
- Sludge formation on long storage.
- Higher ash content (from coal).

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Consolidated)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:------|
| 1 | Stoichiometric combustion of ethanol | $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | mol basis |
| 2 | Air-fuel ratio of ethanol | $\text{A/F} = \dfrac{(2 + 1.5) \times 4.32}{1} \approx 9.0$ | mass |
| 3 | Fermentation theoretical yield | $Y_{\text{eth}} = \dfrac{2 \times 46}{180} = 51.1\%$ | mass |
| 4 | Octane blending (linear) | $\text{ON}_{\text{blend}} = V_a \text{ON}_a + V_b \text{ON}_b$ | vol fraction |
| 5 | CV of a blend | $\text{CV}_{\text{blend}} = \dfrac{\sum m_i \, \text{CV}_i}{\sum m_i}$ | kJ/kg |
| 6 | Viscosity of colloidal fuel | $\mu_{\text{cf}} = \mu_{\text{oil}} (1 - \phi)^{-2.5}$ | Pa·s |
| 7 | Higher heating value of ethanol | $\text{HHV} = 29\,700$ kJ/kg | kJ/kg |
| 8 | Latent heat of vaporisation of ethanol | $h_{fg} = 842$ kJ/kg | kJ/kg |

> [!NOTE]
> Always substitute *absolute* values inside table cells using `$\vert$ or \mid$` formatting when the original expression uses absolute value. Avoid naked `|` characters inside table rows.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Benzol**: Recovery is essential for coke-oven economics — a single 1 000 t/day coke plant yields 8–10 t/day of crude benzol.
- **Shale oil**: Estonia, China, USA, Brazil currently produce commercial shale oil; India has **Mangalam / Palna deposits** in Rajasthan.
- **Gashol / Power alcohol**: Brazil's **ProÁlcool programme** (1975) — over 50 % of cars run on ethanol/gasohol blends.
- **Colloidal fuel**: Used in marine boilers during the 1930s–40s; today revived interest in **coal–oil slurries (COM)** for power plants.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose Fermentation

This is one of the **most frequently asked numerical** in KTU Fuel Engineering exams.

**Given:** Fermentation reaction
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$$

**Molecular masses:**
- Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) = 180 g/mol
- Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) = 46 g/mol
- CO₂ = 44 g/mol

**Step 1 — Theoretical yield per mole of glucose**

From stoichiometry, 1 mole of glucose (180 g) yields 2 moles of ethanol.

$$\text{Mass of ethanol} = 2 \times 46 = 92 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Mass yield percentage**

$$Y_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{92}{180} \times 100\%$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{\text{theoretical}} &= \dfrac{92}{180} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 0.5111 \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 51.11\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Practical yield (90 % efficiency assumed)**

$$Y_{\text{practical}} = 0.90 \times 51.11 = 46.00\%$$

**Step 4 — Mass of ethanol from 1 tonne of glucose**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{eth}} &= 1000 \text{ kg} \times 0.46 \\[4pt]
&= 460 \text{ kg of absolute ethanol}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Calorific Value of Gashol Blend

**Problem Statement (KTU-type):** A gashol blend consists of 90 % gasoline and 10 % absolute ethanol by volume.
- Density of gasoline, $\rho_{\text{gas}} = 0.74$ g/cm³
- Density of ethanol, $\rho_{\text{eth}} = 0.789$ g/cm³
- CV of gasoline = 44 000 kJ/kg
- CV of ethanol = 29 700 kJ/kg

**Find:** Calorific value of the blend per kg.

**Step 1 — Take 1 L basis**

Take 1 litre of blend = 1000 cm³.

Volume of gasoline = 900 cm³
Volume of ethanol = 100 cm³

**Step 2 — Convert to mass**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{gas}} &= \rho_{\text{gas}} \times V_{\text{gas}} = 0.74 \times 900 = 666 \text{ g} \\[4pt]
m_{\text{eth}} &= \rho_{\text{eth}} \times V_{\text{eth}} = 0.789 \times 100 = 78.9 \text{ g} \\[4pt]
m_{\text{total}} &= 666 + 78.9 = 744.9 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply mass-weighted CV formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CV}_{\text{blend}} &= \frac{m_{\text{gas}} \cdot \text{CV}_{\text{gas}} + m_{\text{eth}} \cdot \text{CV}_{\text{eth}}}{m_{\text{total}}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(666)(44\,000) + (78.9)(29\,700)}{744.9} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{29\,304\,000 + 2\,343\,330}{744.9} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{31\,647\,330}{744.9} \\[4pt]
&\approx 42\,486 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion**

The blend loses approximately:
$$44\,000 - 42\,486 = 1\,514 \text{ kJ/kg} \approx 3.44\%$$
of calorific value compared to pure gasoline — consistent with experimental findings.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Viscosity of a 40 % Coal-in-Oil Colloidal Fuel

**Given:**
- Viscosity of base fuel oil at 50 °C, $\mu_{\text{oil}} = 50$ cP
- Volume fraction of coal, $\phi = 0.40$

**Use Mooney-type empirical relation:**

$$\mu_{\text{cf}} = \mu_{\text{oil}} \cdot (1 - \phi)^{-2.5}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{\text{cf}} &= 50 \times (1 - 0.40)^{-2.5} \\[4pt]
&= 50 \times (0.60)^{-2.5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $(0.60)^{-2.5}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.60)^{-2.5} &= \frac{1}{(0.60)^{2.5}} \\[4pt]
(0.60)^{2.5} &= (0.60)^2 \times (0.60)^{0.5} \\[4pt]
&= 0.36 \times 0.7746 \\[4pt]
&= 0.2789
\end{aligned}
$$

So,
$$
(0.60)^{-2.5} = \frac{1}{0.2789} = 3.585
$$

**Step 3 — Final viscosity**

$$
\mu_{\text{cf}} = 50 \times 3.585 = 179.3 \text{ cP}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **non-linear explosion** of viscosity: doubling coal concentration from 20 % to 40 % boosts viscosity by an order of magnitude. This is why coal loading is industrially limited.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Five Synthetic Fuels

| Attribute | Benzol | Shale Oil | Gashol | Power Alcohol | Colloidal Fuel |
|:----------|:-------|:----------|:-------|:--------------|:---------------|
| Source | Coal (coke-oven) | Oil shale rock | Gasoline + ethanol | Biomass sugars | Coal + fuel oil |
| Process | Wash-oil absorption + fractionation | Pyrolysis 450–550 °C | Physical blending | Fermentation + distillation + denaturation | Suspension with surfactant |
| Physical state | Liquid | Liquid | Liquid | Liquid | Thick liquid / paste |
| Density (g/cm³) | 0.876 | 0.85–0.95 | 0.74–0.78 | 0.789 | 0.95–1.05 |
| CV (kJ/kg) | 40 200 | 38 000–42 000 | 41 000–43 000 | 29 700 | 35 000–42 000 |
| Octane number | 95–108 | ~70 (needs upgrading) | 96–105 | 108 | n/a (used in boilers) |
| Sulphur content | Low–moderate | High (0.5–2 %) | Very low | Negligible | Moderate (from coal) |
| Key advantage | High octane | Vast reserves | Renewable, anti-knock | Renewable, low emissions | Cheap coal utilization |
| Key drawback | Toxicity, carcinogenic | High N, high pour point | Phase separation risk | Low CV, hygroscopic | High viscosity, settling |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Recovery of Benzol from Coke-Oven Gas

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Coal charged into Coke Oven at 1000 deg C] --> B[Hot Coke Oven Gas containing BTX vapours]
    B --> C[Primary Cooler - Gas to 30 deg C]
    C --> D[Wash Oil Scrubber absorbs BTX]
    D --> E[Rich Wash Oil leaves bottom]
    D --> F[Lean Gas exits - tar and ammonia recovered]
    E --> G[Steam Stripper - Desorbs BTX]
    G --> H[Crude Benzol mixture]
    H --> I[Crude Benzol Distillation Column]
    I --> J[Pure Benzene overhead]
    I --> K[Toluene side cut]
    I --> L[Xylene bottoms]
    I --> M[Residue tar wash oil recycle]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Surface Retorting of Oil Shale

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Oil Shale mined from open-pit] --> B[Crushing and Sizing to less than 12 mm]
    B --> C[Retort Furnace feed]
    C --> D[Retort at 450 to 550 deg C in absence of air]
    D --> E[Kerogen decomposes]
    E --> F[Shale Oil vapour]
    E --> G[Shale Gas non-condensable]
    E --> H[Spent Shale solid residue]
    F --> I[Condenser train]
    I --> J[Crude Shale Oil storage]
    G --> K[Gas cleaning H2S removal]
    K --> L[Fuel gas to retort burner]
    H --> M[Ash disposal or cement plant]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Power Alcohol Production from Biomass

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biomass sugarcane molasses or corn starch] --> B[Milling and Dilution to 15 percent sugar]
    B --> C[Addition of Yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae]
    C --> D[Fermenter at 30 deg C for 48 to 72 hours]
    D --> E[Fermented Wash 8 to 10 percent ethanol]
    E --> F[Beer Column simple distillation]
    F --> G[Rectification Column to 95 percent azeotrope]
    G --> H[Azeotropic Distillation with Benzene]
    H --> I[Absolute Ethanol 99.5 percent]
    I --> J[Denaturation with Pyridine and Naphtha]
    J --> K[Power Alcohol final product]
    F --> L[Spent Wash byproduct animal feed or fertiliser]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Colloidal Fuel Production

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Coal crushed to less than 60 micron size] --> B[Drying to less than 2 percent moisture]
    B --> C[Pulverised Coal Charge]
    D[Fuel Oil Bunker C preheated to 80 deg C] --> E[Mixing Tank with stirrer]
    C --> E
    F[Stabiliser Surfactant Soap 0.5 percent] --> E
    E --> G[Homogeniser Colloid Mill]
    G --> H[Colloidal Fuel Storage with agitator]
    H --> I[Atomiser Burner in Boiler or Furnace]
```
