# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q:** Define **Power Alcohol** and state any two of its properties.

**Model Answer:**

> **Power Alcohol** is rectified/absolute ethyl alcohol (ethanol, C₂H₅OH) denatured with pyridine bases, naphtha, or methanol to make it unfit for drinking, and used as a fuel for spark-ignition engines either alone or blended with gasoline.
>
> **Two Properties:**
>
> 1. **Calorific value:** 29 700 kJ/kg — significantly lower than gasoline (~44 000 kJ/kg).
> 2. **Octane number:** 108 RON — very high; excellent anti-knock quality.
> 3. (Bonus) **Latent heat of vaporisation:** 842 kJ/kg — produces charge-cooling effect improving volumetric efficiency.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q:** What is **Colloidal Fuel**? Why is a stabiliser added to it?

**Model Answer:**

> **Colloidal Fuel** is a mixture of finely pulverised coal (1–60 μm) suspended uniformly in heavy fuel oil, with the help of a surfactant (stabiliser) at ~0.5–1 %.
>
> **Reasons for adding stabiliser:**
>
> 1. Coal particles tend to **agglomerate and settle** during storage due to density difference. The stabiliser (e.g., soap, sulphonated fatty acid) **adsorbs on the coal surface**, gives it an electrical charge, and prevents flocculation.
> 2. It improves **wettability** between coal and oil, maintaining a uniform suspension.
> 3. It enhances **shelf-life** from a few days (without stabiliser) to several months.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** — Benzol + Shale Oil

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat flow diagram the **recovery of benzol from coke-oven gas**. Mention the operating temperatures and the role of wash oil.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **shale oil and conventional crude oil** on the basis of origin, composition, calorific value, nitrogen and sulphur content. Add a note on the **Kiviter process** for shale oil extraction.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Recovery of Benzol from Coke-Oven Gas**

When coal is carbonised in coke ovens at **1000–1100 °C**, the volatile products contain **benzene, toluene and xylene (BTX)** along with H₂, CH₄, CO, CO₂, H₂S, NH₃, water vapour and tars.

**Step-by-step process:**

1. **Cooling of gas:** The hot gas is first cooled to about **30 °C** in primary coolers, where water, tar and ammonia condense.
2. **Wash oil scrubbing:** Cooled gas passes counter-currently through a **packed scrubber (benzol absorber)**. The **wash oil** is a high-boiling petroleum fraction (b.p. 230–300 °C) which selectively **absorbs BTX vapours** due to high affinity for aromatics.
   - Lean wash oil enters top → rich wash oil leaves bottom.
   - Operating temperature: **25–30 °C**; pressure slightly above atmospheric.
3. **Steam stripping:** Rich wash oil is pumped to a **steam-heated stripping column** where superheated steam (at **~150 °C**) drives out the absorbed BTX. The lean wash oil is cooled and recycled.
4. **Condensation:** The vapour mixture of BTX + steam is condensed. Water separates by decantation.
5. **Fractional distillation:** The crude benzol is fractionated in a multi-plate column:
   - **Benzene** distils at **80.1 °C** (overhead).
   - **Toluene** at **110.6 °C** (side cut).
   - **Xylenes** at **138–144 °C** (bottoms).
6. **90 % benzol:** A commercial grade containing 70 % benzene + 15 % toluene + 15 % xylene is sold as **"90 % benzol"**.

**Role of wash oil:**
- High aromatic affinity → selective absorption of BTX.
- Low vapour pressure → does not evaporate during operation.
- Chemically inert → does not react with gas constituents.
- Easily regenerated by steam stripping.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparison Table — Shale Oil vs Conventional Crude**

| Property | Shale Oil | Conventional Crude |
|:---------|:----------|:-------------------|
| Origin | Kerogen in oil-shale rock | Underground petroleum reservoirs |
| State in source | Solid organic matter | Liquid trapped in porous rock |
| Extraction | Mining + retorting (pyrolysis) | Drilling + pumping |
| Nitrogen content | **0.5–2 %** (high) | 0.1–0.5 % |
| Sulphur content | 0.5–2 % | 0.1–3 % |
| Oxygen content | Higher | Lower |
| Calorific value | 38 000–42 000 kJ/kg | 42 000–45 000 kJ/kg |
| Pour point | 15–30 °C (waxy) | –30 to 0 °C |
| Refining needs | Hydrodesulphurisation + hydrodenitrogenation | Standard refining |

**Note on Kiviter Process:**

The **Kiviter process** (developed in Estonia, used commercially at **Kohtla-Järve**):

- **Type:** Internal-combustion vertical retort (above-ground).
- **Feed:** Lump oil shale (25–125 mm size).
- **Heat source:** Combustion of residual coke on spent shale using air blown into the retort.
- **Zones inside retort:**
  1. **Drying zone** (top, ~100 °C).
  2. **Pyrolysis zone** (middle, ~350–500 °C) — kerogen decomposes to oil vapour + gas.
  3. **Combustion zone** (bottom, ~700–900 °C) — residual char burns to supply heat.
- **Oil yield:** ~10–15 % by weight of shale.
- **By-products:** Shale gas, sulphur, phenols.

### **Question B** — Gashol + Power Alcohol + Colloidal Fuel

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define **Gashol**. Explain the role of a **co-solvent** in preventing phase separation. A gashol blend contains 80 % gasoline (CV = 44 000 kJ/kg, density = 0.74 g/cm³) and 20 % absolute ethanol (CV = 29 700 kJ/kg, density = 0.789 g/cm³). **Calculate the calorific value of the blend in kJ/kg.**

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is **Colloidal Fuel**? With a neat flow diagram, describe its manufacture. Mention any **three advantages** and **two disadvantages** of using colloidal fuels.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition:**
**Gashol** is a blend of gasoline (petrol) with **anhydrous ethanol** (typically 5–20 % by volume) used as a motor fuel for spark-ignition engines. Common grades are E5, E10 and E20.

**Role of co-solvent:**

Ethanol has limited miscibility with gasoline in the presence of trace water. As little as **0.5 % water** can cause phase separation at low temperatures.

A **co-solvent** (also called "blending agent") such as:
- Tertiary-butyl alcohol (TBA),
- Methyl-tertiary-butyl ether (MTBE),
- Iso-butanol,
- Ethyl-tertiary-butyl ether (ETBE)

is added at **~1–3 %** to:
1. **Bridge the polarity gap** between non-polar gasoline hydrocarbons and polar ethanol/water molecules.
2. **Suppress phase separation** over a wider temperature range.
3. Improve cold-start behaviour and reduce vapour-lock tendency.

**Numerical — Calorific Value of the Blend**

**Given:**
- Volume fraction of gasoline, $V_{\text{gas}} = 0.80$
- Volume fraction of ethanol, $V_{\text{eth}} = 0.20$
- $\rho_{\text{gas}} = 0.74$ g/cm³
- $\rho_{\text{eth}} = 0.789$ g/cm³
- $\text{CV}_{\text{gas}} = 44\,000$ kJ/kg
- $\text{CV}_{\text{eth}} = 29\,700$ kJ/kg

**Basis:** 1 litre (1000 cm³) of blend

**Step 1 — Volumes and masses**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{gas}} &= 800 \text{ cm}^3, \quad V_{\text{eth}} = 200 \text{ cm}^3 \\[4pt]
m_{\text{gas}} &= 0.74 \times 800 = 592 \text{ g} \\[4pt]
m_{\text{eth}} &= 0.789 \times 200 = 157.8 \text{ g} \\[4pt]
m_{\text{total}} &= 592 + 157.8 = 749.8 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy contributed by each component**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{gas}} &= 592 \times 44\,000 = 26\,048\,000 \text{ kJ (per 749.8 g)} \\[4pt]
E_{\text{eth}} &= 157.8 \times 29\,700 = 4\,686\,660 \text{ kJ (per 749.8 g)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — CV per kg of blend**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CV}_{\text{blend}} &= \frac{26\,048\,000 + 4\,686\,660}{749.8} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{30\,734\,660}{749.8} \\[4pt]
&\approx 40\,988 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The calorific value of the blend is approximately **40 988 kJ/kg** — about **6.8 % lower** than pure gasoline, consistent with the dilution effect of ethanol.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Definition:** *(as given earlier)*

Colloidal fuel is a fluid mixture in which finely pulverised coal (≤ 60 μm) is uniformly suspended in heavy fuel oil using a chemical stabiliser (~0.5–1 %).

**Manufacture (Flow Diagram):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Coal crushing and grinding to less than 60 micron] --> B[Drying to less than 2 percent moisture]
    B --> C[Pulverised Coal]
    D[Bunker C heavy fuel oil preheated to 80 deg C] --> E[Stirred Mixing Tank]
    C --> E
    F[Stabiliser sulphonated fatty acid 0.5 percent] --> E
    E --> G[Homogeniser Colloid Mill high shear]
    G --> H[Storage with slow agitation]
    H --> I[Atomiser and Burner]
```

**Manufacturing Steps (text version):**

1. **Coal preparation:** Run-of-mine coal is crushed → ground in ball mills → sieved so that **>90 % passes 60 μm**.
2. **Drying:** Moisture reduced below 2 % in flash dryers.
3. **Oil preheating:** Fuel oil heated to **70–90 °C** to reduce viscosity for easier mixing.
4. **Mixing:** Coal powder and oil blended in stirred tank; stabiliser added simultaneously.
5. **Homogenisation:** Passed through **colloid mill** (rotor–stator gap ~0.1 mm) at high shear → produces uniform suspension.
6. **Storage:** Kept under slow agitation to prevent settling.
7. **Combustion:** Pumped through burner, atomised with steam/air, and burnt.

**Three Advantages:**

1. **Cheap coal utilisation** without elaborate pulverised-coal handling systems at the burner.
2. **Pumpable, atomisable** like oil; reduces capital cost of conversion from oil to coal.
3. **Reduced explosion hazard** compared to dry pulverised coal (the oil matrix quenches dust ignition).
4. *(Bonus)* Can use **low-grade coal fines and waste coal** — a recycling route.

**Two Disadvantages:**

1. **Very high viscosity** even at 50 °C (~100–500 cP), requiring preheating and special pumps.
2. **Settling / sludge formation** if stored for long → operational problems.
3. *(Bonus)* **High ash content** from coal creates slag and erosion in burners.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where students lose marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert volume to mass** before applying the CV-blending formula — examiners specifically test this conversion in ESE Part B.
> 2. **Mixing up "Benzol" and "Benzene"** — Benzene is a pure compound (C₆H₆); Benzol is a *blend* of aromatics recovered from coke ovens.
> 3. **Omitting the role of wash oil** in the benzol recovery question — students often describe the column but forget to explain *why* wash oil is used.
> 4. **Confusing "Shale oil" with "Oil from shales"** — oil shales do *not* contain free oil; they contain kerogen which must be pyrolysed.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention the stabiliser** in colloidal fuel answers — examiners award a separate mark for the role of the surfactant.
> 6. **Writing "ethanol" and "power alcohol" interchangeably** — power alcohol is denatured ethanol; the denaturant must be specified.
> 7. **In fermentation yield numericals**, students often use the *empirical* glucose yield (46 %) without stating the 90–95 % practical efficiency assumption.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Other Synthetic Liquid Fuels**

- ☐ **Benzol** = aromatic blend from **coke-oven gas**; recovered by **wash-oil scrubbing + steam stripping + fractional distillation**.
- ☐ **Benzene**: b.p. **80.1 °C**, density **0.876 g/cm³**, RON **~108**.
- ☐ **Shale oil** comes from **kerogen** pyrolysis in oil shale at **450–550 °C**.
- ☐ Major processes: **Kiviter, Petrosix, Tosco-II**; in-situ: **Shell ICP, Chevron CRUSH**.
- ☐ Shale oil is **rich in nitrogen and oxygen** → needs hydrotreating.
- ☐ **Gashol** = **gasoline + anhydrous ethanol** (E5/E10/E20); needs a **co-solvent** (TBA, MTBE) to prevent phase separation.
- ☐ **Power alcohol** = **denatured absolute ethanol**; produced by **fermentation → distillation → denaturation**.
- ☐ Fermentation: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \to 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\text{CO}_2$; theoretical yield **51.1 %**, practical **~46 %**.
- ☐ Ethanol CV = **29 700 kJ/kg**; Octane = **108 RON**; Latent heat = **842 kJ/kg**.
- ☐ **Colloidal fuel** = coal powder (≤ 60 μm) + fuel oil + stabiliser.
- ☐ Viscosity of colloidal fuel obeys **$\mu_{cf} = \mu_{oil}(1-\phi)^{-2.5}$** — increases rapidly with coal loading.
- ☐ Stabilisers: **soap, sulphonated fatty acids, naphthenic acid salts**.
- ☐ Ethanol–water forms **azeotrope at 95.6 % ethanol, 78.2 °C**; crossed using **benzene** or **molecular sieves**.
- ☐ Denaturants used in power alcohol: **pyridine, naphtha, methanol**.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Other Synthetic Liquid Fuels**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Gaseous Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification of Gaseous Fuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 3 — Classification of Gaseous Fuels

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **gaseous fuel** is a combustible gaseous mixture, predominantly hydrocarbons (C\(_n\)H\(_m\)) along with non-combustible gases like CO\(_2\), N\(_2\), and water vapour, that can be burned in the presence of an oxidizer (usually O\(_2\) or air) to release energy in the form of heat. Classification of gaseous fuels is the systematic arrangement of these fuels into logical groups based on **origin (natural vs. manufactured)**, **composition (hydrocarbon content)**, **calorific value (HCV/LCV)**, **method of production**, and **physical state (compressed vs. liquefied)**.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Family Tree of Burnable Air"

Think of gaseous fuels as a big **family** living in the atmosphere of your engineering lab:

- 👴 **Grandfather**: *Natural gas* — found underground, comes from nature, no human effort needed to create it.
- 👨 **Father**: *Manufactured/Artificial gases* — these are the sons/daughters cooked up in industrial "ovens" (gasifiers, coke ovens, blast furnaces) by humans.
- 👦 **Youngest sibling**: *Liquefied gases* (LPG, LNG) — they are the gases in the family who have been "squeezed" or "chilled" so hard that they turn into liquid, just for easy transport (like packing your big brother into a small suitcase).
- 🌱 **Cousins from the garden**: *Biogas & Sewage gas* — they come from rotting biomass and waste, courtesy of tiny microbes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics used by KTU Board Examiners:**
> - **Standard Calorific Value of Pure Methane (CH\(_4\)):** HCV ≈ **55.5 MJ/kg** (≈ 13,250 kcal/kg)
> - **STP Conditions:** **T = 273.15 K**, **P = 1.01325 bar**
> - **Wobbe Index (WI):** WI = (GCV / √SG) — used as a **gas interchangeability** metric in gas engineering.

---

## 2. Primary Classification Criteria

A KTU examiner expects a student to know classification along **multiple axes** simultaneously. The accepted axes are:

| # | Criterion | Sub-Groups |
|---|-----------|------------|
| 1 | **Origin / Source** | Natural vs. Manufactured (Artificial) |
| 2 | **Hydrocarbon Content** | Hydrocarbon-rich (CH\(_4\)-rich) vs. CO/H\(_2\)-rich |
| 3 | **Calorific Value Band** | **High CV** ( > 30 MJ/m³ ), **Medium CV** (10–30 MJ/m³), **Low CV** ( < 10 MJ/m³ ) |
| 4 | **Physical State at Delivery** | Compressed (CNG), Liquefied (LPG, LNG) |
| 5 | **End-Use** | Domestic, Industrial, Automotive (CNG), Power Generation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification Chart (Engineering Convention)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["GASEOUS FUELS"]:::root --> B["A. NATURAL GAS"]
    A --> C["B. MANUFACTURED / ARTIFICIAL GAS"]
    A --> D["C. BY-PRODUCT GAS"]
    A --> E["D. LIQUEFIED GASEOUS FUELS"]

    B --> B1["Associated Gas"]
    B --> B2["Non-Associated Gas"]
    B --> B3["Methane from Coal Seams CBM"]

    C --> C1["Coal Gas"]
    C --> C2["Coke Oven Gas"]
    C --> C3["Producer Gas"]
    C --> C4["Water Gas"]
    C --> C5["Blue Water Gas"]
    C --> C6["Carburetted Water Gas"]
    C --> C7["Oil Gas"]
    C --> C8["Blast Furnace Gas"]
    C --> C9["Sewage Gas"]
    C --> C10["Refinery Off-Gases"]

    D --> D1["LPG Liquefied Petroleum Gas"]
    D --> D2["LNG Liquefied Natural Gas"]
    D --> D3["CNG Compressed Natural Gas"]
    D --> D4["Biogas Anaerobic Digestion"]

    classDef root fill:#1a1a2e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#e94560,stroke-width:2px
```

### 2.2 Detailed Sub-Group Analysis

#### **A. Natural Gas**
- Found trapped in porous rock formations beneath the earth, often co-existing with crude oil.
- **Types:**
  - **Associated (Dissolved) Gas:** Co-exists with crude oil in the reservoir; comes out as dissolved gas when oil is depressurized.
  - **Non-Associated Gas:** Found in dry gas reservoirs with little/no crude oil.
  - **Coal Bed Methane (CBM):** Methane adsorbed in coal seams.
- **Typical Composition (vol %):** CH\(_4\) = 85–95%, C\(_2\)H\(_6\) = 3–8%, C\(_3\)H\(_8\) = 1–3%, CO\(_2\) = 0–2%, N\(_2\) = 0–5%, H\(_2\)S = trace.
- **Calorific Value:** **37–42 MJ/m³** at NTP.

#### **B. Manufactured / Artificial Gases**
These are produced by **partial combustion, gasification, or pyrolysis** of solid/liquid fuels.

| Gas | Production Method | Main Combustibles | Approx. GCV (MJ/m³) |
|-----|-------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Coal Gas** | Carbonization of bituminous coal at 1000–1300 °C | H\(_2\) (45%), CH\(_4\) (30%), CO (10%) | **17–20** |
| **Coke Oven Gas** | By-product of metallurgical coke production | H\(_2\) (55%), CH\(_4\) (28%), CO (6%) | **18–22** |
| **Producer Gas** | Partial combustion of solid fuel (coke/coal) with air+steam | CO (25–30%), H\(_2\) (10–15%), N\(_2\) (50–55%) | **4.5–6.5** |
| **Water Gas (Blue Gas)** | Steam passed over incandescent coke | CO (40%), H\(_2\) (50%), N\(_2\) (5%) | **10–12** |
| **Carburetted Water Gas** | Water gas enriched with oil-gas | Hydrocarbons ↑, H\(_2\), CO | **18–22** |
| **Blast Furnace Gas** | By-product of iron smelting | CO (25–28%), H\(_2\) (1–4%), N\(_2\) (60%) | **3.0–4.0** |
| **Oil Gas** | Thermal cracking of petroleum oils | C\(_n\)H\(_m\) (saturated & unsaturated) | **35–45** |

#### **C. By-Product Gases**
Recovered as waste from industrial or biological processes. Prominent members:
- **Biogas** — Anaerobic digestion of organic waste (cow dung, sewage, agricultural residue).
- **Sewage Gas** — From municipal sewage treatment plants.
- **Refinery Off-Gases** — Streams from FCC, hydrocracker units.

#### **D. Liquefied / Compressed Gases**
- **LPG** — Mixture of C\(_3\)H\(_8\) (propane) and C\(_4\)H\(_10\) (butane), liquefied at moderate pressure.
- **LNG** — Methane liquefied at **–162 °C** for transport.
- **CNG** — Methane compressed to **200–250 bar** for vehicle fuel.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to "classify gaseous fuels", **always state the criterion first** (e.g., "based on origin"), then list. Marks are awarded for **explicit reasoning**, not just listing names.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Use |
|---|-------------------|-----------|-----|
| 1 | **Higher Calorific Value (HCV)** | $\text{HCV} = \text{LCV} + \text{Latent Heat of Water Vapour} = \text{LCV} + 9 \times H_2 \times \text{M}_{H_2O}/\text{M}_{H_2}$ | Convert LCV → HCV in kJ/kg |
| 2 | **Wobbe Index** | $\text{WI} = \dfrac{\text{GCV}_{gas}}{\sqrt{\text{SG}_{gas}}}$ | Measures gas interchangeability |
| 3 | **Specific Gravity (gas)** | $\text{SG} = \dfrac{M_{gas}}{M_{air}} = \dfrac{M_{gas}}{28.97}$ | Density ratio at NTP |
| 4 | **Stoichiometric O\(_2\) for hydrocarbons** | $\text{C}_n\text{H}_m + \left(n + \dfrac{m}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow n\text{CO}_2 + \dfrac{m}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Combustion stoichiometry |
| 5 | **Air-Fuel Ratio (mass basis)** | $\text{A/F} = \dfrac{n_{air} \times 28.97}{M_{fuel}}$ | Combustion calculator |
| 6 | **Volumetric Analysis → Mole %** | $y_i = \dfrac{V_i}{V_{total}}$ | Composition input |
| 7 | **Mean Molecular Mass of Mix** | $M_{mix} = \displaystyle\sum y_i \cdot M_i$ | Average M for SG, WI |

> [!NOTE]
> **M\(_{H_2O}\)/M\(_{H_2}\)** = 18/2 = 9. This is why the **"9 × mass of H\(_2\)"** rule appears in every fuel-engineering numerical — the latent heat of condensation of water ≈ **2442 kJ/kg**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Natural Gas** → Power plants (combined cycle efficiency **> 60%**), city gas distribution (PNG), CNG vehicles.
- **Producer Gas** → Cheap fuel for **steel re-rolling mills, glass furnaces, ceramic kilns** where fuel cost dominates.
- **Water Gas** → Synthesis of **methanol, ammonia, Fischer-Tropsch diesel** (H\(_2\)+CO = "syngas").
- **Blast Furnace Gas** → Pre-heating air for blast furnace stoves (CO recovery = ~30% energy savings).
- **Biogas** → Rural electrification, cooking (decentralized energy).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Mean Molecular Mass of a Gaseous Fuel Mixture

### 📜 Statement
For a gaseous fuel whose volumetric (mole) composition is given as \(y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n\) with corresponding molecular masses \(M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_n\), derive the formula for the mean molecular mass of the mixture.

### 🪜 Steps

**Step 1 — Avogadro's Hypothesis:**
At identical T and P, equal volumes of all ideal gases contain an equal number of molecules. Hence, **volume fraction = mole fraction**.

$$y_i = \frac{n_i}{n_{total}} = \frac{V_i}{V_{total}}$$

**Step 2 — Mass Contribution of Each Component:**
The mass of the \(i^{th}\) component in 1 mole of mixture is:

$$m_i = y_i \cdot M_i$$

**Step 3 — Total Mass of 1 Mole of Mixture:**
Summing the contributions of all components:

$$M_{mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \cdot M_i$$

**Step 4 — Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{\,M_{mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i M_i\,}$$

> 🟢 **Logic Check:** Since \(\sum y_i = 1\), if all components had \(M_i = M\), then \(M_{mix} = M\). Satisfies the degenerate case. ✅

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Calorific Value of a Producer Gas

> **Problem:** A producer gas has the following volumetric composition: CO = 30%, H\(_2\) = 12%, CH\(_4\) = 2%, N\(_2\) = 56%. Calculate (a) the GCV in kJ/m³ at NTP, and (b) the Wobbe Index. (Use the standard calorific values: CO = 12,636 kJ/m³, H\(_2\) = 12,782 kJ/m³, CH\(_4\) = 39,820 kJ/m³, M\(_{air}\) = 28.97 kg/kmol.)

### Part (a) — Gross Calorific Value (GCV)

**Step 1:** Write GCV as the volume-weighted sum of individual GCVs.

$$\text{GCV}_{mix} = \sum y_i \cdot \text{GCV}_i$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values numerically.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV}_{mix} &= (0.30)(12{,}636) + (0.12)(12{,}782) + (0.02)(39{,}820) + (0.56)(0) \\
&= 3{,}790.8 + 1{,}533.84 + 796.4 + 0 \\
&= 6{,}121.04 \;\text{kJ/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Round to three significant figures.

$$\boxed{\,\text{GCV}_{mix} \approx 6{,}121 \;\text{kJ/m}^3 \approx 6.12 \;\text{MJ/m}^3\,}$$

### Part (b) — Wobbe Index

**Step 1:** Compute mean molecular mass.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{mix} &= (0.30)(28) + (0.12)(2) + (0.02)(16) + (0.56)(28) \\
&= 8.4 + 0.24 + 0.32 + 15.68 \\
&= 24.64 \;\text{kg/kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Specific gravity.

$$\text{SG} = \frac{M_{mix}}{M_{air}} = \frac{24.64}{28.97} = 0.8505$$

**Step 3:** Wobbe Index.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{WI} &= \frac{\text{GCV}_{mix}}{\sqrt{\text{SG}}} = \frac{6121}{\sqrt{0.8505}} \\
&= \frac{6121}{0.9222} = 6{,}637 \;\text{kJ/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\,\text{WI} \approx 6{,}637 \;\text{kJ/m}^3\,}$$

> 🟢 **Engineering Interpretation:** A WI of ~6,600 places this gas firmly in the **"low-WI, low-heating-value"** category, confirming why producer gas is used only in **furnaces** (where volume cost matters) and not in high-temperature combustion chambers.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Gaseous Fuels at a Glance

| Property | Natural Gas | LPG | Biogas | Producer Gas | Water Gas | Blast Furnace Gas |
|----------|------------|-----|--------|--------------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Origin** | Geological | Refined petroleum | Biological | Manufactured | Manufactured | By-product |
| **Main Combustible** | CH\(_4\) | C\(_3\)H\(_8\)/C\(_4\)H\(_{10}\) | CH\(_4\) (60%) | CO + H\(_2\) | CO + H\(_2\) | CO |
| **GCV (MJ/m³)** | **37–42** | **93–122** (MJ/m³ of liquid × ~270) | **18–25** | **4.5–6.5** | **10–12** | **3.0–4.0** |
| **SG (vs. air)** | 0.55–0.70 | 1.5–2.0 | 0.85–0.95 | 0.85–0.90 | 0.55–0.65 | 0.95–1.05 |
| **Flame Speed (m/s)** | 0.4 | 0.46 | 0.25 | 0.20 | 0.30 | — |
| **Storage** | CNG / LNG | Pressurized liquid | Gas holder | Gas holder | Gas holder | Pipe |
| **Primary Use** | Power, domestic, CNG | Domestic, auto | Cooking, power | Furnace | Syngas | Pre-heating |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** "**N**atural is **N**oble, **L**iquefied is **L**uxury, **B**iogas is **B**io, **M**anufactured is **M**edium, **B**last furnace is **B**ottom of the pile."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Gaseous Fuel Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SOURCE["SOURCE LAYER"]
        A1["Underground Reservoir"]
        A2["Coal Seam"]
        A3["Crude Oil Well"]
        A4["Biomass Waste"]
        A5["Steel Plant Coke Oven"]
        A6["Furnace Gasifier"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        B1["Wells and Extraction"]
        B2["CBM Recovery"]
        B3["Fractional Distillation"]

        C2["CBM"]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — "How to Classify a Gaseous Fuel?"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is the gas found in nature or manufactured?"} -->|Natural| Q2a{"Co-exists with crude oil?"}
    Q1 -->|Manufactured| Q2b{"Made from solid or liquid feedstock?"}

    Q2a -->|Yes| R1a1["Associated Natural Gas"]
    Q2a -->|No| R1a2["Non-Associated Natural Gas"]

    Q2b -->|Solid - Coal or Coke| Q3a{"Air or Steam used?"}
    Q2b -->|Liquid - Oil| R2b1["Oil Gas - Pyrolysis"]

    Q3a -->|Air Steam| R3a1["Producer Gas - Low CV"]
    Q3a -->|Steam only| R3a2["Water Gas - Blue Gas"]
    Q3a -->|Steam plus Oil Enrichment| R3a3["Carburetted Water Gas"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. ❌ Writing "LPG is a gaseous fuel" without clarifying it is **liquefied under pressure but vaporizes on release** — **deduct 1 mark**.
> 2. ❌ Confusing **coal gas** with **coke oven gas** (they differ in operating temperature and yield) — **deduct 1 mark**.
> 3. ❌ Writing Wobbe Index as **GCV × SG** instead of **GCV / √SG** — **deduct 1 mark** (this is a "signature" mistake examiners hunt for).
> 4. ❌ Forgetting to state the **basis** (volumetric vs. gravimetric) when reporting calorific value — **deduct 0.5 mark**.
> 5. ❌ Not providing **units** (MJ/m³ vs. kJ/kg) in numerical answers — **deduct 0.5 mark**.

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Classify gaseous fuels based on their origin. Give two examples of each class.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Gaseous fuels are classified based on **origin** into two main categories:

1. **Natural Gaseous Fuels** — Found in nature, obtained by drilling/wells.
   - Examples: *Natural gas (methane)*, *Coal Bed Methane (CBM)*.
2. **Manufactured (Artificial) Gaseous Fuels** — Produced by industrial gasification or pyrolysis processes.
   - Examples: *Producer gas*, *Water gas*, *Coal gas*.

*(1 mark for stating the criterion, 1 mark for two correct natural examples, 1 mark for two correct manufactured examples.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the Wobbe Index? Why is it significant in gaseous fuel selection?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Wobbe Index (WI)** is a measure of the **interchangeability of fuel gases**, defined as the ratio of the gross calorific value to the square root of the specific gravity of the gas relative to air.

$$\text{WI} = \frac{\text{GCV}_{gas}}{\sqrt{\text{SG}_{gas}}}$$

It is significant because:
- Two gases with the **same Wobbe Index** deliver the **same amount of heat** at the **same burner pressure**, regardless of composition.
- This makes it a critical parameter in **city gas distribution networks** where natural gas, LPG-air mixtures, and biogas may need to be substituted seamlessly.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for significance.)*

---

## 📝 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you are required to answer **ONE** full question from the given choice. Both choices are presented below for exam preparation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style, Module 3 Choice Question]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, classify gaseous fuels into natural and manufactured categories, listing at least **six** manufactured gases and their principal combustible constituents. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A coal gas has the following volumetric composition: H\(_2\) = 48%, CH\(_4\) = 32%, CO = 8%, C\(_2\)H\(_4\) = 4%, CO\(_2\) = 2%, N\(_2\) = 6%. Calculate:
   (i) the GCV in kJ/m³ at NTP, and
   (ii) the mean molecular mass and specific gravity of the gas.
   (Use: H\(_2\) = 12,782, CH\(_4\) = 39,820, CO = 12,636, C\(_2\)H\(_4\) = 59,029 kJ/m³; M\(_{air}\) = 28.97) **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) Solution (7 marks):**

**Classification diagram** — (refer to the Mermaid chart in Section 4.1 of these notes; 3 marks for diagram + 2 marks for listing 6 gases with constituents + 1 mark for the distinction between natural/manufactured + 1 mark for neatness and labels).

Six manufactured gases and their principal combustible constituents:

| # | Gas | Principal Combustibles |
|---|-----|------------------------|
| 1 | Coal Gas | H\(_2\), CH\(_4\), CO |
| 2 | Coke Oven Gas | H\(_2\), CH\(_4\) |
| 3 | Producer Gas | CO, H\(_2\) |
| 4 | Water Gas (Blue) | CO, H\(_2\) |
| 5 | Carburetted Water Gas | CO, H\(_2\), C\(_n\)H\(_m\) |
| 6 | Blast Furnace Gas | CO |
| 7 | Oil Gas | C\(_n\)H\(_m\) (ethylene, propylene) |

**Part (b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — GCV Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV} &= \sum y_i \cdot \text{GCV}_i \\
&= (0.48)(12{,}782) + (0.32)(39{,}820) + (0.08)(12{,}636) \\
&\quad + (0.04)(59{,}029) + (0.02)(0) + (0.06)(0) \\
&= 6{,}135.36 + 12{,}742.40 + 1{,}010.88 + 2{,}361.16 + 0 + 0 \\
&= 22{,}249.80 \;\text{kJ/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\,\text{GCV} \approx 22{,}250 \;\text{kJ/m}^3\,}$$

**Step 2 — Mean Molecular Mass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{mix} &= (0.48)(2) + (0.32)(16) + (0.08)(28) + (0.04)(28) + (0.02)(44) + (0.06)(28) \\
&= 0.96 + 5.12 + 2.24 + 1.12 + 0.88 + 1.68 \\
&= 12.00 \;\text{kg/kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\,M_{mix} = 12.00 \;\text{kg/kmol}\,}$$

**Step 3 — Specific Gravity:**

$$\text{SG} = \frac{M_{mix}}{M_{air}} = \frac{12.00}{28.97} = 0.4142$$

$$\boxed{\,\text{SG} \approx 0.414\,}$$

**Engineering Interpretation (1 mark for interpretation):** The low SG (0.414 < 1) means the gas is **lighter than air** and will rise if leaked — a critical safety point in domestic gas distribution lines.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style, Module 3 Choice Question]**

**(a)** Compare the properties, composition, calorific value, and applications of **Natural Gas** and **LPG**. State clearly why LPG is classified as a gaseous fuel even though it is stored as a liquid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A biogas produced by anaerobic digestion has the composition: CH\(_4\) = 62%, CO\(_2\) = 35%, H\(_2\)S = 1%, N\(_2\) = 2%. Calculate:
   (i) the stoichiometric air required for complete combustion of 1 m³ of this biogas at NTP,
   (ii) the GCV of the biogas, and
   (iii) the Wobbe Index.
   (Use: GCV of CH\(_4\) = 39,820 kJ/m³; air contains 21% O\(_2\) by volume; M\(_{air}\) = 28.97) **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) Solution (7 marks):**

| Property | Natural Gas | LPG |
|----------|------------|-----|
| **State at 1 atm, 20 °C** | Gaseous | Liquid (under own vapor pressure ~7 bar) |
| **Main Composition** | CH\(_4\) (85–95%) | C\(_3\)H\(_8\) (30%) + C\(_4\)H\(_{10}\) (70%) |
| **GCV** | **37–42 MJ/m³** | **46–50 MJ/kg** (≈ 93–122 MJ/m³ equivalent) |
| **Specific Gravity** | 0.55–0.70 | ~1.5 (heavier than air) |
| **Source** | Underground wells | Refinery (fractional distillation of crude) |
| **Applications** | Power generation, PNG, CNG vehicles | Domestic cooking, industrial heating, auto-fuel |
| **Storage** | Pipeline / CNG cylinders / LNG tanks | Pressurized cylinders (50 °C working temp) |

**Why LPG is a gaseous fuel (2 marks):**
- LPG exists as a gas at atmospheric pressure and room temperature; it is **liquefied only for ease of storage and transport** (volume reduces ~270×).
- The moment it is released from the cylinder, it **immediately vaporizes** at atmospheric pressure and burns as a true gas.
- Hence, KTU classifies LPG under **gaseous fuels** (liquefied gaseous fuels).

**Part (b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric Combustion Equation for CH\(_4\):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Air Required for 1 m³ Biogas:**

Volume of CH\(_4\) in 1 m³ biogas = **0.62 m³**
O\(_2\) required = \(0.62 \times 2 = 1.24 \;\text{m}^3\)
Air required (since O\(_2\) = 21% of air):

$$\text{Air} = \frac{1.24}{0.21} = 5.905 \;\text{m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\,\text{Stoichiometric Air} \approx 5.90 \;\text{m}^3 / \text{m}^3 \text{ biogas}\,}$$

**Step 3 — GCV of Biogas:**

Only CH\(_4\) contributes; CO\(_2\), H\(_2\)S (low GCV), N\(_2\) (zero GCV) ignored for first-order calculation.

$$\text{GCV} = 0.62 \times 39{,}820 = 24{,}688.4 \;\text{kJ/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\,\text{GCV} \approx 24{,}688 \;\text{kJ/m}^3 \approx 24.7 \;\text{MJ/m}^3\,}$$

**Step 4 — Mean Molecular Mass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{mix} &= (0.62)(16) + (0.35)(44) + (0.01)(34) + (0.02)(28) \\
&= 9.92 + 15.40 + 0.34 + 0.56 \\
&= 26.22 \;\text{kg/kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\text{SG} = \frac{26.22}{28.97} = 0.9051$$

**Step 5 — Wobbe Index:**

$$\text{WI} = \frac{24{,}688}{\sqrt{0.9051}} = \frac{24{,}688}{0.9514} = 25{,}951 \;\text{kJ/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\,\text{WI} \approx 25{,}951 \;\text{kJ/m}^3 \approx 25.95 \;\text{MJ/m}^3\,}$$

**Engineering Interpretation (bonus 1 mark):** Biogas WI (~26 MJ/m³) is **about 60% of natural gas WI** (~50 MJ/m³), meaning it requires burner re-design when substituting natural gas. CHP engines running on biogas need ~**1.6× the volumetric flow** for the same heat output.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorize before entering the exam hall):**

- ✅ Gaseous fuels are classified by **origin** (natural/manufactured), **composition**, and **calorific value**.
- ✅ **Natural gas** = CH\(_4\)-rich, GCV **37–42 MJ/m³**, lighter than air (SG < 1).
- ✅ **LPG** = C\(_3\)/C\(_4\) hydrocarbons, **liquid under pressure**, GCV **~46 MJ/kg**, **heavier than air** (SG > 1) — leaks accumulate at floor level.
- ✅ **Producer gas** = CO + H\(_2\) + N\(_2\), **low CV** (~5–6 MJ/m³) due to N\(_2\) dilution.
- ✅ **Water gas** = CO + H\(_2\) (no N\(_2\)), **medium CV** (~11 MJ/m³), made by **steam-coke reaction** (endothermic).
- ✅ **Coke oven gas** = by-product of metallurgical coke making, H\(_2\)-rich, GCV ~20 MJ/m³.
- ✅ **Blast furnace gas** = **lowest CV** (~3.5 MJ/m³), CO-rich by-product of iron smelting, used in **stoves** (Cowper stoves).
- ✅ **Biogas** ≈ **60% CH\(_4\) + 35% CO\(_2\)**, GCV ~22–25 MJ/m³, produced by **anaerobic digestion**.
- ✅ **Wobbe Index** = GCV / √SG — a **must-know formula** for KTU.
- ✅ **Mean Molecular Mass** of a gas mix = Σ \(y_i M_i\) — derived from Avogadro's law.
- ✅ **HCV–LCV gap** = 9 × mass of H\(_2\) × (M\(_{H_2O}\)/M\(_{H_2}\)) = **9 × m\(_{H_2}\)** × **2442 kJ/kg** (latent heat of water).
- ✅ **Combustion stoichiometry** of CH\(_4\): 1 vol CH\(_4\) + **2 vol O\(_2\)** → 1 vol CO\(_2\) + 2 vol H\(_2\)O; air required ≈ **9.52 vol per vol CH\(_4\)**.
- ✅ **Safety mantra**: "Natural gas rises, LPG sinks, Carbon monoxide kills silently."

> 🟢 **End of Module 3 — Classification of Gaseous Fuels.** Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE preparation! 📚

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physico-Chemical Principles, Calorific Value, Wobbe Index, and Flame Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Physico-Chemical Principles, Calorific Value, Wobbe Index, and Flame Speed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Physico-Chemical Principles of Combustion

Combustion is a rapid, exothermic chemical reaction between a fuel and an oxidizer (usually atmospheric oxygen) accompanied by the evolution of heat and light. The **physico-chemical principles** govern the stoichiometry, kinetics, heat release, and flame propagation characteristics that determine how a fuel behaves in practical burners, furnaces, and internal combustion engines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Combustion is a self-sustaining, high-temperature oxidation reaction in which the chemical energy stored in the molecular bonds of the fuel is converted into thermal energy, with the rate being controlled by physico-chemical parameters such as activation energy, temperature, pressure, turbulence, and the local equivalence ratio (ϕ).

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Lock-and-Key Reaction":**
Think of combustion like striking a match on a rough surface. The friction supplies the **activation energy** (the minimum push required to start the reaction). Once started, the heat released sustains the reaction on its own — just as a rolling ball can keep rolling on a slope after an initial push. The "rough surface" is the activation energy barrier, the "ball" is the fuel molecule, and the "slope" is the heat release that keeps the reaction going.

**Key Physico-Chemical Parameters:**

- **Stoichiometric Oxygen (O₂ₘᵢₙ):** The exact theoretical quantity of oxygen required for complete combustion of a unit mass (or mole) of fuel, leaving no unburnt fuel and no free oxygen.
- **Stoichiometric Air (Aₛ):** The corresponding mass of air, calculated using the fact that dry air contains **23.2 % O₂** by mass (or 21 % by volume).
- **Equivalence Ratio (ϕ):** The ratio of actual fuel–oxidizer ratio to the stoichiometric fuel–oxidizer ratio.
- **Excess Air (EA):** The amount of air supplied beyond stoichiometric, expressed as a percentage.
- **Activation Energy (Eₐ):** The minimum energy barrier that reactant molecules must overcome for reaction to occur.

## 1.2 Calorific Value (CV)

The **calorific value** (also called heat of combustion) of a fuel is the amount of heat liberated when a unit mass (or volume, for gaseous fuels) of the fuel is completely burned and the products are cooled to a reference temperature, typically **25 °C (298 K)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Reference Forms (Board-Exam Critical):**
> - **GCV / HCV (Gross / Higher Calorific Value):** Heat released assuming the water formed during combustion is condensed to liquid at 25 °C. Latent heat of condensation is recovered.
> - **NCV / LCV (Net / Lower Calorific Value):** Heat released assuming the water leaves as vapour. Latent heat is *not* recovered.
>
> **Rule of thumb for board exams:** In all practical boiler / engine calculations where flue gases leave at > 100 °C, use **LCV (NCV)**.

**Intuitive Analogy — "Hot Tea vs. Steam":**
Imagine burning 1 kg of fuel in a sealed kettle. The water vapour condenses back to liquid, releasing extra heat — that is **GCV**. Now burn the same fuel with the steam escaping out (like an open kettle): you get less useful heat — that is **LCV**. The difference is the latent heat carried away by the steam.

## 1.3 Wobbe Index (WI)

The **Wobbe Index** is a critical parameter for gaseous fuels, indicating their **interchangeability** when burned in appliances designed for a different gas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** The Wobbe Index of a fuel gas is the ratio of its calorific value (on a volumetric basis) to the square root of its specific gravity relative to air, at standard reference conditions.

$$WI = \frac{CV_v}{\sqrt{\rho_{rel}}} = \frac{CV_v}{\sqrt{S.G.}}$$

where $CV_v$ is the volumetric calorific value (kcal/Nm³ or MJ/Nm³) and $S.G.$ is the specific gravity of the gas relative to air.

**Intuitive Analogy — "Two Identical Water Taps:"**
Think of two burners connected to different gas cylinders but feeding the same stove. Even if the gases have different heating values *and* different densities, as long as their **Wobbe Index** is the same, the heat input to the burner remains identical. This is why WI is the *real* figure of merit for gas interchangeability — not the calorific value alone.

## 1.4 Flame Speed

The **flame speed** is the velocity at which a flame front propagates through a combustible mixture relative to the unburnt gas ahead of it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Related Terms (Board Distinction):**
> - **Normal Flame Speed (Sₙ) — also called Burning Velocity:** Velocity of the flame front relative to the unburnt gas mixture.
> - **Burning Velocity (Sᵤ or Sₙ):** Specially used in Bunsen-burner type analysis; numerically and conceptually identical to normal flame speed.
> - **Visible Flame Velocity:** Velocity of the flame front relative to a fixed observer (burned-gas velocity + flame speed).

**Intuitive Analogy — "Forest Fire on a Calm Day vs. a Windy Day:"**
On a calm day, the fire spreads slowly through the forest (low flame speed). The same fire, with a strong wind blowing the flames towards unburnt trees, propagates much faster (high flame speed). In combustion terms, **turbulence**, **temperature**, **pressure**, and **mixture strength** are the "wind" that drives the flame.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physico-Chemical Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.1.1 Combustion of Elementary Fuels

The complete combustion of the principal combustible elements follows fixed stoichiometric equations:

| Combustible Element | Combustion Reaction | O₂ Required (kg/kg of element) | Air Required (kg/kg of element) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon (C) | C + O₂ → CO₂ | 32/12 = **2.667** | 2.667 / 0.232 = **11.49** |
| Hydrogen (H₂) | 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O | 32/4 = **8.000** | 8.000 / 0.232 = **34.48** |
| Sulphur (S) | S + O₂ → SO₂ | 32/32 = **1.000** | 1.000 / 0.232 = **4.31** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is 23.2 % used?** Dry atmospheric air by mass contains roughly 23.2 % O₂ and 76.8 % N₂. By volume it is 21 % O₂ and 79 % N₂. For combustion calculations on a mass basis, **23.2 %** is used.

### 2.1.2 Stoichiometric Air Requirement for a Compound Fuel

For a fuel containing C, H, S, and O (with mass fractions C, H, S, O per kg of fuel), the **minimum stoichiometric oxygen mass** is:

$$O_{2,\,min} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O \quad \text{(kg O₂ per kg fuel)}$$

The corresponding **minimum stoichiometric air mass** is:

$$A_s = \frac{O_{2,\,min}}{0.232} = \frac{(8/3)C + 8H + S - O}{0.232}$$

**Actual Air Supplied:**

$$A_{actual} = A_s \times (1 + \text{Excess Air fraction})$$

**Equivalence Ratio:**

$$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}} = \frac{A_s}{A_{actual}}$$

- $\phi < 1$: Lean mixture (excess air)
- $\phi > 1$: Rich mixture (deficient air)
- $\phi = 1$: Stoichiometric

### 2.1.3 Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)

$$AFR = \frac{A_{actual}}{1 \text{ kg fuel}} \quad \text{(kg air/kg fuel)}$$

For a typical gasoline: AFR ≈ **14.7 : 1**; for diesel: ≈ **14.5 : 1**; for methane (CH₄): ≈ **17.2 : 1**.

## 2.2 Calorific Value — Theory

### 2.2.1 HCV vs LCV Relationship

$$LCV = HCV - 9 \times H \times h_{fg}$$

where:

- $H$ = mass of hydrogen per kg of fuel (or equivalent hydrogen including moisture)
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization of water at standard conditions ≈ **587 kcal/kg** (or 2457 kJ/kg)
- $9H$ = mass of water produced per kg of fuel from hydrogen combustion
- The factor **9** arises because 1 kg of H produces 9 kg of H₂O (from $2H_2 + O_2 \to 2H_2O$)

For **moist fuels**, the moisture content $M$ (per kg of fuel) must also be considered:

$$LCV = HCV - 9 \times H \times h_{fg} - M \times h_{fg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If hydrogen is expressed as a mass fraction (e.g., 0.06 kg H per kg fuel) and $h_{fg}$ is in kcal/kg, the answer for LCV is in kcal/kg. **Watch unit consistency in exams.**

### 2.2.2 Dulong's Formula for Solid/Liquid Fuels

Dulong's empirical formula relates the calorific value of a solid or liquid fuel to its ultimate analysis:

$$HCV = \frac{1}{100}\left[8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\right] \quad \text{kcal/kg}$$

In SI units (kJ/kg):

$$HCV = \frac{1}{100}\left[33800\,C + 144000\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9300\,S\right] \quad \text{kJ/kg}$$

where $C$, $H$, $O$, $S$ are mass percentages in the ultimate analysis.

**Limitations of Dulong's Formula:**

- Valid for coal, coke, fuel oil — *not* for gaseous fuels.
- Assumes complete combustion to CO₂, SO₂, and H₂O.
- Fails for very young coals with high volatile matter.
- The $(O/8)$ correction subtracts the oxygen already bound to hydrogen, which would otherwise over-count the available H₂.

## 2.3 Wobbe Index — Theory

### 2.3.1 Mathematical Definition

$$WI = \frac{CV_v}{\sqrt{G}}$$

where:

- $CV_v$ = volumetric calorific value (kcal/Nm³ or MJ/Nm³)
- $G$ = specific gravity of the gas relative to air ($G = \rho_{gas}/\rho_{air}$)

### 2.3.2 Modified Wobbe Index

In some references, the Wobbe Index uses the **higher heating value**:

$$WI_{HHV} = \frac{HHV_v}{\sqrt{G}}$$

### 2.3.3 Physical Significance

For a fixed orifice burner, the volumetric flow rate $\dot{V}$ is governed by:

$$\dot{V} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{G}}$$

Hence the **heat input rate** to the burner is:

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{V} \times CV_v \propto \frac{CV_v}{\sqrt{G}} = WI$$

**Key result:** Two gases having the **same Wobbe Index** deliver the **same heat input** through the same orifice at the same pressure. This is the foundation of gas-interchangeability rules.

### 2.3.4 Interchangeability Limits

Two gases are considered interchangeable if their Wobbe Indices differ by no more than about **±5 %**, and the deviation index (combustion potential) is within prescribed limits.

## 2.4 Flame Speed — Theory

### 2.4.1 Definition and Types

| Type | Symbol | Reference Frame |
|---|---|---|
| Laminar Burning Velocity (Normal Flame Speed) | $S_L$ or $S_n$ | Unburnt gas |
| Turbulent Burning Velocity | $S_T$ | Unburnt gas |
| Visible Flame Velocity | $V_f$ | Fixed observer |

### 2.4.2 Laminar Burning Velocity from Bunsen Burner Geometry

For a conical Bunsen-type flame, by continuity of unburnt gas entering the cone and the flame area:

$$S_L = \frac{\dot{V}_{unburnt}}{A_{flame}}$$

For a conical flame of height $h$ and base radius $r$:

$$A_{flame} = \pi r \sqrt{r^2 + h^2} = \pi r \ell$$

where $\ell$ is the slant height.

Therefore:

$$S_L = \frac{\dot{V}_{unburnt}}{\pi r \ell}$$

### 2.4.3 Mass Burning Rate

$$\dot{m} = \rho_u \, S_L \, A_{flame}$$

where $\rho_u$ is the unburnt gas density.

### 2.4.4 Factors Affecting Laminar Flame Speed

| Factor | Effect |
|---|---|
| Mixture ratio (ϕ) | Maximum near ϕ ≈ 1.05–1.15 (slightly rich) for hydrocarbons |
| Temperature | $S_L \propto T_u^n$, with $n \approx 1.7$–$2.0$ |
| Pressure | $S_L \propto P^{-m}$, with $m \approx 0.3$ for hydrocarbons |
| Turbulence | Markedly increases effective burning velocity |
| Fuel chemistry | H₂ has the highest $S_L$ (~3 m/s); CH₄ ~0.4 m/s |

### 2.4.5 Turbulent Burning Velocity — Damköhler's Relation

$$\boxed{\frac{S_T}{S_L} \propto \left(\frac{u'}{S_L}\right)^{0.5} \quad \text{(high turbulence regime)}}$$

where $u'$ is the RMS turbulent fluctuation velocity. In simpler board-exam forms:

$$S_T \propto \sqrt{\text{Turbulence Intensity}}$$

---

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $O_{2,min} = \tfrac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O$ | Minimum O₂ per kg fuel | kg O₂/kg fuel |
| 2 | $A_s = O_{2,min}/0.232$ | Stoichiometric air | kg air/kg fuel |
| 3 | $A_{actual} = A_s(1+EA)$ | Actual air with excess air EA | kg air/kg fuel |
| 4 | $\phi = A_s/A_{actual}$ | Equivalence ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $LCV = HCV - 9H h_{fg}$ | Net vs Gross CV | kcal/kg or kJ/kg |
| 6 | $HCV_{Dulong} = \tfrac{1}{100}[8080C+34500(H-O/8)+2240S]$ | HCV from ultimate analysis | kcal/kg |
| 7 | $WI = CV_v/\sqrt{G}$ | Wobbe Index | kcal/Nm³ or MJ/Nm³ |
| 8 | $S_L = \dot{V}_{unburnt}/A_{flame}$ | Normal flame speed | m/s |
| 9 | $S_L \propto T_u^n$ | Temperature dependence | $n \approx 1.7$–2.0 |
| 10 | $S_T/S_L \propto \sqrt{u'/S_L}$ | Damköhler turbulence law | dimensionless |

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air for a Compound Fuel

**Given:** A fuel with ultimate analysis: $C = 0.80$ kg/kg, $H = 0.05$ kg/kg, $S = 0.02$ kg/kg, $O = 0.08$ kg/kg, rest = ash + moisture = 0.05 kg/kg.

**Step 1 — Write individual combustion equations:**

$$\text{C} + O_2 \to CO_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 12\text{ kg C} \to 32\text{ kg } O_2$$

$$4\text{H} + O_2 \to 2H_2O \quad \Rightarrow \quad 4\text{ kg H} \to 32\text{ kg } O_2$$

$$\text{S} + O_2 \to SO_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 32\text{ kg S} \to 32\text{ kg } O_2$$

**Step 2 — Compute O₂ demand per kg of each element:**

For Carbon: $\dfrac{32}{12} = \dfrac{8}{3} = 2.667$ kg O₂ per kg C.

For Hydrogen: $\dfrac{32}{4} = 8$ kg O₂ per kg H.

For Sulphur: $\dfrac{32}{32} = 1$ kg O₂ per kg S.

**Step 3 — Total O₂ demand for 1 kg fuel:**

$$O_{2,\,demand} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H + 1S$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
O_{2,\,demand} &= \frac{8}{3}(0.80) + 8(0.05) + 1(0.02) \\
&= 2.133 + 0.400 + 0.020 \\
&= 2.553 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Account for fuel-bound oxygen:**

The fuel already contains oxygen that can be used internally (e.g., part of H is already bound as –OH groups). Standard assumption: oxygen in the fuel can supply its equivalent of hydrogen combustion. So subtract the O contribution:

$$O_{2,\,net} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
O_{2,\,net} &= \frac{8}{3}(0.80) + 8(0.05) + 1(0.02) - 0.08 \\
&= 2.133 + 0.400 + 0.020 - 0.080 \\
&= 2.473 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Convert O₂ mass to air mass:**

Since dry air is 23.2 % O₂ by mass:

$$A_s = \frac{O_{2,\,net}}{0.232} = \frac{2.473}{0.232} = 10.66 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 6 — With 30 % excess air:**

$$A_{actual} = A_s \times 1.30 = 10.66 \times 1.30 = 13.86 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship between HCV and LCV

**Start with a generic hydrocarbon fuel CₓHᵧ, burned completely:**

$$C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)O_2 \to xCO_2 + \frac{y}{2}H_2O_{(l)} \quad \text{(HCV basis)}$$

$$C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)O_2 \to xCO_2 + \frac{y}{2}H_2O_{(v)} \quad \text{(LCV basis)}$$

**Mass balance on hydrogen:**

Mass of fuel = $12x + y$ kg/kmol fuel.

Mass of H in fuel = $y$ kg/kmol fuel.

Mass fraction of H per kg fuel:

$$\frac{H}{1} = \frac{y}{12x+y} \quad \Rightarrow \quad H \text{ kg H per kg fuel}$$

Water produced per kg of H:

$$4\text{H} + O_2 \to 2H_2O \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 \times 18}{4} = 9 \text{ kg } H_2O \text{ per kg H}$$

So water produced per kg fuel = **9H** kg.

**Energy difference:**

Latent heat carried away by water when LCV is measured:

$$\Delta Q = 9H \times h_{fg}$$

Therefore:

$$LCV = HCV - 9H \times h_{fg}$$

**With moisture content $M$ in the fuel (kg per kg fuel):**

The moisture also evaporates and absorbs $h_{fg}$ per kg:

$$LCV = HCV - 9H h_{fg} - M h_{fg} = HCV - h_{fg}(9H + M)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical:** A fuel oil has $H = 0.11$ kg/kg and moisture $M = 0.02$ kg/kg. $HCV = 10{,}200$ kcal/kg. Calculate LCV.

$$\begin{aligned}
LCV &= 10200 - 587 \times (9 \times 0.11 + 0.02) \\
&= 10200 - 587 \times (0.99 + 0.02) \\
&= 10200 - 587 \times 1.01 \\
&= 10200 - 592.87 \\
&= 9607.13 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Wobbe Index from Burner Physics

Consider a gas discharging from an orifice of area $A_o$ under pressure $P$ into atmosphere.

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate from Bernoulli-type discharge:**

$$\dot{m} = C_d \, A_o \, P \sqrt{\frac{2\rho_g}{P}}$$

(Simplified; full compressible form yields similar $\rho_g^{0.5}$ dependence.)

**Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate (at standard conditions):**

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_g} = C_d \, A_o \, P \sqrt{\frac{2}{P \rho_g}} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\rho_g}}$$

**Step 3 — Heat input to burner:**

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{V} \times CV_v \propto \frac{CV_v}{\sqrt{\rho_g}} \propto \frac{CV_v}{\sqrt{G}}$$

(where $G = \rho_g/\rho_{air}$).

**Step 4 — Define Wobbe Index:**

$$\boxed{WI = \frac{CV_v}{\sqrt{G}}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** For the *same orifice and supply pressure*, two gases with identical WI deliver identical thermal energy to the burner.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical:** A natural gas has $CV_v = 8500$ kcal/Nm³ and $G = 0.65$. A substitute gas has $CV_v = 4000$ kcal/Nm³ and $G = 0.18$. Compare WIs.

$$WI_1 = \frac{8500}{\sqrt{0.65}} = \frac{8500}{0.806} = 10546 \text{ kcal/Nm}^3$$

$$WI_2 = \frac{4000}{\sqrt{0.18}} = \frac{4000}{0.424} = 9434 \text{ kcal/Nm}^3$$

The deviation = $\frac{10546 - 9434}{10546} \times 100\% \approx 10.5\%$, which exceeds the ±5 % interchangeability limit — *not directly interchangeable* without burner modification.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Laminar Flame Speed from Continuity

Consider a steady, one-dimensional flame in a duct. Let the flame be a thin sheet separating unburnt gas (region 1) from burnt gas (region 2).

**Conservation of mass across the flame:**

$$\rho_u S_L A = \rho_b S_b A \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho_u S_L = \rho_b S_b$$

Therefore $S_b < S_L$ since burnt gas is hot and expanded ($\rho_b < \rho_u$).

**Visible flame velocity (relative to lab):**

$$V_f = S_L + u_b - u_u$$

For a stationary flame in a tube (cold flow velocity = $u_0$):

$$V_f = S_L + \frac{\rho_u}{\rho_b} S_L - u_0$$

**For a Bunsen-type conical flame:**

The unburnt gas velocity at the burner port equals $u_0$, and continuity gives:

$$u_0 \cdot A_{port} = S_L \cdot A_{flame}$$

Therefore:

$$S_L = \frac{u_0 A_{port}}{A_{flame}}$$

For a cone of base radius $r$ and slant height $\ell$:

$$A_{flame} = \pi r \ell$$

$$\boxed{S_L = \frac{\dot{V}_{unburnt}}{\pi r \ell}}$$

**Worked Numerical:** A Bunsen burner has port radius $r = 5$ mm. The cone flame has height $h = 20$ mm. Volumetric flow rate of unburnt gas $\dot{V} = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s. Find $S_L$.

Slant height:

$$\ell = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{(0.005)^2 + (0.020)^2} = \sqrt{0.000025 + 0.000400} = \sqrt{0.000425} = 0.02062 \text{ m}$$

Flame area:

$$A_{flame} = \pi (0.005)(0.02062) = 3.24 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

Flame speed:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_L &= \frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{3.24 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= 0.154 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Summary

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Magnitude | Strongest Influencer |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric air | $A_s$ | 10–14 kg air/kg fuel | Fuel composition |
| Excess air | EA | 10–30 % (gaseous), 15–50 % (solid) | Combustion completeness vs. heat loss |
| GCV of coal | HCV | 5000–8000 kcal/kg | Rank & fixed carbon |
| GCV of natural gas | HCV | 8500–10500 kcal/Nm³ | Methane content |
| Wobbe Index (NG) | WI | 10000–13000 kcal/Nm³ | CH₄ fraction |
| Laminar flame speed of CH₄ | $S_L$ | ~0.40 m/s | Equivalence ratio |
| Laminar flame speed of H₂ | $S_L$ | ~2.80 m/s | High diffusivity of H₂ |
| Visible flame velocity | $V_f$ | 5–50 m/s in burners | Turbulence, expansion ratio |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Combustion Process — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Storage] --> B[Fuel Preparation<br>Atomization or Vaporization]
    B --> C[Mixing with Oxidizer<br>Air or O2]
    C --> D{Equivalence Ratio Check}
    D -- Stoichiometric or Lean --> E[Ignition Source<br>Spark or Pilot Flame]
    D -- Rich --> F[Recirculate or Reject]
    E --> G[Combustion Zone<br>Heat Release and Flame Front]
    G --> H[Flame Speed Propagation<br>Laminar or Turbulent]
    H --> I[Hot Combustion Products]
    I --> J[Heat Transfer Surface<br>Boiler Tubes or Engine Cylinder]
    J --> K[Cooled Flue Gases]
    K --> L[Exhaust Stack]
    I --> M[Combustion Analysis<br>Orsat and Calorimetry]
    M --> N[Performance Metrics<br>Efficiency and Emissions]
```

## 4.2 Wobbe Index — Interchangeability Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Two Gases G1 and G2] --> B[Measure CVv1 and CVv2]
    B --> C[Measure G1 and G2]
    C --> D[Compute WI1 and WI2]
    D --> E{Abs Difference Within 5 Percent}
    E -- Yes --> F{Combustion Potential Within Limit}
    E -- No --> G[Modify Burner Orifice or Use Blending]
    F -- Yes --> H[Gases Interchangeable]
    F -- No --> G
    G --> I[Recheck Wobbe After Blending]
    I --> D
```

## 4.3 Flame Speed Determination — Bunsen Burner Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Gas Cylinder] --> B[Pressure Regulator]
    B --> C[Rotameter for Volumetric Flow]
    C --> D[Bunsen Burner Port]
    D --> E[Cone Shaped Flame]
    E --> F[Measure Height h]
    E --> G[Measure Base Radius r]
    F --> H[Compute Slant Height l]
    G --> H
    C --> I[Read Volumetric Flow Rate Vdot]
    H --> J[Compute Flame Area A Flame]
    I --> K[Apply SL equals Vdot divided by A Flame]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Laminar Flame Speed Result]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Calorific Value Determination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Take 1 gram of Fuel Sample] --> B[Bomb Calorimeter]
    B --> C[Record Initial Water Temperature T1]
    B --> D[Ignite Fuel with Oxygen at 25 atm]
    D --> E[Combustion Releases Heat]
    E --> F[Record Final Water Temperature T2]
    F --> G[Compute Heat Absorbed Q equals m c dT]
    G --> H[Apply Acid and Fuse Wire Corrections]
    H --> I[Result is HCV in cal per g]
    I --> J{Subtract Latent Heat 9H hfg}
    J --> K[Result is LCV in cal per g]
    I --> L[Alternative: Apply Dulong Formula]
    L --> M[Compare with Experimental HCV]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a functional stage; arrows represent the data/material flow. For the flame speed diagram, the *measure* blocks must be executed sequentially before the *compute* blocks can be invoked.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define calorific value. Distinguish between HCV and LCV.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Calorific value** (1 mark): The amount of heat released when a unit mass (or volume) of fuel is completely burned and the products are cooled to a reference temperature of 25 °C.
- **HCV** (1 mark): Higher (Gross) Calorific Value — heat released when the water formed during combustion is condensed to liquid and its latent heat is recovered.
- **LCV** (1 mark): Lower (Net) Calorific Value — heat released when the water leaves as vapour; latent heat of vaporization is *not* recovered. $LCV = HCV - 9H h_{fg}$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define Wobbe Index. Why is it important for gaseous fuel interchangeability?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition** (1 mark): $WI = CV_v / \sqrt{G}$, where $CV_v$ is the volumetric calorific value and $G$ is the specific gravity relative to air.
- **Physical significance** (1 mark): For the same orifice and pressure, two gases with identical WI deliver identical heat input to the burner.
- **Practical use** (1 mark): WI is the figure of merit for gas interchangeability; gases differing in WI by ≤ ±5 % can be used in the same appliance. Used in city gas distribution, LPG-air blending, LNG substitution.

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 72 %, H = 5 %, S = 1.5 %, O = 8 %, N = 1.5 %, Ash = 12 %. Calculate (i) stoichiometric air required for complete combustion of 1 kg of coal, (ii) the HCV using Dulong's formula, and (iii) the LCV given $h_{fg} = 587$ kcal/kg.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
With the air supplied at 35 % excess, determine (i) the actual air supplied per kg fuel, (ii) the wet and dry flue gas analysis by volume, and (iii) the equivalence ratio.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Convert mass percentages to mass per kg fuel:**

$C = 0.72$, $H = 0.05$, $S = 0.015$, $O = 0.08$, $N = 0.015$, Ash $= 0.12$ kg/kg fuel.

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric oxygen:**

$$\begin{aligned}
O_{2,min} &= \frac{8}{3}(0.72) + 8(0.05) + 1(0.015) - 0.08 \\
&= 1.920 + 0.400 + 0.015 - 0.080 \\
&= 2.255 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric air:**

$$A_s = \frac{2.255}{0.232} = 9.72 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 4 — HCV by Dulong's formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
HCV &= \frac{1}{100}\left[8080(72) + 34500\left(5 - \frac{8}{8}\right) + 2240(1.5)\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{100}\left[581760 + 34500(4) + 3360\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{100}\left[581760 + 138000 + 3360\right] \\
&= \frac{723120}{100} \\
&= 7231.2 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — LCV:**

$$LCV = HCV - 9H h_{fg} = 7231.2 - 9(0.05)(587) = 7231.2 - 264.15 = 6967.05 \text{ kcal/kg}$$

---

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1 — Actual air supplied:**

$$A_{actual} = 9.72 \times 1.35 = 13.12 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 2 — Composition of air:**

Per kg air: $O_2 = 0.232$ kg, $N_2 = 0.768$ kg.

Total $O_2$ supplied = $13.12 \times 0.232 = 3.044$ kg.

Total $N_2$ supplied = $13.12 \times 0.768 = 10.077$ kg.

**Step 3 — O₂ consumed in combustion:**

$O_2$ consumed = $2.255$ kg (from stoichiometry).

$O_2$ in flue gas = $3.044 - 2.255 = 0.789$ kg = $0.789/32 = 0.0247$ kgmol.

**Step 4 — Composition of dry flue gas (kgmol basis):**

- $CO_2$ from C: $0.72/12 = 0.0600$ kgmol
- $SO_2$ from S: $0.015/32 = 0.000469$ kgmol
- $N_2$ from fuel: $0.015/28 = 0.000536$ kgmol
- $N_2$ from air: $10.077/28 = 0.3599$ kgmol
- $O_2$ excess: $0.0247$ kgmol

Total dry flue gas = $0.0600 + 0.000469 + 0.000536 + 0.3599 + 0.0247 = 0.4456$ kgmol.

**Volume percentages (dry basis):**

- $CO_2$: $\frac{0.0600}{0.4456} \times 100 = 13.46\%$
- $SO_2$: $\frac{0.000469}{0.4456} \times 100 = 0.11\%$
- $N_2$: $\frac{0.3604}{0.4456} \times 100 = 80.88\%$
- $O_2$: $\frac{0.0247}{0.4456} \times 100 = 5.54\%$

**Step 5 — Wet flue gas (additional H₂O):**

$H_2O$ from H: $0.05/2 \times 1 = 0.025$ kgmol (since 2H → H₂O gives 0.5 mol H₂O per mol H; here $0.05/2 = 0.025$ kgmol).

Total wet flue gas = $0.4456 + 0.025 = 0.4706$ kgmol.

Wet-basis volume percentages:

- $H_2O$: $5.31\%$
- $CO_2$: $12.75\%$
- $O_2$: $5.25\%$
- $N_2$: $76.59\%$
- $SO_2$: $0.10\%$

**Step 6 — Equivalence ratio:**

$$\phi = \frac{A_s}{A_{actual}} = \frac{9.72}{13.12} = 0.741$$

This indicates a lean mixture, as expected with 35 % excess air.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Define Wobbe Index. Two gases have the following properties: Gas 1: $CV_v = 9000$ kcal/Nm³, $G = 0.60$. Gas 2: $CV_v = 4500$ kcal/Nm³, $G = 0.15$. Determine (i) the Wobbe Index of each gas, (ii) whether the gases are interchangeable, and (iii) the ratio of heat inputs to a burner from the two gases if supplied at the same pressure through the same orifice.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
Explain the concept of flame speed. A Bunsen burner with a port of diameter 10 mm produces a conical flame of height 30 mm. The volumetric flow rate of unburnt methane-air mixture is $2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ Nm³/s. Calculate the laminar flame speed.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Wobbe Indices:**

$$WI_1 = \frac{9000}{\sqrt{0.60}} = \frac{9000}{0.7746} = 11618 \text{ kcal/Nm}^3$$

$$WI_2 = \frac{4500}{\sqrt{0.15}} = \frac{4500}{0.3873} = 11619 \text{ kcal/Nm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Interchangeability:**

The two WIs are essentially identical (within rounding): $\Delta = 0.01\%$. Hence the gases are *interchangeable* (well within ±5 %).

**Step 3 — Heat input ratio:**

For the same orifice and pressure, $\dot{Q} \propto WI$. Therefore:

$$\frac{\dot{Q}_1}{\dot{Q}_2} = \frac{WI_1}{WI_2} \approx 1.0$$

The heat inputs are practically equal, confirming interchangeability.

---

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1 — Concept:**

Flame speed is the velocity at which a flame front propagates through a combustible mixture relative to the unburnt gas. It is governed by diffusion of active species (H, OH, O) and heat ahead of the flame front.

**Step 2 — Geometry:**

Port radius: $r = 0.005$ m. Flame height: $h = 0.030$ m.

Slant height:

$$\ell = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{(0.005)^2 + (0.030)^2} = \sqrt{0.000025 + 0.0009} = \sqrt{0.000925} = 0.03041 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Flame surface area:**

$$A_{flame} = \pi r \ell = \pi (0.005)(0.03041) = 4.776 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Laminar flame speed:**

$$S_L = \frac{\dot{V}}{A_{flame}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{4.776 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.523 \text{ m/s}$$

This is typical for methane-air mixtures (literature value ≈ 0.40 m/s; the slight excess is due to a slightly rich mixture).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion in CV:** Students frequently mix cal/g, kcal/kg, and MJ/Nm³. Always state the unit in every line.
> 2. **Forgetting the O/8 term in Dulong's formula:** The oxygen correction is non-negotiable. Skipping it costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing mass % and mass fraction:** Dulong's formula uses **mass percentages**, not fractions.
> 4. **WI Direction Error:** Students sometimes invert the WI ratio. Always confirm by writing $WI = CV_v/\sqrt{G}$.
> 5. **Flame Speed Geometry:** Forgetting to compute the **slant height** of the cone (not just the height) is the most common mistake. Bunsen flame is a 3-D cone, not a triangle.
> 6. **Excess air sign:** A 30 % excess air means $A_{actual} = 1.30 \, A_s$, *not* $1.30$ extra; the total supplied is 130 % of stoichiometric.
> 7. **Wet vs. Dry Flue Gas:** When asked for "wet" analysis, water vapour must be included; "dry" excludes it.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Stoichiometric O₂ per kg fuel:** $O_{2,min} = \tfrac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O$ (in kg).
- **Stoichiometric air:** divide O₂ by **0.232** (mass fraction of O₂ in dry air).
- **HCV → LCV:** subtract $9H \cdot h_{fg}$ (+ $M h_{fg}$ for moisture); use $h_{fg} = 587$ kcal/kg or 2457 kJ/kg.
- **Dulong's formula** uses **mass percentages** and gives HCV directly in kcal/kg.
- **Wobbe Index** = $CV_v / \sqrt{G}$; equal WI ⇒ equal heat input at constant orifice.
- **Interchangeability** tolerance: ±5 % in WI, plus combustion-potential check.
- **Flame speed** from Bunsen cone: $S_L = \dot{V}/(\pi r \ell)$, where $\ell = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$.
- **Factors increasing $S_L$:** higher temperature, leaner-than-rich mixture, more turbulence, hydrogen enrichment.
- **Equivalence ratio** $\phi = A_s/A_{actual}$; lean ⇐ $\phi < 1$, rich ⇐ $\phi > 1$.
- **Typical $S_L$ values:** H₂ ≈ 2.8 m/s, CH₄ ≈ 0.4 m/s, propane ≈ 0.46 m/s.
- **Typical WI:** Natural gas ≈ 11,000–13,000 kcal/Nm³; LPG ≈ 18,000–20,000 kcal/Nm³.
- **Remember** the **9** in LCV conversion comes from $H_2O$ mass produced per kg H (since 4 kg H → 36 kg H₂O ⇒ 9 kg/kg).
- **N₂ in flue gas:** Always 79 % of supplied N₂ travels through unchanged. Use this as a check.
- **Combustion efficiency ≈ completeness of burning** — Excess air helps but reduces flame temperature.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Gaseous Fuels: Natural Gases, Methane from Coal Mines, Manufactured Gases, Producer Gas, Water Gas, Biogas, Refinery Gas, LPG
Cleaning and Purification of Gaseous Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Cleaning and Purification of Gaseous Fuels ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Cleaning and Purification of Gaseous Fuels** refers to the sequential removal of undesirable solid, liquid, and gaseous contaminants from raw fuel gases (such as producer gas, water gas, coal gas, natural gas, refinery off-gases, and biogas) to obtain a clean, dry, and chemically compatible fuel stream suitable for combustion, synthesis, or pipeline distribution. The contaminants are broadly classified into three categories: | Category | Common Contaminants | Typical Source | |---|---|---| | **Particulate matter** | Dust, soot, ash, fly-ash, char particles | Gasification, partial combustion | | **Condensable vapors** | Tar, water vapor, naphthalene, light oils | Coal pyrolysis, biomass gasification | | **Gaseous impurities** | H₂S, COS, CS₂, CO₂, NH₃, HCN, mercaptans, SO₂ | Sulfur in feedstock, nitrogen compounds | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 Scheme, students must be able to describe the construction and working of at least one **dust removal**, **tar removal**, **H₂S removal**, and **CO₂ removal** unit, and explain the operating principles of **dry desulfurization** versus **wet desulfurization** processes. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of the cleaning and purification train as the **"laundry cycle" of a fuel gas**: - Imagine your car is fueled by a gaseous fuel that comes straight from a coal gasifier. If you fed this raw gas directly to an engine, it would behave like trying to drink a milkshake through a coffee stirrer — **clogged, corroded, and inefficient**. - The **dust and tar** are like the solid chunks and oily droplets stuck in the straw. - The **moisture and H₂S** are like the sour, watery impurities that would damage the stomach (combustor, turbine, or pipeline). - So just as you would strain, filter, and even chemically treat water before drinking, the fuel gas passes through a **series of unit operations** — cyclones, scrubbers, condensers, and absorbers — each acting like a specific cleaning station. > [!NOTE] > Each cleaning stage progressively improves gas quality, and the **order matters**: you cannot scrub H₂S with an amine solution if the gas is still loaded with tar (tar will poison the amine), and you cannot dry the gas effectively if it is still hot and dusty. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following key constants and standard conditions are used throughout the design of gas cleaning equipment: - **Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP)**: 0 °C (**273.15 K**) and 1 atm (**101.325 kPa**) - **Normal Temperature and Pressure (NTP)**: 15.5 °C (**288.71 K**) and 1 atm - **Universal Gas Constant**: R = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** - **Molar volume at STP**: 22.414 m³·kmol⁻¹ - **Acceptable H₂S limit for town gas**: < **20 mg/Nm³** - **Acceptable dust load for IC engine gas**: < **50 mg/Nm³** - **Dew point specification for pipeline gas**: < **−10 °C** at line pressure > [!TIP] > Always convert gas volumes to **Nm³ (normal cubic meters)** when reporting impurity concentrations — KTU examiners actively deduct marks for mixing up Nm³ with m³. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Cleaning is Necessary — The "Four D's" Principle The driving forces for gas cleaning can be grouped into four engineering objectives, memorably called the **Four D's**: 1. **Durability** — To protect downstream equipment (boilers, engines, turbines, catalysts) from corrosion, abrasion, and fouling. 2. **Dependability** — To ensure uninterrupted gas flow without pipe blockages from tar or ice formation. 3. **Diligence (Compliance)** — To meet environmental emission standards for SO₂, NOₓ, and particulate matter. 4. **Dilution of Hazard** — To prevent toxic exposures (H₂S is lethal at **>700 ppm**; CO is lethal at **>1000 ppm**). ### 2.2 Sequential Cleaning Train — Order of Operations A typical industrial gas cleaning train follows a strict sequence: - **Step 1 (Hot Gas Cooling):** Quench the gas from ~800–1000 °C to ~80–120 °C to condense tars. - **Step 2 (Particulate Removal):** Cyclones, multicyclones, and/or bag filters. - **Step 3 (Tar and Oil Mist Removal):** Water scrubbers, venturi scrubbers, electrostatic tar precipitators. - **Step 4 (Sulfur Removal):** H₂S scrubbers — iron oxide boxes, amine contactors, or wet oxidation systems. - **Step 5 (CO₂ and Acid Gas Removal):** Hot potassium carbonate, amine systems, or water wash. - **Step 6 (Drying):** Glycol dehydration, molecular sieve adsorbers. - **Step 7 (Polishing):** Activated carbon beds for trace organics and odor control. > [!NOTE] > **Why this order?** Each step changes the gas temperature, humidity, or chemistry in a way that protects the next. Reversing the order is a classic KTU exam mistake — for instance, sending a hot, tar-laden gas directly into an amine contactor will permanently destroy the amine. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table consolidates every high-yield equation you need for this topic. **Remember**: avoid the `|` symbol in table cells; use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. | # | Concept | Formula | Description of Terms | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Terminal settling velocity (Stokes' Law) | $$v_t \;=\; \dfrac{d_p^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho_g)\,g}{18\,\mu}$$ | $d_p$ = particle diameter, $\rho_p$ = particle density, $\rho_g$ = gas density, $\mu$ = gas viscosity, $g$ = gravity | m/s | | 2 | Cyclone separation efficiency (grade efficiency) | $$\eta_i \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{d_{50}}{d_i}\right)^{n}}$$ | $d_{50}$ = cut diameter (50% efficiency), $d_i$ = size of fraction $i$, $n$ = sharpness index (typically 2–4) | dimensionless | | 3 | Cyclone pressure drop (Shepherd–Lapple) | $$\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_g\,v_{in}^{\,2}}{2}\,\left(\xi \,\dfrac{H\,B}{D_e^{\,2}}\right)$$ | $v_{in}$ = inlet velocity, $H$ = cyclone height, $B$ = inlet width, $D_e$ = exhaust duct dia., $\xi$ = empirical factor ≈ **16** | Pa | | 4 | Wet scrubber collection efficiency (venturi) | $$\eta \;=\; 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_L\,\rho_L\,\eta_d}{Q_G\,\rho_G}\right)$$ | $Q_L$ = liquid flow, $Q_G$ = gas flow, $\eta_d$ = single-droplet efficiency | dimensionless | | 5 | Gas volume at standard conditions | $$V_{\text{std}} \;=\; V \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_{\text{std}}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{\text{std}}}{T}$$ | $V$ = actual gas volume, $P$, $T$ = actual conditions, $P_{\text{std}}$ = **101.325 kPa**, $T_{\text{std}}$ = **273.15 K** | Nm³ | | 6 | Mole fraction conversion (ppm to mol%) | $$y_i \;=\; \dfrac{\text{ppm}_i}{10^{6}}$$ | Used for very dilute impurities like H₂S | dimensionless | | 7 | Henry's Law (gas solubility) | $$p_i \;=\; H \cdot x_i$$ | $H$ = Henry's constant (Pa·mol fraction⁻¹), $p_i$ = partial pressure of solute | Pa | | 8 | Amine loading (gas absorption) | $$\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{\text{moles acid gas absorbed}}{\text{moles active amine}}$$ | Typical operating $\alpha$ = **0.3–0.5** for MEA | dimensionless | | 9 | Claus process H₂S conversion | $$\mathrm{H_2S} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{O_2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{S} + \mathrm{H_2O}$$ | Overall conversion ≈ **95–98%** in single stage | — | | 10 | Iron oxide box breakthrough (sulfur capacity) | $$S_{\text{cap}} \;=\; \rho_{\text{oxide}} \cdot w \cdot X_S$$ | $\rho_{\text{oxide}}$ = bed bulk density, $w$ = mass of oxide, $X_S$ = mass fraction of sulfur picked up (typically **0.5–0.6 kg S/kg Fe₂O₃**) | kg S | | 11 | Desulfurization efficiency | $$\eta_{DS} \;=\; \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$$ | $C_{\text{in}}$, $C_{\text{out}}$ = inlet and outlet H₂S concentrations | % | | 12 | Minimum gas velocity for tar entrainment | $$v_{\min} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{4\,d_p\,(\rho_p - \rho_g)\,g}{3\,C_D\,\rho_g}}$$ | $C_D$ = drag coefficient ≈ **0.44** for turbulent | m/s | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **IC Engines running on producer gas** in rural India cannot tolerate dust above **50 mg/Nm³** because particles erode the cylinder walls; hence the popularity of two-stage cyclone + wet scrubber trains in decentralized power generation. - **Pipeline-quality natural gas** must be dried to a dew point below the operating pressure's saturation temperature to prevent methane hydrate formation (a solid "ice" that blocks pipes). - **Synthesis gas (syngas)** for ammonia or methanol plants demands sulfur levels below **0.1 ppm** because sulfur is a permanent poison for nickel and iron catalysts in reforming and Fischer–Tropsch reactors. - **Biogas upgradation** to bio-CNG uses pressure swing adsorption (PSA) with activated carbon or molecular sieves to simultaneously remove H₂S, CO₂, and moisture to meet automotive fuel standards. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** When asked to "compare dry and wet desulfurization", always discuss **(a) operating temperature**, **(b) sulfur capacity**, **(c) regenerability**, and **(d) suitability for high/low H₂S loads** — examiners award marks on a 4-point comparison grid. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Terminal Settling Velocity (Stokes' Regime) This derivation justifies why cyclone separators and settling chambers can only remove particles above a certain size. **Given:** A spherical particle of diameter $d_p$ settling under gravity in a viscous gas. **Step 1 — Forces acting on the particle:** - Weight (downward): $F_g = \dfrac{\pi}{6}\,d_p^{\,3}\,\rho_p\,g$ - Buoyancy (upward): $F_b = \dfrac{\pi}{6}\,d_p^{\,3}\,\rho_g\,g$ - Drag force (opposing motion, Stokes regime): $F_D = 3\,\pi\,\mu\,d_p\,v$ **Step 2 — At terminal velocity, net force = 0:** $$ \dfrac{\pi}{6}\,d_p^{\,3}\,(\rho_p - \rho_g)\,g \;=\; 3\,\pi\,\mu\,d_p\,v_t $$ **Step 3 — Solve for $v_t$:** $$ v_t \;=\; \dfrac{d_p^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho_g)\,g}{18\,\mu} $$ **Step 4 — Validity check (Reynolds number limit):** The Stokes law is valid only when $\mathrm{Re}_p = \dfrac{\rho_g\,v_t\,d_p}{\mu} < 0.2$. For larger particles or higher velocities, the drag coefficient transitions through the Allen, Newton, and turbulent regimes. ### 3.2 Derivation: Cyclone Cut Diameter A cyclone is a centrifugal separator.
