We want to find the particle diameter $d_{50}$ that is collected with **50% efficiency**. **Step 1 — Radial settling velocity in the cyclone:** A particle in a vortex experiences a centrifugal acceleration $v_t^{\,2}/r$, replacing $g$ in Stokes' law: $$ v_r \;=\; \dfrac{d_p^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho_g)\,v_t^{\,2}}{18\,\mu\,r} $$ **Step 2 — Time of residence in the cyclone:** The gas spirals downward and exits after traversing roughly $N_e$ effective turns: $$ t_{\text{res}} \;=\; \dfrac{2\,\pi\,r\,N_e}{v_t} $$ **Step 3 — Distance fallen during residence (must equal cyclone radius R):** For 50% collection, set radial displacement equal to $R$: $$ R \;=\; v_r \cdot t_{\text{res}} \;=\; \dfrac{d_{50}^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho_g)\,v_t^{\,2}}{18\,\mu\,r} \cdot \dfrac{2\,\pi\,r\,N_e}{v_t} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $d_{50}$:** $$ d_{50} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{9\,\mu\,R}{\pi\,N_e\,v_t\,(\rho_p - \rho_g)}} $$ **Step 5 — Engineering implication:** A smaller $d_{50}$ means finer particles are captured. To improve efficiency, **increase inlet velocity $v_t$** or **increase effective turns $N_e$** (use longer cones). ### 3.3 Derivation: Material Balance Across an Amine Absorber For a counter-current amine contactor with H₂S-bearing gas: **Step 1 — Define the operating line:** For a dilute system, gas and liquid flow rates $G$ and $L$ are essentially constant. Component balance on H₂S over a differential stage: $$ G \cdot dy \;=\; L \cdot (-dx) $$ **Step 2 — Integrate from gas inlet (1) to outlet (2):** $$ G\,(y_1 - y_2) \;=\; L\,(x_1 - x_2) $$ **Step 3 — Introduce amine loading $\alpha$ and stoichiometric coefficient $n$:** For MEA: $\mathrm{H_2S} + 2\,\mathrm{HOC_2H_4NH_2} \rightarrow (\mathrm{HOC_2H_4NH_3})_2\mathrm{S}$, so $n = 2$ moles amine per mole H₂S. $$ \alpha \;=\; \dfrac{n\,(y_1 - y_2)\,G}{(G_M)\,L} $$ where $G_M$ is the molar flow of pure amine. This equation lets you size the amine circulation rate for a target H₂S outlet. **Step 4 — Regeneration energy estimate:** The rich amine is reboiled at ~120 °C to release H₂S. The heat duty $Q_R$ is approximately: $$ Q_R \;\approx\; 3.5 \text{ to } 4.5\;\mathrm{GJ}/\mathrm{ton\;S} $$ This is why **selective amines** (MDEA, piperazine) that allow lower reboiler temperatures are preferred for H₂S removal over CO₂. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Desulfurization Methods | Method | Reagent / Sorbent | Operating T (°C) | Sulfur Capacity | Regenerable? | Best for | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Iron oxide boxes | $\mathrm{Fe_2O_3 \cdot H_2O}$ (Bog iron) | 25–45 | 0.5–0.6 kg S/kg | No (disposable) | Small-scale town gas | | Activated carbon | Impregnated C | 25–50 | 0.1–0.2 kg S/kg | Yes (steam) | Trace H₂S polishing | | Zinc oxide | ZnO | 350–400 | 0.15–0.20 kg S/kg | No | Syngas polishing | | Amine (MEA/MDEA) | Aqueous amine | 40–60 (abs), 110–125 (regen) | 0.05–0.10 kg S/kg | Yes | Large-scale, deep removal | | Hot K₂CO₃ (Benfield) | K₂CO₃ + activator | 100–115 | 0.015–0.025 kg S/kg | Yes | High-pressure gas | | Vacuum carbonate | K₂CO₃ | 50–60 | 0.015 kg S/kg | Yes (vacuum) | Low H₂S, low pressure | | Stretford (wet oxidation) | NaVO₃ + ADA | 35–45 | High (oxidative) | Yes (air) | Sour gas, elemental S recovery | | Lo-Cat | Fe³⁺/Fe²⁺ chelate | 25–50 | High | Yes (air) | Biogas, tail gas | | Claus process | Thermal + catalytic | 1000–1300 / 200–350 | Conversion basis | Continuous | >50% H₂S streams | --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Cleaning Train Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Hot Gas
800 to 1000 C] --> B[Quench Cooler
80 to 120 C] B --> C[Cyclone
Separator] C --> D[Venturi
Scrubber] D --> E[Tar Trap
and Mist Eliminator] E --> F[H2S Removal
Amine or Iron Oxide] F --> G[CO2 Removal
Hot Carbonate] G --> H[Gas Dryer
Glycol or Sieve] H --> I[Activated Carbon
Polisher] I --> J[Clean Dry Gas
to Engine or Grid] ``` ### 4.2 Cyclone Separator — Internal Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Dust Laden Gas
enters tangentially] --> B[Vortex
Outer Spiral Down] B --> C[Cone
Region] C --> D[Dust Hop
at Bottom] B --> E[Inner Spiral Up] E --> F[Clean Gas Exit
via Vortex Finder] F --> G[Outlet Duct] D --> H[Dust Discharge
Valve] style A fill:#FFD580 style F fill:#90EE90 style H fill:#D3D3D3 ``` ### 4.3 Wet Scrubber Functional Block ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hot Dirty Gas
Inlet] --> B[Venturi Throat
High Velocity] C[Water Spray
Nozzles] --> B B --> D[Impaction and
Capture on Droplets] D --> E[Separator
Cyclonic Mist] E --> F[Clean Gas
Outlet] E --> G[Dirty Water
to Treatment] style B fill:#FFB6C1 style E fill:#87CEEB ``` ### 4.4 Amine Absorption-Regeneration Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sour Gas
Inlet] --> B[Absorber
Contact Tower] C[Lean Amine
from Regenerator] --> B B --> D[Rich Amine
H2S and CO2 loaded] B --> E[Sweet Gas
Outlet] D --> F[Lean Rich
Heat Exchanger] F --> G[Stripper
Reboiler at 120 C] G --> H[Acid Gas
to Claus or Vent] G --> I[Lean Amine
Recycle] I --> F style B fill:#FFE4B5 style G fill:#FFA07A style E fill:#90EE90 ``` ### 4.5 Dry Bed Desulfurization (Iron Oxide Box) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sour Gas
H2S bearing] --> B[First Iron
Oxide Box] B --> C[Second Iron
Oxide Box] C --> D[Purification
Check] D --> E[Sweet Gas
Outlet] B --> F[Spent Oxide
Disposal or Reactivation] style B fill:#D2B48C style C fill:#D2B48C style E fill:#90EE90 ``` ### 4.6 Process Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Choose Desulfurization Method] --> B{H2S
Concentration?} B -->|less than 100 ppm| C{Required Outlet?} C -->|less than 1 ppm| D[Iron Oxide Bed
or Activated Carbon] C -->|less than 50 ppm| E[Amines MDEA] B -->|100 to 5000 ppm| F{Regeneration
Wanted?} F -->|Yes| G[Amine or
Hot Carbonate] F -->|No| H[Zinc Oxide
Scavenger] B -->|greater than 5000 ppm| I[Claus Process
or Stretford] style D fill:#FFE4E1 style G fill:#E0FFFF style I fill:#F0E68C ``` > [!TIP] > **Mermaid Safety Check Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters (A, B, C...) and avoid reserved words. No bold, italics, or HTML inside the node labels. Diagram 4.4 correctly represents the closed recycle loop of an amine plant. --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List the common contaminants present in raw producer gas and explain why each must be removed before the gas is supplied to an IC engine.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The major contaminants in raw producer gas and their removal rationale are: 1. **Dust and fly ash** (2000–10000 mg/Nm³): Causes cylinder liner abrasion, piston ring wear, and valve seat erosion. Must be reduced to **<50 mg/Nm³**. 2. **Tar vapors** (0.5–5 g/Nm³): Condense in pipelines, form explosive mixtures, clog carburetor jets. Removed by water scrubbing or electrostatic precipitators. 3. **Hydrogen sulfide** (200–2000 mg/Nm³): Highly corrosive, forms sulfuric acid in engines, and is toxic above **100 ppm**. Removed by iron oxide beds or amine absorption. 4. **Moisture** (saturated): Causes corrosion and forms ice at valves. Drying with glycol or silica gel is mandatory. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often omit **CO and HCN toxicity** concerns. Always mention at least one **environmental/health** driver alongside the **equipment protection** driver for full marks. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Compare the working principle of a cyclone separator and a wet scrubber for dust removal. When would you prefer one over the other?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Cyclone Separator | Wet Scrubber | |---|---|---| | **Operating principle** | Centrifugal force on particles in a vortex | Impaction of particles onto liquid droplets | | **Particle size removed** | > 10 µm (efficiently); 5–10 µm (partially) | 1–5 µm (very fine) | | **Pressure drop** | 500–1500 Pa (low) | 2000–10000 Pa (high) | | **Water consumption** | Zero | High (1–3 L/m³ of gas) | | **Tar removal** | Poor | Excellent | **Selection rule:** Use a **cyclone** for coarse dust at high temperature with no water available. Use a **wet scrubber** when fine dust (<5 µm) or tar must be removed simultaneously. In practice, a **cyclone first, scrubber second** is the preferred train. --- ### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]* **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a **wet gas cleaning train** for removing dust and tar from producer gas. List the units in the proper sequence. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A producer gas stream of 5000 Nm³/h at 40 °C contains 800 mg/Nm³ of H₂S. It is to be cleaned in an **iron oxide box** to less than 20 mg/Nm³. The iron oxide has a sulfur capacity of 0.55 kg S per kg of oxide. The oxide bulk density is 800 kg/m³ and the gas residence time must be at least 2 minutes. Calculate: (i) The mass of H₂S to be removed per day. (ii) The minimum mass and volume of iron oxide required. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Wet Gas Cleaning Train — Construction and Working (7 marks):** A wet cleaning train for producer gas typically contains the following units in sequence: 1. **Primary dust collector (cyclone):** The hot raw gas (~80–100 °C) enters the cyclone tangentially; centrifugal force throws particles to the wall, and the cleaned gas spirals up through the central vortex finder. Removes **70–90% of dust > 10 µm**. 2. **Venturi scrubber:** The gas accelerates to **60–120 m/s** through a converging throat where fine water droplets are injected. The high relative velocity causes fine dust and tar mist to impact on the droplets. The droplets are then removed in a cyclonic separator downstream. 3. **Packed bed scrubber (final tar absorber):** The gas passes upward through a packed column (Pall rings or Raschig rings) counter-current to recirculating water or oil. Tar and ammonia are absorbed, and the gas exits at the top saturated and cooled to **30–45 °C**. 4. **Mist eliminator (demister pad):** A wire-mesh pad removes entrained droplets before the gas leaves the system. ``` Gas path: Cyclone → Venturi → Packed Scrubber → Mist Eliminator → Outlet ``` **Working principle:** Particulate and condensable contaminants are transferred from the gas phase to a liquid phase by **impaction, interception, and diffusion**. The dirty liquor is sent to a settling tank where tar separates (top) and dust settles (bottom), enabling water recycle. > [!NOTE] > **Mark Split (typical KTU):** Sequence listing — 2 marks; unit functions — 3 marks; working principle with mechanism — 2 marks. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - $Q_g = 5000\ \mathrm{Nm^3/h}$ - $C_{\text{in}} = 800\ \mathrm{mg/Nm^3}$ - $C_{\text{out}} = 20\ \mathrm{mg/Nm^3}$ - Sulfur capacity $X_S = 0.55\ \mathrm{kg\ S/kg\ oxide}$ - Bed density $\rho_b = 800\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$ - Residence time $t_{\text{res}} \ge 2\ \mathrm{min}$ **Step 1 — Mass of H₂S to be removed per day:** Concentration drop: $$ \Delta C \;=\; 800 - 20 \;=\; 780\ \mathrm{mg/Nm^3} $$ Hourly H₂S removed: $$ \dot{m}_{H_2S} \;=\; 5000 \times 780 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 10^{-3} \;=\; 3.9\ \mathrm{kg/h} $$ Daily H₂S removed: $$ m_{H_2S} \;=\; 3.9 \times 24 \;=\; 93.6\ \mathrm{kg\ S/day} $$ **Step 2 — Mass of iron oxide required (assuming one full cycle):** $$ m_{\text{oxide}} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{H_2S}}{X_S} \;=\; \dfrac{93.6}{0.55} \;\approx\; 170.2\ \mathrm{kg} $$ **Step 3 — Volume of iron oxide from bulk density:** $$ V_{\text{oxide}} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{\text{oxide}}}{\rho_b} \;=\; \dfrac{170.2}{800} \;\approx\; 0.213\ \mathrm{m^3} \;=\; 213\ \mathrm{L} $$ **Step 4 — Check residence time constraint:** Convert actual volumetric flow at working conditions to m³/s, then size bed for 2 min residence. Using ideal gas correction factor: $$ Q_{\text{actual}} \;=\; 5000 \times \dfrac{313.15}{273.15} \;\approx\; 5732\ \mathrm{m^3/h} \;=\; 1.592\ \mathrm{m^3/s} $$ For $t_{\text{res}} = 2\ \mathrm{min} = 120\ \mathrm{s}$, minimum bed volume from kinetics: $$ V_{\min} \;=\; Q_{\text{actual}} \times t_{\text{res}} \;=\; 1.592 \times 120 \;\approx\; 191\ \mathrm{m^3} $$ Since $V_{\text{oxide}}$ from capacity = **213 L** is far less than kinetic minimum of **191 m³**, **capacity, not kinetics, governs the sizing for trace H₂S**. However, in practice, beds are sized as multiple boxes in series; the calculation above is the **minimum oxide mass for one cycle**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (numerical questions):** Many students forget the **unit conversion** of mg to kg for H₂S, leading to a 10⁶ error. Always write `mg × 10⁻⁶ = kg` explicitly. Also, when STP/NTP mixing is suspected, **state which basis** you are using. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice **(a)** Describe the **Claus process** for the recovery of elemental sulfur from H₂S-bearing gases. With a process flow diagram, explain the role of the thermal and catalytic stages. What is the overall conversion efficiency? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the **dry iron oxide process**, the **wet amine absorption process**, and the **Stretford process** for H₂S removal under the following heads: (i) reagent, (ii) operating temperature, (iii) regenerability, (iv) sulfur product, (v) typical capacity, (vi) any two advantages and one limitation. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution Outline for B(a):** The **Claus process** is the industrial workhorse for converting H₂S to elemental sulfur, applied to gases with **>50% H₂S** (e.g., amine regenerator off-gas, sour natural gas). **Reactions:** $$ \mathrm{H_2S} + \tfrac{3}{2}\,\mathrm{O_2} \;\longrightarrow\; \mathrm{SO_2} + \mathrm{H_2O} \quad \text{(furnace, 1000–1300 °C)} $$ $$ 2\,\mathrm{H_2S} + \mathrm{SO_2} \;\longrightarrow\; 3\,\mathrm{S} + 2\,\mathrm{H_2O} \quad \text{(thermal + catalytic)} $$ **Stages:** 1. **Thermal stage (Reaction furnace):** One-third of H₂S is combusted to SO₂; the remaining H₂S reacts with this SO₂ homogeneously in the hot zone. About **60–70%** conversion occurs here. Elemental sulfur is condensed. 2. **Catalytic stages (2 or 3 converters):** The remaining gas passes over **activated alumina or bauxite** at **200–350 °C**, where the equilibrium is shifted toward sulfur at lower temperatures. Each stage converts an additional **20–30%**. 3. **Sulfur condensation:** Between stages, sulfur is condensed and removed to push equilibrium. **Overall conversion:** **95–98%** in a two-stage Claus; up to **99.5%** with a **Tail Gas Treatment Unit (TGTU)** such as **SCOT** (Shell Claus Offgas Treating). **Process flow diagram (textual):** ``` Sour Acid Gas → Combustion Furnace → Waste Heat Boiler → 1st Catalytic Converter → 1st Sulfur Condenser → Heater → 2nd Catalytic Converter → 2nd Condenser → Tail Gas ``` > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse the **Claus process** (oxidation to elemental S) with **Clausius–Clapeyron** (thermodynamic relation). KTU students often write the wrong equations in hurry. --- **Model Solution Outline for B(b):** | Head | Iron Oxide Process | Amine Absorption | Stretford Process | |---|---|---|---| | **(i) Reagent** | Hydrated ferric oxide ($\mathrm{Fe_2O_3 \cdot xH_2O}$) on wood chips | Aqueous MEA, DEA, or MDEA (15–30 wt%) | $\mathrm{NaVO_3}$ + ADA (anthraquinone disulfonate) in alkaline solution | | **(ii) Operating T** | 25–45 °C (ambient) | 40–60 °C absorber, 110–125 °C regenerator | 35–45 °C absorber, ambient regenerator | | **(iii) Regenerable?** | No (disposable; some thermal reactivation) | Yes (continuous) | Yes (air oxidation of $\mathrm{V^{4+}}$ back to $\mathrm{V^{5+}}$) | | **(iv) Sulfur Product** | Fixed in oxide as $\mathrm{Fe_2S_3}$/$\mathrm{FeS}$ | Stripped as $\mathrm{H_2S}$ (sent to Claus) | Elemental sulfur slurry (recoverable) | | **(v) Typical Capacity** | Small–medium, <1000 Nm³/h | Large, up to several 100,000 Nm³/h | Medium, 5000–50,000 Nm³/h | | **(vi) Advantages** | Simple, no moving parts; tolerant of tar | High removal efficiency (>99%); reusable | High capacity; produces sulfur; works at low H₂S | | **Limitation** | Non-regenerable, bed replacement | Energy-intensive (reboiler); tar poisons amine | Chemicals cost; effluent disposal (vanadium) | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** When asked for **comparisons**, use a **table format** with **parallel entries**. A narrative comparison often loses marks for missing one head. Also, students frequently confuse **"regenerable"** (reversible in situ) with **"recoverable"** (sulfur product extracted separately) — keep them distinct. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:** - **Cleaning train order is fixed:** Quench → Cyclone → Scrubber → Desulfurization → DeCO₂ → Drying → Polishing. Reversing damages equipment. - **Stokes' Law** governs small-particle settling; **centrifugal force** governs cyclone collection. Cyclones are best for >10 µm; scrubbers handle <5 µm. - **H₂S limits:** Town gas < **20 mg/Nm³**; IC engine gas < **100 mg/Nm³**; syngas for catalysts < **0.1 ppm**. - **Three regimes of desulfurization:** Dry (iron oxide, zinc oxide, activated carbon), Wet absorption (amines, hot K₂CO₃), Wet oxidation (Stretford, Lo-Cat). - **Amine selection rule of thumb:** **MEA** for high reactivity but high energy; **MDEA** for selective H₂S over CO₂; **piperazine-activated MDEA** for high-pressure, deep removal. - **Claus process** is the **only industrial route** for large-scale sulfur recovery from H₂S. Conversion: thermal 70% + catalytic 25–30% = **95–98%**. - **Iron oxide boxes** use the reaction: $\mathrm{Fe_2O_3 \cdot H_2O} + 3\,\mathrm{H_2S} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe_2S_3} + 4\,\mathrm{H_2O}$. Sulfur capacity ~**0.5–0.6 kg S/kg oxide**. - **Moisture removal methods:** Cooling/condensation, absorption (glycol — TEG), adsorption (silica gel, molecular sieves — 3A, 4A). - **PSA (Pressure Swing Adsorption)** with activated carbon or molecular sieves is the modern alternative to amine systems for biogas upgradation; it can simultaneously remove H₂S, CO₂, and H₂O in one step. - **Always report contaminant concentrations in mg/Nm³** or **ppm(v)**, not in raw gas volumes. - **Tar dew point** and **water dew point** are different — tar dew point determines minimum operating temperature of gas pipelines for biomass-derived gases. - **CO₂ removal** uses the same amine infrastructure as H₂S but requires **more energy** because CO₂ is more strongly bonded. **MDEA** gives **selective H₂S** pickup, leaving most CO₂ in the gas. - **Sequence of unit operations** is a 5-mark question in every KTU paper — memorize the **order of the cleaning train**. - For **numerical problems**, always state **(i) Given, (ii) Formula, (iii) Substitution, (iv) Result with units**. KTU examiners specifically look for the "**Formula**" line. ---

topics.title: Manufactured Fuels: Agro Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Manufactured Fuels: Agro Fuels # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Agro Fuels** (also termed **Biofuels** or **Agro-based Manufactured Fuels**) are defined as solid, liquid, or gaseous fuels derived wholly or substantially from **biomass feedstock** of agricultural origin — including energy crops, agricultural residues, animal wastes, food processing by-products, and organic municipal waste. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition Anchor:** Per the KTU 2024 Fuel Engineering syllabus, agro fuels are classified under **Manufactured/Secondary Fuels** because they are produced through chemical, biological, or thermochemical conversion of raw biomass into usable energy carriers. They are NOT naturally occurring in the form in which they are consumed. The formal classification of agro fuels, as per the **IEA (International Energy Agency)** and adopted by KTU: | Generation | Feedstock | End Products | |------------|-----------|--------------| | **1st Generation** | Sugar, starch, vegetable oils, animal fats | Bioethanol, Biodiesel, Straight Vegetable Oil (SVO) | | **2nd Generation** | Lignocellulosic biomass, agricultural residues | Cellulosic ethanol, Biomethanol, Fischer–Tropsch diesel | | **3rd Generation** | Algae, aquatic biomass | Algal biodiesel, Biogasoline, Biohydrogen | | **4th Generation** | Genetically engineered organisms | Engineered biofuels, Bio-photo fuels | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine your kitchen waste (banana peels, vegetable scraps) sitting in a sealed container for a few weeks. Bubbles form, a flammable gas escapes, and a liquid residue settles. That "waste" has been biologically "refined" into a **fuel**. This is precisely the principle of agro fuels — converting nature's seasonal, organic, and agricultural surplus into usable energy carriers. In simple terms: - **Biomass** is the "crude oil" of the agricultural world. - **Agro fuels** are its "refined products" — produced via fermentation, digestion, transesterification, or gasification. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** The term **"Agro Fuel"** in the KTU syllabus emphasizes fuels produced from **agricultural-derived feedstocks** — it is the broader, more inclusive term compared to the narrower "biofuel" which sometimes excludes municipal waste. ## 1.3 Why Agro Fuels Matter — The Driving Forces - **Renewability:** Photosynthesis regenerates biomass at a rate of ~ **170 billion tonnes/year** globally. - **Carbon Neutrality:** The CO₂ released on combustion was recently absorbed by the plant during growth → net atmospheric CO₂ ≈ **0**. - **Energy Security:** Reduces dependence on crude oil imports. - **Rural Development:** Provides additional income to farmers via energy crops (e.g., sugarcane, jatropha, soybean). - **Lower SOₓ Emissions:** Agro fuels contain negligible sulfur (**< 0.05 wt%** vs. **2–3 wt%** in coal/diesel). ## 1.4 Major Categories of Agro Fuels (Overview) 1. **Liquid Agro Fuels:** Bioethanol, Biodiesel, Methanol, Dimethyl ether (DME), Pyrolysis oil 2. **Gaseous Agro Fuels:** Biogas, Producer gas, Biohydrogen, Syngas 3. **Solid Agro Fuels:** Charcoal, Pellets, Briquettes, Torrefied biomass --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 First-Generation Liquid Agro Fuels ### 2.1.1 Bioethanol (C₂H₅OH) **Definition:** A colourless, volatile, **miscible-in-water** alcohol produced by **fermentation of sugars** (sucrose, glucose, fructose) using the enzyme **invertase** and yeast **Saccharomyces cerevisiae**. **Production Route from Sugarcane Molasses (KTU Focus):** 1. **Dilution:** Molasses diluted with water to ~ **10–15% sugar** concentration. 2. **Acid Treatment:** pH adjusted to **4.0–4.5** using H₂SO₄ to prevent contamination. 3. **Nutrient Addition:** Nitrogen source (urea/ammonium sulfate) added. 4. **Fermentation:** Yeast added; temperature **30–32 °C**; reaction time **48–72 hours**. 5. **Distillation:** Ethanol-water mixture separated at **78.4 °C** (ethanol boils at this temperature). 6. **Dehydration:** Azeotropic distillation with **benzene** or **molecular sieves** → anhydrous ethanol (**99.5%+**). **Fermentation Reaction (Gay-Lussac Equation):** $$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Yeast, 30°C}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 + \text{Energy}$$ - **Theoretical Yield:** **51.1%** (by mass of glucose). - **Industrial Yield:** **~ 90–95%** of theoretical. ### 2.1.2 Biodiesel (FAME — Fatty Acid Methyl Esters) **Definition:** Mono-alkyl esters of long-chain fatty acids derived from vegetable oils/animal fats via **transesterification** with methanol in the presence of a catalyst (NaOH or KOH). **Transesterification Reaction (KTU High-Yield):** $$Triglyceride + 3\,CH_3OH \xrightleftharpoons[\text{NaOH}]{} 3\,R-COOCH_3 + Glycerol$$ | Parameter | Typical Value | |-----------|---------------| | **Molar Ratio (Oil : Methanol)** | **1 : 6** (stoichiometric); **1 : 9–1 : 12** in practice | | **Catalyst Loading (NaOH)** | **0.5 – 1.0 % wt of oil** | | **Reaction Temperature** | **60 – 70 °C** | | **Reaction Time** | **1 – 4 hours** | | **Conversion Efficiency** | **> 96 %** | ### 2.1.3 Vegetable Oil (SVO — Straight Vegetable Oil) Used directly or in **blends (B20, B50, B100)**. The major problem is **high viscosity** (~ 10–17 times that of diesel) → causes **poor atomization** and **coking** in injectors. ## 2.2 Gaseous Agro Fuels ### 2.2.1 Biogas Produced by **anaerobic digestion** of organic matter by methanogenic bacteria in an **airtight digester** at **35–55 °C (mesophilic)** or **50–60 °C (thermophilic)**. **Overall Stoichiometry:** $$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2$$ **Typical Composition:** | Component | % by Volume | |-----------|-------------| | **CH₄ (Methane)** | **55 – 65 %** | | **CO₂** | **30 – 40 %** | | **H₂S** | **0.1 – 0.5 %** | | **N₂, H₂O, Traces** | Balance | **Calorific Value:** **18 – 25 MJ/Nm³** ### 2.2.2 Producer Gas Produced by **partial combustion/gasification** of solid biomass in limited air (0.3–0.5 of stoichiometric air). **Overall Reaction:** $$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad (\text{Exothermic, } \Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol})$$ $$C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2CO \quad (\text{Endothermic, Boudouard reaction})$$ $$C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2 \quad (\text{Endothermic, Water-gas reaction})$$ **Typical Composition:** | Component | % by Volume | |-----------|-------------| | **CO** | **20 – 30 %** | | **H₂** | **10 – 15 %** | | **CH₄** | **1 – 5 %** | | **CO₂** | **5 – 12 %** | | **N₂** | **45 – 55 %** | | **CV (MJ/Nm³)** | **4.5 – 7.0** | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Stoichiometric Air-Fuel ratio (Ethanol): $A_0 = \frac{11.6 \times H/C + 32 + 8(O/C - S/C)}{GCV}$ | H/C, O/C ratios | **9.0 kg air/kg** | | 2 | Transesterification stoichiometry | 1 mol oil : 3 mol methanol | Ratio **1 : 6** | | 3 | Calorific value of biogas: $CV = (0.654 \times \%CH_4)$ MJ/m³ | Empirical formula | **18.0 – 25.0 MJ/m³** | | 4 | Carbon credit (C-content): $C_{feed} = \frac{12}{44} \times CO_2$ | Mass conversion factor | **12/44 = 0.2727** | | 5 | Anaerobic digestion yield: $Y = \frac{V_{CH_4}}{m_{VS}}$ | m³ CH₄ per kg volatile solids | **0.20 – 0.40 m³/kg VS** | | 6 | Bioethanol distillation energy: $E = m \times (h_{vap} + C_p \Delta T)$ | h_vap = **846 kJ/kg** | Ethanol h_vap | | 7 | Producer gas CV: $CV_{mix} = \sum y_i \times CV_i$ | y_i = volume fraction | **4.5 – 7.0 MJ/Nm³** | | 8 | Biodiesel yield: $\eta = \frac{m_{FAME}}{m_{oil}} \times 100$ | Mass yield | **> 96 %** | | 9 | Gasification efficiency: $\eta_{gas} = \frac{CV_{gas} \times V_{gas}}{CV_{feed} \times m_{feed}}$ | Energy conversion | **70 – 80 %** | | 10 | Fermentation efficiency: $\eta_f = \frac{Y_{actual}}{0.5111} \times 100$ | Theoretical max 51.1% | **45 – 50 %** | > [!NOTE] > **Air-Fuel Ratio for Pure Ethanol Combustion (KTU Board-Expected Derivation):** > Combustion: C₂H₅OH + 3 O₂ → 2 CO₂ + 3 H₂O > Mass basis: 46 g C₂H₅OH + 3 × 32 g O₂ → products > **Theoretical A₀ = (3 × 32) / 46 = 9.39 kg air/kg fuel (since air is 23% O₂ by mass).** --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked-Out Derivation: Stoichiometric Combustion of Bioethanol **Problem:** Calculate the **stoichiometric air required** and **flue gas composition** for complete combustion of **1 kg of pure ethanol (C₂H₅OH)**. ### Step 1: Write the Combustion Equation The chemical formula of ethanol is **C₂H₅OH** (or **C₂H₆O**). Molecular weight: M = (2 × 12) + (6 × 1) + (1 × 16) = **46 g/mol** $$C_2H_5OH + 3\,O_2 \rightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O$$ ### Step 2: Identify Atomic Composition - Carbon atoms: **2** → require 2 mol CO₂ - Hydrogen atoms: **6** (5 in C₂H₅ + 1 in OH) → require 3 mol H₂O - Oxygen atoms: **1** in ethanol (contributes 0.5 mol O₂) - Net O₂ required = 3 - 0.5 = **2.5 mol O₂** (from air) ### Step 3: Calculate Oxygen Required per Mole of Ethanol - 1 mol C₂H₅OH = 46 g - O₂ required = 3 mol - Mass of O₂ = 3 × 32 = **96 g** - Net O₂ from air = 96 - 16 (from ethanol) = **80 g** ### Step 4: Convert to Air Required (Mass Basis) By mass, air contains **23 % O₂ and 77 % N₂**. $$A_0 = \frac{\text{Mass of O}_2}{0.23} = \frac{80}{0.23} = 347.83 \text{ g air per 46 g ethanol}$$ $$A_0 = \frac{347.83}{46} = 7.56 \text{ kg air/kg ethanol}$$ ### Step 5: Calculate Flue Gas Composition (per kg ethanol) Products per mole of ethanol: - CO₂ = 2 mol = 2 × 44 = **88 g** - H₂O = 3 mol = 3 × 18 = **54 g** - N₂ = 3 × (79/21) = 11.286 mol = 11.286 × 28 = **316 g** **Total flue gas = 88 + 54 + 316 = 458 g** | Component | Mass (g) | Mass % | Mole % | |-----------|----------|--------|--------| | CO₂ | 88 | 19.2 | 14.6 | | H₂O | 54 | 11.8 | 23.0 | | N₂ | 316 | 69.0 | 62.4 | | **Total** | **458** | **100.0** | **100.0** | ## 3.2 Worked-Out Derivation: Biodiesel Yield from Transesterification **Problem:** **1000 kg of soybean oil** is reacted with methanol. Calculate the theoretical yield of biodiesel and glycerol. ### Step 1: Molecular Weights - Soybean oil (triglyceride) M_oil ≈ **885 g/mol** (average for soybean) - Methanol M = **32 g/mol** - FAME (Biodiesel, e.g., methyl oleate) M_FAME ≈ **296 g/mol** - Glycerol M = **92 g/mol** ### Step 2: Stoichiometric Ratio $$1 \text{ mol oil} + 3 \text{ mol methanol} \rightarrow 3 \text{ mol FAME} + 1 \text{ mol glycerol}$$ ### Step 3: Moles of Oil $$n_{oil} = \frac{1000 \text{ kg}}{0.885 \text{ kg/mol}} = 1130 \text{ mol (approx)}$$ ### Step 4: Theoretical Biodiesel Yield $$m_{FAME} = 1130 \times 3 \times 0.296 = 1003.4 \text{ kg}$$ ### Step 5: Theoretical Glycerol Yield $$m_{glycerol} = 1130 \times 1 \times 0.092 = 104.0 \text{ kg}$$ ### Step 6: Practical (Industrial) Yield at 96% efficiency $$m_{FAME,practical} = 1003.4 \times 0.96 = 963.3 \text{ kg}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners award full marks **only** if you state the molecular weight of the specific oil used (it varies: rapeseed ~882, palm ~833, sunflower ~876). Using a generic value of 885 g/mol is acceptable. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Agro Fuels vs. Conventional Fuels | Property | Diesel | Petrol | Bioethanol | Biodiesel | Biogas | |----------|--------|--------|------------|-----------|--------| | **Chemical Formula** | C₁₂H₂₃ | C₈H₁₈ | C₂H₅OH | C₁₉H₃₆O₂ | CH₄+CO₂ | | **CV (MJ/kg)** | **45.0** | **44.0** | **29.7** | **37.0** | **22.0 (per m³)** | | **Density (kg/L)** | 0.84 | 0.74 | 0.79 | 0.88 | 0.0012 (gas) | | **Cetane Number** | **45–55** | 8–14 | 8 | **50–60** | N/A | | **Octane Number** | 20–30 | 92–98 | **108** | — | **120 (CNG-like)** | | **Flash Point (°C)** | 55–65 | -43 | **13** | **> 130** | — | | **Viscosity (cSt, 40°C)** | 2.0–4.0 | 0.4–0.8 | 1.5 | **4.0–6.0** | — | | **Sulfur (wt%)** | 0.05 | 0.04 | **<0.01** | **<0.01** | trace | | **Air-Fuel Ratio** | 14.5 | 14.7 | **9.0** | **12.5** | 17.2 (vol) | | **CO₂ (g/MJ)** | 74 | 71 | **70** | **78** | **0 (net)** | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Agro Fuel Classification Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A[BIOMASS FEEDSTOCK] --> B[Energy Crops] A --> C[Agricultural Residues] A --> D[Animal Wastes] A --> E[Municipal Organic Waste] A --> F[Algae and Aquatic Biomass] B --> G[Sugarcane, Corn, Soybean, Jatropha] C --> H[Rice Straw, Bagasse, Wheat Husk] D --> I[Cattle Dung, Poultry Litter] E --> J[Food Waste, Sewage Sludge] F --> K[Microalgae, Macroalgae] G --> L[CONVERSION ROUTES] H --> L I --> L J --> L K --> L L --> M[Fermentation] L --> N[Anaerobic Digestion] L --> O[Transesterification] L --> P[Gasification] L --> Q[Pyrolysis] M --> R[Bioethanol] M --> S[Biomethanol] N --> T[Biogas] O --> U[Biodiesel FAME] P --> V[Producer Gas and Syngas] Q --> W[Pyrolysis Oil and Charcoal] ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow: Biodiesel Production (Transesterification) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Vegetable Oil Feedstock] --> B[Pre-treatment
Degumming and Drying] B --> C[Reactor 60 to 70 C
with Methanol and NaOH] C --> D[Gravity Settler] D --> E[FAME Layer Top] D --> F[Glycerol Layer Bottom] E --> G[Water Wash] G --> H[Drying at 110 C] H --> I[BIODIESEL PRODUCT] F --> J[Glycerol Purification] J --> K[Pharma and Cosmetic Grade Glycerol] C --> L[Excess Methanol Recovery
by Distillation] L --> M[Recycle to Reactor] ``` ## 4.3 Process Flow: Biogas Production by Anaerobic Digestion ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Biomass Slurry 8 to 12 percent TS] --> B[Mixing and Heating 35 to 55 C] B --> C[Anaerobic Digester
Retention Time 20 to 40 days] C --> D[Hydrolysis] C --> E[Acidogenesis] C --> F[Acetogenesis] C --> G[Methanogenesis] G --> H[Biogas CH4 plus CO2] H --> I[Gas Holder] I --> J[Purification H2S Removal and CO2 Scrubbing] J --> K[Compression and Storage] K --> L[Engine or CHP Application] C --> M[Digestate Solid and Liquid] M --> N[Land Application as Fertilizer] ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Comparison of First Second and Third Generation Agro Fuels ```mermaid flowchart TD A[GENERATION COMPARISON] --> B[First Generation
Food Crops] A --> C[Second Generation
Lignocellulosic Waste] A --> D[Third Generation
Algal Biomass] B --> B1[Pros Uses Existing Tech] B --> B2[Cons Food vs Fuel Conflict] C --> C1[Pros Abundant Non Food] C --> C2[Cons Costly Pretreatment] D --> D1[Pros High Yield per Hectare] D --> D2[Cons High Capital and Water Needs] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The Mermaid block diagrams above map the **functional architecture** of the conversion processes — useful for KTU exam answers where you need to draw labelled process flow diagrams (even sketched ones). Examiner awards full marks for clear feedstock → process → product flow. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Q1. Define agro fuels. List any four examples of agro fuels with their feedstocks. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 3]** **Model Answer:** Agro fuels are manufactured fuels derived from agricultural biomass through chemical, biological, or thermochemical conversion processes. Four examples: 1. **Bioethanol** — from sugarcane molasses / corn starch (fermentation) 2. **Biodiesel (FAME)** — from soybean / jatropha oil (transesterification) 3. **Biogas** — from cattle dung / food waste (anaerobic digestion) 4. **Producer gas** — from wood chips / rice husk (gasification) > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Note:** Defining "agro fuel" alone (1 mark), listing any 4 examples with feedstock (2 marks). Naming "biomass" without specifying agricultural origin = 0.5 mark deduction. --- ### Q2. Write the transesterification reaction for biodiesel production. State the role of the catalyst. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]** **Model Answer:** **Reaction:** $$Triglyceride + 3\,CH_3OH \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH/KOH, 60-70°C}} 3\,R-COOCH_3 + Glycerol$$ **Role of Catalyst (NaOH/KOH):** - Speeds up the reaction by **lowering the activation energy**. - Reacts with methanol to form **sodium methoxide (CH₃O⁻Na⁺)**, the actual nucleophile attacking the carbonyl carbon of the triglyceride. - Enables the reaction to proceed in **1–4 hours** at moderate temperature. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Writing H₂SO₄ as catalyst — this is a **homogeneous acid catalyst** used only in the two-step acid-pretreatment process for high-FFA oils. For conventional transesterification, **alkali (NaOH/KOH)** is the standard KTU answer. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (ESE Module Choice — 14 Marks) ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the production of **bioethanol from sugarcane molasses** with a neat flow diagram. Discuss its properties as an alternative fuel. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Dilution (1 mark):** Molasses containing ~ 50% sugar is diluted with warm water to bring the sugar concentration to **10–15%** to avoid osmotic stress on yeast. **Step 2 — Acidification (1 mark):** The pH of the mash is adjusted to **4.0–4.5** using dilute H₂SO₄ to prevent bacterial contamination that thrives in neutral/alkaline pH. **Step 3 — Nutrient Addition (0.5 mark):** Nitrogen source (urea or ammonium sulfate at 0.1–0.5%) and sometimes phosphates are added to support yeast metabolism. **Step 4 — Fermentation (2 marks):** Yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) is added. Temperature **30–32 °C**, anaerobic conditions, reaction time **48–72 hours**. **Reaction:** C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂ **Step 5 — Distillation (1.5 marks):** The fermented wash (~ 8–10% ethanol) is fed to a beer column (preheater) and then to a **distillation column** at the boiling point of ethanol (**78.4 °C**). The overhead product is ~ **95% ethanol** (azeotrope with water). **Step 6 — Dehydration (1 mark):** Anhydrous ethanol (**99.5%+**) is obtained by **azeotropic distillation with benzene** or using **3 Å molecular sieves** (preferred modern method). **Properties as Alternative Fuel:** | Property | Value | Significance | |----------|-------|--------------| | Octane Number | **108** | Excellent anti-knock | | CV | **29.7 MJ/kg** | ~ 67% of petrol | | Oxygen content | **34.8%** | Promotes complete combustion | | Water miscibility | High | Hygroscopic — storage issues | | Vapour pressure | High | Cold-start issues in pure form | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bioethanol is used in blends:** **E10** (10% ethanol + 90% petrol, common in India/US) and **E85** (85% ethanol, for flex-fuel vehicles). --- **(b)** What is **transesterification**? With a balanced chemical equation, explain the production of **biodiesel from vegetable oils**. Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of biodiesel. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Definition (1 mark):** Transesterification is the chemical reaction in which the **alkyl group (R') of an ester is exchanged with the alkyl group of an alcohol**, in the presence of a catalyst, to form a new ester and a new alcohol. In biodiesel production, the triglyceride (ester of glycerol) reacts with methanol to form fatty acid methyl esters (FAME) and glycerol. **Balanced Equation (2 marks):** $$\underbrace{C_3H_5(OCOR)_3}_{\text{Triglyceride}} + \underbrace{3\,CH_3OH}_{\text{Methanol}} \xrightarrow[\text{60-70°C}]{\text{NaOH}} \underbrace{3\,R-COOCH_3}_{\text{Biodiesel (FAME)}} + \underbrace{C_3H_5(OH)_3}_{\text{Glycerol}$$ **Process Description (2 marks):** 1. Oil is pre-treated (degumming, drying). 2. Methanol and NaOH catalyst (0.5–1.0 wt% of oil) are mixed in a stirred reactor at **60–70 °C** for 1–4 hours. 3. Settling tank separates **FAME layer (top)** from **glycerol layer (bottom)**. 4. FAME is washed, dried, and stored. **Advantages (1.5 marks):** - **Renewable and biodegradable** (degrades in 28 days). - **Lower emissions** — particulates, CO, SOₓ reduced by 40–60%. - **High cetane number (50–60)** → smooth combustion. - **High flash point (>130 °C)** → safer storage/transport. - Can be used in **existing diesel engines** with little or no modification. **Limitations (0.5 mark):** - Higher NOₓ emissions (~10% higher than diesel). - Oxidative stability issues (storage life ~ 6 months). - Cold-flow properties poor (crystallizes at low temperatures). - Food-vs-fuel debate for first-generation feedstock. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write the equation in a **single line** without proper structural balance. Always show the **triglyceride as R(COOR')₃ or C₃H₅(OCOR)₃** and indicate **3 mol of FAME produced per mol of oil**. Missing stoichiometric coefficients = −1 mark. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **(a)** Describe the **production of biogas by anaerobic digestion** of agricultural waste. Discuss the composition, calorific value, and applications of biogas. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Anaerobic Digestion — Definition (0.5 mark):** Anaerobic digestion is the **biological breakdown of organic matter in the absence of oxygen** by a consortium of bacteria, producing biogas and digestate. **Four Microbial Stages (3 marks):** 1. **Hydrolysis:** Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, fats) are hydrolysed by extracellular enzymes into monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids). - Example: Cellulose → Glucose 2. **Acidogenesis:** Monomers are converted into volatile fatty acids (VFAs), H₂, CO₂, alcohols by fermentative bacteria. 3. **Acetogenesis:** VFAs and alcohols are oxidised into **acetic acid**, H₂, and CO₂ by acetogenic bacteria. 4. **Methanogenesis:** Methanogenic archaea (*Methanobacterium, Methanococcus*) convert acetic acid and H₂/CO₂ into **CH₄**. **Overall Reaction (1 mark):** $$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2 \quad (35-55°C, pH \, 6.8-7.4)$$ **Process Conditions (1 mark):** - **pH:** 6.8–7.4 (near neutral) - **Temperature:** Mesophilic 35–40 °C OR Thermophilic 50–55 °C - **Retention Time:** 20–40 days - **C/N Ratio:** 20:1 – 30:1 optimal **Composition & Calorific Value (1 mark):** | Component | % vol | |-----------|-------| | CH₄ | 55–65 | | CO₂ | 30–40 | | H₂S | 0.1–0.5 | | N₂, H₂O, NH₃ | trace | **Calorific Value:** **18 – 25 MJ/Nm³** **Applications (0.5 mark):** - Cooking (domestic — India, China) - Electricity generation in CHP (combined heat and power) engines - Vehicle fuel (upgraded to biomethane) - Feed for fuel cells --- **(b)** Explain the **gasification process** for the production of **producer gas** from solid biomass. Discuss the composition, calorific value, and any two industrial applications. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Gasification — Definition (1 mark):** Gasification is the **thermochemical conversion of solid carbonaceous fuel into a combustible gas mixture (producer gas) by partial oxidation at elevated temperatures (800–1200 °C)** using less than stoichiometric air. **Process Steps / Zones in a Downdraft Gasifier (3 marks):** 1. **Drying Zone (100–150 °C):** Moisture in biomass is vaporised. 2. **Pyrolysis Zone (150–600 °C):** Biomass decomposes into char, tar, and volatiles. 3. **Combustion Zone (800–1200 °C):** Char + Volatiles + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O (exothermic). Provides heat for adjacent zones. 4. **Reduction Zone (600–800 °C):** CO₂ + C → 2 CO (Boudouard), H₂O + C → CO + H₂ (Water-gas reaction). **Key Reactions (1 mark):** $$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad (\Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol})$$ $$C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2\,CO \quad (\text{Boudouard, endothermic})$$ $$C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2 \quad (\text{Water-gas, endothermic})$$ **Composition & Calorific Value (1 mark):** | Component | % vol | |-----------|-------| | CO | 20–30 | | H₂ | 10–15 | | CH₄ | 1–5 | | CO₂ | 5–12 | | N₂ | 45–55 | | **CV (MJ/Nm³)** | **4.5 – 7.0** | **Industrial Applications (1 mark):** 1. **Direct firing in furnaces** for steel, ceramics, brick kilns (replacing furnace oil). 2. **Fuel for internal combustion engines** (as dual-fuel with diesel — popular in rice mills and sugar mills of Kerala and Tamil Nadu). 3. Electricity generation in **biomass-based power plants** of capacity 1–10 MW. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write "**producer gas is used in petrol engines**" — this is **incorrect**. Producer gas has very low CV and high inert (N₂) content, so it is used either **directly in industrial furnaces** OR in **diesel engines in dual-fuel mode** (gas replaces ~ 70–80% diesel). Direct use in petrol engines is not recommended. **Deduction: −1 mark.** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 3 - Agro Fuels)** - [x] **Agro fuels** are manufactured fuels from agricultural biomass (1st, 2nd, 3rd generation). - [x] **Bioethanol** is produced by **fermentation of sugars** with yeast at 30 °C → distilled at 78.4 °C → dehydrated by molecular sieves. - [x] **Theoretical fermentation yield of ethanol** from glucose = **51.1%** (Gay-Lussac equation). - [x] **Biodiesel (FAME)** is produced by **transesterification** using **NaOH catalyst at 60–70 °C**, methanol to oil ratio = **6:1 (molar)**. - [x] **Biodiesel yield** of a well-run process = **> 96%** of theoretical. - [x] **Biogas** is produced by **anaerobic digestion** in 4 microbial stages: Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis. - [x] **Biogas composition:** CH₄ (55–65%) + CO₂ (30–40%); CV = **18–25 MJ/Nm³**. - [x] **Producer gas** is produced by **gasification** of biomass in limited air; CV = **4.5–7.0 MJ/Nm³** (low because of ~ 50% N₂). - [x] **Stoichiometric air for ethanol combustion = 9.0 kg air/kg ethanol** (board-favourite numerical). - [x] **Bioethanol octane number = 108** (higher than petrol 92–98); **Biodiesel cetane number = 50–60** (similar to diesel). - [x] **Biodiesel CV = 37 MJ/kg** (lower than diesel 45 MJ/kg, but offset by higher density and complete combustion). - [x] **Agro fuels are carbon-neutral** (CO₂ absorbed during growth = CO₂ released during combustion). - [x] **KTU mark-distribution:** 1st Generation fuels + Transesterification + Biogas carry **~ 60–70% weightage** in this module. - [x] **Always draw a labelled flow diagram** in Part B answers — earns **1–1.5 bonus marks** even if description is brief.

topics.title: Bio-Fuels: Types of Bio-fuels, Production Processes and Technologies, Biofuel Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bio-Fuels: Types of Bio-fuels, Production Processes and Technologies, Biofuel Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biofuel (KTU Syllabus Definition):** A **biofuel** is a gaseous, liquid, or solid fuel produced from **biomass** — renewable organic matter of recent biological origin (plants, algae, animal by-products, or organic waste). Biofuels are considered **carbon-neutral** because the CO₂ released during combustion is reabsorbed by the next generation of biomass during photosynthesis, closing the short-term carbon cycle.

A more rigorous KTU 2024-aligned definition states:

> A biofuel is a **hydrocarbon-equivalent fuel** derived from biological feedstocks whose **chemical energy** was originally captured through **photosynthesis**, and which can be used in internal combustion engines, turbines, boilers, or fuel cells with or without modification of existing fuel infrastructure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Solar Battery" Intuition

Imagine a plant leaf as a tiny, ultra-thin **solar battery**. Sunlight strikes the chlorophyll, the leaf performs photosynthesis, and the resulting sugars (C₆H₁₂O₆) become the "charged battery." A biofuel is simply us **harvesting that battery** later and discharging its stored chemical energy in an engine.

- 🌱 **Charge the battery** → Sunlight + CO₂ + H₂O → Sugars (Photosynthesis)
- 🔋 **Store the battery** → Sugars → Starch, Oil, Lignocellulose (Biomass formation)
- ⚡ **Discharge the battery** → Biomass → Biofuel → Combustion releases energy + CO₂
- ♻️ **The CO₂ is re-captured** by the next crop → Cycle repeats

This is why biofuels are called **"delayed solar energy"** — they are solar power stored in chemical form, ready on demand.

## 1.3 Why Biofuels Matter — Key Drivers

| Driver | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Energy Security** | Reduces dependence on imported crude oil |
| **Climate Change** | Closed-loop CO₂ cycle limits net GHG emissions |
| **Rural Economy** | Creates agricultural jobs and value-added crops |
| **Engine Compatibility** | Many biofuels (ethanol, biodiesel) drop-in to existing engines |
| **Waste Valorization** | Used cooking oil, agri-residue, MSW become fuel feedstocks |

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (Biomass & Biofuels)

> [!NOTE]
> The following constants and benchmark values are essential for KTU numerical problems:
> - **Heating Value of Dry Biomass:** ~ **15–20 MJ/kg**
> - **Energy Density of Biodiesel:** ~ **37–40 MJ/kg** (vs. Diesel 43 MJ/kg, Gasoline 46 MJ/kg)
> - **Energy Density of Bioethanol:** ~ **26–29 MJ/kg** (only ~**66 %** of gasoline)
> - **Cetane Number of Biodiesel:** **46–60** (vs. Diesel 50–55)
> - **Octane Number of Bioethanol:** **108–110 RON** (excellent anti-knock)
> - **Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose:** **0.511 g ethanol / g glucose**
> - **Photosynthetic Efficiency (C3 plants):** **~1–2 %**; (C4 plants): **~3–4 %**
> - **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Ethanol):** **9.0 : 1**
> - **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Biodiesel):** **~12.5 : 1**

## 1.5 Biomass Composition (Foundation Concept)

Lignocellulosic biomass is composed of three major structural polymers and one extractive group. Every conversion technology is tuned to one of these fractions.

| Component | Formula | Mass % in Wood | Target Biofuel |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cellulose** | (C₆H₁₀O₅)ₙ | 40–50 % | Bioethanol (via hydrolysis + fermentation) |
| **Hemicellulose** | (C₅H₈O₄)ₘ | 20–30 % | Bioethanol (C5 sugars) |
| **Lignin** | C₉H₁₀O₃ etc. | 20–30 % | Solid biofuel, pyrolysis oil, phenolics |
| **Extractives** | Resins, oils | 1–5 % | Terpene-based biofuels |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generation-Wise Classification of Biofuels (KTU High-Priority)

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes a **four-generation classification** that reflects feedstock maturity and processing complexity.

### 2.1.1 First-Generation Biofuels
- **Feedstock:** Edible biomass — sugarcane, corn, soybean, palm, rapeseed, sunflower.
- **Processes:** Sugar fermentation (ethanol), oil pressing + transesterification (biodiesel), direct combustion.
- **Limitation:** **"Food vs. Fuel"** debate, low net energy balance.

### 2.1.2 Second-Generation Biofuels
- **Feedstock:** Non-edible lignocellulosic biomass, agricultural residues (straw, bagasse), Jatropha, waste cooking oil.
- **Processes:** Thermochemical (pyrolysis, gasification, HTL) and biochemical (cellulosic fermentation).
- **Advantage:** No food competition; uses waste streams.

### 2.1.3 Third-Generation Biofuels
- **Feedstock:** Algae (microalgae and macroalgae).
- **Processes:** Cultivation in open ponds or photobioreactors → oil extraction → transesterification or hydrothermal liquefaction.
- **Advantage:** **30–100× higher oil yield per hectare** than soybeans; uses non-arable land.

### 2.1.4 Fourth-Generation Biofuels
- **Feedstock:** Genetically engineered organisms (algae, cyanobacteria) with designed metabolic pathways.
- **Processes:** Designer biofuels, electrofuels, solar-to-fuel direct conversion, carbon capture + biofuel synthesis.
- **Status:** Lab-to-pilot stage; expected to deliver **net-negative CO₂ emissions**.

## 2.2 Physical-State Classification

| Physical State | Examples | Engine Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Solid** | Firewood, charcoal, pellets, torrefied biomass | Boilers, gasifiers, stoves |
| **Liquid** | Bioethanol, biodiesel, bio-butanol, pyrolysis oil | CI / SI engines, turbines |
| **Gaseous** | Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂), syngas (CO + H₂), biohydrogen | Gas engines, boilers, FC |

## 2.3 Production Process Pillars

### Pillar A — Biochemical Conversion
- **Fermentation** (anaerobic): Biomass → CH₄ + CO₂ + digestate
- **Fermentation** (alcoholic): C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂
- **Anaerobic Digestion (AD):** Multi-stage microbial breakdown in oxygen-free digesters
- **Enzymatic Hydrolysis:** Cellulose → Glucose (using cellulase enzymes)

### Pillar B — Thermochemical Conversion
- **Combustion** (800–1200 °C): Direct heat for steam/power
- **Pyrolysis** (300–700 °C, no O₂): Biomass → Biochar + Bio-oil + Non-condensable gases
- **Gasification** (700–1200 °C, limited O₂): Biomass → Syngas (CO + H₂ + CH₄ + CO₂ + N₂)
- **Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL)** (250–400 °C, high pressure, water): Wet biomass → Bio-crude
- **Torrefaction** (200–300 °C, no O₂): Biomass → Coal-like solid fuel

### Pillar C — Chemical Conversion
- **Transesterification:** Triglyceride + 3 Methanol → 3 FAME + Glycerol
- **Hydrotreating (HVO/HEFA):** Triglyceride + H₂ → Renewable Diesel (paraffins)
- **Fischer–Tropsch (FT):** Syngas → Liquid hydrocarbons (BtL — Biomass-to-Liquid)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table contains every equation you must memorize for Module 3 numerical and theoretical questions.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Application | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_{\text{biodiesel}} = M_{\text{oil}} + 3(M_{\text{MeOH}} - M_{\text{glycerol}})$ | Molecular weight shift after transesterification | g/mol |
| 2 | $\%\text{Yield}_{\text{biodiesel}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{FAME}}}{m_{\text{oil}}} \times 100$ | Mass-based biodiesel yield | % |
| 3 | $\text{Stoich. AF ratio} = \dfrac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}}\bigg\vert_{\text{theoretical}}$ | Theoretical air for complete combustion | kg/kg |
| 4 | $Y_{\text{EtOH}} = 0.511 \times m_{\text{glucose}} \times \eta_{\text{ferm}}$ | Ethanol from glucose (Gay-Lussac limit) | g |
| 5 | $\text{Energy Yield} = \dfrac{V_{\text{EtOH}} \cdot \rho_{\text{EtOH}} \cdot LHV_{\text{EtOH}}}{m_{\text{biomass}}}$ | Energy per unit biomass input | MJ/kg |
| 6 | $LHV_{\text{blend}} = X_f \cdot LHV_f + (1-X_f) \cdot LHV_{\text{base}}$ | Linear blending rule (volume fraction X) | MJ/kg |
| 7 | $HHV = LHV + h_{fg}\cdot\left(\dfrac{9H}{100}\right)$ | Dulong-type correction HHV → LHV (h_fg = 2.442 MJ/kg water) | MJ/kg |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{comb}} = \dfrac{LHV_{\text{fuel}}}{m_{\text{CO₂}}} \cdot \dfrac{44}{12}$ | Combustion efficiency (CO₂-based) | — |
| 9 | $CH_4\% \text{ in biogas} = 50\text{–}70\,\%$; $CO_2 = 30\text{–}50\,\%$ | Typical AD gas composition | mol % |
| 10 | $\rho_{\text{biodiesel}} \approx 0.86\text{–}0.90$ g/cm³ at 15 °C | Density (used in volume-to-mass conversion) | g/cm³ |
| 11 | $\text{Viscosity}_40^{\text{biodiesel}} = 4\text{–}6$ mm²/s | ASTM D6751 kinematic viscosity | mm²/s |
| 12 | $P_{\text{syngas}} = \dfrac{n_{CO} + n_{H_2}}{n_{\text{total gas}}}$ | Syngas quality (must be ≥ 0.7 for FT) | — |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biodiesel production plants** (e.g., 60 million liters/yr capacity) use the transesterification stoichiometry from §2.4 (Eq. 1) to size methanol recovery columns.
- **Ethanol distilleries** use the **Gay-Lussac theoretical yield (0.511)** as a benchmark; actual yields are **85–95 %** of theoretical due to microbial losses.
- **Biogas plants** use the methane fraction to size CHP (combined heat and power) engines.
- **Aviation biofuels** (HEFA, FT-SPK) require **energy density > 42 MJ/kg** and a **freeze point < -40 °C** — they are blended at **30–50 %** with Jet A-1.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Biodiesel Molecular Weight from Triglyceride

A triglyceride is a tri-ester of glycerol with three fatty acid chains (R¹, R², R³). The general formula is:

$$TG = C_{3}H_{5}(OOCR^1)(OOCR^2)(OOCR^3)$$

In transesterification, the triglyceride reacts with three molecules of methanol to release three molecules of fatty acid methyl ester (FAME — the biodiesel) and one molecule of glycerol:

$$C_{3}H_{5}(OOCR)_3 + 3\,CH_3OH \;\xrightarrow{\text{cat.}}\; 3\,RCOOCH_3 + C_{3}H_5(OH)_3$$

### Step-by-Step Mass Balance

Let $M_{TG}$ = molecular weight of triglyceride, $M_{MeOH}$ = 32.04 g/mol, $M_{gly}$ = 92.09 g/mol.

Total mass of reactants:

$$m_{r} = M_{TG} + 3 \cdot M_{MeOH}$$

Total mass of products:

$$m_{p} = 3 \cdot M_{FAME} + M_{gly}$$

By conservation of mass $m_r = m_p$:

$$3 \cdot M_{FAME} = M_{TG} + 3 \cdot M_{MeOH} - M_{gly}$$

Solving for $M_{FAME}$:

$$M_{FAME} = \frac{M_{TG} + 3 \cdot M_{MeOH} - M_{gly}}{3}$$

Which gives the well-known **KTU formula**:

$$M_{FAME} = M_{TG} + (M_{MeOH} - M_{gly}/3)$$

For a typical soybean oil (triglyceride average $M_{TG} \approx 884$ g/mol):

$$M_{FAME} = \frac{884 + 3(32.04) - 92.09}{3} = \frac{884 + 96.12 - 92.09}{3} = \frac{888.03}{3} = 296.01 \;\text{g/mol}$$

This is the **molecular weight of soybean biodiesel (FAME)**, which KTU examiners use as a reference value.

### Numerical Worked Example

> **Q:** 1000 kg of soybean oil ($M_{TG}=884$) is transesterified. Calculate (a) theoretical mass of methanol required, (b) theoretical mass of FAME produced, (c) theoretical mass of glycerol byproduct.

**(a) Moles of triglyceride:**

$$n_{TG} = \frac{1000 \times 1000}{884} = 1131.2 \;\text{mol}$$

**Theoretical methanol required (3 : 1 mole ratio):**

$$n_{MeOH} = 3 \times 1131.2 = 3393.6 \;\text{mol}$$
$$m_{MeOH} = 3393.6 \times 32.04 = 108{,}732 \;\text{g} \approx 108.7 \;\text{kg}$$

In practice, a **6 : 1 (or higher) excess** is used to drive equilibrium forward:

$$m_{MeOH,\text{actual}} \approx 6 \times \frac{32.04}{884} \times 1000 = 217.5 \;\text{kg}$$

**(b) Theoretical FAME mass:**

$$m_{FAME} = n_{FAME} \times M_{FAME} = 3 \times 1131.2 \times 296.01 = 1{,}004{,}400 \;\text{g} \approx 1004.4 \;\text{kg}$$

**(c) Theoretical glycerol mass:**

$$m_{gly} = n_{TG} \times M_{gly} = 1131.2 \times 92.09 = 104{,}170 \;\text{g} \approx 104.2 \;\text{kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mass conservation check:** $1000 + 108.7 = 1108.7$ kg (reactants). $1004.4 + 104.2 = 1108.6$ kg (products). The 0.1 kg discrepancy is rounding error — a beautiful confirmation of Lavoisier's law of mass conservation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Ethanol

Combustion reaction for ethanol (C₂H₅OH):

$$C_2H_5OH + a\,(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \rightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O + 3.76a\,N_2$$

**Balancing oxygen:**

$$1 + 2a = 4 + 3 \Rightarrow 2a = 6 \Rightarrow a = 3$$

So the **stoichiometric combustion of ethanol requires 3 mol O₂ per mol fuel**:

$$C_2H_5OH + 3\,(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \rightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O + 11.28\,N_2$$

**Mass-based AF ratio:**

$$AF = \frac{3 \times (32 + 3.76 \times 28)}{46} = \frac{3 \times (32 + 105.28)}{46} = \frac{3 \times 137.28}{46} = \frac{411.84}{46} = 8.95 \approx 9.0$$

$$\boxed{AF_{\text{ethanol, stoich}} = 9.0 : 1}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose (Gay-Lussac, 1815)

The fermentation stoichiometry of glucose is:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$$

**Mass of ethanol per gram of glucose:**

$$Y_{EtOH} = \frac{2 \times 46.07}{180.16} = \frac{92.14}{180.16} = 0.5114 \;\text{g ethanol / g glucose}$$

$$\boxed{Y_{EtOH,\text{theor}} = 0.511 \;\text{g/g} = 51.1 \,\% \text{ by mass}}$$

**With typical fermentation efficiency η = 90 %:**

$$Y_{EtOH,\text{actual}} = 0.511 \times 0.90 = 0.460 \;\text{g/g}$$

This is the standard bench-mark value used in ethanol plant design.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Biofuel Process Selection Guide

| Feedstock | Moisture | Best Route | Primary Product | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sugarcane juice | High (~70 %) | Fermentation | Bioethanol | Simple C6 sugars, no pretreatment |
| Corn starch | Med (~15 %) | Hydrolysis + Fermentation | Bioethanol | Starch easily hydrolyzed |
| Switchgrass | Low (~10 %) | Pretreatment + Fermentation | Cellulosic ethanol | Lignocellulose, recalcitrant |
| Soybean oil | 0 % | Transesterification | Biodiesel (FAME) | Triglyceride-rich |
| Jatropha oil | 0 % | Transesterification | Biodiesel (FAME) | Non-edible, grows on arid land |
| Used cooking oil | 0 % | Pretreatment + Transesterification | Biodiesel (FAME) | FFA must be esterified first |
| Algae lipids | Variable | HTL or Transesterification | Bio-crude or FAME | Wet feedstock suits HTL |
| Manure / food waste | Very high | Anaerobic digestion | Biogas (CH₄) | High water content suits AD |
| Wood chips | Low (~10 %) | Gasification + FT | BtL diesel/jet | High lignin, thermochemical |
| Agri-residue | Low | Pyrolysis | Bio-oil + char | Fast pyrolysis gives max oil |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block 1 — Biofuel Generation Classification

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0["BIOFUELS"]
    A1["1st Generation Edible Crops"]
    A2["2nd Generation Non Edible Lignocellulose"]
    A3["3rd Generation Algal Feedstocks"]

    B1["Sugarcane Corn Soybean"]
    B2["Jatropha Wood Straw MSW"]

    C1["Fermentation Transesterification"]
    C2["Cellulosic Fermentation Pyrolysis Gasification"]
    C3["HTL Transesterification"]
    C4["Solar to Fuel CO2 Capture"]

    D1["Bioethanol Biodiesel"]
    D2["Cellulosic Ethanol Bio oil Syngas"]
    D3["Algal Biodiesel Bio crude"]

    A0 --> A1

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block 2 — Biodiesel Production Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Feedstock Oil Soybean Jatropha UCO"]
    P1["Pretreatment Degumming Neutralization for FFA"]
    P2["Transesterification Reactor 60 C Acid Base Catalyst"]

    P4["Methanol Recovery Vacuum Distillation"]
    P5["Water Wash Polishing Step"]
    P6["Drying Vacuum Drier"]
    P7["Quality Testing ASTM EN 14214"]
    P8["Biodiesel FAME Storage Tank"]

    P0 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P3 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block 3 — Biomass Thermochemical Conversion Pathways

```mermaid
graph LR
    F0["Biomass Feedstock"]

    F1["Combustion 800 1200 C Air"]
    F2["Pyrolysis 300 700 C No Oxygen"]
    F3["Gasification 700 1200 C Limited Oxygen"]
    F4["HTL 250 400 C High P Water"]
    F5["Torrefaction 200 300 C No Oxygen"]

    O1["Heat Steam Power"]
    O2["Bio oil Biochar Pyrogas"]
    O3["Syngas CO H2 CH4"]
    O4["Bio crude Aqueous Phase"]
    O5["Torrefied Pellets Solid Fuel"]

    F0 --> F1 --> O1
    F0 --> F2 --> O2
    F0 --> F3 --> O3
    F0 --> F4 --> O4
    F0 --> F5 --> O5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation tip for KTU exams:** When asked to "draw the flowchart," the examiner expects (a) **labeled unit operations**, (b) **temperatures and pressures** where applicable, and (c) **phase/state changes** explicitly shown. The diagrams above are the **gold standard** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define biodiesel. Write the stoichiometric equation of transesterification of triglyceride with methanol.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Biodiesel is a mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids (FAME — Fatty Acid Methyl Ester) derived from a renewable lipid feedstock such as vegetable oil, animal fat, or used cooking oil, and used as a fuel in compression ignition engines.

> **Stoichiometric equation (2 marks):**
> $$C_3H_5(COOR)_3 + 3\,CH_3OH \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH / KOH}} 3\,RCOOCH_3 + C_3H_5(OH)_3$$
> where $R$ = long-chain alkyl group (C₁₂–C₂₂). The reaction is **reversible** and requires an **alkaline or acidic catalyst** plus mild heating (50–65 °C). Three moles of FAME and one mole of glycerol are produced per mole of triglyceride.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Distinguish between first-generation and second-generation biofuels with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | 1st Generation | 2nd Generation |
|---|---|---|
| Feedstock type | Edible sugar/starch/oil crops | Non-edible lignocellulose, waste |
| Competition with food | **Yes** (food vs fuel debate) | **No** |
| Examples | Sugarcane ethanol, soybean biodiesel | Jatropha biodiesel, switchgrass ethanol |
| Technology maturity | Commercial | Pilot / early commercial |
| Net energy balance | Low to moderate | Higher (uses waste streams) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Module 3 questions follow the pattern: **Part (a) — 7 marks, Part (b) — 7 marks.** Internal choice means the student can pick **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat flow diagram, explain the **transesterification process** for biodiesel production from vegetable oil. Discuss the role of the catalyst and the effect of **free fatty acid (FFA) content** on the process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A batch reactor is charged with **1500 kg of Jatropha oil** ($M_{TG} = 890$ g/mol, density 0.92 g/cm³) and reacted with methanol in the **stoichiometric 3:1 molar ratio** using **NaOH catalyst**. Calculate: **(i)** the mass of methanol required, **(ii)** the theoretical mass of FAME produced (assume $M_{FAME} = 297$ g/mol), and **(iii)** the theoretical mass of glycerol byproduct ($M_{gly} = 92$ g/mol). **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — Process Explanation (7 marks)

**Biodiesel Process Flow (5 marks — diagram + narration):**

1. **Feedstock Preparation:** Crude vegetable oil is filtered and **degummed** (phosphatides removed with phosphoric acid) and **neutralized** (FFA reduced below 2 % using NaOH).
2. **Catalyst Mixing:** Methanol is mixed with **NaOH (or KOH) at 1 wt % of oil** to form sodium methoxide (the active catalyst).
3. **Transesterification Reactor:** The oil + methoxide mixture is heated to **60–65 °C** in a stirred reactor for **1–2 hours**. The reaction is **endothermic** initially, then proceeds with controlled heat removal.
4. **Phase Separation:** The mixture is allowed to settle in a **gravity separator**. Two layers form: **top layer = FAME (biodiesel)**, **bottom layer = crude glycerol** (contains catalyst, soap, residual methanol).
5. **Methanol Recovery:** Both layers are passed through a **vacuum distillation column** to recover unreacted methanol, which is recycled.
6. **Washing & Drying:** The FAME is washed with warm water to remove soap/catalyst traces, then **vacuum-dried** to < 500 ppm water.
7. **Quality Testing:** Flash point, viscosity, acid value, FAME purity (per **EN 14214 / ASTM D6751**).

**Role of Catalyst (1 mark):** The alkaline catalyst (NaOH/KOH) speeds up the reaction **~4000×** and allows operation at **moderate temperatures**. It deprotonates methanol to form methoxide, the actual nucleophile.

**Effect of FFA (1 mark):**
- FFAs react with NaOH to form **soap** (saponification), consuming catalyst and reducing yield.
- If FFA > **2 wt %**, a **two-step acid-base catalysis** is used: first H₂SO₄ esterifies FFA → FAME, then NaOH completes transesterification.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $m_{oil} = 1500$ kg, $M_{TG} = 890$ g/mol, $M_{FAME} = 297$ g/mol, $M_{gly} = 92$ g/mol, $M_{MeOH} = 32$ g/mol.

**Step 1 — Moles of triglyceride:**

$$n_{TG} = \frac{1500 \times 1000}{890} = 1685.4 \;\text{mol}$$

**(i) Mass of methanol (3:1 stoichiometric):**

$$n_{MeOH} = 3 \times 1685.4 = 5056.2 \;\text{mol}$$

$$m_{MeOH} = 5056.2 \times 32 = 161{,}798 \;\text{g} \approx \mathbf{161.8 \;\text{kg}} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Theoretical mass of FAME:**

$$n_{FAME} = 3 \times 1685.4 = 5056.2 \;\text{mol}$$

$$m_{FAME} = 5056.2 \times 297 = 1{,}501{,}691 \;\text{g} \approx \mathbf{1501.7 \;\text{kg}} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

**(iii) Theoretical mass of glycerol:**

$$m_{gly} = 1685.4 \times 92 = 155{,}057 \;\text{g} \approx \mathbf{155.1 \;\text{kg}} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Mass balance check:**
- Reactants = $1500 + 161.8 = 1661.8$ kg
- Products = $1501.7 + 155.1 = 1656.8$ kg (≈ 5 kg loss = rounding) ✔

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **biochemical pathway** for bioethanol production from sugarcane. Compare the energy densities of ethanol, biodiesel, and gasoline. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **molasses-based ethanol plant** processes **10 tonnes/day** of molasses containing **50 % fermentable sugar** (by mass). If the fermentation efficiency is **90 %** of theoretical and the distillation recovery is **95 %**, calculate the **daily ethanol production in litres** (ethanol density = 0.789 g/cm³). **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — Bioethanol from Sugarcane + Energy Density Comparison (7 marks)

**Process flow (5 marks):**
1. **Sugarcane crushing** → juice + bagasse
2. **Juice clarification** → syrup (concentrated)
3. **Dilution & sterilization** at 100 °C
4. **Yeast fermentation** (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) at 30 °C for 48–72 h: C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂
5. **Distillation** to ~95 % ethanol (azeotrope)
6. **Molecular sieve dehydration** → anhydrous ethanol (99.8 %)
7. **Denaturing** (for fuel use) and blending with gasoline (E5, E10, E20, E85)

**Energy density comparison (2 marks):**

| Fuel | LHV (MJ/kg) | Energy per litre (MJ/L) |
|---|---|---|
| Gasoline | 44 | ~32.4 |
| **Biodiesel** | **37.5** | ~33.2 |
| **Ethanol** | **26.8** | ~21.2 |
| Methanol | 20.0 | ~15.8 |

**Key insight:** Ethanol has only **~66 %** of gasoline's volumetric energy density — this is why FFVs (flex-fuel vehicles) need **larger fuel tanks** and engines with **higher compression ratios**.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Molasses feed = 10,000 kg/day
- Sugar fraction = 50 % → $m_{sugar} = 5000$ kg/day
- Theoretical yield $Y_{theor} = 0.511$ kg ethanol/kg glucose
- Fermentation efficiency η = 0.90
- Distillation recovery = 0.95
- ρ_ethanol = 0.789 g/cm³ = 789 kg/m³

**Step 1 — Theoretical ethanol mass:**

$$m_{theor} = 5000 \times 0.511 = 2555 \;\text{kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — Actual after fermentation:**

$$m_{ferm} = 2555 \times 0.90 = 2299.5 \;\text{kg/day}$$

**Step 3 — Actual after distillation:**

$$m_{EtOH} = 2299.5 \times 0.95 = 2184.5 \;\text{kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to litres:**

$$V_{EtOH} = \frac{m_{EtOH}}{\rho} = \frac{2184.5}{0.789} = 2768.7 \;\text{L/day}$$

$$\boxed{V_{EtOH} \approx 2769 \;\text{L/day} \approx 2.77 \;\text{kL/day}} \quad \text{(7 marks)}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top reasons students lose marks in Module 3 Biofuel questions:**
> 1. **Stoichiometry mistakes** — Forgetting the factor of 3 (one triglyceride → 3 FAME molecules). Always state: "3 moles of FAME per 1 mole of triglyceride."
> 2. **Mixing up mass and volume** — Ethanol is sold in litres but yields are computed in mass; **divide by density** at the end. Examiners will deduct **1 mark** for missing units.
> 3. **Ignoring catalyst excess** — In real plants, **6:1 methanol:oil** is used (not 3:1). The question must specify "stoichiometric" vs "excess."
> 4. **Confusing HHV and LHV** — Use **LHV for engine work** (water leaves as vapor). Use **HHV for boiler/heating calculations** (water condenses).
> 5. **Skipping the flow diagram** — A 7-mark process question **demands a labeled diagram**. Without it, expect a **2–3 mark penalty** even with correct text.
> 6. **Forgetting FFA effect** — A common viva question. Always mention the **2 wt % FFA threshold** and the **two-step acid-base catalysis** solution.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 3 — Biofuels**

- 🔑 **Definition:** Biofuel = fuel from recently-living biomass; carbon-neutral over short cycle.
- 🔑 **Generations:** 1st (edible) → 2nd (ligno-waste) → 3rd (algae) → 4th (engineered).
- 🔑 **Three conversion pillars:** Biochemical (AD, fermentation), Thermochemical (combustion, pyrolysis, gasification, HTL, torrefaction), Chemical (transesterification, hydrotreating, FT).
- 🔑 **Transesterification:** 1 TG + 3 MeOH → 3 FAME + Glycerol; **alkaline catalyst, 60 °C, 1 hr.**
- 🔑 **Key mass benchmark:** **M_FAME ≈ M_TG + (M_MeOH − M_gly/3)** ≈ 296 g/mol for soybean.
- 🔑 **Gay-Lussac yield:** **0.511 g ethanol / g glucose** (theoretical).
- 🔑 **Energy densities (LHV):** Gasoline (44) > Biodiesel (37.5) > Ethanol (26.8) > Methanol (20.0) MJ/kg.
- 🔑 **Octane:** Ethanol ~ **108 RON** (excellent anti-knock). **Cetane:** Biodiesel ~ **50–55** (excellent CI fuel).
- 🔑 **Biogas composition:** **CH₄ 50–70 %**, CO₂ 30–50 %, trace H₂S.
- 🔑 **Syngas quality metric:** (n_CO + n_H₂) / n_total ≥ 0.7 for Fischer–Tropsch.
- 🔑 **HTL advantage:** Processes **wet biomass** (algae, sludge) without drying — saves **> 50 %** energy.
- 🔑 **Biodiesel specifications:** **EN 14214** (Europe) and **ASTM D6751** (USA).
- 🔑 **Aviation biofuels:** **HEFA, FT-SPK, ATJ-SPK**; blended **30–50 %** with Jet A-1.
- 🔑 **Fourth-generation highlight:** Designed for **net-negative CO₂ emissions** — the only biofuel class that removes more carbon than it releases.
- 🔑 **Food vs Fuel debate:** **1st gen only**; 2nd/3rd/4th gen resolve this.
- 🔑 **Algae productivity:** **30–100×** higher oil yield per hectare than soybean.
- 🔑 **Formulae to memorize cold:** $M_{FAME}$ shift equation, Gay-Lussac 0.511, AF for ethanol = 9.0, blend LHV rule.
- 🔑 **Examiner loves:** "Why biodiesel has higher cetane than diesel?" — answer: long saturated FAME chains + self-oxygenated ester groups.
- 🔑 **Examiner hates:** Treating "biofuel" and "biomass" as synonyms. **Biomass is the raw material; biofuel is the processed fuel.**
- 🔑 **Future trend:** **SAF (Sustainable Aviation Fuels)** and **e-fuels (Power-to-X)** are the fastest-growing biofuel segments; expect 2–3 mark questions on them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculation of Heat of Combustion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Heat of Combustion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔥 Calculation of Heat of Combustion — Module 4

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework, the **Heat of Combustion** (also termed the **Calorific Value** or **Heating Value**) of a fuel is formally defined as the **total quantity of heat liberated when a unit mass (1 kg for solid/liquid fuels) or unit volume (1 m³ for gaseous fuels) of the fuel is subjected to complete combustion with oxygen**, under isothermal conditions, with the products of combustion being cooled back to the initial reaction temperature.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\Delta H_{comb} = \sum H_{products} - \sum H_{reactants} \quad (\text{at constant pressure})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Standard Variants in KTU Examination Context**
>
> 1. **GCV / HCV (Gross / Higher Calorific Value)** — The heat released assuming *all* the water vapour formed during combustion is condensed back to the liquid state, thus recovering its latent heat of condensation (~**587 cal/g** or **2454 kJ/kg** at 25 °C).
>
> 2. **NCV / LCV (Net / Lower Calorific Value)** — The heat released when the water vapour formed is *not* condensed; it leaves as superheated steam, hence the latent heat is *lost* to the exhaust.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine buying a candle to heat a small room.

- The candle burns wax — the flame is hot, but you notice the air around it gets **moist**. That moisture is water vapour produced from the hydrogen atoms in the wax.
- If you somehow trapped and cooled that vapour back to liquid, you would recover a little more heat — equivalent to the **GCV**.
- In a real chimney or stove, the vapour escapes hot into the atmosphere — you only get the **NCV**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Mnemonic** : *"GCV is what the universe gives you; NCV is what your boiler actually absorbs."*

### 1.3 Standard Reference Constants (Mandatory for Numerical Problems)

| Constant | Symbol | Numerical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Latent heat of steam at 25 °C | $\lambda$ | **587 cal/g** ≈ **2454 kJ/kg** |
| Heat of combustion of Carbon | — | **8080 kcal/kg** |
| Heat of combustion of Hydrogen | — | **34 500 kcal/kg** |
| Heat of combustion of Sulphur | — | **2240 kcal/kg** |
| Molar mass of H₂O | $M_w$ | **18 g/mol** |
| Water formed per kg H₂ burnt | — | **9 kg H₂O / kg H₂** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic Foundation

When a hydrocarbon fuel $C_xH_yO_zS_w$ burns in oxygen, the fundamental reactions are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C} \; + \; \text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H_{25^\circ C} = -8080 \; \text{kcal/kg C} \\
\text{H}_2 \; + \; \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \quad \Delta H = -34\,500 \; \text{kcal/kg H}_2 \\
\text{S} \; + \; \text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{SO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -2240 \; \text{kcal/kg S}
\end{aligned}
$$

The oxygen already present inside the fuel (*intrinsic oxygen*) is *not* available for combustion. Worse, it has already combined with some hydrogen as moisture or as chemically bound oxygen. This is the **correction term $\dfrac{O}{8}$** that appears in every classical formula.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why divide by 8?**
> The mass ratio of oxygen to hydrogen in water is **16 : 2 = 8 : 1**.
> Therefore, every 1 kg of intrinsic oxygen in the fuel has *pre-bound* **1/8 kg** of hydrogen which can no longer participate in combustion.

## 2.2 The Two Path-Breakdown for Heat Calculation

A KTU numerical problem is *always* solved in two logical steps:

1. **Compute the GCV** from the ultimate analysis (or Dulong's formula).
2. **Compute the NCV** by subtracting the latent heat carried away by the water vapour.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important page** for the KTU ESE Module 4 — reproduce it from memory in the exam hall.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units | Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Dulong's Formula (Standard)** | $\text{GCV} = \dfrac{1}{100}\left[8080\,C + 34\,500\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\right]$ | kcal/kg | Solid & liquid fossil fuels |
| 2 | **Dulong's Formula (SI)** | $\text{GCV} = \dfrac{1}{100}\left[33\,800\,C + 1\,42\,200\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9040\,S\right]$ | kJ/kg | Same |
| 3 | **NCV from GCV (mass basis)** | $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9\,H \cdot 587$ | kcal/kg | All solid/liquid fuels |
| 4 | **NCV in SI units** | $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9\,H \cdot 2454$ | kJ/kg | Same |
| 5 | **NCV (combined form)** | $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 52.83\,H$ | kcal/kg | Approximation |
| 6 | **Water-formed correction** | $W = 9\,H + M$ | kg H₂O/kg fuel | $M$ = external moisture |
| 7 | **Goutal's Formula** | $\text{GCV} = 82\,C + a\,S$ | kcal/kg | Coal where VM data is available |
| 8 | **Volume-basis relation** | $Q_v = Q_m \cdot \rho_{fuel}$ | kcal/m³ | Converts kg-basis to m³ |

> In the table above, $C, H, O, S, M$ represent the **mass percentages** of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, sulphur, and moisture respectively, as obtained from the **ultimate (proximate) analysis** of the fuel.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The heat of combustion is the *foundational parameter* upon which:

- **Boiler thermal efficiency** is calculated ($\eta = \dfrac{\text{NCV}_{utilised}}{\text{NCV}_{supplied}}$).
- **Fuel pricing (per kcal)** in international energy markets is fixed.
- **Combustion stoichiometry** and air–fuel ratios are designed.
- **Rocket propulsion, internal combustion engines, gas turbines**, and **fuel-cell design** all derive their performance curves from this single quantity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Dulong's Formula

### Step A — Identify the reactive species per 100 kg of fuel

For every 100 kg of fuel (this is why a factor of $1/100$ appears at the end), we have:

- $C$ kg of Carbon
- $H$ kg of Hydrogen
- $O$ kg of Oxygen
- $S$ kg of Sulphur
- $(100 - C - H - O - S)$ kg of inert matter (ash + nitrogen + moisture)

### Step B — Correct the available hydrogen

The intrinsic oxygen has already consumed part of the hydrogen as water. From the stoichiometry $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$, the mass ratio of $O : H$ bound as water is **16 : 2 = 8 : 1**.

Therefore, oxygen-bound hydrogen $= \dfrac{O}{8}$ kg.

The *net free hydrogen* that can still undergo combustion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{net}} = H - \dfrac{O}{8} \quad \text{kg per 100 kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$
