### Step C — Write individual heat contributions

Using the standard heats of combustion for each element:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_C &= 8080 \cdot C \quad \text{kcal} \\
Q_H &= 34\,500 \cdot \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) \quad \text{kcal} \\
Q_S &= 2240 \cdot S \quad \text{kcal}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step D — Sum and divide by the mass basis (per kg)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV per kg} &= \dfrac{Q_C + Q_H + Q_S}{100} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{100}\left[8080\,C + 34\,500\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\right] \quad \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **Dulong's Formula** — the textbook standard.

## 3.2 Derivation of the NCV-GCV Relationship

From the chemical equation $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(g)}$ versus the same yielding liquid water, the difference is the **latent heat of vaporisation** at the chosen reference temperature.

For every 1 kg of *free* hydrogen burnt, **9 kg of water** are produced (since $18/2 = 9$).

Therefore, the heat loss due to water leaving as steam per kg of fuel is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{loss}} = 9\,H \cdot \lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\lambda = 587$ cal/g = 5870 kcal/kg = 2454 kJ/kg.

Subtracting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9\,H \cdot \lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

In SI units with $\lambda = 2454$ kJ/kg:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NCV (kJ/kg)} = \text{GCV (kJ/kg)} - 9\,H \cdot 2454
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 KTU Worked Numerical — Solved with Full Logic

### **Problem Statement** (Sample KTU Pattern)

A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass:

$$C = 84\%, \quad H = 5\%, \quad O = 4\%, \quad S = 1.5\%, \quad N = 1.0\%, \quad \text{Ash} = 4.5\%$$

Calculate **(a)** the GCV using Dulong's formula and **(b)** the NCV in both kcal/kg and kJ/kg.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given** (per 100 kg fuel): $C = 84$, $H = 5$, $O = 4$, $S = 1.5$.

#### Part (a) — GCV Calculation

Step 1 — Compute the oxygen-corrected hydrogen:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{net}} &= H - \dfrac{O}{8} \\
&= 5 - \dfrac{4}{8} \\
&= 5 - 0.5 \\
&= 4.5 \; \text{kg per 100 kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Plug into Dulong's formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV} &= \dfrac{1}{100}\Big[8080\,C + 34\,500\,H_{\text{net}} + 2240\,S\Big] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{100}\Big[8080(84) + 34\,500(4.5) + 2240(1.5)\Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Evaluate each term individually:

$$
\begin{aligned}
8080 \times 84 &= 6\,78\,720 \\
34\,500 \times 4.5 &= 1\,55\,250 \\
2240 \times 1.5 &= 3\,360
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Sum and divide by 100:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum} &= 6\,78\,720 + 1\,55\,250 + 3\,360 \\
&= 8\,37\,330 \; \text{kcal per 100 kg fuel} \\
\text{GCV} &= \dfrac{8\,37\,330}{100} = 8373.30 \; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — NCV Calculation

Step 5 — Compute latent heat loss (using $\lambda = 587$ kcal/kg at 25 °C):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{loss}} &= 9 \cdot H \cdot \lambda \\
&= 9 \times 5 \times 587 \\
&= 26\,415 \; \text{kcal/kg} \; \text{(per 100 kg basis would be } 2\,64\,150 \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 6 — Subtract:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NCV (kcal/kg)} &= 8373.30 - 264.15 \\
&= 8109.15 \; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 7 — Convert to kJ/kg using $1 \; \text{kcal} = 4.184 \; \text{kJ}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NCV (kJ/kg)} &= 8109.15 \times 4.184 \\
&= 33\,928.6 \; \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Answer Summary**

| Quantity | kcal/kg | kJ/kg |
|---|---|---|
| **GCV** | **8373.30** | **35 027.84** |
| **NCV** | **8109.15** | **33 928.60** |

> [!NOTE]
> This problem has a **direct analogue** in every KTU University Exam paper — typically carrying 7 marks for GCV computation and 7 marks for NCV, totalling 14 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Heat of Combustion Calculation Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the full computational architecture a student should follow when faced with any heat-of-combustion numerical.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Fuel Ultimate Analysis Data] --> B[Extract mass percentages: C, H, O, S, M]
    B --> C{Net Hydrogen Available?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Compute H_net = H minus O divided by 8]
    C -- No --> E[Set H_net = H]
    D --> F[Apply Dulong Formula: GCV = 8080C + 34500 H_net + 2240S divided by 100]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Convert GCV units if required: kcal per kg to kJ per kg via 4.184]
    G --> H{Need NCV also?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Compute water formed: W = 9H kg per kg fuel]
    H -- No --> K[Output: GCV only]
    I --> J[NCV = GCV minus W times lambda, lambda = 587 or 2454]
    J --> L[Output: GCV and NCV in requested units]
    K --> M[End]
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Heat Accounting Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT DOMAIN]
        P1[C: Carbon content]
        P2[H: Hydrogen content]
        P3[O: Oxygen content]
        P4[S: Sulphur content]
    end

    subgraph REACTIONS[CHEMICAL CONTRIBUTIONS]
        Q1[Heat from C to CO2: 8080 C]
        Q2[Heat from H to H2O liq: 34500 H_net]
        Q3[Heat from S to SO2: 2240 S]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT METRICS]
        R1[GCV: Gross Calorific Value]
        R2[Latent Heat Loss: 9H times 587]
        R3[NCV: Net Calorific Value]
    end

    P1 --> Q1
    P2 --> Q3
    P3 --> Q3
    P4 --> Q3
    Q1 --> R1
    Q2 --> R1
    Q3 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1{Type of Fuel?} -- Solid Coal --> S2{VM data available?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Use Goutal Formula: 82C plus aS]
    S2 -- No --> S4[Use Dulong Standard Formula]
    S1 -- Liquid Petroleum --> S5[Use Dulong Standard Formula]
    S1 -- Gaseous --> S6[Use Volume-basis: Qv = Qm times rho]
    S3 --> END1[Report GCV]
    S4 --> END1
    S5 --> END1
    S6 --> END1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define GCV and NCV. Why is NCV always lower than GCV? Which value is used for designing industrial boiler efficiency and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **GCV (Gross Calorific Value)** is the heat liberated when 1 kg of fuel undergoes complete combustion, with products cooled to the initial temperature and *all water vapour condensed to liquid* (1 mark).
- **NCV (Net Calorific Value)** is the heat liberated under the same conditions, *without condensation* of the water vapour produced (1 mark).
- **NCV is lower** because the latent heat of vaporisation of water (~587 cal/g) is not recovered.
- **Industrial boilers use NCV** because in practice the flue gases exit above 100 °C, so the water vapour cannot condense inside the furnace; the latent heat is physically lost (1 mark).

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Why is a correction term $\dfrac{O}{8}$ subtracted from the hydrogen content in Dulong's formula?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The intrinsic oxygen in the fuel has already combined with a fraction of the hydrogen to form water (1 mark).
- The mass ratio of oxygen to hydrogen in water is 16:2 = 8:1 (1 mark).
- Hence, every 1 kg of fuel-bound oxygen has rendered $1/8$ kg of hydrogen unavailable for combustion; this quantity must be subtracted from the gross hydrogen content (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency**: Students frequently mix kcal/kg with kJ/kg mid-calculation — you will lose **3 marks instantly** if the final unit does not match what is asked. Always write the unit next to *every* intermediate value.
> 2. **Forgetting the division by 100**: Many students compute the numerator correctly but forget to divide by 100. This is the single most common error — **2 marks deducted** consistently.
> 3. **Using $H$ instead of $H_{net} = H - O/8$**: This silently inflates GCV by ~10–15%. **Partial credit may still be awarded** if method is shown.
> 4. **Forgetting the $1\,kcal = 4.184\,kJ$** conversion when answering in SI units.

---

### **Question B (Choice 1) — Option A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A lignite coal has the following ultimate analysis:
$$C = 65\%, \quad H = 4.8\%, \quad O = 12\%, \quad S = 1.2\%, \quad N = 0.8\%, \quad \text{Ash} = 16.2\%$$

**(a)** Calculate the GCV of the fuel using Dulong's formula in kcal/kg.
**(b)** Determine the NCV in kJ/kg, given that the latent heat of steam at 25 °C is 2454 kJ/kg.

#### **Model Solution — Option A**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Step 1 — Compute net hydrogen:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{net}} &= 4.8 - \dfrac{12}{8} = 4.8 - 1.5 = 3.3 \; \text{kg/100 kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Apply Dulong's formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV} &= \dfrac{1}{100}\Big[8080(65) + 34\,500(3.3) + 2240(1.2)\Big] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{100}\Big[5\,25\,200 + 1\,13\,850 + 2688\Big] \\
&= \dfrac{6\,41\,738}{100} \\
&= 6417.38 \; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Step 3 — Latent heat loss in kcal/kg:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{loss}} &= 9 \times 4.8 \times 587 \\
&= 25\,358.4 \; \text{cal/kg} \\
&= 25.358 \; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — NCV in kcal/kg:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NCV (kcal/kg)} &= 6417.38 - 25.36 \\
&= 6392.02 \; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5 — Convert to kJ/kg:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NCV (kJ/kg)} &= 6392.02 \times 4.184 \\
&= 26\,744.21 \; \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- GCV = **6417.38 kcal/kg**
- NCV = **26 744.21 kJ/kg**

---

### **Question B (Choice 1) — Option B (Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A bituminous coal has the following composition by mass:
$$C = 78\%, \quad H = 6\%, \quad O = 8\%, \quad S = 1.5\%, \quad N = 1.5\%, \quad \text{Moisture} = 5\%$$

**(a)** Calculate the GCV using Dulong's formula in kJ/kg.
**(b)** Calculate the NCV in kcal/kg.

#### **Model Solution — Option B**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Step 1 — Net hydrogen:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{net}} &= 6 - \dfrac{8}{8} = 6 - 1 = 5 \; \text{kg/100 kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Using the SI version of Dulong's formula (kJ/kg):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV (kJ/kg)} &= \dfrac{1}{100}\Big[33\,800(78) + 1\,42\,200(5) + 9040(1.5)\Big] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{100}\Big[26\,36\,400 + 7\,11\,000 + 13\,560\Big] \\
&= \dfrac{33\,60\,960}{100} \\
&= 33\,609.60 \; \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Step 3 — Convert GCV to kcal/kg:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV (kcal/kg)} &= \dfrac{33\,609.60}{4.184} = 8032.50 \; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Latent heat loss:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{loss}} &= 9 \times 6 \times 587 = 31\,698 \; \text{cal/kg} = 31.70 \; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5 — NCV:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NCV (kcal/kg)} &= 8032.50 - 31.70 \\
&= 8000.80 \; \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- GCV = **33 609.60 kJ/kg**
- NCV = **8000.80 kcal/kg**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Dulong's formula** is *the* standard for KTU Module 4 numerical problems; memorise it verbatim including the factor of $1/100$.
- Always **subtract $O/8$** from hydrogen to correct for fuel-bound oxygen.
- **GCV** assumes water is condensed → higher value.
- **NCV** assumes water escapes as steam → lower value by exactly $9H\lambda$.
- Use **$\lambda = 587$ cal/g** (or **2454 J/g**) for 25 °C reference.
- For SI conversions, **1 kcal = 4.184 kJ** — write this at the top of every numerical answer.
- **Goutal's formula** ($82C + aS$) is an alternative when volatile-matter data is provided.
- The ash, nitrogen, and moisture fractions do **not** contribute to heat — they appear in the ultimate analysis only to balance the mass percentage to 100%.
- **Industrial boiler efficiency calculations use NCV**, never GCV.
- The mass ratio **$O:H = 8:1$** in water is the conceptual key — every "1 mark" question is testing this.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theoretical and Actual Combustion Processes - Air-Fuel Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Theoretical and Actual Combustion Processes - Air-Fuel Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theoretical (Stoichiometric) Combustion** is the idealized combustion process in which a fuel is burned with **exactly** the chemically correct quantity of oxygen (and hence air) required for the complete oxidation of all combustible elements (C → CO₂, H₂ → H₂O, S → SO₂), leaving no free oxygen and no unburnt combustible matter in the products of combustion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Actual Combustion Process** is the real-world process carried out in furnaces, boilers, IC engines, and gas turbines. It invariably requires supplying **more** air than the theoretical minimum to achieve practical completeness of combustion, compensate for imperfect mixing, ensure flame stability, and limit the formation of CO, soot, and unburnt hydrocarbons.

The **Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F)** is the dimensionless mass (or sometimes molar) ratio of the mass of air supplied per unit mass of fuel burned:

$$A/F \;=\; \dfrac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}}$$

It is classified as:

- **Theoretical (Stoichiometric) Air-Fuel Ratio** — denoted $(A/F)_{stoich}$ or $(A/F)_{th}$
- **Actual Air-Fuel Ratio** — denoted $(A/F)_{actual}$

> [!NOTE]
> **By mass, dry atmospheric air contains 23.2 % O₂ and 76.8 % N₂**. Therefore, for every 1 kg of O₂ required, the mass of air supplied is $1/0.232 = \mathbf{4.318 \; kg}$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are cooking **rice** for a meal.

- **Theoretical air** is like the *exact* quantity of water required to perfectly cook the rice — neither a drop more, nor a drop less.
- **Actual air** is what you *practically* pour into the vessel. Because some water spills, evaporates, or fails to reach every grain, you inevitably **add extra water** to ensure every grain cooks.
- That "extra water" in our analogy is **excess air** — a real-world necessity, not a design flaw.

In combustion, oxygen molecules must find and react with every carbon and hydrogen atom of the fuel. Since turbulence, residence time, and mixing are never perfect in real equipment, we always supply **more oxygen than the stoichiometric minimum** so that essentially *all* of the fuel gets oxidised.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

| Metric | Value / Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Mass fraction of O₂ in dry air | **0.232** (≈ 23.2 %) | kg O₂ / kg air |
| Mass fraction of N₂ in dry air | **0.768** (≈ 76.8 %) | kg N₂ / kg air |
| Molecular weight of air | **28.97** | kg/kg-mol |
| Molecular weight of O₂ | **32.00** | kg/kg-mol |
| Stoichiometric mass air / O₂ | **4.318** (= 1/0.232) | kg air / kg O₂ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stoichiometric Combustion Equations (Per kg of Pure Element)

For each combustible constituent of a fuel, the chemical reaction with pure oxygen is:

$$\mathrm{C \;+\; O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; CO_2}$$

$$\mathrm{H_2 \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; H_2O}$$

$$\mathrm{S \;+\; O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; SO_2}$$

The minimum oxygen required **per kilogram of each element** is:

| Element | Reaction | O₂ required (kg/kg element) | Air required (kg/kg element) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon (C) | C + O₂ → CO₂ | $\dfrac{32}{12} = \mathbf{2.667}$ | $2.667 \times 4.318 = \mathbf{11.52}$ |
| Hydrogen (H₂) | H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O | $\dfrac{16}{2} = \mathbf{8.000}$ | $8.000 \times 4.318 = \mathbf{34.56}$ |
| Sulphur (S) | S + O₂ → SO₂ | $\dfrac{32}{32} = \mathbf{1.000}$ | $1.000 \times 4.318 = \mathbf{4.32}$ |

### 2.2 Generalised Formula for Solid / Liquid Fuels

If a fuel has ultimate analysis by mass: **C % + H % + O % + S % + (N + ash) % = 100 %**, then the minimum (theoretical) oxygen needed per kg of fuel is:

$$O_{2,\,min} \;=\; \dfrac{8}{3}C \;+\; 8H_2 \;-\; O_2 \quad \text{(kg O}_2\text{ / kg fuel)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The sign is **negative** for the fuel's own oxygen because that intrinsic oxygen already contributes to oxidising some H and C inside the fuel, reducing the external O₂ demand.

Multiplying by 4.318 gives the **theoretical air per kg of fuel**:

$$\boxed{\;(A)_{\text{theo}} \;=\; 11.6\,C \;+\; 34.56\,H_2 \;+\; 4.32\,S \;-\; 4.32\,O_2 \quad \text{kg air / kg fuel}\;}$$

The **theoretical (stoichiometric) air-fuel ratio by mass** is simply:

$$(A/F)_{\text{theo}} \;=\; \dfrac{(A)_{\text{theo}}}{1} \;=\; 11.6C + 34.56H_2 + 4.32S - 4.32O_2$$

### 2.3 Actual Combustion — Role of Excess Air

Real combustion cannot achieve 100 % oxidation with stoichiometric air because of finite mixing time, temperature gradients, and kinetic limitations. Hence:

$$\boxed{\;(A)_{\text{actual}} \;=\; (A)_{\text{theo}} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{\%EA}{100}\right)\;}$$

where **%EA** is the **percentage excess air** supplied:

$$\%EA \;=\; \dfrac{(A)_{\text{actual}} - (A)_{\text{theo}}}{(A)_{\text{theo}}} \times 100$$

### 2.4 Equivalence Ratio (Φ) — Important KTU Concept

The equivalence ratio is the reciprocal parameter used in combustion engineering:

$$\boxed{\;\Phi \;=\; \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}\;}$$

- $\Phi = 1$ → stoichiometric
- $\Phi < 1$ → **lean** mixture (excess air)
- $\Phi > 1$ → **rich** mixture (deficient air)

### 2.5 Gaseous Fuels — Volumetric Basis

For gaseous fuels, air is conveniently expressed on a **volumetric basis** because combustion air and fuel volumes are measured at the same reference state. If fuel composition is by volume (mole %) as CH₄, C₂H₆, C₃H₈ … H₂, CO, then:

$$O_{2,\,min} \;=\; 2\text{CH}_4 + \tfrac{7}{2}\text{C}_2\text{H}_6 + 5\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{CO} - \text{O}_2 \quad \text{(mole O}_2\text{ / mole fuel)}$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Minimum O₂ per kg fuel | $O_{2,min} = \tfrac{8}{3}C + 8H_2 + S - O_2$ | kg O₂/kg fuel |
| 2 | Theoretical air per kg fuel | $(A)_{th} = 11.6C + 34.56H_2 + 4.32S - 4.32O_2$ | kg air/kg fuel |
| 3 | Air-to-O₂ mass factor | $f = 1/0.232 = 4.318$ | kg air/kg O₂ |
| 4 | Excess air % | $\%EA = \dfrac{A_{act}-A_{th}}{A_{th}} \times 100$ | % |
| 5 | Actual air | $A_{act} = A_{th}(1 + \%EA/100)$ | kg air/kg fuel |
| 6 | Equivalence ratio | $\Phi = (A/F)_{th} / (A/F)_{act}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | A/F (mass) | $A/F = m_{air}/m_{fuel}$ | kg/kg |
| 8 | A/F (volumetric, gas) | $A/F = V_{air}/V_{fuel}$ at same T,P | m³/m³ |

### 2.7 Engineering Utility

- **Boiler design**: Excess air of 15–25 % is typical for pulverised coal firing; too much excess air carries away heat as sensible heat of N₂, dropping efficiency.
- **IC engines**: Spark-ignition engines operate near stoichiometric (Φ ≈ 0.95–1.05) for catalytic converter operation; diesel engines always run lean (Φ = 0.7–0.85).
- **Pollution control**: Lower excess air reduces NOₓ (thermal) but raises CO and soot if too low.
- **Combustion stability**: A minimum of 5–10 % excess air is generally needed to ensure flame stability in gas-fired furnaces.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Stoichiometric O₂ Requirement for Carbon

Consider the elementary reaction:

$$\mathrm{C \;+\; O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; CO_2}$$

Atomic / molecular masses:
- 1 kg-mol of C weighs **12 kg**
- 1 kg-mol of O₂ weighs **32 kg**
- 1 kg-mol of CO₂ weighs **44 kg**

Hence **per 1 kg of carbon**:

$$\text{Mass of O}_2 \;=\; \dfrac{32 \;\text{kg O}_2}{12 \;\text{kg C}} \;=\; \mathbf{2.667 \;kg \;O_2 / kg \;C}$$

Mass of air (using the 4.318 factor):

$$\text{Mass of air} \;=\; 2.667 \times 4.318 \;=\; \mathbf{11.52 \;kg \;air / kg \;C}$$

### 3.2 Derivation for Hydrogen

$$\mathrm{H_2 \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; H_2O}$$

Molecular masses: H₂ = 2 kg/kg-mol, O₂ = 32 kg/kg-mol.

Per 1 kg of H₂:

$$\text{Mass of O}_2 \;=\; \dfrac{(1/2)(32)}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{16}{2} \;=\; \mathbf{8 \;kg \;O_2 / kg \;H_2}$$

$$\text{Mass of air} \;=\; 8 \times 4.318 \;=\; \mathbf{34.56 \;kg \;air / kg \;H_2}$$

### 3.3 Derivation for Sulphur

$$\mathrm{S \;+\; O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; SO_2}$$

Molecular masses: S = 32 kg/kg-mol, O₂ = 32 kg/kg-mol.

Per 1 kg of S:

$$\text{Mass of O}_2 \;=\; \dfrac{32}{32} \;=\; \mathbf{1.0 \;kg \;O_2 / kg \;S}$$

$$\text{Mass of air} \;=\; 1 \times 4.318 \;=\; \mathbf{4.32 \;kg \;air / kg \;S}$$

### 3.4 Generalised Derivation for a Composite Fuel

Let the ultimate analysis of 1 kg of fuel be: $C$ kg C, $H$ kg H, $O$ kg O, $S$ kg S, plus inert N + ash.

**Step 1 — Total O₂ demanded (gross):**

$$\text{O}_{2,\,\text{gross}} \;=\; \dfrac{8}{3}C \;+\; 8H \;+\; 1 \cdot S \quad \text{(kg O}_2\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Subtract intrinsic O₂ already present in the fuel:**

$$\text{O}_{2,\,min} \;=\; \dfrac{8}{3}C \;+\; 8H \;+\; S \;-\; O \quad \text{(kg O}_2\text{ per kg fuel)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mass of theoretical air:**

$$(A)_{th} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O\right) \times \dfrac{100}{23.2}$$

$$\boxed{\;(A)_{th} \;=\; 11.6\,C + 34.56\,H + 4.32\,S - 4.32\,O \quad \text{kg air/kg fuel}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Actual air with excess air EA%:**

$$(A)_{act} \;=\; (A)_{th} \left(1 + \dfrac{EA}{100}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Air-Fuel Ratios:**

$$(A/F)_{th} \;=\; (A)_{th}/1 \;=\; 11.6C + 34.56H + 4.32S - 4.32O$$

$$(A/F)_{act} \;=\; (A)_{act}/1 \;=\; (A/F)_{th}\,(1 + EA/100)$$

### 3.5 Numerical Illustration (Worked Example)

> [!NOTE]
> **Given**: A fuel oil has ultimate analysis: C = 84 %, H = 12 %, S = 3 %, O = 1 % (by mass). Combustion is carried out with **25 % excess air**. Find (a) theoretical air, (b) actual air supplied, (c) actual A/F ratio, (d) equivalence ratio.

**(a) Theoretical air per kg fuel**

$$(A)_{th} \;=\; 11.6(0.84) + 34.56(0.12) + 4.32(0.03) - 4.32(0.01)$$

Compute term by term:

$$11.6 \times 0.84 \;=\; 9.744$$

$$34.56 \times 0.12 \;=\; 4.1472$$

$$4.32 \times 0.03 \;=\; 0.1296$$

$$4.32 \times 0.01 \;=\; 0.0432$$

Sum:

$$(A)_{th} \;=\; 9.744 + 4.1472 + 0.1296 - 0.0432 \;=\; \mathbf{13.978 \;\approx\; 13.98 \;kg \;air/kg \;fuel}$$

**(b) Actual air supplied**

$$(A)_{act} \;=\; 13.98 \times (1 + 0.25) \;=\; 13.98 \times 1.25 \;=\; \mathbf{17.475 \;kg \;air/kg \;fuel}$$

**(c) Actual A/F ratio**

$$(A/F)_{act} \;=\; 17.475 \;\text{kg/kg}$$

**(d) Equivalence ratio**

$$\Phi \;=\; \dfrac{(A/F)_{th}}{(A/F)_{act}} \;=\; \dfrac{13.98}{17.475} \;=\; \mathbf{0.80}$$

Since $\Phi < 1$, the mixture is **lean**, confirming the 25 % excess air.

### 3.6 Conversion to Mole Basis (Gaseous Fuels)

For gaseous fuels the molar air requirement is often requested. Since air = O₂ + 3.76 N₂ on a mole basis, the air-to-O₂ **molar** ratio is:

$$\dfrac{\text{moles air}}{\text{moles O}_2} \;=\; 1 + 3.76 \;=\; \mathbf{4.76}$$

To convert a mass ratio to volume ratio at the same T, P, multiply by the ratio of molecular weights:

$$\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{air}}}{V_{\text{fuel}}}\right) \;=\; \left(\dfrac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}}\right) \times \dfrac{M_{\text{fuel}}}{M_{\text{air}}}$$

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Solving A/F Problems

| Step | Question to ask | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Is the fuel solid/liquid or gaseous? | Solid/liquid → mass basis; gaseous → mole/volume basis. |
| 2 | Is ultimate analysis given by mass or volume? | Use as-is; substitute directly into the appropriate formula. |
| 3 | Is theoretical or actual air asked? | Theoretical → use $(A)_{th}$ formula; actual → multiply by $(1 + EA/100)$. |
| 4 | Is % excess air known? | Compute $(A)_{act}$ from $(A)_{th}$ and EA. |
| 5 | Is equivalence ratio asked? | $\Phi = (A/F)_{th} / (A/F)_{act}$. |
| 6 | Cross-check units | All masses in kg; all % in percent (not fraction). |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Theoretical vs Actual Combustion Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Ultimate Analysis] --> B[Identify Combustible Elements C H S]
    B --> C[Apply Stoichiometric Reactions]
    C --> D[Compute Minimum O2 Requirement]
    D --> E[Convert to Theoretical Air using factor 4.318]
    E --> F[(A/F)th Stoichiometric Ratio]
    F --> G{Operating Condition}
    G --> G1[Lean Mixture Excess Air greater than 0]
    G --> G2[Stoichiometric Excess Air equals 0]
    G --> G3[Rich Mixture Deficient Air]
    F --> H[Inject Excess Air for Real Combustion]
    H --> I[(A)act Actual Air Supplied]
    I --> J[Compute Equivalence Ratio Phi]
    J --> K[Combustion Products CO2 H2O SO2 O2 N2]
    K --> L[Heat Release Flue Gas Analysis]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — A/F Calculation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I1[Input Module Ultimate Analysis C H O S] --> P1[Process Module 1 Stoichiometric O2 Demand]
    P1 --> P2[Process Module 2 Theoretical Air Mass]
    P2 --> P3[Process Module 3 Apply Excess Air Factor]
    P3 --> P4[Process Module 4 Actual Air Mass]
    P4 --> O1[Output Module A F Actual]
    P4 --> O2[Output Module Equivalence Ratio Phi]
    P4 --> O3[Output Module Air Requirement m3 per kg fuel]
    P1 --> V1[Validation Module Check Mass Balance 100 percent]
    P2 --> V1
    P3 --> V1
```

### 4.3 Comparative Decision Map — Lean / Stoichiometric / Rich

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Compare A F Actual vs A F Theoretical] --> C1{A F Actual greater than A F Th}
    C1 -- YES --> L[Lean Mixture Phi less than 1 Excess O2 in flue]
    C1 -- NO --> C2{A F Actual equals A F Th}
    C2 -- YES --> ST[Stoichiometric Phi equals 1 No free O2]
    C2 -- NO --> R[Rich Mixture Phi greater than 1 Unburnt fuel CO soot]
    L --> END[Combustion Outcome]
    ST --> END
    R --> END
```

### 4.4 Schematic Mapping of Combustion Equations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EL[C element 12] --> RE1[C plus O2 yields CO2]
    EL2[H2 element 2] --> RE2[H2 plus 0.5 O2 yields H2O]
    EL3[S element 32] --> RE3[S plus O2 yields SO2]
    RE1 --> M[Minimum O2 Demand]
    RE2 --> M
    RE3 --> M
    M --> M2[Subtract Intrinsic Fuel Oxygen]
    M2 --> AF[Theoretical A F Ratio]
    AF --> ACT[Apply Excess Air Multiplier]
    ACT --> PROD[Products of Combustion]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each flowchart above maps the **logical flow of calculation** for A/F problems rather than a literal chemical process diagram. This protects against complex physical drawing errors and provides a clean functional reference for the student's exam-prep.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> Define the terms (i) Stoichiometric air, (ii) Excess air, and (iii) Equivalence ratio as applied to combustion of fuels. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Stoichiometric air** is the minimum quantity of air theoretically required for complete combustion of a fuel so that all carbon forms CO₂, all hydrogen forms H₂O, and all sulphur forms SO₂, with no free oxygen remaining in the products.

(ii) **Excess air** is the quantity of air supplied in *actual* combustion in excess of the stoichiometric minimum, expressed as a percentage:

$$\%EA = \dfrac{A_{act} - A_{th}}{A_{th}} \times 100$$

(iii) **Equivalence ratio** is the ratio of the stoichiometric A/F to the actual A/F:

$$\Phi = \dfrac{(A/F)_{th}}{(A/F)_{act}}$$

If $\Phi < 1$ the mixture is **lean**, if $\Phi = 1$ it is **stoichiometric**, and if $\Phi > 1$ it is **rich**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> A fuel has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 80 %, H = 6 %, O = 8 %, S = 2 %, remaining N + ash. Calculate the stoichiometric air required per kg of fuel. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Ultimate analysis per kg fuel: $C = 0.80$, $H = 0.06$, $O = 0.08$, $S = 0.02$.

Apply the theoretical-air formula:

$$(A)_{th} = 11.6C + 34.56H + 4.32S - 4.32O$$

Substituting:

$$(A)_{th} = 11.6(0.80) + 34.56(0.06) + 4.32(0.02) - 4.32(0.08)$$

$$(A)_{th} = 9.280 + 2.074 + 0.086 - 0.346$$

$$(A)_{th} = \mathbf{11.09 \;kg \;air / kg \;fuel}$$

Stoichiometric air ≈ **11.09 kg/kg fuel**.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive, from first principles, the expression for the theoretical air required per kg of a solid fuel whose ultimate analysis by mass is C, H, O, S. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A coal sample has the following composition by mass: C = 70 %, H = 5 %, O = 8 %, S = 2 %, N = 1 %, ash = 14 %. Combustion is carried out with **30 % excess air**. Calculate:
  (i) The actual mass of air supplied per kg of coal
  (ii) The actual A/F ratio by mass
  (iii) The equivalence ratio
  (iv) The percentage of O₂ by volume in the dry flue gas. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

Write the three elementary combustion reactions:

$$\mathrm{C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2}, \quad \mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O}, \quad \mathrm{S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2}$$

Using molecular weights (C = 12, H₂ = 2, O₂ = 32, S = 32):

- Per kg C: O₂ needed = 32/12 = **2.667 kg**
- Per kg H: O₂ needed = 16/2 = **8.000 kg**
- Per kg S: O₂ needed = 32/32 = **1.000 kg**

For 1 kg fuel containing C kg C, H kg H, O kg O (intrinsic), S kg S:

**Gross O₂ required** = (8/3)C + 8H + S

**Net O₂ required** = (8/3)C + 8H + S − O (subtract fuel's intrinsic O₂)

Since dry air is 23.2 % O₂ by mass:

$$(A)_{th} = \dfrac{(8/3)C + 8H + S - O}{0.232}$$

$$\boxed{(A)_{th} = 11.6\,C + 34.56\,H + 4.32\,S - 4.32\,O \;\;\text{kg air/kg fuel}}$$

---

**Model Solution (b):**

Per kg coal: $C = 0.70$, $H = 0.05$, $O = 0.08$, $S = 0.02$.

**Theoretical air:**

$$(A)_{th} = 11.6(0.70) + 34.56(0.05) + 4.32(0.02) - 4.32(0.08)$$

$$= 8.120 + 1.728 + 0.0864 - 0.3456$$

$$= \mathbf{9.589 \;kg \;air/kg \;coal}$$

**(i) Actual air with 30 % excess air:**

$$(A)_{act} = 9.589 \times 1.30 = \mathbf{12.466 \;kg \;air/kg \;coal}$$

**(ii) Actual A/F ratio:**

$$(A/F)_{act} = 12.466 \;\text{kg/kg}$$

**(iii) Equivalence ratio:**

$$\Phi = \dfrac{9.589}{12.466} = \mathbf{0.769}$$

Since $\Phi < 1$, the mixture is **lean**.

**(iv) O₂ % in dry flue gas:**

First, find the O₂ supplied in the actual air:

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ supplied} = 12.466 \times 0.232 = 2.892 \;\text{kg}$$

Theoretical O₂ needed = $(A)_{th} \times 0.232 = 9.589 \times 0.232 = 2.225 \;\text{kg}$.

**Excess O₂** in flue gas = 2.892 − 2.225 = **0.667 kg**.

Now compute each dry product component:

$$\text{CO}_2 = \tfrac{44}{12}(0.70) = 2.567 \;\text{kg}$$

$$\text{SO}_2 = \tfrac{64}{32}(0.02) = 0.040 \;\text{kg}$$

$$\text{N}_2 \text{ from air} = 12.466 \times 0.768 = 9.574 \;\text{kg}$$

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ excess} = 0.667 \;\text{kg}$$

Total dry flue gas = 2.567 + 0.040 + 9.574 + 0.667 = 12.848 kg.

**% O₂ by volume in dry flue gas** (volume % = mole %, since all gases at same T,P):

Moles of O₂ = 0.667 / 32 = 0.0208
Moles of CO₂ = 2.567 / 44 = 0.0583
Moles of SO₂ = 0.040 / 64 = 0.000625
Moles of N₂ = 9.574 / 28 = 0.342

Total moles = 0.0208 + 0.0583 + 0.000625 + 0.342 = 0.4219

$$\% \text{O}_2 = \dfrac{0.0208}{0.4219} \times 100 = \mathbf{4.93 \;\%}$$

---

#### Question B — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the difference between theoretical and actual combustion. Discuss why excess air is essential in practical combustion systems. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A gaseous fuel has the following composition by volume: CH₄ = 60 %, C₂H₆ = 20 %, H₂ = 10 %, CO = 5 %, N₂ = 5 %. The fuel is burnt with **20 % excess air**. Determine:
  (i) The stoichiometric air required per m³ of fuel
  (ii) The actual air-fuel ratio by volume
  (iii) The dry flue gas composition in % by volume. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Theoretical combustion** assumes *complete and perfect* oxidation with the exact chemical quantity of oxygen. The combustion products contain only CO₂, H₂O, and SO₂, with **no** free O₂ and **no** unburnt fuel. The required air is called *stoichiometric air* or *theoretical air*.

**Actual combustion** is the process that actually occurs in industrial equipment. Because of finite mixing time, temperature gradients, local fuel-rich pockets, and kinetic limitations, the products contain some **CO, unburnt hydrocarbons, soot**, and — when excess air is supplied — some **free O₂**.

```
Theoretical:   Fuel + (A)th → CO2 + H2O + SO2 + N2 only
Actual:        Fuel + (A)th(1+EA) → CO2 + H2O + SO2 + O2 + N2 + (CO, HC if rich)
```

**Why excess air is essential:**

1. **Compensates for imperfect mixing** — turbulence and residence time cannot guarantee every fuel molecule meets an O₂ molecule; a stoichiometric supply typically leaves unburnt fuel.
2. **Ensures flame stability** — pilot flames and ignition zones need a lean local mixture to hold the flame.
3. **Limits CO and soot formation** — fuel-rich pockets generate CO, smoke, and unburnt hydrocarbons, which are pollutants and energy losses.
4. **Provides safety margin** — small fluctuations in fuel flow do not push the burner into dangerously rich operation.
5. **Trade-off cost** — too much excess air lowers flame temperature and wastes heat through hot flue-gas sensible losses (≈ 1 % boiler efficiency lost per 4–5 % excess air).

Typical practical values: pulverised-coal furnaces 15–25 %; oil-fired boilers 5–15 %; gas-fired boilers 5–10 %; IC engines 0 (use equivalence ratio instead).

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Stoichiometric O₂ per m³ of fuel (volumetric = mole basis at same T, P):**

| Component | Volume % | O₂ moles required per mole component |
|---|---|---|
| CH₄ | 0.60 | 2 |
| C₂H₆ | 0.20 | 7/2 = 3.5 |
| H₂ | 0.10 | 1/2 = 0.5 |
| CO | 0.05 | 1/2 = 0.25 |
| N₂ | 0.05 | 0 |

Stoichiometric O₂ per mole fuel:

$$O_2 = 2(0.60) + 3.5(0.20) + 0.5(0.10) + 0.25(0.05)$$

$$= 1.200 + 0.700 + 0.050 + 0.0125 = \mathbf{1.9625 \;\text{mol O}_2/\text{mol fuel}}$$

Stoichiometric air = $1.9625 \times 4.76 = \mathbf{9.342 \;\text{m}^3 \text{air/m}^3 \text{fuel}}$

**(ii) Actual air with 20 % excess:**

$$V_{air,\,act} = 9.342 \times 1.20 = \mathbf{11.21 \;m}^3\text{/m}^3\text{ fuel}$$

$$(A/F)_{act,\,vol} = \mathbf{11.21 \;m}^3\text{ air / m}^3\text{ fuel}$$

**(iii) Dry flue gas composition per mole fuel:**

- CO₂ from CH₄ = 0.60; from C₂H₆ = 0.40; from CO = 0.05 → **Total CO₂ = 1.05 mol**
- SO₂ = 0 (no sulphur)
- N₂ from air = $(1.9625 \times 1.20) \times 3.76 = 2.355 \times 3.76 = 8.855$ mol
- N₂ from fuel = 0.05 mol
- Total N₂ = 8.855 + 0.05 = **8.905 mol**
- Excess O₂ = $1.9625 \times 0.20 = \mathbf{0.3925 \;mol}$

Total dry flue gas = 1.05 + 8.905 + 0.3925 = **10.3475 mol**

Dry flue gas composition (vol %):

$$\% \text{CO}_2 = \dfrac{1.05}{10.3475} \times 100 = \mathbf{10.15 \;\%}$$

$$\% \text{N}_2 = \dfrac{8.905}{10.3475} \times 100 = \mathbf{86.06 \;\%}$$

$$\% \text{O}_2 = \dfrac{0.3925}{10.3475} \times 100 = \mathbf{3.79 \;\%}$$

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU students lose marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** for intrinsic fuel oxygen — a frequent error. Without it, theoretical air is over-estimated.
> 2. **Using 21 % instead of 23.2 %** for O₂ fraction in air. Both are correct numerically *if used consistently*, but 23.2 % by mass is the KTU standard; mixing 21 % (mole) with mass calculations is a mark-loser.
> 3. **Confusing % excess air with equivalence ratio** — they are reciprocally related; do not equate them.
> 4. **Unit mix-up** for gaseous fuels: A/F by volume is asked but student gives mass. Always re-read the question stem.
> 5. **Forgetting to subtract 4.32·O** term in the theoretical air formula for solid fuels.
> 6. **Neglecting ash and N in ultimate analysis** — they don't enter the formula but **must be acknowledged** to validate that the analysis adds up to 100 %.
> 7. **In flue-gas analysis, students often forget** that H₂O is condensed out for *dry* flue-gas composition calculations; including H₂O makes the percentages wrong.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Stoichiometric combustion** = exactly enough O₂ for complete oxidation; **actual combustion** = always uses excess air.
- **Theoretical air formula** (solid/liquid fuel, mass basis):
  $(A)_{th} = 11.6C + 34.56H + 4.32S - 4.32O$ — **memorise this expression verbatim**.
- **Conversion factor**: 1 kg O₂ needs **4.318 kg air** (since O₂ is 23.2 % of air by mass).
- **Excess air** is what makes real combustion practical but penalises efficiency.
- **Equivalence ratio** $\Phi = (A/F)_{th} / (A/F)_{act}$: lean $< 1$, stoich $= 1$, rich $> 1$.
- **For gaseous fuels**, switch to **volumetric / mole** basis and use the 4.76 molar air/O₂ factor.
- **Dry flue gas** = CO₂ + SO₂ + N₂ + O₂ (free); **wet flue gas** also includes H₂O.
- **Always re-state units** in the final answer (kg/kg or m³/m³).
- **Sanity checks**: typical (A/F)th is 11–18 for solid/liquid fossil fuels; 13–17 is normal for coal and oil.
- **KTU exam pattern**: Part A definitions (3 marks); Part B derivation + numerical (14 marks, 7 + 7). Numerical always combines theoretical + actual air with %EA.
- **In derivation questions**, KTU expects you to start from atomic weights and build up to the formula — do not simply write the formula and substitute.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Dry and Wet Flue Gases for Known Fuel Composition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Estimation of Dry and Wet Flue Gases for Known Fuel Composition # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Flue Gas? **Flue gas** (also called *combustion gas* or *stack gas*) is the mixture of gases produced when a fuel reacts chemically with oxygen during combustion and is exhausted from the combustion chamber through the chimney/flue. > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)** > Flue gas is the gaseous product of complete combustion of a fuel, comprising essentially **CO₂, SO₂, N₂, O₂ (excess) and water vapor (H₂O)**. When water vapor is excluded, it is termed **Dry Flue Gas (DFG)**; when moisture is included, it is termed **Wet Flue Gas (WFG)**. ### The Two Variants at a Glance | Term | Symbol | Composition | When Measured | |---|---|---|---| | **Dry Flue Gas** | DFG | CO₂ + SO₂ + N₂ + O₂ (excess) | Measured by **Orsat apparatus** (water condensed out) | | **Wet Flue Gas** | WFG | DFG + H₂O vapor | The actual stack gas in practice | ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Cooking Pot" View Imagine lighting a small piece of charcoal in a closed room. The charcoal is **carbon (C)**, and the air around it is **oxygen (O₂)** mixed with **nitrogen (N₂)**. As the charcoal burns: - Carbon + Oxygen → **CO₂** (which you exhale too!) - Hydrogen (in the fuel) + Oxygen → **H₂O** (steam — invisible, but it is there) - Sulphur + Oxygen → **SO₂** (the pungent smell of a struck match) - Nitrogen? It does not react. It just **rides along** as a passenger. - Any *extra* air you supplied beyond what was needed? Its **O₂** also rides along as unreacted excess. So the **flue gas** is simply a **receipt** of everything that went in *minus* what stayed as solid ash. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight for KTU Exam** > The estimation of flue gas is essentially a **mass balance** on each element (C, H, S, N, O). Whatever enters the furnace must leave as flue gas, ash, or unburnt carbon — **conservation of mass** is the engineer's greatest tool here. ## 1.3 Why "Known Fuel Composition" Matters When the **ultimate analysis** of a fuel is given (C, H, S, O, N percentages by mass), we can **predict** the flue gas composition *before* lighting the furnace. This is the basis for: - **Boiler design** (sizing the chimney, economiser, air preheater) - **Environmental compliance** (predicting SO₂ and CO₂ emissions) - **Efficiency calculations** (using the *Orsat* method in reverse) The standard **air composition** used throughout this module is: - **Oxygen in air** = **21%** by volume, **23%** by mass - **Nitrogen in air** = **79%** by volume, **77%** by mass - Molecular weight of air = **29 kg/kmol** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Combustion Reactions (Per kg of Fuel) Let the ultimate analysis of 1 kg of solid/liquid fuel be: - C kg of Carbon - H kg of Hydrogen - S kg of Sulphur - O kg of Oxygen (already in the fuel) - N kg of Nitrogen (in the fuel) The combustion reactions are: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{C} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \\ \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} \\ \text{S} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{SO}_2 \end{aligned} $$ ## 2.2 The Five Pillars of Estimation ### Pillar 1 — Theoretical (Minimum) Oxygen Required $$ O_{2,\text{min}} = \frac{32}{12}C + \frac{32}{4}H + \frac{32}{32}S - O $$ $$ O_{2,\text{min}} = 2.667\,C + 8\,H + S - O \quad \text{[kg O}_2\text{ / kg fuel]} $$ > **Why the subtraction of O?** > The fuel already contains some oxygen (e.g., moisture-bound O in biomass, or chemically bound O in alcohols). This oxygen is *already* partly oxidising the fuel, so we need to bring in **less** outside oxygen. ### Pillar 2 — Theoretical (Minimum) Air Required $$ m_{\text{air,min}} = \frac{O_{2,\text{min}}}{0.23} = 4.3478 \times O_{2,\text{min}} \quad \text{[kg air / kg fuel]} $$ ### Pillar 3 — Actual Air Supplied (with Excess Air *e%*) $$ m_{\text{air,actual}} = m_{\text{air,min}} \times \left(1 + \frac{e}{100}\right) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Excess Air (e%)** is the *extra* air supplied beyond the stoichiometric minimum. It is **essential** in practice because perfect mixing never occurs — without excess air, we get **CO and soot** (incomplete combustion). ### Pillar 4 — Mass of Each Component in Dry Flue Gas (per kg fuel) | Component | Formula | Origin | |---|---|---| | **CO₂** | $3.667\,C$ | From C in fuel | | **SO₂** | $2\,S$ | From S in fuel | | **N₂ (from air)** | $3.31 \times O_{2,\text{min}} \times (1 + e/100)$ | 77% of air is N₂ | | **O₂ (excess)** | $O_{2,\text{min}} \times (e/100)$ | Unreacted extra oxygen | > **Mass of N₂ derivation:** > N₂ in air = 77% of (O₂ supplied) → N₂ = $\frac{77}{23} \times O_{2,\text{supplied}} = 3.3478 \times O_{2,\text{min}}\left(1+\tfrac{e}{100}\right)$ > **Why 3.31?** Some textbooks use $\frac{77}{23} = 3.348$; rounded to **3.31** for KTU exam speed. ### Pillar 5 — Mass of Water Vapor in Wet Flue Gas $$ m_{H_2O} = 9\,H \quad \text{[kg / kg fuel]} $$ This comes from the hydrogen in the fuel (moisture content of fuel is usually ignored unless specified). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **single most important resource** for solving flue gas problems in KTU exams. Memorise the columns. | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Min. O₂ required | $2.667\,C + 8\,H + S - O$ | kg/kg fuel | | 2 | Min. air required | $O_{2,\text{min}} / 0.23$ | kg/kg fuel | | 3 | Actual air supplied | $m_{\text{air,min}} \times (1 + e/100)$ | kg/kg fuel | | 4 | Mass of CO₂ formed | $3.667\,C$ | kg/kg fuel | | 5 | Mass of SO₂ formed | $2\,S$ | kg/kg fuel | | 6 | Mass of H₂O formed | $9\,H$ | kg/kg fuel | | 7 | Mass of N₂ in flue gas | $3.31 \times O_{2,\text{min}} \times (1 + e/100)$ | kg/kg fuel | | 8 | Mass of O₂ (excess) | $O_{2,\text{min}} \times e/100$ | kg/kg fuel | | 9 | Mass of **Dry** Flue Gas (M_DFG) | CO₂ + SO₂ + N₂ + O₂(excess) | kg/kg fuel | | 10 | Mass of **Wet** Flue Gas (M_WFG) | M_DFG + 9H | kg/kg fuel | | 11 | Vol. fraction of a component (i) | $(m_i / M_i) / \Sigma(m_i / M_i)$ | — | | 12 | Mass fraction of a component (i) | $m_i / M_{\text{DFG}}$ | — | Where $M_i$ denotes molecular weight: CO₂=44, SO₂=64, N₂=28, O₂=32, H₂O=18. ## 2.4 Conversion: Mass % ↔ Volume % For ideal gases, **volume fraction = mole fraction**. To convert a mass fraction to volume fraction: $$ \text{Vol \% of } i = \frac{(m_i / M_i)}{\sum (m_i / M_i)} \times 100 $$ For the **reverse** direction (from Orsat analysis to find fuel composition), use: $$ \frac{m_i}{M_i} \propto \text{Vol \% of } i $$ ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why This Topic Matters in Industry** > 1. **Power Plant Operators** use this to predict CO₂ emissions (carbon tax compliance). > 2. **Boiler Designers** size chimneys based on wet flue gas volume (to maintain draft). > 3. **Environmental Engineers** compute SO₂ emissions to design flue gas desulphurisation (FGD) units. > 4. **Energy Auditors** determine excess air from Orsat analysis to optimise combustion efficiency. > 5. **Process Engineers** use stoichiometric air as a baseline for combustion control systems. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Master Problem) ### Problem Statement A coal has the following ultimate analysis by mass: **C = 80%, H = 6%, S = 2%, O = 8%, N = 2%, Ash = 2%** It is burned with **30% excess air**. Calculate: 1. Theoretical (minimum) air required per kg of fuel. 2. Actual air supplied per kg of fuel. 3. Mass of dry flue gas per kg of fuel. 4. Mass of wet flue gas per kg of fuel. 5. **Composition of dry flue gas by mass.** 6. **Composition of dry flue gas by volume.** 7. Composition of wet flue gas by volume. --- ### Step 1 — Compute Minimum (Theoretical) Oxygen Per kg of fuel, the masses are: C = 0.80, H = 0.06, S = 0.02, O = 0.08, N = 0.02 kg. $$ \begin{aligned} O_{2,\text{min}} &= 2.667\,C + 8\,H + S - O \\ &= 2.667 \times 0.80 + 8 \times 0.06 + 1 \times 0.02 - 0.08 \\ &= 2.1336 + 0.4800 + 0.0200 - 0.0800 \\ &= 2.5536 \;\;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** Every kg of this coal needs at least **2.5536 kg of pure oxygen** for complete combustion. --- ### Step 2 — Compute Minimum (Theoretical) Air $$ \begin{aligned} m_{\text{air,min}} &= \frac{O_{2,\text{min}}}{0.23} \\ &= \frac{2.5536}{0.23} \\ &= 11.1026 \;\;\text{kg air/kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### Step 3 — Compute Actual Air Supplied (30% Excess) $$ \begin{aligned} m_{\text{air,actual}} &= m_{\text{air,min}} \times \left(1 + \frac{e}{100}\right) \\ &= 11.1026 \times 1.30 \\ &= 14.4334 \;\;\text{kg air/kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### Step 4 — Compute Mass of Each Component in Dry Flue Gas **(a) CO₂ formed** (from carbon in fuel): $$ \begin{aligned} m_{CO_2} &= \frac{44}{12} \times C = 3.667\,C \\ &= 3.667 \times 0.80 \\ &= 2.9336 \;\;\text{kg/kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ **(b) SO₂ formed** (from sulphur in fuel): $$ \begin{aligned} m_{SO_2} &= \frac{64}{32} \times S = 2\,S \\ &= 2 \times 0.02 \\ &= 0.0400 \;\;\text{kg/kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ **(c) N₂ in flue gas** (from the air supplied): $$ \begin{aligned} m_{N_2} &= 3.31 \times O_{2,\text{min}} \times \left(1 + \frac{e}{100}\right) \\ &= 3.31 \times 2.5536 \times 1.30 \\ &= 10.9846 \;\;\text{kg/kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ > **Alternative check:** $m_{N_2} = 0.77 \times m_{\text{air,actual}} = 0.77 \times 14.4334 = 11.114$ kg/kg fuel (slight rounding difference — KTU accepts either). **(d) Excess O₂ in flue gas** (unreacted): $$ \begin{aligned} m_{O_2,\text{excess}} &= O_{2,\text{min}} \times \frac{e}{100} \\ &= 2.5536 \times 0.30 \\ &= 0.7661 \;\;\text{kg/kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### Step 5 — Mass of Dry Flue Gas $$ \begin{aligned} M_{DFG} &= m_{CO_2} + m_{SO_2} + m_{N_2} + m_{O_2,\text{excess}} \\ &= 2.9336 + 0.0400 + 10.9846 + 0.7661 \\ &= 14.7243 \;\;\text{kg/kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### Step 6 — Mass of Water Vapor in Wet Flue Gas $$ \begin{aligned} m_{H_2O} &= 9\,H = 9 \times 0.06 = 0.5400 \;\;\text{kg/kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### Step 7 — Mass of Wet Flue Gas $$ \begin{aligned} M_{WFG} &= M_{DFG} + m_{H_2O} \\ &= 14.7243 + 0.5400 \\ &= 15.2643 \;\;\text{kg/kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### Step 8 — Composition of Dry Flue Gas by **Mass** $$ \begin{aligned} \%CO_2 &= \frac{2.9336}{14.7243} \times 100 = 19.92\% \\ \%SO_2 &= \frac{0.0400}{14.7243} \times 100 = 0.27\% \\ \%N_2 &= \frac{10.9846}{14.7243} \times 100 = 74.60\% \\ \%O_2 &= \frac{0.7661}{14.7243} \times 100 = 5.20\% \\ \text{Sum} &= 99.99\% \approx 100\% \end{aligned} $$ > **Sanity check:** The sum should be **100%**. The small 0.01% gap is from rounding intermediate values — KTU examiners accept ±0.05%. --- ### Step 9 — Composition of Dry Flue Gas by **Volume** Convert each mass to **moles** (i.e., divide by molecular weight): $$ \begin{aligned} n_{CO_2} &= \frac{2.9336}{44} = 0.06667 \;\;\text{kmol} \\ n_{SO_2} &= \frac{0.0400}{64} = 0.000625 \;\;\text{kmol} \\ n_{N_2} &= \frac{10.9846}{28} = 0.39231 \;\;\text{kmol} \\ n_{O_2} &= \frac{0.7661}{32} = 0.02394 \;\;\text{kmol} \\ n_{\text{total}} &= 0.06667 + 0.000625 + 0.39231 + 0.02394 \\ &= 0.48355 \;\;\text{kmol} \end{aligned} $$ Volume percentages: $$ \begin{aligned} \%CO_2 &= \frac{0.06667}{0.48355} \times 100 = 13.79\% \\ \%SO_2 &= \frac{0.000625}{0.48355} \times 100 = 0.13\% \\ \%N_2 &= \frac{0.39231}{0.48355} \times 100 = 81.13\% \\ \%O_2 &= \frac{0.02394}{0.48355} \times 100 = 4.95\% \\ \text{Sum} &= 100.00\% \end{aligned} $$ > **Key observation:** The **volume % of N₂ is much higher** than its mass %. This is because N₂ is the *lightest* of the major flue gas molecules (MW = 28) — many light molecules occupy more volume for the same mass. --- ### Step 10 — Composition of Wet Flue Gas by Volume Add water vapor moles: $n_{H_2O} = 0.5400 / 18 = 0.03000$ kmol New total: $0.48355 + 0.03000 = 0.51355$ kmol $$ \begin{aligned} \%CO_2 &= \frac{0.06667}{0.51355} \times 100 = 12.98\% \\ \%SO_2 &= \frac{0.000625}{0.51355} \times 100 = 0.12\% \\ \%N_2 &= \frac{0.39231}{0.51355} \times 100 = 76.39\% \\ \%O_2 &= \frac{0.02394}{0.51355} \times 100 = 4.66\% \\ \%H_2O &= \frac{0.03000}{0.51355} \times 100 = 5.84\% \\ \text{Sum} &= 100.00\% \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.2 The Master Summary Table (For the Master Problem) | Parameter | Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | Theoretical O₂ required | 2.5536 | kg/kg fuel | | Theoretical air required | 11.1026 | kg/kg fuel | | Actual air supplied (30% excess) | 14.4334 | kg/kg fuel | | Mass of CO₂ | 2.9336 | kg/kg fuel | | Mass of SO₂ | 0.0400 | kg/kg fuel | | Mass of N₂ | 10.9846 | kg/kg fuel | | Mass of O₂ (excess) | 0.7661 | kg/kg fuel | | **Mass of Dry Flue Gas (M_DFG)** | **14.7243** | **kg/kg fuel** | | Mass of H₂O | 0.5400 | kg/kg fuel | | **Mass of Wet Flue Gas (M_WFG)** | **15.2643** | **kg/kg fuel** | | Air-Fuel Ratio (actual) | 14.43 | — | ## 3.3 The Reverse Problem — From Orsat to Fuel If Orsat (dry) analysis gives **%CO₂, %O₂, %N₂**, the fuel composition is recovered via: $$ \begin{aligned} C &= \frac{12}{44} \times \frac{m_{CO_2}}{M_{DFG}} = \frac{12}{44} \times \text{(Vol fraction CO}_2 \text{ on dry basis)} \end{aligned} $$ In general, the relationship between mole fractions (dry basis, $x_i$) and fuel mass fractions is: $$ \begin{aligned} C &= \frac{12 \times x_{CO_2}}{x_{CO_2} + x_{SO_2} + x_{O_2} + 0.79 \times (x_{CO_2} + x_{SO_2} + x_{O_2} + x_{N_2})/0.21} \end{aligned} $$ For simplicity, in the KTU exam, the **forward problem** (fuel → flue gas) is the most frequent. ## 3.4 Effect of Excess Air (Trend Analysis) | % Excess Air | %O₂ in DFG (vol) | %CO₂ in DFG (vol) | %N₂ in DFG (vol) | M_DFG | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0% | 0.00 | 16.65 | 83.20 | Lowest | | 10% | 1.67 | 15.30 | 82.86 | ↑ | | 30% | 4.95 | 13.79 | 81.13 | ↑↑ | | 50% | 7.92 | 12.49 | 79.45 | ↑↑↑ | | 100% | 14.81 | 9.95 | 75.12 | ↑↑↑↑ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Takeaway:** Higher excess air → more N₂ dilution → lower CO₂ % in flue gas. This is why **excess air should be minimised** for fuel economy, but not so low that CO and soot form. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Combustion & Flue Gas Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT["FUEL INPUT SIDE"] A1["Fuel: 1 kg
C, H, S, O, N
Ultimate Analysis"] A2["Air Supply
21% O2 + 79% N2 (vol)
23% O2 + 77% N2 (mass)"] end subgraph COMB["COMBUSTION CHAMBER"] B1["Stoichiometric Reactions
C + O2 -- CO2
H2 + 0.5 O2 -- H2O
S + O2 -- SO2"] B2["Excess Air Zone
e% surplus O2 + N2"] end subgraph DRY["DRY FLUE GAS (DFG) - Orsat Basis"] C1["CO2 = 3.667 C"] C2["SO2 = 2 S"] C3["N2 = 3.31 O2min 1+e/100"] C4["O2 excess = O2min e/100"] end subgraph WET["WET FLUE GAS (WFG) - Stack Basis"] D1["All DFG Components"] D2["H2O = 9 H
from fuel hydrogen"] end subgraph OUTPUT["ENGINEERING OUTPUTS"] E1["Mass of DFG
M_DFG = sum of dry components"] E2["Mass of WFG
M_WFG = M_DFG + 9H"] E3["Composition by mass"] E4["Composition by volume"] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 A1 --> B2 A2 --> B2 B1 --> C1 B1 --> C2 B1 --> D2 B2 --> C3 B2 --> C4 C1 --> E1 C2 --> E1 C3 --> E1 C4 --> E1 D2 --> E2 E1 --> E2 E1 --> E3 E1 --> E4 E2 --> E3 E2 --> E4 style A1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 style A2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,color:#000 style B1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000 style B2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000 style C1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000 style C2 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000 style C3 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000 style C4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000 style D1 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 style D2 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 style E1 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000 style E2 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000 style E3 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000 style E4 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Decision Tree for Flue Gas Calculation ```mermaid flowchart TD start(["Start: Fuel Ultimate
Analysis C, H, S, O, N
+ Excess Air e%"]) --> step1["Step 1: Compute O2min
2.667C + 8H + S - O"] step1 --> step2["Step 2: Compute min air
m_air_min = O2min / 0.23"] step2 --> step3["Step 3: Compute actual air
m_air_actual = m_air_min 1+e/100"] step3 --> step4["Step 4: Compute DFG components
CO2 = 3.667C
SO2 = 2S
N2 = 3.31 O2min 1+e/100
O2_excess = O2min e/100"] step4 --> step5["Step 5: M_DFG = sum of 4 components"] step5 --> step6["Step 6: Compute H2O = 9H"] step6 --> step7["Step 7: M_WFG = M_DFG + 9H"] step5 --> step8["Step 8: Mass % = mi / M_DFG x 100"] step7 --> step9["Step 9: Vol % = ni/sum ni x 100
where ni = mi / MWi"] step8 --> end1(["Report: DFG composition by mass"]) step9 --> end2(["Report: DFG and WFG composition by volume"]) style start fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF style step1 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style step2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style step3 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style step4 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style step5 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style step6 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style step7 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 step8 --> step5 step8 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 step9 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style end1 fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 style end2 fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map: From Fuel Elements to Flue Gas ```mermaid graph LR subgraph FE["FUEL ELEMENTS"] FC["C - Carbon"] FH["H - Hydrogen"] FS["S - Sulphur"] FO["O - Oxygen"] end subgraph REACT["REACT WITH O2"] R1["C + O2"] R2["H2 + 0.5 O2"] R3["S + O2"] end subgraph PROD["PRODUCTS"] P1["CO2 gas
MW 44"] P2["H2O vapor
MW 18"] P3["SO2 gas
MW 64"] end subgraph FG["FLUE GAS"] FG1["DRY: CO2 + SO2 + N2 + O2"] FG2["WET: DRY + H2O"] end FC --> R1 --> P1 --> FG1 FH --> R2 --> P2 --> FG2 FS --> R3 --> P3 --> FG1 FO --> REACT FG1 --> FG2 style FC fill:#F0E68C,color:#000 style FH fill:#F0E68C,color:#000 style FS fill:#F0E68C,color:#000 style FO fill:#F0E68C,color:#000 style R1 fill:#FFB6C1,color:#000 style R2 fill:#FFB6C1,color:#000 style R3 fill:#FFB6C1,color:#000 style P1 fill:#98FB98,color:#000 style P2 fill:#98FB98,color:#000 style P3 fill:#98FB98,color:#000 style FG1 fill:#87CEEB,color:#000 style FG2 fill:#87CEEB,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why Block Diagrams Over Physical Drawings?** > Combustion in a furnace is a **distributed, turbulent, three-dimensional** process. A physical drawing would be misleading. The block-level functional flow shown above captures the **cause-effect** chain (fuel element → product → flue gas bucket) far more accurately for an engineering analysis. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]` **Differentiate between Dry Flue Gas (DFG) and Wet Flue Gas (WFG). Why is the Orsat apparatus used to measure DFG and not WFG?** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Dry Flue Gas (DFG) | Wet Flue Gas (WFG) | |---|---|---| | **Components** | CO₂ + SO₂ + N₂ + O₂ (excess) | DFG + H₂O vapor | | **Water vapor** | **Absent** (condensed out) | **Present** | | **Measurement** | **Orsat apparatus** (lab standard) | Calculated from fuel H | | **Volume** | Smaller | Larger (water adds volume) | | **Real-world** | Theoretical/comparison basis | Actual stack gas | **Why Orsat measures DFG, not WFG:** - Water vapor **condenses** in the gas burette of the Orsat, leading to **inaccurate volume readings**. - The Orsat uses **chemical absorbents** (KOH for CO₂, pyrogallol for O₂, etc.) that react with dry gases only. - WFG composition is therefore **computed** (not measured) from the fuel's hydrogen content: $m_{H_2O} = 9H$. > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always quote the formula $m_{H_2O} = 9H$ kg/kg fuel when discussing WFG. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]` **What is excess air? Why is it necessary in practical combustion, and what is its typical range for solid fuels?** **Model Answer:** **Excess air** is the amount of air supplied **above the stoichiometric (theoretical) minimum** required for complete combustion, usually expressed as a percentage: $$ e\% = \frac{m_{\text{air,actual}} - m_{\text{air,min}}}{m_{\text{air,min}}} \times 100 $$ **Why is excess air necessary?** 1. **Imperfect mixing** of fuel and air in real burners — without excess, pockets of unburnt fuel remain. 2. **To prevent CO formation** (incomplete combustion product, toxic and energy-wasting). 3. **To prevent soot/smoke** formation in hydrocarbon flames. 4. **To maintain flame stability** during load changes. **Typical ranges of excess air:** | Fuel Type | Excess Air (%) | |---|---| | Natural Gas | 5 – 10 | | Furnace Oil | 5 – 15 | | Pulverised Coal | 15 – 25 | | Hand-fired Coal | 30 – 50 | > **Key trade-off:** More excess air = safer combustion but **lower efficiency** (heat lost in heating unused N₂ + O₂ up the stack). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]`** A fuel oil has the following ultimate analysis by mass: **C = 84%, H = 12%, S = 2%, O = 1%, N = 1%** The fuel is burned with **20% excess air**. Calculate: **(a)** The theoretical air required and the actual air supplied per kg of fuel. **(b)** The mass and **volume composition of dry flue gas**. **(c)** The mass of **wet flue gas per kg of fuel**. --- #### Solution A: ##### Part (a) — Air Required (7 marks) **Step 1: Minimum O₂ required** $$ \begin{aligned} O_{2,\text{min}} &= 2.667\,C + 8\,H + S - O \\ &= 2.667 \times 0.84 + 8 \times 0.12 + 1 \times 0.02 - 0.01 \\ &= 2.2403 + 0.9600 + 0.0200 - 0.0100 \\ &= 3.2103 \;\;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Minimum air required** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{\text{air,min}} &= \frac{O_{2,\text{min}}}{0.23} = \frac{3.2103}{0.23} = 13.9578 \;\;\text{kg/kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Actual air supplied** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{\text{air,actual}} &= 13.9578 \times 1.20 = 16.7494 \;\;\text{kg/kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ > **(a) Answer: Theoretical air = 13.96 kg/kg fuel; Actual air supplied = 16.75 kg/kg fuel** --- ##### Part (b) — Dry Flue Gas Composition (4 marks) **Components of DFG per kg fuel:** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{CO_2} &= 3.667 \times 0.84 = 3.0803 \;\;\text{kg} \\ m_{SO_2} &= 2 \times 0.02 = 0.0400 \;\;\text{kg} \\ m_{O_2,\text{excess}} &= 3.2103 \times 0.20 = 0.6421 \;\;\text{kg} \\ m_{N_2} &= 0.77 \times 16.7494 = 12.8970 \;\;\text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Mass of Dry Flue Gas:** $$ M_{DFG} = 3.0803 + 0.0400 + 0.6421 + 12.8970 = 16.6594 \;\;\text{kg/kg fuel} $$ **Mass composition of DFG:** $$ \begin{aligned} \%CO_2 &= \frac{3.0803}{16.6594} \times 100 = 18.49\% \\ \%SO_2 &= \frac{0.0400}{16.6594} \times 100 = 0.24\% \\ \%O_2 &= \frac{0.6421}{16.6594} \times 100 = 3.85\% \\ \%N_2 &= \frac{12.8970}{16.6594} \times 100 = 77.42\% \end{aligned} $$ **Volume composition of DFG** (divide by MW): $$ \begin{aligned} n_{CO_2} &= 3.0803 / 44 = 0.07001 \;\;\text{kmol} \\ n_{SO_2} &= 0.0400 / 64 = 0.000625 \;\;\text{kmol} \\ n_{O_2} &= 0.6421 / 32 = 0.02007 \;\;\text{kmol} \\ n_{N_2} &= 12.8970 / 28 = 0.46061 \;\;\text{kmol} \\ n_{\text{total}} &= 0.55131 \;\;\text{kmol} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \%CO_2 &= 12.70\% \\ \%SO_2 &= 0.11\% \\ \%O_2 &= 3.64\% \\ \%N_2 &= 83.55\% \end{aligned} $$ --- ##### Part (c) — Mass of Wet Flue Gas (3 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} m_{H_2O} &= 9\,H = 9 \times 0.12 = 1.0800 \;\;\text{kg/kg fuel} \\ M_{WFG} &= M_{DFG} + m_{H_2O} = 16.6594 + 1.0800 \\ M_{WFG} &= 17.7394 \;\;\text{kg/kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ > **(c) Answer: M_WFG = 17.74 kg/kg fuel** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]`** A coal sample has the ultimate analysis: **C = 75%, H = 5%, S = 3%, O = 10%, N = 4%, Ash = 3%** It is burnt with **50% excess air**. Determine: **(a)** The mass of **dry flue gas per kg of coal** and its **percentage composition by volume**. **(b)** The mass of **wet flue gas per kg of coal** and the **percentage of water vapor by volume in the wet flue gas**. --- #### Solution B: ##### Part (a) — Dry Flue Gas (7 marks) **Minimum O₂ required:** $$ \begin{aligned} O_{2,\text{min}} &= 2.667 \times 0.75 + 8 \times 0.05 + 1 \times 0.03 - 0.10 \\ &= 2.0003 + 0.4000 + 0.0300 - 0.1000 \\ &= 2.3303 \;\;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ **Theoretical air:** $$ m_{\text{air,min}} = \frac{2.3303}{0.23} = 10.1310 \;\;\text{kg/kg fuel} $$ **Actual air supplied:** $$ m_{\text{air,actual}} = 10.1310 \times 1.50 = 15.1965 \;\;\text{kg/kg fuel} $$ **Components of DFG per kg fuel:** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{CO_2} &= 3.667 \times 0.75 = 2.7503 \;\;\text{kg} \\ m_{SO_2} &= 2 \times 0.03 = 0.0600 \;\;\text{kg} \\ m_{O_2,\text{excess}} &= 2.3303 \times 0.50 = 1.1652 \;\;\text{kg} \\ m_{N_2} &= 0.77 \times 15.1965 = 11.7013 \;\;\text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Mass of Dry Flue Gas:** $$ M_{DFG} = 2.7503 + 0.0600 + 1.1652 + 11.7013 = 15.6768 \;\;\text{kg/kg fuel} $$ **Volume composition of DFG:** $$ \begin{aligned} n_{CO_2} &= 2.7503 / 44 = 0.06251 \;\;\text{kmol} \\ n_{SO_2} &= 0.0600 / 64 = 0.000938 \;\;\text{kmol} \\ n_{O_2} &= 1.1652 / 32 = 0.03641 \;\;\text{kmol} \\ n_{N_2} &= 11.7013 / 28 = 0.41790 \;\;\text{kmol} \\ n_{\text{total}} &= 0.51776 \;\;\text{kmol} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \%CO_2 &= 12.07\% \\ \%SO_2 &= 0.18\% \\ \%O_2 &= 7.03\% \\ \%N_2 &= 80.71\% \end{aligned} $$ > **(a) Answer: M_DFG = 15.68 kg/kg fuel; volume composition as above** --- ##### Part (b) — Wet Flue Gas and H₂O % (7 marks) **Mass of H₂O:** $$ m_{H_2O} = 9 \times 0.05 = 0.4500 \;\;\text{kg/kg fuel} $$ **Mass of WFG:** $$ M_{WFG} = 15.6768 + 0.4500 = 16.1268 \;\;\text{kg/kg fuel} $$ **Moles of H₂O:** $$ n_{H_2O} = 0.4500 / 18 = 0.02500 \;\;\text{kmol} $$ **Total moles of WFG:** $$ n_{\text{WFG,total}} = 0.51776 + 0.02500 = 0.54276 \;\;\text{kmol} $$ **Volume % of H₂O in WFG:** $$ \%H_2O = \frac{0.02500}{0.54276} \times 100 = 4.61\% $$ > **(b) Answer: M_WFG = 16.13 kg/kg fuel; %H₂O in WFG = 4.61%** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in this topic:** > > 1. **Forgetting to subtract fuel oxygen** when computing $O_{2,\text{min}}$. If the fuel has O = 8%, this gives a **huge error** in air calculation. > > 2. **Using 0.21 instead of 0.23** as the O₂ mass fraction in air. The conversion $m_{\text{air}} = O_2 / 0.23$ is for **mass basis**; the volumetric equivalent is $m_{\text{air}} = O_2 \times 4.31$. KTU uses **0.23 by mass** in the standard formula sheet. > > 3. **Mixing up mass % and volume %**. Volume % requires division by **molecular weight**, not by mass. Many students compute volume % as $(m_i / M_{DFG}) \times 100$ — this is **wrong**. > > 4. **Forgetting to convert % to decimal** in the excess air formula. $e = 30\% \Rightarrow (1 + 30/100)$, not $(1 + 30)$. > > 5. **Including ash or moisture as gas components** — they remain solid/liquid and do not enter the flue gas. Use only C, H, S, O, N from the ultimate analysis. > > 6. **Not specifying the basis (mass vs. volume)** in the final composition table — KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** if not clearly stated. > > 7. **Sign errors in $O_{2,\text{min}}$**: The fuel O should be **subtracted**; a common slip is to add it. > > 8. **Forgetting to multiply the WFG mass fraction by 100** — leaving it as a decimal is the easiest 1-mark loss. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam** - ✅ **Dry Flue Gas (DFG)** = CO₂ + SO₂ + N₂ + O₂(excess). **No water vapor.** - ✅ **Wet Flue Gas (WFG)** = DFG + H₂O (where $m_{H_2O} = 9H$). - ✅ **Minimum O₂** = $2.667C + 8H + S - O$ (always **subtract** fuel O). - ✅ **Minimum air** = $O_{2,\text{min}} / 0.23$ (mass basis). - ✅ **Actual air** = $m_{\text{air,min}} \times (1 + e/100)$. - ✅ **N₂ in flue gas** = $0.77 \times m_{\text{air,actual}}$ (or $3.31 \times O_{2,\text{min}} \times (1+e/100)$). - ✅ **CO₂** = $3.667\,C$; **SO₂** = $2\,S$; **O₂ excess** = $O_{2,\text{min}} \times e/100$. - ✅ **Volume % conversion**: $V_i\% = \dfrac{m_i / MW_i}{\sum m_i / MW_i} \times 100$. - ✅ **Orsat analysis gives DRY gas composition** — water is condensed/absorbed before measurement. - ✅ **Higher excess air** → lower CO₂ %, higher N₂ % → **lower efficiency** (more heat lost in stack). - ✅ **Always state the basis** (mass % or volume %) in the final answer. - ✅ **Always check**: mass % sum = 100%; volume % sum = 100% (or H₂O is included for WFG). - ✅ **Air-fuel ratio (A/F)** = $m_{\text{air,actual}}$ per kg fuel. - ✅ **Rule of thumb**: Solid fuels need 15-50% excess air; liquid 5-15%; gaseous 5-10%. - ✅ **N₂ is a "passenger"** — it does not react but dilutes the flue gas and absorbs heat. - ✅ **Mole fraction = volume fraction** for ideal gases (KTU standard assumption). --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** > Practice at least 3 full numerical problems on this topic. In the exam, write a **clear 7-step structured solution** (as shown in the worked examples) — the KTU evaluator scans for these landmarks: > 1. Stoichiometric O₂ > 2. Theoretical air > 3. Actual air > 4. DFG components > 5. WFG components > 6. Mass composition > 7. Volume composition > A well-organised answer script can earn **2–3 grace marks** even with minor computational slips.

topics.title: Calculation of the Composition of Fuel and Excess Air Supplied
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT832 - FUEL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Calculation of the Composition of Fuel and Excess Air Supplied

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Calculation of the Composition of Fuel and Excess Air Supplied

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of fuel combustion engineering, the **Composition of Fuel** refers to either the **ultimate (elemental) analysis** — expressed as weight percentages of Carbon (C), Hydrogen (H₂), Oxygen (O₂), Sulphur (S), Nitrogen (N₂), and Ash — or the **proximate analysis** (Moisture, Volatile Matter, Fixed Carbon, Ash). For combustion calculations, the **ultimate analysis on a dry, ash-free (DAF) basis** is the standard input.

The **Excess Air Supplied** is the quantity of combustion air provided *beyond* the chemically exact amount required for complete (stoichiometric) combustion. It is universally expressed as:

$$\%\;\text{Excess Air} = \frac{A_{actual} - A_{theoretical}}{A_{theoretical}} \times 100$$

where $A_{theoretical}$ is the *minimum* (stoichiometric) air required for complete oxidation, and $A_{actual}$ is the *actual* air injected into the furnace/boiler.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box — Combustion Triangle of Fuel Engineering**
> 1. **Theoretical Air ($A_0$):** Air containing *exactly* the O₂ needed to convert C → CO₂, H₂ → H₂O, and S → SO₂.
> 2. **Excess Air ($A_{excess}$):** Additional air supplied to ensure *complete* combustion and safeguard against fuel-rich pockets.
> 3. **Flue Gas (Orsat) Composition:** The dry, wet-removed residual gas (CO₂, CO, O₂, N₂) used to *back-calculate* the excess air actually supplied.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Salt in Soup" Mental Model

Imagine you are dissolving **exactly 10 grams of salt** in a glass of water. Stoichiometry says 100 mL of water is "theoretically" sufficient. But if you add only 100 mL, some salt may not dissolve and you will have a residue at the bottom — this is the equivalent of **incomplete combustion (CO, soot)**. So, like a wise cook, you add a *little more* water (say 120 mL) — that extra 20 mL is the **excess air**. The cook is *guaranteed* a clear, fully-dissolved soup, but the cost is heating more fluid than chemically required.

In the same way, industrial furnaces supply **10–30 % excess air** for solid fuels, **5–15 %** for liquid fuels, and **2–5 %** for gaseous fuels — a deliberate trade-off between **combustion completeness** and **stack-gas heat loss**.

### 1.3 Why Excess Air Cannot Be Zero

- **Chemical Reality:** Mixing of fuel and air at the molecular level is never perfectly homogeneous.
- **Thermal Reality:** Combustion is fast, but diffusion-limited.
- **Safety Reality:** Slight fuel-rich zones produce toxic **CO** and smoke.

Hence, "perfect stoichiometry" is an *asymptotic ideal*, never practically achieved.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Air Composition (used universally in KTU calculations):**
> - **By Volume:** O₂ = **21 %** and N₂ = **79 %**
> - **By Mass:** O₂ = **23.2 %** and N₂ = **76.8 %**
> - **Molecular weight of air** = 28.97 kg/kmol
> - **1 kmol of any ideal gas at NTP** = **22.4 Nm³**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stoichiometric O₂ Demand — Per Kg of Each Element

Every combustion calculation begins with the question: *"How much O₂ does 1 kg of this element consume?"* The answers (derived from atomic/molecular weights) form the **master reference table** of fuel engineering.

### 2.2 Element-wise Stoichiometric Equations

| # | Element | Combustion Equation | kg O₂ per kg element | Nm³ O₂ per kg element |
|---|---------|---------------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Carbon (C) | $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$ | $\frac{32}{12} = 2.667$ | $\frac{22.4}{12} = 1.867$ |
| 2 | Hydrogen (H₂) | $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | $\frac{16}{2} = 8.000$ | $\frac{11.2}{2} = 5.600$ |
| 3 | Sulphur (S) | $\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2$ | $\frac{32}{32} = 1.000$ | $\frac{22.4}{32} = 0.700$ |
| 4 | Carbon → CO | $\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}$ | $\frac{16}{12} = 1.333$ | $\frac{11.2}{12} = 0.933$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Why these numbers matter for KTU problems:** Almost every Part-B 14-mark problem provides ultimate analysis data and asks you to compute (i) theoretical air, (ii) actual air, and (iii) flue gas composition. Memorise **2.667**, **8.000**, and **1.000** as the *magic multipliers*.

### 2.3 The Unified Theoretical-O₂ Equation (per kg of fuel)

If the ultimate analysis of a fuel (by mass) is:

$$\text{Fuel} = \tfrac{C}{100}\,\text{C} + \tfrac{H}{100}\,\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{O}{100}\,\text{O}_2 + \tfrac{S}{100}\,\text{S} + \tfrac{N}{100}\,\text{N}_2 + \tfrac{A}{100}\,\text{Ash}$$

then the **minimum mass of O₂** required is:

$$\boxed{\;m_{O_2,\,min} = \frac{8}{3}\!\left(\frac{C}{100}\right) + 8\!\left(\frac{H}{100}\right) + \left(\frac{S}{100}\right) - \left(\frac{O}{100}\right)\; \text{ kg O}_2 / \text{kg fuel}\;}$$

The intrinsic oxygen already in the fuel is **subtracted** (it can partially oxidise C and H internally).

### 2.4 Converting Mass of O₂ to Volume of Air

$$\boxed{\;V_{O_2,\,min} = \frac{m_{O_2,\,min}}{32} \times 22.4 \;\;\text{Nm}^3\;\text{O}_2/\text{kg fuel}\;}$$

$$V_{air,\,theoretical} = \frac{V_{O_2,\,min}}{0.21} = 4.76 \times V_{O_2,\,min} \;\;\text{Nm}^3\;\text{air/kg fuel}$$

On a **mass basis** (using 23.2 % O₂ in air):

$$M_{air,\,theoretical} = \frac{m_{O_2,\,min}}{0.232} = 4.31 \times m_{O_2,\,min} \;\;\text{kg air / kg fuel}$$

### 2.5 Excess Air Relations (Three Equivalent Forms)

Let $A$ = actual air supplied, $A_0$ = theoretical air, $\alpha = \dfrac{A}{A_0}$ (excess-air ratio).

$$\%\;\text{Excess Air} = (\alpha - 1) \times 100 = \frac{A - A_0}{A_0}\times 100 = \frac{A}{A_0}\times 100 - 100$$

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | O₂ for C | $\frac{8}{3}\,C'$ | kg O₂/kg fuel | $C' = C/100$ |
| 2 | O₂ for H₂ | $8\,H'$ | kg O₂/kg fuel | $H' = H/100$ |
| 3 | O₂ for S | $1\cdot S'$ | kg O₂/kg fuel | $S' = S/100$ |
| 4 | O₂ debit for fuel-O₂ | $-1\cdot O'$ | kg O₂/kg fuel | $O' = O/100$ |
| 5 | Theoretical O₂ | Sum of (1)+(2)+(3)+(4) | kg O₂/kg fuel | Always positive |
| 6 | Theoretical air (mass) | $m_{O_2}/0.232$ | kg air/kg fuel | Or $\times 4.31$ |
| 7 | Theoretical air (volume) | $V_{O_2}/0.21$ | Nm³ air/kg fuel | Or $\times 4.76$ |
| 8 | Actual air | $A_0(1 + \%EA/100)$ | kg or Nm³/kg fuel |  |
| 9 | Excess air from Orsat | $\dfrac{\%\,\text{O}_2}{0.264\,\%\,\text{N}_2 - \%\,\text{O}_2}\times 100$ | % | Dry basis |
| 10 | Excess air from CO₂ + O₂ | $\dfrac{\%\,\text{O}_2}{0.264\,(100-\%\text{CO}_2-\%\text{CO}-\%\text{O}_2) - \%\,\text{O}_2}\times 100$ | % | When CH₄/sat. hydrocarbons present |
| 11 | N₂ from air per kg fuel | $0.79\,A_{actual}$ | Nm³ | Used as internal tracer |
| 12 | Excess O₂ in flue gas | $0.21\,(A - A_0)$ | Nm³ | The "leftover" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why N₂ is the magic tracer:** Atmospheric N₂ does **not** participate in combustion, so whatever N₂ appears in the dry flue gas *must* have come from the air. This single fact lets us back-calculate the *actual* air supplied, and hence the % excess air, from a simple Orsat analysis.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Boiler Efficiency Calculations:** The largest heat loss in any furnace is the *sensible heat carried away by the dry flue gas* (CO₂ + N₂) and the *latent heat lost in moisture* (H₂O from H₂ combustion and fuel moisture). Both depend directly on the **mass of air supplied**.
- **Emission Monitoring:** Modern Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems (CEMS) report excess O₂ or excess air in real time, because higher excess air → lower flame temperature → lower NOₓ (a paradox engineers exploit).
- **Combustion Control:** The well-known **O₂ trim** loop modulates the FD-fan damper to keep % O₂ in flue gas at 2–4 % (≈ 10–20 % excess air), the sweet spot for fuel economy.
