# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Solutions

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass of O₂ Required per kg of Carbon (C + O₂ → CO₂)

We start from the balanced chemical equation:

$$\text{C}_{(s)} + \text{O}_{2\,(g)} \longrightarrow \text{CO}_{2\,(g)}$$

Reading off molecular masses: 1 kmol C (= 12 kg) reacts with 1 kmol O₂ (= 32 kg).

$$\text{Mass ratio} = \frac{m_{O_2}}{m_C} = \frac{32 \text{ kg}}{12 \text{ kg}} = 2.667 \;\frac{\text{kg O}_2}{\text{kg C}}$$

In volume (at NTP, 22.4 Nm³ per kmol):

$$\text{Volume ratio} = \frac{22.4 \text{ Nm}^3\,\text{O}_2}{12 \text{ kg C}} = 1.867 \;\frac{\text{Nm}^3\,\text{O}_2}{\text{kg C}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass of O₂ Required per kg of Hydrogen

$$2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Per 2 kmol H₂ (4 kg) we need 1 kmol O₂ (32 kg).

$$\text{Mass ratio} = \frac{32}{4} = 8.00 \;\frac{\text{kg O}_2}{\text{kg H}_2}$$

Volume basis: 2 kmol H₂ = 2 × 22.4 Nm³ → consumes 1 kmol O₂ = 22.4 Nm³.

$$\text{Volume ratio} = \frac{22.4 \text{ Nm}^3}{4 \text{ kg H}_2} = 5.60 \;\frac{\text{Nm}^3\,\text{O}_2}{\text{kg H}_2}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — % Excess Air from Orsat (Dry Flue-Gas) Analysis

**GIVEN:** Dry flue gas composition by volume = %CO₂ + %CO + %O₂ + %N₂ = 100 %.

**STEP A — N₂ balance (the cornerstone identity).**

The N₂ in dry flue gas originates *entirely* from the supply air. Per kg of fuel:

$$N_{2,\,flue} = 0.79 \times A_{actual} \quad (\text{Nm}^3)$$

**STEP B — O₂ balance.**

The actual O₂ entering with the air = $0.21\,A_{actual}$. The O₂ consumed in combustion = $0.21\,A_0$. The *unreacted* O₂ appearing in dry flue gas is:

$$O_{2,\,flue} = 0.21\,(A_{actual} - A_0) = 0.21\,A_{excess} \quad (\text{Nm}^3)$$

**STEP C — Forming the ratio.**

$$\frac{O_{2,\,flue}}{N_{2,\,flue}} = \frac{0.21\,(A - A_0)}{0.79\,A}$$

But $\%\text{Excess Air} = \dfrac{A - A_0}{A_0}\times 100$, so we need a relation with $A$ on both sides. Substituting $A_0 = A - \dfrac{O_{2,\,flue}}{0.21}$:

$$\%\text{EA} = \frac{O_{2,\,flue}/0.21}{A - O_{2,\,flue}/0.21}\times 100 = \frac{O_{2,\,flue}}{0.21\,A - O_{2,\,flue}}\times 100$$

Now, $A = N_{2,\,flue}/0.79$, hence:

$$\%\text{EA} = \frac{O_{2,\,flue}}{0.21 \cdot (N_{2,\,flue}/0.79) - O_{2,\,flue}}\times 100 = \frac{O_{2,\,flue}}{0.2658\,N_{2,\,flue} - O_{2,\,flue}}\times 100$$

In percentage units:

$$\boxed{\;\%\text{Excess Air} = \frac{\%\,\text{O}_2}{0.264\,\%\,\text{N}_2 - \%\,\text{O}_2}\times 100\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **0.264** is the ratio $\frac{21}{79}$ (mole-fraction ratio of O₂ to N₂ in air) — committed to memory, this formula is the **single most-tested relation** in KTU Fuel Engineering papers.

## 3.4 Worked Example A — Theoretical Air from Ultimate Analysis

**Problem.** A coal has the following ultimate analysis (by mass): C = 80 %, H₂ = 5 %, O₂ = 3 %, S = 2 %, N₂ = 1.5 %, Ash = 8.5 %, Moisture = 0 %. Calculate:
(a) The theoretical mass of air required per kg of coal.
(b) The theoretical volume of air in Nm³ per kg of coal.
(c) The actual air required if 30 % excess air is supplied.

**SOLUTION (Step-by-step, no shortcut).**

**(a) Mass of O₂ required per kg of fuel:**

| Component | kg in 1 kg fuel | kg O₂ per kg element | kg O₂ contributed |
|-----------|-----------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| C | 0.80 | 2.667 | $0.80 \times 2.667 = 2.1336$ |
| H₂ | 0.05 | 8.000 | $0.05 \times 8.000 = 0.4000$ |
| S | 0.02 | 1.000 | $0.02 \times 1.000 = 0.0200$ |
| O₂ (in fuel) | 0.03 | –1.000 | $0.03 \times (-1) = -0.0300$ |

$$m_{O_2,\,min} = 2.1336 + 0.4000 + 0.0200 - 0.0300 = 2.5236 \;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

$$M_{air,\,theoretical} = \frac{2.5236}{0.232} = 10.878 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

$$\boxed{M_{air,\,theoretical} \approx 10.88 \;\text{kg air/kg coal}}$$

**(b) Volume basis:**

$$V_{O_2,\,min} = \frac{2.5236}{32} \times 22.4 = 1.7665 \;\text{Nm}^3\,\text{O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

$$V_{air,\,theoretical} = \frac{1.7665}{0.21} = 8.412 \;\text{Nm}^3\,\text{air/kg fuel}$$

$$\boxed{V_{air,\,theoretical} \approx 8.41 \;\text{Nm}^3\,\text{air/kg coal}}$$

**(c) With 30 % excess air:**

$$A_{actual} = A_0 \times (1 + 0.30) = 8.412 \times 1.30 = 10.936 \;\text{Nm}^3\,\text{air/kg fuel}$$

$$\boxed{A_{actual} \approx 10.94 \;\text{Nm}^3\,\text{air/kg coal}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification tip:** Always re-check by using the mass ratio. $10.878 \times 1.30 = 14.14$ kg air/kg fuel. Multiplying by the ratio $\frac{22.4}{28.97} = 0.7733$, we get $14.14 \times 0.7733 = 10.94$ Nm³/kg — matches perfectly.

## 3.5 Worked Example B — Excess Air from Orsat Analysis

**Problem.** A furnace uses a fuel oil. The dry flue-gas analysis by Orsat is: CO₂ = 12 %, O₂ = 4 %, CO = 1 %, N₂ = 83 %. Calculate the % excess air supplied.

**SOLUTION.**

**Check:** $12 + 4 + 1 + 83 = 100$ ✓

**Apply the master formula:**

$$\%\text{EA} = \frac{\%\,\text{O}_2}{0.264\,\%\,\text{N}_2 - \%\,\text{O}_2}\times 100$$

$$= \frac{4}{0.264 \times 83 - 4}\times 100 = \frac{4}{21.912 - 4}\times 100 = \frac{4}{17.912}\times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%\text{Excess Air} = 22.33\,\%}$$

**Cross-verification by direct method (N₂-tracer).**

Assume 100 Nm³ of dry flue gas. Then $N_{2,\,flue} = 83$ Nm³. Since this N₂ came from air:

$$A_{actual} = \frac{83}{0.79} = 105.06 \;\text{Nm}^3\;\text{air per 100 Nm}^3\;\text{dry flue}$$

O₂ entering = $0.21 \times 105.06 = 22.06$ Nm³. O₂ in flue = 4 Nm³. O₂ consumed = 18.06 Nm³.

Theoretical air: $A_0 = 18.06 / 0.21 = 86.0$ Nm³ air.

$$\%\text{EA} = \frac{105.06 - 86.0}{86.0}\times 100 = 22.16\,\%$$

(Slight rounding difference from 22.33 % — acceptable in KTU valuations.)

## 3.6 Worked Example C — Flue Gas Composition for Known Excess Air

**Problem.** A methane (CH₄) gas is burned with 20 % excess air. Compute the dry flue-gas composition.

**SOLUTION.**

Combustion: $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$

Theoretical O₂ = 2 kmol per kmol CH₄. With 20 % EA → actual O₂ = 2 × 1.2 = 2.4 kmol.

Air supplied = $2.4 / 0.21 = 11.429$ kmol air → N₂ from air = $0.79 \times 11.429 = 9.029$ kmol.

**Products (per kmol CH₄):**

| Species | kmol produced | Source |
|---------|---------------|--------|
| CO₂ | 1.000 | from C |
| H₂O | 2.000 | from H₂ (condenses out in Orsat) |
| O₂ (excess) | 0.400 | $2.4 - 2.0$ |
| N₂ | 9.029 | from air |

**Dry flue gas total** = $1.000 + 0.400 + 9.029 = 10.429$ kmol

| Component | % by volume (dry) |
|-----------|-------------------|
| CO₂ | $\frac{1.000}{10.429}\times 100 = 9.59\,\%$ |
| O₂ | $\frac{0.400}{10.429}\times 100 = 3.84\,\%$ |
| N₂ | $\frac{9.029}{10.429}\times 100 = 86.57\,\%$ |

$$\boxed{\text{Dry Flue Gas} \approx 9.6\,\%\;\text{CO}_2,\; 3.8\,\%\;\text{O}_2,\; 86.6\,\%\;\text{N}_2}$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Master Algorithm for Excess-Air Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Fuel Combustion Problem"] --> B{"Type of Input?"}
    B -- Ultimate Analysis --> C["Calculate Minimum O2 Demand"]
    C --> D["Apply C, H2, S stoichiometry"]
    D --> E["Subtract intrinsic fuel O2"]
    E --> F["Compute Theoretical Air A0"]
    F --> G["Apply Excess Air % to get Actual Air A"]
    B -- Orsat Flue Gas Analysis --> H["Identify % CO2, % CO, % O2, % N2"]
    H --> I["Apply N2 Tracer: N2 flue = 0.79 x A"]
    I --> J["Compute O2 excess = 0.21 x A - O2 used"]
    J --> K["Solve % EA = O2 flue / 0.264 N2 flue - O2 flue x 100"]
    G --> L["Compute Flue Gas Composition on Dry Basis"]
    K --> L
    L --> M["Validate: Sum of percentages = 100"]
    M --> N["End: Final Combustion Report"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Combustion Reactor with Mass Balances

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN["INPUT STREAMS"]
        F["Fuel (kg) - C, H2, S, O2, N2, Ash"]
        AIR["Actual Air (Nm3) - 21% O2 + 79% N2"]
    end
    subgraph RXN["FURNACE / COMBUSTOR"]
        BURN["Combustion Reactions: C + O2 -> CO2, H2 + 0.5 O2 -> H2O, S + O2 -> SO2"]
    end
    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT STREAMS"]
        FG["Dry Flue Gas (Nm3) - CO2, CO, O2, N2, SO2"]
        H2O["Water Vapour (Nm3) - H2O from H2 combustion + Moisture"]
        ASH["Solid Ash (kg)"]
    end
    F --> BURN
    AIR --> BURN
    BURN --> FG
    BURN --> H2O
    BURN --> ASH
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Cause-Effect Chain of Excess Air Variation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Increase Excess Air"] --> P2["More N2 + O2 enters furnace"]
    P2 --> P3["Larger mass of flue gas"]
    P3 --> P4["Higher sensible heat loss in stack"]
    P4 --> P5["Lower thermal efficiency"]
    P1 --> P6["Lower peak flame temperature"]
    P6 --> P7["Reduced NOx formation"]
    P6 --> P8["Slower reaction rate at low T"]
    P8 --> P9["Possible rise in CO and soot"]
```

## 4.4 Decision Logic — Choosing the Right Formula

| Given Input | Use Formula | Final Unknown |
|-------------|-------------|---------------|
| Ultimate analysis + %EA | $A = A_0(1 + \%EA/100)$ | Actual air, wet & dry flue gas |
| Orsat analysis only | $\%\text{EA} = \frac{\%\,\text{O}_2}{0.264\,\%\,\text{N}_2 - \%\,\text{O}_2}\times 100$ | % Excess air |
| Fuel composition + actual air | $A_0 = A/(1 + \%EA/100)$ | %EA, theoretical air |
| Pure gas (CH₄, C₂H₆, CO, H₂) | Mole balance, not mass balance | Flue gas in mole % |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 marks = 6 marks)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the terms: (i) Stoichiometric Air, (ii) Excess Air, and (iii) Air-Fuel Ratio. How does excess air affect the thermal efficiency of a furnace?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(i) Stoichiometric Air (1 m):** The minimum quantity of air required to supply just enough oxygen for the complete combustion of a unit mass (or volume) of fuel. Also called *theoretical* or *required* air.
- **(ii) Excess Air (1 m):** The quantity of air supplied *over and above* stoichiometric air, expressed as a percentage of the theoretical air. Its purpose is to ensure complete combustion despite imperfect fuel-air mixing.
- **(iii) Air-Fuel Ratio (½ m):** The mass (or volume) of air supplied per unit mass (or volume) of fuel.
- **Effect on efficiency (½ m):** Increasing excess air lowers flame temperature, increases the volume of hot flue gas leaving the stack, and consequently *reduces* furnace thermal efficiency due to higher stack losses. However, insufficient excess air produces CO, smoke, and unburnt fuel — also reducing efficiency.

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**The Orsat analysis of a flue gas is: CO₂ = 14 %, O₂ = 5 %, CO = 1 %, N₂ = 80 %. Calculate the percentage of excess air supplied.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$\%\text{EA} = \frac{\%\,\text{O}_2}{0.264\,\%\,\text{N}_2 - \%\,\text{O}_2}\times 100 = \frac{5}{0.264 \times 80 - 5}\times 100$$

$$= \frac{5}{21.12 - 5}\times 100 = \frac{5}{16.12}\times 100 = 31.02\,\%$$

$$\boxed{\%\;\text{Excess Air} \approx 31.0\,\%}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **Attempt any ONE full question.**

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**A petroleum fuel has the following ultimate analysis: C = 84 %, H = 12 %, S = 2 %, O = 1 %, N = 0.5 %, Ash = 0.5 %. It is burned with 25 % excess air. Calculate:**
**(a)** The theoretical mass and volume of air required per kg of fuel. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The composition of dry flue gas by volume. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — A(a)

**Given:** C = 0.84, H = 0.12, S = 0.02, O = 0.01, N = 0.005, Ash = 0.005 kg/kg fuel.

**Step 1 — Minimum O₂ demand per kg of fuel:**

$$m_{O_2,\,min} = \frac{8}{3}(0.84) + 8(0.12) + 1(0.02) - 1(0.01)$$

$$= 2.240 + 0.960 + 0.020 - 0.010 = 3.210\;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical mass of air:**

$$M_{air,\,0} = \frac{3.210}{0.232} = 13.84\;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical volume of air:**

$$V_{O_2,\,min} = \frac{3.210}{32} \times 22.4 = 2.247\;\text{Nm}^3\,\text{O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

$$V_{air,\,0} = \frac{2.247}{0.21} = 10.70\;\text{Nm}^3\,\text{air/kg fuel}$$

$$\boxed{M_{air,\,0} = 13.84\;\text{kg/kg fuel} \quad ; \quad V_{air,\,0} = 10.70\;\text{Nm}^3/\text{kg fuel}}$$

#### Model Solution — A(b)

**Step 1 — Actual air supplied (25 % excess):**

$$A = A_0(1.25) \Rightarrow A_{mass} = 13.84 \times 1.25 = 17.30\;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

$$A_{vol} = 10.70 \times 1.25 = 13.375\;\text{Nm}^3\,\text{air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 2 — Flue-gas components per kg of fuel:**

| Species | Produced / Supplied | Calculation | Nm³/kg fuel |
|---------|---------------------|-------------|-------------|
| CO₂ | from C: $0.84 \times \frac{22.4}{12}$ | $0.84 \times 1.867$ | **1.568** |
| SO₂ | from S: $0.02 \times \frac{22.4}{32}$ | $0.02 \times 0.700$ | **0.014** |
| O₂ (excess) | $0.21 \times A_{excess} = 0.21 \times 0.25\,A_0$ | $0.21 \times 0.25 \times 10.70$ | **0.562** |
| N₂ (from air) | $0.79 \times A$ | $0.79 \times 13.375$ | **10.566** |
| N₂ (from fuel) | $0.005 \times \frac{22.4}{28}$ | $0.005 \times 0.800$ | **0.004** |
| **Total dry flue** | | | **12.714** |

**Step 3 — Composition in %:**

$$\%\text{CO}_2 = \frac{1.568}{12.714}\times 100 = 12.33\,\%$$

$$\%\text{SO}_2 = \frac{0.014}{12.714}\times 100 = 0.11\,\%$$

$$\%\text{O}_2 = \frac{0.562}{12.714}\times 100 = 4.42\,\%$$

$$\%\text{N}_2 = \frac{10.566 + 0.004}{12.714}\times 100 = 83.14\,\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Dry Flue Gas:}\;12.33\%\,\text{CO}_2,\; 0.11\%\,\text{SO}_2,\; 4.42\%\,\text{O}_2,\; 83.14\%\,\text{N}_2}$$

**Verification:** $12.33 + 0.11 + 4.42 + 83.14 = 100.00$ ✓

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**A furnace burns a coal with the following ultimate analysis: C = 70 %, H = 5 %, O = 8 %, S = 1 %, N = 1.5 %, Ash = 14.5 %. The Orsat analysis of the dry flue gas is: CO₂ = 13 %, CO = 1 %, O₂ = 6 %, N₂ = 80 %.**
**(a)** Calculate the percentage of excess air supplied. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Determine (i) the mass of air actually supplied per kg of coal, and (ii) the mass of dry flue gas produced per kg of coal. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — B(a)

**Step 1 — Direct formula:**

$$\%\text{EA} = \frac{\%\,\text{O}_2}{0.264\,\%\,\text{N}_2 - \%\,\text{O}_2}\times 100 = \frac{6}{0.264 \times 80 - 6}\times 100$$

$$= \frac{6}{21.12 - 6}\times 100 = \frac{6}{15.12}\times 100 = 39.68\,\%$$

$$\boxed{\%\;\text{Excess Air} \approx 39.7\,\%}$$

#### Model Solution — B(b)

**Step 1 — Theoretical O₂ demand per kg of coal:**

$$m_{O_2,\,min} = \frac{8}{3}(0.70) + 8(0.05) + 1(0.01) - 1(0.08)$$

$$= 1.8667 + 0.4000 + 0.0100 - 0.0800 = 2.1967\;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg coal}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical air (mass basis):**

$$A_0 = \frac{2.1967}{0.232} = 9.469\;\text{kg air/kg coal}$$

**Step 3 — Actual air with 39.68 % excess:**

$$A_{actual} = 9.469 \times 1.3968 = 13.224\;\text{kg air/kg coal}$$

$$\boxed{A_{actual} = 13.22\;\text{kg air/kg coal}}$$

**Step 4 — Dry flue-gas mass per kg of coal (component-wise on mass basis):**

For 1 kg of coal, the dry flue gas consists of:
- CO₂: from C → $0.70 \times \frac{44}{12} = 2.567$ kg
- CO: from C (incomplete) → assume all C unaccounted goes to CO. Total C = 0.70 kg; let $x$ = kg C → CO₂, $y$ = kg C → CO. From flue gas, $x = 0.70 - y$.
  - Mass CO₂ produced = $0.70 - y$, mass CO produced = $\frac{28}{12}y = 2.333\,y$
  - From flue gas volume: CO₂ : CO = 13 : 1 by volume, hence mass ratio = $13 \times 44 : 1 \times 28 = 572 : 28$
  - So $(0.70 - y) : (2.333\,y) = 572 : 28 \Rightarrow$ solving gives $y \approx 0.014$ kg C.
  - C to CO₂ = 0.686 kg → CO₂ mass = $0.686 \times 44/12 = 2.515$ kg.
  - C to CO = 0.014 kg → CO mass = $0.014 \times 28/12 = 0.033$ kg.
- O₂ (excess): $0.21 \times (A - A_0)$ on volume basis; convert to mass using $32/22.4 = 1.429$ kg/Nm³.
  - $V_{O_2,\,excess} = 0.21 \times 0.3968 \times (9.469 / 1.2041)$... *(simpler: use N₂ tracer)*
  - N₂ in flue = $0.80 \times V_{dry} = 0.79 \times V_{air,actual} \Rightarrow V_{air,actual} = 0.80\,V_{dry}/0.79$
  - Excess O₂ volume = $0.06\,V_{dry}$ (from Orsat); mass O₂ = $0.06\,V_{dry} \times 1.429$
- SO₂: from S → $0.01 \times 64/32 = 0.020$ kg
- N₂: from air → $0.79 \times V_{air,actual} \times 1.2507$ kg; plus N₂ from fuel = $0.015$ kg

**Step 5 — Cleaner approach: N₂-tracer method.**

Assume 100 Nm³ of dry flue gas. Then:
- $V_{N_2} = 80$ Nm³ → $V_{air,actual} = 80/0.79 = 101.27$ Nm³ air per 100 Nm³ dry FG.
- $V_{O_2,\,supplied} = 0.21 \times 101.27 = 21.27$ Nm³.
- $V_{O_2,\,flue} = 6$ Nm³ → $V_{O_2,\,consumed} = 15.27$ Nm³.
- $V_{air,\,theoretical} = 15.27/0.21 = 72.71$ Nm³ per 100 Nm³ dry FG.

Per kg of coal: $V_{air,\,0} = 9.469 \times (22.4/28.97) = 7.323$ Nm³/kg coal.

Scale factor: $1\text{ kg coal} \leftrightarrow \frac{7.323}{72.71}\times 100 = 10.07$ Nm³ dry FG/kg coal.

**Step 6 — Mass of dry flue gas per kg of coal:**

Average molecular weight of dry FG:
$$M_{FG} = 0.13(44) + 0.01(28) + 0.06(32) + 0.80(28) = 5.72 + 0.28 + 1.92 + 22.40 = 30.32\;\text{kg/kmol}$$

$$m_{FG} = \frac{10.07}{22.4} \times 30.32 = 0.4496 \times 30.32 = 13.63\;\text{kg dry FG/kg coal}$$

$$\boxed{m_{FG,\,dry} \approx 13.63\;\text{kg dry flue gas/kg coal}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract intrinsic O₂ in fuel** (−1 to −2 marks). If a fuel has 8 % O₂, you *must* subtract 0.08 from the O₂ demand.
> 2. **Mixing volume and mass percentages.** 21 % O₂ in air is *by volume*. 23.2 % is *by mass*. Using the wrong one wrecks the entire answer.
> 3. **Using 1/0.21 (= 4.76) instead of 1/0.23 (= 4.31)** when the basis changes. Always state "**on a mass basis**" or "**on a volume basis**".
> 4. **Forgetting N₂ from fuel** when fuel contains nitrogen (coal, heavy oil). It is small but examiners do deduct.
> 5. **Omitting the 0.264 (=21/79) constant** in the Orsat formula. It is a derivation mark.
> 6. **Not verifying the dry-flue-gas total to 100 %.** Examiners reward the verification step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorise This Section**

- ✅ **Stoichiometric O₂ multipliers (kg O₂ per kg element):** $\text{C} \rightarrow 2.667$; $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 8.000$; $\text{S} \rightarrow 1.000$; subtract the **intrinsic O₂** in the fuel.
- ✅ **Universal air conversion factors:** $1/0.21 = 4.76$ (vol); $1/0.232 = 4.31$ (mass). **State the basis explicitly.**
- ✅ **Air composition standard:** 21 % O₂ + 79 % N₂ (volume) — never the reverse.
- ✅ **Master excess-air formula from Orsat:** $\%\text{EA} = \dfrac{\%\text{O}_2}{0.264\,\%\text{N}_2 - \%\text{O}_2}\times 100$. The **0.264 = 21/79** constant is the most-tested number.
- ✅ **N₂ is the tracer:** N₂ in flue gas ÷ 0.79 = actual air supplied (volume basis).
- ✅ **Excess O₂ in flue gas** = 0.21 × (Actual air − Theoretical air).
- ✅ **CO + CO₂ split:** When C forms both CO₂ and CO, the ratio of their *volumes* equals the *mole* ratio (1 kmol CO₂ = 12 kg C; 1 kmol CO = 12 kg C).
- ✅ **Always verify:** sum of dry-flue-gas percentages = 100.
- ✅ **Industrial excess-air values (rule of thumb):** Solid fuels ≈ 15–30 %; Liquid fuels ≈ 5–15 %; Gaseous fuels ≈ 2–5 %.
- ✅ **Effects of excess air:** ↑ excess air → ↓ flame T → ↑ stack loss → ↓ efficiency, but ↑ combustion completeness.
- ✅ **Higher Heating Value (HHV) vs Lower Heating Value (LHV):** All combustion calculations in this module use LHV-equivalent stoichiometry; the latent heat of H₂O in flue gas is *lost* unless condensed.

> **Master the three core sub-skills:**
> 1. **Forward problem** (Ultimate Analysis → Air Required) — for design.
> 2. **Inverse problem** (Orsat Analysis → % Excess Air) — for monitoring.
> 3. **Flue-gas composition problem** (Fuel + %EA → Dry Flue Gas) — for emission and efficiency work.

These three sub-skills cover ~90 % of every KTU Part-B question on this topic. Practise at least two numericals from each category before the exam.
